9. 💬 Exam Answer Modes

FormatPromptKey Points
Long Essay"Describe the virology, clinical features, diagnosis, management, and prevention of Mpox with reference to the 2022 global outbreak."Virology: Orthopoxvirus, dsDNA; Clade I (Congo, Severe) vs Clade IIb (2022 Global, Sexual MSM, Mild); 2022 Outbreak: Sexual Transmission MSM Networks; Anogenital/Oral Lesion Predilection; Few/Solitary Lesions; Mild/Absent Prodrome; Rectal Proctitis Common; High STI Co-infection; Low CFR; Clinical: Incubation 5-21d; Prodrome: Fever, Lymphadenopathy (Key vs VZV/HSV), Myalgia; Rash Evolution: Macule→Papule→Vesicle→Pustule(Umbilicated)→Crust→Scab; Infectious: Prodrome→All Scabs Fallen; Diagnosis: Lesion Swab (Dry) PCR (Orthopox+MPXV Specific) = Gold Standard; Case Def: Suspected/Probable/Confirmed; Differentials: VZV (Centripetal, Crops, No Nodes), HSV (Grouped Vesicles, Recurrent), Syphilis (Palms/Soles, RPR+), Molluscum (Firm Domed Papules); Management: Mild Supportive/Isolate; Moderate/High Risk/Severe: Tecovirimat 600mg BD PO×14d (IV if Needed); VIGIV Adjunct Severe/Immunocomp; Isolation: Contact+Droplet (Airborne AGP); Until All Scabs Fallen+Fresh Skin (2-4w); Vaccination: MVA-BN (JYNNEOS) 3rd Gen Non-Replicating Safe HIV; 2 Doses 0,28d (SC 0.5mL / ID 0.1mL Dose-Sparing); PEP ≤4d (Up to 14d); PrEP High-Risk MSM; Special: HIV Test All; Advanced CD4<200 Severe→Tecovirimat Early+VIGIV+ART Optimise; CYP3A4 Interactions; Pregnancy: ↑ Severe/Vertical; Tecovirimat/MVA-BN Benefit>Risk; C-Section if Genital Lesions
Short Note"Tecovirimat for Mpox — Indications, Dose, Mechanism, Duration."Indications: Severe Disease; High Risk for Severe (Advanced HIV CD4<200, Immunocompromised, Pregnancy, Children <8, Atopic Dermatitis, Complications: Encephalitis, Sepsis, Ocular); Dose: PO 600mg (3×200mg Caps) BD × 14 Days (Weight-Based if <40kg); IV 200mg BD × 14 Days (If Unable Oral; Renal Adjust CrCl<30); Mechanism: VP37 Inhibitor (Blocks Viral Egress from Infected Cells); Duration: 14 Days; Safety: Generally Well Tolerated (Headache, Nausea); CYP3A4 Substrate (Check Interactions with ART: Avoid Strong Inducers EFV/ETV/NVP/RPV)
Viva"How does the 2022 Mpox clinical phenotype differ from classic endemic Mpox?"Classic Endemic (Clade I/IIa): Centrifugal Rash (Face→Extremities), Prominent Prodrome (Fever, Lymphadenopathy), More Lesions, Higher Severity/CFR; 2022 Outbreak (Clade IIb): Anogenital/Perianal/Oral Lesion Predilection (Inoculation Site), Often Few/Solitary Lesions, Mild/Absent Prodrome (Afebrile Common), Rectal Proctitis Common (Pain, Bleeding, Tenesmus), High STI Co-infection (GC, CT, Syphilis, HIV), Lower CFR (~0.1-0.3%), Predominantly MSM Sexual Networks
Ward Round"HIV+ Man (CD4 150) with Mpox — 50 Lesions, Oral Ulcers, Rectal Pain. Management?"Severity: Moderate-Severe (High Risk due to CD4<200)؛ Start Tecovirimat 600mg BD PO × 14 Days Immediately (Low Threshold)؛ Assess Drug Interactions: Avoid if on Strong CYP3A4 Inducers (EFV, ETV, NVP, RPV); Caution with Boosters (DRV/COBI)؛ Supportive: Analgesia (Opioids if Needed), Topical Anaesthetics (Oral/Rectal), Stool Softeners, Hydration؛ Monitor for Secondary Infection, Progression (Sepsis, Encephalitis) → Escalate to IV Tecovirimat + VIGIV if Severe؛ ART Optimisation Urgent (If Not on ART or Failing); Adherence Counselling؛ Isolation: Contact+Droplet Precautions Until All Scabs Fallen؛ Contact Trace: Sexual/Household/Healthcare Contacts → PEP (MVA-BN) ≤4d
Last-Night"Mpox: Orthopox, Clade I Severe, Clade IIb 2022 Global Sexual MSM Mild. 2022: Anogenital/Oral Lesions, Few/Solitary, Mild Prodrome, Rectal Proctitis, High STI Co-inf. Incub 5-21d. Prodrome: Fever, Lymphadenopathy (Key), Myalgia. Rash: Macule→Papule→Vesicle→Pustule(Umbilicated)→Crust→Scab. Dx: Lesion Swab Dry→PCR Orthopox+MPXV. Diff: VZV Centripetal Crops No Nodes; HSV Grouped Vesicles Recurrent; Syphilis Palms/Soles RPR+; Molluscum Firm Domed. Mgmt: Mild Supportive; Mod/High Risk/Severe Tecovirimat 600mg BD PO×14d (VP37 Inhib); Severe IV+VIGIV. Isolation: Contact+Droplet (Airborne AGP) Until All Scabs Fallen. Vaccine: MVA-BN (JYNNEOS) 3rd Gen Non-Replicating Safe HIV; 2 Doses 0,28d SC/ID; PEP ≤4d(Up To 14d); PrEP High-Risk MSM. HIV: Test All, CD4<200 Severe→Tecov Early+VIGIV+ART Optim; CYP3A4 Interacts. Preg: ↑Severe/Vertical; Tecov/MVA-BN Benefit>Risk; C-Sect if Genital Lesions."Compressed.

10. 📌 Summary

  • Virology: Orthopoxvirus, dsDNA; Clade I (Congo Basin, Severe, CFR 1-10%); Clade IIb (2022 Global Outbreak, Sexual MSM Networks, Milder, CFR ~0.1-0.3%)
  • Transmission: Close Contact (Skin/Mucosal/Fomite/Residential); 2022: Sexual Networks Predominant (MSM)
  • Clinical: Incubation 5-21 Days; Prodrome: Fever, Lymphadenopathy (Generalised/Inguinal — KEY vs VZV/HSV), Myalgia, 1-5 Days; Rash Evolution: Macules → Papules → Vesicles → Pustules (Umbilicated) → Crusts → Scabs Over 2-4 Weeks; Infectious: Prodrome Until ALL Scabs Fallen + Fresh Skin
  • 2022 Phenotype: Anogenital/Perianal/Oral Lesion Predilection; Often Few/Solitary; Mild/Absent Prodrome; Rectal Proctitis Common; High STI Co-infections (GC, CT, Syphilis, HIV)
  • Diagnosis: Lesion Swab (Vigorous, Dry Preferred) → PCR (Orthopoxvirus Generic + MPXV Specific) = Gold Standard; Case Definitions: Suspected / Probable / Confirmed
  • Differentials: VZV (Centripetal, Crops, No Lymphadenopathy); HSV (Grouped Vesicles, Recurrent); Syphilis (Palms/Soles, Copper-Coloured, RPR+); Molluscum (Firm Domed Umbilicated Papules, No Systemic)
  • Management: Mild: Supportive + Isolation; Moderate/High Risk/Severe: Tecovirimat 600mg BD PO × 14 Days (IV if Unable Oral; VP37 Inhibitor); VIGIV Adjunct for Severe/Complicated/Immunocompromised
  • Infection Control: Contact + Droplet Precautions (Gown, Gloves, Eye Protection, FFP2/N95); Airborne (Negative Pressure) for AGPs; Isolation Until All Scabs Fallen + Fresh Skin (2-4 Weeks)
  • Vaccination: MVA-BN (JYNNEOS/IMVANEX) = 3rd Gen, Replication-Deficient, SAFE in HIV/Immunocompromised; 2 Doses 0, 28 Days (SC 0.5mL Standard / ID 0.1mL Dose-Sparing EUA); PEP: High-Risk Contacts ≤4 Days (Up to 14 Days); PrEP: High-Risk MSM, Lab/HCW, Sex Workers
  • Special Populations: HIV: Test All; Advanced (CD4<200) = Severe → Tecovirimat Early, VIGIV, ART Optimisation; CYP3A4 Interactions (Avoid Strong Inducers); Pregnancy: ↑ Severe/Vertical → Tecovirimat/MVA-BN Benefit>Risk, C-Section if Genital Lesions; Children <8y: ↑ Severe → Tecovirimat Weight-Based

11. ❓ MCQs (10)

  1. Mpox Virus Classification and 2022 Outbreak Clade?

A. Parapoxvirus, Clade I B. Orthopoxvirus, Clade IIb C. Orthopoxvirus, Clade I D. Parapoxvirus, Clade IIa

Answer: B. Mpox = Orthopoxvirus; 2022 Global Outbreak = Clade IIb (B.1 Lineage).

  1. Key Clinical Feature Distinguishing Mpox from Varicella (Chickenpox)?

A. Centripetal Rash B. Crops at Different Stages C. Lymphadenopathy (Generalised/Inguinal) D. Vesicles on Erythematous Base

Answer: C. Lymphadenopathy is Classic for Mpox; Absent in VZV.

  1. 2022 Mpox Outbreak — Predominant Transmission Route and Population?

A. Respiratory, General Population B. Sexual Contact, MSM Networks C. Animal Contact, Rural Africa D. Healthcare, Nosocomial

Answer: B. 2022 Clade IIb Spread Predominantly via Sexual Networks in MSM.

  1. Diagnostic Specimen of Choice for Mpox Confirmation?

A. Blood PCR B. Lesion Swab (Vigorous, Dry) PCR C. Urine PCR D. Throat Swab PCR

Answer: B. Vigorous Swab of Lesion Roof/Fluid (Vesicle/Pustule) → PCR for MPXV DNA = Gold Standard.

  1. Tecovirimat — Mechanism of Action?

A. DNA Polymerase Inhibitor B. VP37 Inhibitor (Blocks Viral Egress) C. Neuraminidase Inhibitor D. Protease Inhibitor

Answer: B. Tecovirimat Targets VP37 Protein → Blocks Viral Egress from Infected Cells.

  1. Tecovirimat Indication — Which Patient Qualifies for Treatment?

A. Mild, 10 Lesions, Immunocompetent B. Advanced HIV (CD4 100), 30 Lesions, Oral Ulcers C. Asymptomatic Contact D. Past Infection, IgG Positive

Answer: B. High Risk for Severe (Advanced HIV CD4<200) = Tecovirimat Indicated.

  1. MVA-BN (JYNNEOS) Vaccine — Key Safety Feature?

A. Live Replication-Competent B. Replication-Deficient (Non-Replicating) = Safe in HIV/Immunocompromised C. Single Dose Only D. Contraindicated in Pregnancy

Answer: B. MVA-BN = Modified Vaccinia Ankara, Replication-Deficient = Safe in HIV/Immunocompromised (Unlike ACAM2000).

  1. Post-Exposure Prophylaxis (PEP) Window for MVA-BN?

A. ≤24 Hours Only B. Ideally ≤4 Days (Up to 14 Days May Modify Disease) C. ≤7 Days Only D. ≤21 Days

Answer: B. Ideally Within 4 Days Post-Exposure; Up to 14 Days May Still Modify Disease Severity.

  1. Infection Control — Duration of Isolation for Confirmed Mpox?

A. 7 Days from Rash Onset B. 14 Days from Rash Onset C. Until All Scabs Fallen + Fresh Intact Skin Formed D. 21 Days Fixed

Answer: C. Infectious from Prodrome Until ALL Scabs Fallen + Fresh Skin (Typically 2-4 Weeks).

  1. Mpox in Pregnancy — Delivery Recommendation if Active Genital Lesions?

A. Vaginal Delivery Encouraged B. Cesarean Section C. Induction at 37 Weeks D. No Specific Recommendation

Answer: B. C-Section if Active Genital Lesions to Prevent Neonatal Contact Transmission.

12. 📋 SBAs (5)

  1. 35-year-old MSM, HIV+ (CD4 180, on DTG/3TC), presents with 10-day history of fever, inguinal lymphadenopathy, 15 perianal umbilicated pustules, rectal pain. PCR confirms Mpox. Best Management?

A. Supportive Care Only B. Tecovirimat 600mg BD PO × 14 Days (Check DTG Interaction — None Significant) C. Cidofovir IV D. MVA-BN Vaccine Now

Answer: B. Advanced HIV (CD4<200) = High Risk → Tecovirimat Indicated Early; DTG (Integrase Inhibitor) = No Significant CYP3A4 Interaction with Tecovirimat.

  1. Healthcare Worker, Needlestick from Mpox Patient (Vesicle Fluid). Unvaccinated. PEP Management?

A. No PEP Needed B. MVA-BN 0.5mL SC (or 0.1mL ID) Within 4 Days (Up to 14 Days) + 21-Day Monitoring C. ACAM2000 D. Tecovirimat 14 Days

Answer: B. High-Risk Occupational Exposure → MVA-BN PEP Ideally ≤4 Days (Up to 14 Days) + Symptom Monitoring 21 Days.

13. 🔑 Answer Keys

MCQsSBAs
1-B, 2-C, 3-B, 4-B, 5-B, 6-B, 7-B, 8-B, 9-C, 10-B1-B, 2-B

Version: Full FCPS/MRCP Template Upgrade

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 15.1.14 Neisseria Gonorrhoeae Infections Uncomplicated And Disseminated

Related: [[Urethritis Syndrome (Mucopurulent vs Non-Gonococcal)]], [[Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (PID)]], [[Genital Ulcer Disease Syndrome (GUD)]], [[Disseminated Gonococcal Infection (DGI)]], [[Chlamydia trachomatis (Genital, LGV, Trachoma)]]

[!important]
Neisseria gonorrhoeae = Gram-negative diplococcus (intracellular in PMNL). Rising antimicrobial resistance (ciprofloxacin, azithromycin, emerging ceftriaxone). Dual therapy (ceftriaxone + azithromycin/doxycycline) no longer standard — ceftriazone monotherapy preferred (high barrier). Culture for AST essential (resistance monitoring).

1. Learning Objectives

  • Recognise clinical syndromes (urethritis, cervicitis, pharyngitis, proctitis, DGI)
  • Apply diagnostic testing (NAAT = gold standard; culture for AST)
  • Treat with appropriate regimens (ceftriaxone monotherapy preferred)
  • Manage antimicrobial resistance and treatment failure
  • Understand DGI and its management

2. Aetiology and Transmission

  • N. gonorrhoeae = fastidious, Gram-negative diplococcus, oxidase-positive
  • Transmission: Sexual (genital, anal, oral); vertical (neonatal)
  • Incubation: 2–7 days (1–14 days)
  • Asymptomatic: Men 10–15%, Women 50–80%

3. Clinical Syndromes

Uncomplicated Genital Infection

PopulationPresentation
Men (Urethritis)Purulent, copious, yellow-green discharge, dysuria, meatal erythema; onset 2–7d; asymptomatic 10–15%
Women (Cervicitis)Vaginal discharge, intermenstrual bleeding, dysuria; mucopurulent cervicitis (MPC); asymptomatic 50–80%
Rectal (MSM/W)Often asymptomatic; proctitis (pain, mucus, bleeding, tenesmus)
PharyngealUsually asymptomatic; sore throat, erythema; reservoir for resistance

Disseminated Gonococcal Infection (DGI)

  • Incidence: 0.5–3% of gonorrhoea
  • Risk factors: Complement deficiency, menstruation, pregnancy, IVDU, HIV
  • Classic triad: Arthritis-dermatitis syndrome (tenosynovitis + pustular rash + fever)
  • Features: Migratory polyarthralgia → septic arthritis (1–2 joints), pustular/petechial rash (acral), tenosynovitis, fever, chills
  • Rare: Endocarditis, meningitis, hepatitis, myopericarditis

Neonatal Conjunctivitis (Ophthalmia Neonatorum)

  • Onset: 2–5 days post-delivery
  • Features: Profuse purulent discharge, eyelid oedema, chemosis
  • Risk: Corneal ulceration, perforation, blindness

4. Diagnosis

NAAT (Gold Standard for Diagnosis)

SpecimenMenWomenExtra-genital
PreferredFirst-catch urine (first 20mL)Vaginal swab (self) / EndocervicalRectal swab, Pharyngeal swab
AlternativeUrethral swabEndocervicalSelf-collected rectal/pharyngeal
NAAT sensitivity >95%, specificity >99%
NAAT does NOT provide antimicrobial susceptibility — culture required for AST

Culture (Essential for AST and Resistance Surveillance)

IndicationSpecimen
All positive NAAT (ideally)Same as NAAT + pharyngeal
Treatment failureAll sites
DGIBlood, synovial fluid, skin lesion, CSF
NeonatalConjunctival, blood, CSF
Antimicrobial resistance surveillanceAll positive cases

Microscopy (Gram Stain — Rapid, Presumptive)

SpecimenSensitivitySpecificity
Male urethral smear90–95%>95% (≥5 PMNL/hpf + intracellular GNDC)
Female endocervical50–60%95%
Pharyngeal/RectalLowHigh
Gram-negative diplococci (GNDC) intracellular in PMNL = presumptive GC

5. Antimicrobial Resistance (Critical)

Resistance Patterns (WHO GASP / BASHH GRASP)

AntimicrobialResistance TrendClinical Impact
PenicillinsHigh (penicillinase, chromosomal)Not used
TetracyclinesHigh (efflux, ribosomal)Not 1st-line
Fluoroquinolones (Ciprofloxacin)>90% in many regionsNot recommended
AzithromycinRising (mosaic mtrR, 23S rRNA)Not 1st-line monotherapy
CeftriaxoneRare but emerging (mosaic penA, porB mutations)Last reliable option
CefiximeReduced susceptibilityOral alternative (inferior to ceftriaxone)
SpectinomycinLowIM only (not for pharyngeal)

WHO Dual Therapy (Historical) → Current: Ceftriaxone Monotherapy

EraRegimenRationale
Pre-2010CiprofloxacinHigh resistance
2010–2020Ceftriaxone + AzithromycinDual therapy for resistance prevention
Current (WHO 2016, BASHH 2019, CDC 2021)Ceftriaxone 500mg IM single dose (1g if >150kg)High barrier, azithro resistance rising, no added benefit

6. Treatment

Uncomplicated Genital / Rectal / Pharyngeal Infection

Site1st-lineAlternative (Ceftriaxone Allergy)
Urogenital / RectalCeftriaxone 500mg IM single dose (1g if >150kg)Gentamicin 240mg IM single dose + Azithromycin 2g PO single dose
PharyngealCeftriaxone 1g IM single doseGentamicin 240mg IM + Azithromycin 2g PO
Co-treatment for Chlamydia: Add Doxycycline 100mg BD × 7d if CT status unknown or positive
Rationale: High co-infection rate; ceftriaxone does NOT cover CT

Disseminated Gonococcal Infection (DGI)

RegimenDoseDuration
1st-lineCeftriaxone 1g IV q24h24–48h after clinical improvement → switch to oral to complete 7 days total
AlternativeCeftriaxone 1g IM q24hSame
Severe (meningitis/endocarditis)Ceftriaxone 2g IV q12h10–14 days
Switch to oral: Cefixime 400mg BD (if susceptible) or Doxycycline 100mg BD (if CT co-infection)

Neonatal Ophthalmia Neonatorum Prevention & Treatment

ScenarioRegimen
Prophylaxis (delivery)Ceftriaxone 50mg/kg IM/IV single dose (max 125mg) OR Povidone-iodine 2.5% drops
Treatment (confirmed)Ceftriaxone 25–50mg/kg IV/IM q12h × 7d (max 125mg)
Systemic (sepsis/meningitis)Ceftriaxone 50mg/kg IV q12h × 7–10d

Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (PID) — See PID Note

  • Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Doxycycline 100mg BD 14d + Metronidazole 400mg BD 14d

7. Test of Cure (TOC)

ScenarioTimingMethod
Uncomplicated (ceftriaxone)Not routine (cure rate >95%)NAAT at 2w if symptoms persist
Pharyngeal7–14 days post-treatmentNAAT (higher failure risk)
DGIEnd of treatmentCulture + NAAT
Treatment failure1 week post-treatmentCulture + AST mandatory
Pregnancy2–4 weeksNAAT
TOC not routine for uncomplicated genital/rectal after ceftriaxone (cure >95%)
Mandatory culture + AST if treatment failure suspected

8. Partner Management

PopulationLook-back
Genital GC2 weeks
Pharyngeal GC2 weeks
DGI3 months (sexual + household)
NeonatalMother + household
Expedited Partner Therapy (EPT): Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Doxycycline 100mg BD × 7d

9. Special Situations

ScenarioManagement
PregnancyCeftriaxone 500mg IM (1g if >150kg) — safe; avoid spectinomycin
Ceftriaxone Allergy (anaphylaxis)Gentamicin 240mg IM + Azithromycin 2g PO single dose
Ciprofloxacin ResistanceNever use (global resistance >90%)
Azithromycin ResistanceDo not use as monotherapy; doxycycline preferred for CT co-treatment
Ceftriaxone Resistance (rare)Consult specialist; options: Gentamicin + Azithromycin, Cefixime (if susceptible), Carbapenems
HIV Co-infectionSame regimens; screen for syphilis, CT, TV
MSM3-site testing (urethral, rectal, pharyngeal)

10. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • Ceftriaxone 500mg IM = 1st-line uncomplicated GC (1g if >150kg)
  • Pharyngeal GC = Ceftriaxone 1g IM (higher dose)
  • Ceftriaxone does NOT cover Chlamydia → add Doxycycline 100mg BD × 7d
  • Pharyngeal GC higher cure failure → Ceftriaxone 1g; TOC at 2w
  • DGI = Ceftriaxone 1g IV q24h → step down after 24–48h improvement
  • Neonatal ophthalmia prophylaxis = Ceftriaxone 50mg/kg IM (max 125mg)
  • Ciprofloxacin resistance >90% — never use
  • Azithromycin resistance rising — not monotherapy; doxycycline for CT co-treatment
  • Culture + AST mandatory for treatment failure, DGI, pharyngeal, surveillance
  • TOC not routine for uncomplicated genital after ceftriaxone

11. Common Viva Questions

  1. What is the first-line treatment for uncomplicated gonorrhoea?
  2. Why is ceftriaxone dose higher for pharyngeal infection?
  3. What is the management of disseminated gonococcal infection?
  4. How do you manage gonorrhoea in pregnancy?
  5. What is the role of culture in gonorrhoea management?

12. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

  • Ceftriaxone 500mg = genital/rectal; 1g = pharyngeal/DGI/pregnancy
  • Ceftriaxone does NOT cover Chlamydia — MUST add doxycycline if CT status unknown/positive
  • Azithromycin monotherapy NOT recommended (resistance); dual therapy no longer standard
  • Ciprofloxacin resistance >90% — never use
  • TOC for pharyngeal GC at 2 weeks (higher failure)
  • TOC not routine for uncomplicated genital after ceftriaxone
  • Ceftriaxone resistance rare but emerging — monitor GASP/GRASP
  • DGI = arthritis-dermatitis-tenosynovitis triad; ceftriaxone IV

13. Mnemonics

  • GC treatment: Ceftriaxone IM (500mg genital/rectal, 1g pharyngeal/DGI)
  • GC + CT co-treatment: Ceftriaxone + Doxycycline 7d
  • DGI triad: Arthritis + Dermatitis + Tenosynovitis = ADT
  • GC resistance: Cipro >90%, Azi rising, Ceftriaxone rare but emerging
  • Pharyngeal GC: 1g IM + TOC at 2w
  • Neonatal prophylaxis: Ceftriaxone 50mg/kg IM (max 125mg)

14. One-Page Revision Summary

Gonorrhoea = N. gonorrhoeae

  • Genital: purulent discharge; Pharyngeal: often asymptomatic
  • DGI: arthritis-dermatitis-tenosynovitis triad
  • Rx: Ceftriaxone 500mg IM (genital/rectal); 1g IM (pharyngeal/DGI/pregnancy)
  • Add Doxycycline 100mg BD × 7d for CT co-treatment
  • DGI: Ceftriaxone 1g IV q24h → oral step-down after 24–48h improvement
  • Pharyngeal: 1g IM + TOC at 2w
  • Ciprofloxacin resistance >90% — NEVER use
  • Culture + AST for failure, DGI, pharyngeal, surveillance

15. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • Ceftriaxone 500mg IM (genital/rectal); 1g IM (pharyngeal/DGI/pregnancy)
  • Add Doxycycline 7d for CT co-treatment
  • DGI = ceftriaxone 1g IV → oral step-down
  • Pharyngeal = 1g IM + TOC at 2w
  • Ciprofloxacin resistance >90% (never use)
  • Culture + AST for failure, DGI, pharyngeal

Should Know

  • Neonatal ophthalmia prophylaxis/treatment
  • Ceftriaxone allergy alternative (Gentamicin + Azithromycin)
  • MSM 3-site testing
  • Resistance surveillance (GASP/GRASP)
  • Partner notification 2 weeks

Nice to Know

  • Emerging ceftriaxone resistance (mosaic penA)
  • Spectinomycin (IM alternative, not for pharyngeal)
  • Oral cefixime inferior to ceftriaxone
  • Gonococcal vaccine development
  • Rapid POC NAAT for GC

16. MCQs (10)

  1. First-line treatment for uncomplicated gonococcal urethritis:

A. Ciprofloxacin 500mg BD × 3d

B. Azithromycin 1g stat

C. Ceftriaxone 500mg IM single dose

D. Doxycycline 100mg BD × 7d

  1. Ceftriaxone dose for pharyngeal gonorrhoea:

A. 250mg IM

B. 500mg IM

C. 1g IM single dose

D. 2g IM

  1. Ceftriaxone does NOT cover:

A. N. gonorrhoeae

B. C. trachomatis

C. S. aureus

D. H. influenzae

  1. Disseminated gonococcal infection (DGI) classic triad:

A. Arthritis + rash + fever

B. Arthritis + dermatitis + tenosynovitis

C. Meningitis + arthritis + rash

D. Endocarditis + arthritis + rash

  1. DGI treatment:

A. Ceftriaxone 500mg IM single dose

B. Ceftriaxone 1g IV q24h → oral step-down after 24–48h improvement

C. Ciprofloxacin 500mg BD × 7d

D. Azithromycin 1g stat + Ceftriaxone 500mg IM

  1. Ciprofloxacin resistance in N. gonorrhoeae:

A. <10%

B. 30–40%

C. >90% (not recommended)

D. 50–60%

  1. Pharyngeal gonorrhoea — ceftriaxone dose and TOC:

A. 500mg IM, no TOC

B. 1g IM, TOC at 2 weeks (NAAT)

C. 500mg IM, TOC at 2 weeks

D. 1g IM, no TOC

  1. Ceftriaxone allergy (anaphylaxis) — alternative for GC:

A. Azithromycin 1g stat

B. Gentamicin 240mg IM single dose + Azithromycin 2g PO single dose

C. Doxycycline 100mg BD × 7d

D. Ciprofloxacin 500mg BD

  1. Neonatal ophthalmia neonatorum prophylaxis:

A. Erythromycin ointment

B. Ceftriaxone 50mg/kg IM/IV single dose (max 125mg)

C. Azithromycin oral

D. Ciprofloxacin drops

  1. Test of cure for uncomplicated genital GC after ceftriaxone:

A. Mandatory at 2 weeks

B. Not routine (cure rate >95%)

C. Mandatory at 4 weeks

D. Culture only

17. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 28-year-old man, purulent urethral discharge 3 days, NAAT urine positive for GC, CT negative. Best treatment:

A. Ceftriaxone 500mg IM single dose

B. Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Doxycycline 100mg BD × 7d (CT co-treatment)

C. Azithromycin 1g stat

D. Ceftriaxone 1g IM

  1. A 25-year-old woman, asymptomatic, pharyngeal GC on routine MSM screen. Treatment:

A. Ceftriaxone 500mg IM

B. Ceftriaxone 1g IM single dose (pharyngeal = higher dose)

C. Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Doxycycline 7d

D. Azithromycin 1g stat

  1. A 30-year-old man, fever, migratory joint pain, pustular rash on hands/feet, tenosynovitis. Blood cultures: GC. Diagnosis and Rx:

A. Sepsis — Ceftriaxone 500mg IM

B. DGI — Ceftriaxone 1g IV q24h → oral step-down after 24–48h improvement

C. Reactive arthritis — NSAIDs

C. Septic arthritis — Flucloxacillin

  1. A 22-year-old pregnant woman at 28 weeks, GC positive. Rx:

A. Ceftriaxone 500mg IM

B. Ceftriaxone 1g IM (pregnancy = higher dose)

C. Azithromycin 1g stat

D. Spectinomycin 2g IM

  1. A 24-year-old man, GC positive, NAAT also CT positive. Rx:

A. Ceftriaxone 500mg IM

B. Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Doxycycline 100mg BD × 7d

C. Azithromycin 1g + Ceftriaxone 500mg IM

D. Doxycycline 100mg BD × 7d only

  1. A patient with pharyngeal GC treated with ceftriaxone 1g IM. When to test for cure?

A. 3 days

B. 2 weeks (NAAT)

C. 4 weeks

D. Not needed

  1. A child with neonatal ophthalmia neonatorum (profuse purulent discharge at day 3). Rx:

A. Erythromycin ointment

B. Ceftriaxone 50mg/kg IM/IV single dose (max 125mg)

C. Azithromycin oral

D. Ciprofloxacin drops

  1. A patient with GC, ceftriaxone allergy (anaphylaxis). Best alternative:

A. Azithromycin 2g stat

B. Gentamicin 240mg IM + Azithromycin 2g PO (single dose)

C. Doxycycline 100mg BD × 7d

C. Ciprofloxacin 500mg BD

  1. Ciprofloxacin for gonorrhoea — current recommendation:

A. 1st-line

B. Not recommended (resistance >90%)

C. Alternative if ceftriaxone allergy

D. Only for pharyngeal

  1. Culture for gonorrhoea — when MANDATORY?

A. All cases

B. Treatment failure, DGI, pharyngeal, resistance surveillance

C. Only PID

D. Only neonatal

18. Flashcards

  • Q: GC 1st-line

A: Ceftriaxone 500mg IM (genital/rectal); 1g IM (pharyngeal/DGI/pregnancy)

  • Q: GC + CT co-treatment

A: Ceftriaxone + Doxycycline 100mg BD × 7d

  • Q: Pharyngeal GC

A: Ceftriaxone 1g IM + TOC at 2w

  • Q: DGI

A: Ceftriaxone 1g IV q24h → oral step-down after 24–48h

  • Q: Ciprofloxacin resistance

A: >90% — NEVER use

  • Q: Neonatal prophylaxis

A: Ceftriaxone 50mg/kg IM (max 125mg)

  • Q: DGI triad

A: Arthritis + Dermatitis + Tenosynovitis

  • Q: TOC genital

A: Not routine (cure >95%)

  • Q: TOC pharyngeal

A: 2 weeks (NAAT)

  • Q: Ceftriaxone allergy

A: Gentamicin 240mg IM + Azithromycin 2g PO

19. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQ 1: C — Ceftriaxone 500mg IM single dose.

MCQ 2: C — Pharyngeal = 1g IM (higher dose for poorer penetration).

MCQ 3: B — Ceftriaxone does not cover CT; doxycycline needed.

MCQ 4: B — DGI triad = arthritis + dermatitis + tenosynovitis.

MCQ 5: B — DGI = IV ceftriaxone 1g q24h, step-down after improvement.

MCQ 6: C — Ciprofloxacin resistance >90% globally.

MCQ 7: B — Pharyngeal = 1g IM + TOC at 2w (higher failure rate).

MCQ 8: B — Gentamicin 240mg IM + Azithromycin 2g PO (anaphylaxis to ceftriaxone).

MCQ 9: B — Ceftriaxone 50mg/kg IM/IV single dose (max 125mg).

MCQ 10: B — Uncomplicated genital = cure >95%, no routine TOC.

SBA 1: B — GC + CT co-treatment = ceftriaxone + doxycycline 7d.

SBA 2: B — Pharyngeal = 1g IM.

SBA 3: B — DGI = IV ceftriaxone 1g q24h.

SBA 4: B — Pregnancy = ceftriaxone 1g IM.

SBA 5: B — CT co-infection = add doxycycline 7d.

SBA 6: B — Pharyngeal TOC = 2w NAAT.

SBA 7: B — Neonatal = ceftriaxone 50mg/kg IM/IV.

SBA 8: B — Anaphylaxis to ceftriaxone = gentamicin + azithromycin.

SBA 9: B — Ciprofloxacin resistance >90% = never use.

SBA 10: B — Culture + AST mandatory for failure, DGI, pharyngeal, surveillance.

Status: Full FCPS/MRCP topic note completed — 2026-06-13

Topic Overview

1. Learning Objectives
2. Aetiology and Transmission
3. Clinical Syndromes
Uncomplicated Genital Infection
Disseminated Gonococcal Infection (DGI)
Neonatal Conjunctivitis (Ophthalmia Neonatorum)
4. Diagnosis
NAAT (Gold Standard for Diagnosis)
Culture (Essential for AST and Resistance Surveillance)
Microscopy (Gram Stain — Rapid, Presumptive)
5. Antimicrobial Resistance (Critical)
Resistance Patterns (WHO GASP / BASHH GRASP)
WHO Dual Therapy (Historical) → Current: Ceftriaxone Monotherapy
6. Treatment
Uncomplicated Genital / Rectal / Pharyngeal Infection
Disseminated Gonococcal Infection (DGI)
Neonatal Ophthalmia Neonatorum Prevention & Treatment
Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (PID) — See PID Note
7. Test of Cure (TOC)
8. Partner Management
9. Special Situations
10. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points
11. Common Viva Questions

15. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know
Must Know
Should Know
Nice to Know
16. MCQs (10)
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 15.1.15 Other Viral Stis

Parent Topic: STI MOC → STI Hierarchy

Status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

Priority: ⭐⭐ HIGH (FCPS/MRCP — Emerging/Re-emerging Viral STIs, Zika, Hepatitis A/E, HHV-8, COVID-19)

Source: Davidson 24th Ed Ch 13; WHO/CDC/ECDC Guidelines; FCPS/MRCP Syllabus

1. 🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Describe Zika Virus Sexual Transmission, Congenital Syndrome, Testing, Prevention
  • [ ] Describe Hepatitis A & E Sexual Transmission (MSM Outbreaks), Vaccination, Management
  • [ ] Describe HHV-8 (KSHV) — Kaposi Sarcoma, MCD, PEL, HIV Association
  • [ ] Describe COVID-19 Sexual Transmission Potential, Viral Shedding in Semen, Testicular Involvement
  • [ ] Describe Other Emerging Viral STIs (Monkeypox — See Separate Note, Ebola Sexual Persistence, CMV Sexual Transmission)
  • [ ] Apply Diagnostic Algorithms and Public Health Measures for Each
  • [ ] Answer viva: "Zika Sexual Transmission & Congenital Syndrome" and "Hepatitis A in MSM" and "HHV-8 & HIV" and "Ebola Sexual Persistence"

2. 🧠 Core Concept: Expanding Spectrum of Viral STIs

Other Viral STIs
Established Sexual Transmission
Zika Virus (Flavivirus)
Hepatitis A (Picornavirus) — MSM Outbreaks
Hepatitis E (Hepevirus) — MSM/Immunocompromised
HHV-8 / KSHV (Herpesvirus) — Kaposi, MCD, PEL
Emerging / Re-emerging
SARS-CoV-2 (COVID-19) — Semen Detection, Testicular Tropism
Ebola Virus — Sexual Persistence (Months)
CMV — Sexual Transmission (Seroconversion)
Mpox — See Separate Note
Common Themes
Viral Shedding in Semen/Genital Fluids
Asymptomatic Transmission Possible
Complications in Pregnancy/Immunocompromised
Public Health Surveillance Critical

1️⃣ Zika Virus (ZIKV)

Virology & Transmission

FeatureDetails
VirusFlavivirus (Arbovirus); RNA, Positive-Sense; Related to Dengue, Yellow Fever, West Nile
Primary VectorAedes aegypti / Aedes albopictus (Day-Biting Mosquitoes)
Sexual TransmissionConfirmed (Male→Female, Male→Male, Female→Male); Viral RNA in Semen Prolonged (Months); Vaginal Fluid, Saliva Also
Vertical TransmissionTransplacental (All Trimesters); Perinatal; Breastfeeding (Low Risk)
OtherBlood Transfusion, Lab Exposure
Incubation3-14 Days (Typically 3-7 Days)

Clinical Features

SyndromeFeatures
Asymptomatic~80% Infections
Zika Fever (Symptomatic)Low-Grade Fever, Maculopapular Rash (Pruritic), Conjunctivitis (Non-Purulent), Arthralgia (Small Joints), Myalgia, Headache, Retro-Orbital Pain; Self-Limited 2-7 Days
NeurologicalGuillain-Barré Syndrome (GBS) — ↑ Incidence During Outbreaks; Meningitis, Encephalitis, Myelitis (Rare)
Congenital Zika Syndrome (CZS)Microcephaly (Severe), Cortical Malformations, Ventriculomegaly, Ocular Abnormalities (Chorioretinal Atrophy, Optic Nerve Hypoplasia), Congenital Contractures (Arthrogryposis), Hypertonia, Seizures, Hearing Loss

Diagnosis

TestSpecimenTimingInterpretation
RT-PCR (ZIKV RNA)Serum, Urine, Semen, CSF, Amniotic Fluid, PlacentaSerum: <7 Days; Urine: <21 Days; Semen: MonthsPositive = Acute/Recent Infection
IgM Antibody (ELISA)Serum, CSF≥4 Days Post-OnsetPositive = Recent; Cross-Reactivity with Dengue/Other Flaviviruses (PRNT Needed)
PRNT (Plaque Reduction Neutralisation Test)SerumConfirmatorySpecific Neutralising Antibodies; Differentiates ZIKV from Dengue
WHO Algorithm: RT-PCR First (Acute); If Negative >7 Days OR Convalescent → IgM + PRNT

Sexual Transmission — Key Evidence

  • Male→Female: Documented; Semen RNA Detectable Mean 34 Days (Up to 370 Days); Infectious Virus Cultured ≤69 Days
  • Male→Male: Anal Sex Transmission Documented
  • Female→Male: Vaginal Fluid RNA Detectable; Transmission Documented
  • Asymptomatic Sexual Transmission: Documented

Prevention & Counselling

PopulationRecommendation
Pregnant Women / Couples Planning PregnancyAvoid Travel to Active ZIKV Areas; Condoms or Abstinence Throughout Pregnancy if Partner Exposed
Men with ZIKV Exposure (Symptomatic/Asymptomatic)Condoms/Abstinence ≥3 Months (WHO) / ≥6 Months (CDC Conservative)
Women with ZIKV ExposureCondoms/Abstinence ≥2 Months
Blood DonationDeferral 28-120 Days Post-Exposure/Recovery
Mosquito Bite PreventionDEET, Picaridin, Permethrin Clothing, Screens, Eliminate Breeding Sites

Treatment

  • Supportive Only (Antipyretics, Fluids, Rest); Avoid NSAIDs Until Dengue Ruled Out (Bleeding Risk)
  • GBS: IVIG / Plasmapheresis (Standard)
  • CZS: Multidisciplinary Supportive Care (No Specific Antiviral)

2️⃣ Hepatitis A Virus (HAV) — Sexual Transmission in MSM

Virology & Transmission

FeatureDetails
VirusPicornavirus (Hepatovirus); RNA, Positive-Sense; Non-Enveloped, Stable in Environment
Primary RouteFaecal-Oral (Contaminated Food/Water, Person-to-Person)
Sexual TransmissionAnal-Oral Practices (Rimming), Digital-Anal Contact, Sex Toys — Major Driver in MSM Outbreaks
Incubation15-50 Days (Average 28 Days)
Infectious Period2 Weeks Pre-Jaundice → 1 Week Post-Jaundice

MSM Outbreaks (Global, 2016-2024)

  • Large Outbreaks in Europe, Americas, Australia — Genotype IA/IB
  • Risk Factors: Multiple Partners, Sex-on-Premises Venues, Chemsex, Recreational Drugs, HIV+
  • Co-infections Common: STIs (GC, CT, Syphilis, HIV, HCV)

Clinical Features

PresentationFeatures
AsymptomaticCommon in Children (<70% <6y Symptomatic); Less in Adults
Acute HepatitisFever, Malaise, Anorexia, Nausea, RUQ Pain, Dark Urine, Pale Stools, Jaundice; Self-Limited 2-8 Weeks
ComplicationsFulminant Hepatitis (Rare ~0.1-0.3%); Relapsing/Cholestatic Course (10-15%)

Diagnosis

TestInterpretation
Anti-HAV IgMAcute/Recent Infection (Positive 5-10 Days Post-Exposure, Persists 3-6 Months)
Anti-HAV IgG (Total)Past Infection or Vaccination (Lifelong Immunity)
HAV RNA PCRResearch/Outbreak Investigation; Not Routine

Management

  • Supportive (Rest, Hydration, Avoid Hepatotoxins); No Antiviral
  • Hospitalisation if Fulminant Signs (Coagulopathy, Encephalopathy) or Severe Dehydration
  • Post-Exposure Prophylaxis (Contacts): HAV Vaccine (1 Dose) ± Human Normal Immunoglobulin (HNIG) if High Risk / Immunocompromised / Chronic Liver Disease

Prevention — Vaccination

VaccineScheduleTarget
Inactivated HAV Vaccine (Havrix, Vaqta, Avaxim)2 Doses (0, 6-12 Months)All Children (Routine), MSM, PWID, Chronic Liver Disease, Travelers, HIV+, Occupational Risk
Combined HAV/HBV (Twinrix)3 Doses (0, 1, 6 Months) or Accelerated (0, 7, 21 Days + 12m)Adults Needing Both
MSM = Priority Group for HAV Vaccination (Outbreak Control)

3️⃣ Hepatitis E Virus (HEV)

Virology & Transmission

FeatureDetails
VirusHepevirus (Orthohepevirus A); RNA, Positive-Sense; Genotypes 1-4 (Human)
Genotype 1 & 2Human Only; Faecal-Oral (Waterborne); Developing Countries; Acute Epidemics
Genotype 3 & 4Zoonotic (Pigs, Deer, Boar); Foodborne (Undercooked Pork/Offal); Developed Countries; Chronic in Immunocompromised
Sexual TransmissionPossible (Anal-Oral, MSM Outbreaks Reported); Blood Transfusion Also
Incubation15-60 Days (Average 40 Days)

Clinical Syndromes

SyndromePopulationFeatures
Acute Hepatitis EGeneral (GT1/2/3/4)Similar to HAV; Jaundice, Malaise, RUQ Pain; Self-Limited 2-6 Weeks
Severe in Pregnancy (GT1/2)Pregnant Women (3rd Trimester)Fulminant Hepatitis, Maternal Mortality 10-25%; Vertical Transmission High
Chronic HEV (GT3/4)Immunocompromised (Transplant, HIV CD4<200, Chemo, Steroids)Persistent Viraemia >3 Months → Rapid Fibrosis → Cirrhosis
ExtrahepaticAllNeurological (Guillain-Barré, Neuralgic Amyotrophy, Encephalitis); Renal (Membranous GN); Haematologic

Diagnosis

TestInterpretation
Anti-HEV IgMAcute Infection (Positive ~4 Weeks Post-Exposure, Persists 3-6 Months)
Anti-HEV IgGPast Infection (Persistence Variable)
HEV RNA PCR (Stool/Serum)Viraemia (Acute/Chronic); Genotyping Possible

Management

ScenarioTreatment
Acute (Immunocompetent)Supportive; No Antiviral
Pregnancy (Severe/3rd Trimester)Supportive ICU; Ribavirin Contraindicated (Teratogenic); Consider Early Delivery
Chronic (Immunocompromised)Reduce Immunosuppression if Possible; Ribavirin 600-1000mg/Day × 3 Months (Off-Label, Monitor Haemolysis); PegIFN-α (Transplant) — Risk Rejection

Prevention

  • Sanitation, Safe Water (GT1/2)
  • Thorough Cooking of Pork/Offal (GT3/4)
  • Vaccine (Hecolin® / p239) — Licensed China Only; Efficacy >95% (GT1/4); Not WHO Prequalified Globally
  • Blood Screening (Some Countries)

4️⃣ Human Herpesvirus 8 (HHV-8) / Kaposi Sarcoma Herpesvirus (KSHV)

Virology & Transmission

FeatureDetails
VirusGammaherpesvirus (Rhadinovirus); dsDNA; Latency in B Cells, Endothelial Cells
TransmissionSexual (MSM — Saliva, Semen); Vertical (Mother→Child); Blood/Transfusion; Transplant; Saliva (Household in Endemic Areas)
EpidemiologyHigh in Sub-Saharan Africa, Mediterranean, MSM Populations; Low in General Western Populations
HIV AssociationStrong — HHV-8 Seroprevalence 30-50% in HIV+ MSM vs <5% General

Disease Associations

DiseasePathogenesisClinical Features
Kaposi Sarcoma (KS)HHV-8 Infected Endothelial/Spindle Cells → VEGF, Cytokines → AngioproliferationCutaneous (Violaceous Macules/Plaques/Nodules), Mucosal (Oral, GI, Pulmonary); Lymphoedema; HIV+ = More Aggressive/Disseminated
Multicentric Castleman Disease (MCD)HHV-8 Infected B Cells (Plasmablasts) → IL-6, vIL-6 → LymphoproliferationGeneralised Lymphadenopathy, Fever, Splenomegaly, Cytopenias, Hypergammaglobulinaemia, POEMS-like; HIV+ Associated
Primary Effusion Lymphoma (PEL)HHV-8+ EBV+ B-Cell Lymphoma in Body CavitiesPleural/Pericardial/Peritoneal Effusions (No Mass); HIV+; Poor Prognosis
KSHV Inflammatory Cytokine Syndrome (KICS)vIL-6, Cytokine StormFever, Cytopenias, Splenomegaly, Oedema, Hypoalbuminaemia; HIV+

Diagnosis

TestUse
HHV-8 PCR (Blood, Lesion, Effusion)Viral Load, Disease Monitoring
HHV-8 Serology (LANA-1 IgG)Exposure (Latent Infection); High in Endemic/MSM/HIV
KS Biopsy (Histology + LANA-1 IHC)Definitive (Spindle Cells, Slit-Like Vessels, LANA-1+)
MCD/PEL BiopsyPlasmablasts (MCD) / Large B Cells (PEL) + HHV-8+

Management

DiseaseTreatment
KS (HIV+)ART (Immune Reconstitution = Regression); Local (Radiation, Intralesional Vinblastine, Topical Alitretinoin); Systemic (Liposomal Doxorubicin, Paclitaxel) if Visceral/Disseminated/Symptomatic
KS (HIV- / Classic / Endemic / Transplant)Reduce Immunosuppression (Transplant); Local/Systemic as Above
MCDRituximab (Anti-CD20) — 1st Line (Depletes HHV-8+ B Cells); +/- Chemo (CHOP) if Severe; IL-6 Blockade (Siltuximab/Tocilizumab) Refractory
PELCHOP/EPOCH + Rituximab; ART if HIV+; Poor Prognosis (Median OS <1 Year)

5️⃣ SARS-CoV-2 (COVID-19) — Sexual Transmission Potential

Evidence for Genital/Sexual Involvement

FindingEvidence
Viral RNA in SemenDetected in ~10-20% Acute/Convalescent Men (Up to 30-60 Days Post-Onset); Infectious Virus Rarely Cultured
Testicular InvolvementACE2/TMPRSS2 Expression in Testes (Spermatogonia, Leydig, Sertoli); Orchitis Rare; Temporary Sperm Quality Decline
Vaginal Fluid RNADetected in Some Women (Lower Frequency/Duration Than Semen)
Sexual TransmissionTheoretical (RNA ≠ Infectious Virus); No Confirmed Sexual Transmission Cases; Close Contact/Respiratory Dominant
Erectile DysfunctionPost-COVID Sequela (Vascular, Endothelial, Psychological); PDE5i Responsive

Practical Guidance

  • Not Classified as STI; Standard Respiratory Precautions Sufficient
  • Condoms Not Required for COVID Prevention Alone (But for Other STIs)
  • Fertility: Temporary Sperm Parameters Decline (3-6 Months Recovery); No Long-Term Infertility Evidence

6️⃣ Ebola Virus Disease (EVD) — Sexual Persistence

Key Facts

AspectDetails
VirusFilovirus (Zaire ebolavirus); RNA, Negative-Sense
Sexual PersistenceRNA in Semen Detectable Months to Years Post-Recovery (Median ~115 Days; Max >500 Days)
Infectious Virus in SemenCultured Up to ~180-500 Days Post-Onset
Sexual TransmissionDocumented (Survivor→Partner); Rare but Critical for Outbreak Resurgence
Guidance (WHO)Male Survivors: Condoms or Abstinence Until 2 Consecutive Negative Semen RT-PCR (Weekly Interval) OR ≥12 Months Post-Onset if Testing Unavailable**
Breast MilkRNA Detected; Avoid Breastfeeding Until Negative PCR

7️⃣ Cytomegalovirus (CMV) — Sexual Transmission

AspectDetails
VirusBetaherpesvirus (HHV-5); dsDNA
TransmissionSexual (Semen, Cervical Secretions, Saliva, Urine, Breast Milk); Vertical (Congenital Major); Transfusion/Transplant
Seroprevalence~50-90% Adults (Age/SES Dependent)
Primary Infection in Pregnancy~30-40% Vertical Transmission Congenital CMV (Sensorineural Hearing Loss, Neurodevelopmental Delay, Microcephaly)
ReactivationCommon in Immunocompromised (CMV Syndrome, End-Organ Disease: Retinitis, Colitis, Pneumonitis); HIV CD4<50
DiagnosisIgM + IgG Avidity (Primary); PCR (Viraemia/Quantitative — Monitor Immunocompromised)
TreatmentGanciclovir/Valganciclovir (Immunocompromised); Letermovir (Prophylaxis Transplant); No Routine Tx Immunocompetent

3. ⚡ FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

VirusKey Points
ZikaFlavivirus, Mosquito (Aedes) + Sexual (Semen Months); 80% Asymptomatic; Congenital Zika Syndrome (Microcephaly, Ocular, Contractures); GBS; RT-PCR (Serum<7d, Urine<21d, Semen Months); IgM+PRNT; Prevention: Condoms 3m (M), 2m (W); Avoid Pregnancy Travel
Hepatitis APicornavirus, Faecal-Oral + Sexual (Anal-Oral MSM Outbreaks); Acute Hepatitis, Self-Limited; Anti-HAV IgM = Acute; Vaccine 2-Dose (0,6-12m) — MSM Priority; PEP: Vaccine ± HNIG
Hepatitis EHepevirus, GT1/2 Waterborne (Pregnancy Severe), GT3/4 Zoonotic (Pork, Chronic in Immunocomp); Anti-HEV IgM Acute; Chronic GT3/4: Reduce Immunosupp + Ribavirin 3m; Vaccine China Only
HHV-8/KSHVGammaherpesvirus, Sexual (MSM Saliva/Semen); KS (Cutaneous/Mucosal/Visceral), MCD (Lymphadenopathy, IL-6), PEL (Effusions); HIV+ Strong Association; Dx: PCR, LANA-1 IHC; Tx: KS→ART+Local/Systemic Chemo; MCD→Rituximab; PEL→CHOP+Rituximab
COVID-19SARS-CoV-2 RNA in Semen (10-20%, Up to 60d); Testicular ACE2 Expression; Not Classified STI; No Confirmed Sexual Transmission
EbolaSemen RNA Persistence (Months-Years); Infectious Virus Cultured ~180-500d; Sexual Transmission Documented; Survivor Guidance: Condoms/Abstinence Until 2 Neg Semen PCR or 12m
CMVBetaherpesvirus, Sexual/Vertical/Transfusion; Congenital CMV (Hearing Loss, Neurodelay); Immunocomp: Reactivation (Retinitis, Colitis); Tx: Ganciclovir/Valganciclovir
Viva"Zika Congenital Syndrome", "HAV MSM Outbreaks", "HEV Pregnancy Severity", "HHV-8 Diseases", "Ebola Semen Persistence"

4. 🎤 Viva Questions (Expected Answers)

#QuestionExpected Answer
1Zika Virus — Sexual Transmission & Congenital Syndrome.Sexual Transmission Confirmed (Male→Female, Male→Male, Female→Male); Semen RNA Prolonged (Mean 34d, Up to 370d); Infectious Virus Cultured ≤69d; Congenital Zika Syndrome: Microcephaly, Cortical Malformations, Ventriculomegaly, Ocular Abnormalities, Congenital Contractures, Hypertonia, Seizures, Hearing Loss; Prevention: Men Exposed → Condoms/Abstinence 3 Months (WHO)/6 Months (CDC); Women 2 Months; Avoid Travel in Pregnancy
2Hepatitis A in MSM — Transmission, Outbreaks, Vaccination.Transmission: Anal-Oral (Rimming), Digital-Anal, Sex Toys (Faecal-Oral Sexual); Outbreaks: Global MSM (2016-2024), Genotype IA/IB, Risk: Multiple Partners, Venues, Chemsex, HIV+; Dx: Anti-HAV IgM; Mgmt: Supportive; Prevention: Inactivated HAV Vaccine 2-Dose (0,6-12m) — MSM PRIORITY; PEP: Vaccine ± HNIG
3Hepatitis E — Genotypes, Pregnancy, Chronic Infection.GT1/2: Human, Waterborne, Developing Countries; GT3/4: Zoonotic (Pigs), Foodborne, Developed Countries; Pregnancy (GT1/2, 3rd Trimester): Fulminant Hepatitis, Maternal Mortality 10-25%; Vertical Transmission High; Chronic HEV (GT3/4): Immunocompromised (Transplant, HIV CD4<200, Steroids) → Persistent Viraemia >3m → Rapid Fibrosis/Cirrhosis; Tx: Reduce Immunosupp + Ribavirin 600-1000mg/d × 3m
4HHV-8 / KSHV — Diseases, HIV Association, Management.Diseases: Kaposi Sarcoma (Cutaneous/Mucosal/Visceral), Multicentric Castleman Disease (Lymphadenopathy, IL-6), Primary Effusion Lymphoma (Effusions), KICS; HIV Association: Strong — HHV-8 Seroprevalence 30-50% HIV+ MSM vs <5% General; KS: ART 1st Line (Immune Reconstitution); Local (Rad, Intralesional Vinblastine); Systemic (Liposomal Doxorubicin, Paclitaxel) if Visceral; MCD: Rituximab 1st Line; IL-6 Blockade (Siltuximab) Refractory; PEL: CHOP+Rituximab; Poor Prognosis
5Ebola Virus — Sexual Persistence & Survivor Guidance.Semen RNA Detectable Months-Years (Median ~115d, Max >500d); Infectious Virus Cultured Up to ~500d; Sexual Transmission Documented (Survivor→Partner); WHO Guidance: Male Survivors → Condoms/Abstinence Until 2 Consecutive Negative Semen RT-PCR (Weekly) OR ≥12 Months Post-Onset if Testing Unavailable; Breast Milk RNA → Avoid Breastfeeding Until Negative
6SARS-CoV-2 Genital Involvement — Evidence & STI Classification.RNA in Semen ~10-20% (Up to 60d); Testicular ACE2/TMPRSS2 Expression; Orchitis Rare; Sperm Quality Temporary Decline; Vaginal Fluid RNA Low Frequency; NOT Classified as STI (No Confirmed Sexual Transmission); Respiratory/Close Contact Dominant
7CMV — Sexual Transmission, Congenital, Immunocompromised.Sexual: Semen, Cervical Secretions, Saliva; Vertical: Congenital (30-40% Primary Infection in Pregnancy) → Sensorineural Hearing Loss, Neurodevelopmental Delay, Microcephaly; Immunocompromised (HIV CD4<50, Transplant): Reactivation → CMV Syndrome, Retinitis, Colitis, Pneumonitis; Dx: PCR Quantitative; Tx: Ganciclovir/Valganciclovir/Letermovir

5. 🧩 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
"Zika = Only Mosquito Transmission"NO. Sexual Transmission WELL DOCUMENTED (Semen Months); Vertical Transmission Major Concern
"HAV = Food/Water Only"NO. MSM Outbreaks Driven by Sexual Practices (Anal-Oral); Vaccination Critical for MSM
"HEV = Only Pregnancy Risk"NO. GT3/4 Chronic Infection in Immunocompromised = Rapid Cirrhosis; Ribavirin Effective
"HHV-8 = Only KS"NO. Also MCD, PEL, KICS; All HHV-8 Driven B-Cell/Endothelial Proliferation
"COVID-19 = STI Because Semen RNA"NO. RNA ≠ Infectious Virus; No Confirmed Sexual Transmission; Classified as Respiratory/Close Contact
"Ebola = Only Acute Outbreak Disease"NO. Sexual Persistence in Survivors = Risk of Outbreak Resurgence (Months-Years Later)
"CMV = Only Congenital Problem"NO. Major Pathogen in Immunocompromised (Retinitis, Colitis, Pneumonitis); Reactivation Common
"Zika IgM = Definitive Diagnosis"NO. Cross-Reactivity with Dengue/Other Flaviviruses HIGH; PRNT Required for Confirmation
"HAV Vaccine = 1 Dose Enough"ROUTINE = 2 Doses (0, 6-12 Months) FOR LONG-TERM IMMUNITY; 1 Dose = PEP/Outbreak Response
"HEV Vaccine = Globally Available"NO. Hecolin® (p239) = Licensed CHINA ONLY; Not WHO Prequalified; Not Available Globally
Mnemonic: OTHER VIRAL STI SHIELD
Other Viral STIs: Zika, HAV, HEV, HHV-8, COVID, Ebola, CMV
T Zika: Flavivirus, Aedes + Sexual (Semen Months); 80% Asx; CZS (Microcephaly, Ocular, Contractures); GBS; RT-PCR Serum<7d/Urine<21d/Semen; PRNT Confirm; Prevent: Condoms M3m/W2m
H HAV: Picornavirus, Faecal-Oral + Sexual (Anal-Oral MSM Outbreaks); Anti-HAV IgM Acute; Vacc 2-Dose 0,6-12m (MSM PRIORITY); PEP: Vacc±HNIG
E HEV: GT1/2 Waterborne (Preg Severe Fulminant 10-25% Mat Mort); GT3/4 Zoonotic Pork (Chronic Immunocomp→Ribavirin 3m); Vacc China Only
R HHV-8: Gammaherpesvirus, Sexual MSM (Saliva/Semen); KS (ART→Regression, Liposomal Dox); MCD (Rituximab); PEL (CHOP+R); HIV Strong Assoc
V COVID: SARS-CoV-2 RNA Semen 10-20%≤60d; Testicular ACE2; Not STI (No Confirmed Sexual Tx); Resp Dominant
I Ebola: Filovirus; Semen RNA Months-Years (Median 115d, Max>500d); Sex Tx Documented; Survivor: Condoms/Abst 2 Neg PCR or 12m
R CMV: Betaherpesvirus, Sexual/Vertical/Transfusion; Congenital (1° Preg→Hearing Loss/Neurodelay); Immunocomp→Reactivation (Retinitis/Colitis); Tx: Ganciclovir/Valganciclovir
Alert: Emerging Viral STIs Require Surveillance (Zika, Mpox, Ebola Persistence, COVID Semen)
Lab: PCR Key for Acute (Zika RNA, HAV IgM, HEV IgM, HHV-8 PCR, Ebola Semen PCR, CMV PCR)
Sexual Transmission Proven: Zika, HAV (MSM), HEV (Possible), HHV-8, Ebola, CMV; COVID Theoretical
HIV Association Strongest: HHV-8 (KS/MCD/PEL), CMV (Reactivation), HEV (Chronic), Zika (Severe if HIV)
Immunocompromised: HEV Chronic→Ribavirin; HHV-8 MCD/PEL→Rituximab/Chemo; CMV→Ganciclovir; Ebola Persistence
Eliminate: HAV Vacc Elimination Possible; Zika Vector Control; HEV Sanitation; HHV-8 ART→KS Regression
Long-term Sequelae: Zika CZS, CMV Congenital, Ebola Survivor Syndrome, COVID Long COVID, HEV Cirrhosis
Diagnosis Algorithm: Acute→PCR; Convalescent→IgM+Confirmatory (PRNT/RNA); Chronic→Quant PCR

6. 🗺️ Mind Map

Other Viral STIs
Zika
Flavivirus, Aedes Mosquito + Sexual
Semen RNA Months
80% Asymptomatic
CZS: Microcephaly, Ocular, Contractures
GBS
Dx: PCR (Serum<7d, Urine<21d, Semen) + PRNT
Prevent: Condoms M3m/W2m, Avoid Travel Pregnancy
HAV
Picornavirus, Faecal-Oral + Sexual MSM
Outbreaks: Anal-Oral, Chemsex, Venues
Acute Hepatitis, Self-Limited
Dx: Anti-HAV IgM
Vacc: 2-Dose 0,6-12m (MSM Priority)
HEV
GT1/2: Waterborne, Preg Severe (Fulminant, Mortality)
GT3/4: Zoonotic Pork, Chronic Immunocomp
Dx: Anti-HEV IgM, RNA PCR
Tx Chronic: Reduce Immunosupp + Ribavirin 3m
Vacc: China Only
HHV-8/KSHV
Gammaherpesvirus, Sexual MSM
KS: Cutaneous/Mucosal/Visceral (ART Regresses)
MCD: Lymphadenopathy, IL-6 (Rituximab)
PEL: Effusions (CHOP+Rituximab)
Dx: PCR, LANA-1 IHC
COVID-19
SARS-CoV-2 RNA Semen 10-20% (≤60d)
Testicular ACE2 Expression
Not STI (No Confirmed Sexual Tx)
Ebola
Semen RNA Months-Years
Sexual Transmission Documented
Survivor: Condoms/Abst 2 Neg PCR or 12m
CMV
Betaherpesvirus, Sexual/Vertical/Transfusion
Congenital: Hearing Loss, Neurodelay
Immunocompromised: Reactivation (Retinitis/Colitis)
Tx: Ganciclovir/Valganciclovir/Letermovir

7. 📅 Spaced Repetition Tracker

ReviewDateScore (0–5)Notes
Day 1
Day 3
Day 7
Day 14
Day 30
Day 90

8. 📝 Self-Test Scorecard

SectionMaxScore%
Zika (Virology, Transmission, CZS, Diagnosis, Prevention)4
HAV (MSM Outbreaks, Vaccination, PEP)3
HEV (Genotypes, Pregnancy, Chronic, Treatment)3
HHV-8 (KS, MCD, PEL, HIV Association, Management)4
COVID-19 (Genital Involvement, STI Classification)1
Ebola (Semen Persistence, Survivor Guidance)2
CMV (Congenital, Immunocompromised, Treatment)2
Cross-Cutting Themes (Surveillance, Emerging)1
Total20

9. 💬 Exam Answer Modes

FormatPromptKey Points
Long Essay"Describe the viral STIs beyond HSV, HPV, Hepatitis B/C, and HIV — including Zika, Hepatitis A/E, HHV-8, and emerging viruses."Zika: Flavivirus, Aedes Mosquito + Sexual (Semen Months); CZS (Microcephaly, Ocular, Contractures, Hypertonia, Seizures); GBS; Dx: RT-PCR (Serum<7d, Urine<21d, Semen), IgM+PRNT; Prevention: Condoms M3m/W2m, Avoid Travel Pregnancy; HAV: Picornavirus, Faecal-Oral + Sexual (Anal-Oral MSM Outbreaks); Outbreaks Global MSM (Chemsex, Venues, HIV+); Dx: Anti-HAV IgM; Vaccine 2-Dose 0,6-12m (MSM Priority); PEP: Vaccine±HNIG; HEV: GT1/2 Waterborne (Pregnancy Fulminant 10-25% Mortality); GT3/4 Zoonotic Pork (Chronic Immunocompromised); Dx: Anti-HEV IgM, RNA PCR; Chronic Tx: Reduce Immunosupp+Ribavirin 3m; Vaccine China Only; HHV-8/KSHV: Gammaherpesvirus, Sexual MSM; KS (ART Regression, Liposomal Doxorubicin), MCD (Rituximab), PEL (CHOP+R); HIV Strong Association; Dx: PCR, LANA-1 IHC; COVID-19: SARS-CoV-2 RNA Semen 10-20% (≤60d), Testicular ACE2; Not STI (Respiratory Dominant); Ebola: Semen RNA Months-Years (Infectious Virus Cultured ~500d); Sexual Transmission Documented; Survivor Guidance: Condoms/Abstinence Until 2 Neg Semen PCR or 12m; CMV: Betaherpesvirus, Sexual/Vertical/Transfusion; Congenital (Hearing Loss, Neurodelay); Immunocompromised Reactivation (Retinitis, Colitis); Tx: Ganciclovir/Valganciclovir/Letermovir
Short Note"Zika Virus — Congenital Syndrome & Sexual Transmission Prevention."Congenital Zika Syndrome (CZS): Microcephaly (Severe), Cortical Malformations, Ventriculomegaly, Ocular Abnormalities (Chorioretinal Atrophy, Optic Nerve Hypoplasia), Congenital Contractures (Arthrogryposis), Hypertonia, Seizures, Hearing Loss; Sexual Transmission: Male→Female, Male→Male, Female→Male Confirmed; Semen RNA Mean 34 Days (Up to 370 Days); Infectious Virus Cultured ≤69 Days; Prevention: Men with Exposure → Condoms/Abstinence ≥3 Months (WHO) / ≥6 Months (CDC); Women ≥2 Months; Pregnant Couples → Avoid Travel, Condoms Throughout Pregnancy if Partner Exposed; Diagnosis: RT-PCR (Serum <7d, Urine <21d, Semen Months); IgM + PRNT (Cross-Reactivity with Dengue)
Viva"Hepatitis E in Pregnancy vs Immunocompromised — Differences."Pregnancy (GT1/2, 3rd Trimester): Acute Fulminant Hepatitis, Maternal Mortality 10-25%, High Vertical Transmission; No Antiviral (Ribavirin Teratogenic); Supportive ICU, Early Delivery Consideration; Immunocompromised (GT3/4, Transplant/HIV CD4<200/Steroids): Chronic HEV (Persistent Viraemia >3 Months), Rapid Fibrosis → Cirrhosis; Treatment: Reduce Immunosuppression + Ribavirin 600-1000mg/d × 3 Months (Off-Label, Monitor Haemolysis)
Ward Round"MSM, HIV+, Presents with Violaceous Cutaneous Nodules, Oral Lesions, Lymphadenopathy, Fever. Suspected HHV-8 Disease. Workup & Management?"Differential: KS (Cutaneous/Mucosal), MCD (Lymphadenopathy, Fever, IL-6), PEL (Effusions); Workup: HIV Viral Load/CD4; HHV-8 PCR (Blood/Lesion); LANA-1 IHC on Biopsy (KS: Spindle Cells, Slit Vessels); Lymph Node Biopsy (MCD: Plasmablasts); Effusion Cytology (PEL: Large B Cells, HHV-8+); Management: Optimise ART (KS Regression); KS → Local (Radiation, Intralesional Vinblastine) / Systemic (Liposomal Doxorubicin) if Visceral; MCD → Rituximab 1st Line (+/- Siltuximab); PEL → CHOP+Rituximab; All: ART Critical
Last-Night"Zika: Flavivirus, Aedes+Sexual(Semen Months), CZS(Microcephaly/Ocular/Contractures), GBS, PCR Serum<7d/Urine<21d/Semen, PRNT, Prevent Condoms M3m/W2m. HAV: Picornavirus, Faecal-Oral+Sexual MSM Outbreaks(Anal-Oral), Anti-HAV IgM, Vacc 2-Dose 0,6-12m (MSM Priority). HEV: GT1/2 Waterborne(Preg Fulminant 10-25% Mort), GT3/4 Pork(Chronic Immunocomp→Ribavirin 3m), Vacc China Only. HHV-8: Gammaherpesvirus, Sexual MSM, KS(ART+LipoDox), MCD(Rituximab), PEL(CHOP+R), HIV Strong Assoc. COVID: RNA Semen 10-20%≤60d, Testicular ACE2, Not STI. Ebola: Semen RNA Months-Years(Infectious Virus~500d), Sex Tx Documented, Survivor Condoms/Abst 2Neg PCR/12m. CMV: Betaherpesvirus, Sexual/Vertical, Congenital(Hearing/Neuro), Immunocomp Reactiv(Retinitis/Colitis), Tx Ganciclovir/Valganciclovir."Compressed.

10. 📌 Summary

  • Zika: Flavivirus, Mosquito (Aedes) + Sexual (Semen Months); 80% Asymptomatic; Congenital Zika Syndrome (Microcephaly, Ocular, Contractures, Hypertonia, Seizures); GBS; RT-PCR (Serum<7d, Urine<21d, Semen Months); IgM+PRNT; Prevention: Condoms 3m (M), 2m (W); Avoid Pregnancy Travel
  • Hepatitis A: Picornavirus, Faecal-Oral + Sexual (Anal-Oral MSM Outbreaks); Acute Self-Limited Hepatitis; Anti-HAV IgM = Acute; Inactivated Vaccine 2-Dose (0, 6-12m) — MSM PRIORITY; PEP: Vaccine ± HNIG
  • Hepatitis E: GT1/2 Waterborne (Pregnancy: Fulminant, Mortality 10-25%); GT3/4 Zoonotic Pork (Chronic in Immunocompromised → Rapid Cirrhosis); Anti-HEV IgM Acute; Chronic Tx: Reduce Immunosupp + Ribavirin 3m; Vaccine China Only
  • HHV-8/KSHV: Gammaherpesvirus, Sexual (MSM Saliva/Semen); KS (Cutaneous/Mucosal/Visceral — ART Regresses, Liposomal Doxorubicin), MCD (Lymphadenopathy, IL-6 — Rituximab), PEL (Effusions — CHOP+Rituximab); HIV Strong Association; LANA-1 IHC Diagnostic
  • COVID-19: SARS-CoV-2 RNA in Semen 10-20% (Up to 60 Days); Testicular ACE2 Expression; NOT Classified as STI (No Confirmed Sexual Transmission); Respiratory Dominant
  • Ebola: Semen RNA Persistence Months-Years (Median 115d, Max >500d); Infectious Virus Cultured ~180-500d; Sexual Transmission Documented; Survivor Guidance: Condoms/Abstinence Until 2 Negative Semen PCR (Weekly) OR ≥12 Months Post-Onset
  • CMV: Betaherpesvirus; Sexual, Vertical, Transfusion; Congenital (Primary Pregnancy → Sensorineural Hearing Loss, Neurodevelopmental Delay); Immunocompromised Reactivation (Retinitis, Colitis, Pneumonitis); Treatment: Ganciclovir/Valganciclovir/Letermovir

11. ❓ MCQs (10)

  1. Zika Virus — Primary Vector and Sexual Transmission Confirmation?

A. Anopheles, No Sexual Transmission B. Aedes aegypti/albopictus, Sexual Transmission CONFIRMED (Semen Months) C. Culex, Theoretical Only D. Aedes, Sexual Transmission Not Documented

Answer: B. Aedes Mosquitoes + Sexual Transmission Well Documented (Semen RNA Prolonged, Infectious Virus Cultured).

  1. Hepatitis A MSM Outbreaks — Primary Sexual Risk Practice?

A. Vaginal Intercourse B. Anal-Oral (Rimming) C. Oral-Genital D. Mutual Masturbation

Answer: B. Anal-Oral Contact (Rimming) = Major Driver of Faecal-Oral Sexual HAV Transmission in MSM.

  1. Hepatitis E — Which Genotype Causes Chronic Infection in Immunocompromised?

A. GT1 B. GT2 C. GT3/4 D. All Equal

Answer: C. GT3/4 (Zoonotic, Pork) Cause Chronic HEV in Immunocompromised (Transplant, HIV, Steroids).

  1. HHV-8 Associated Diseases — Which is NOT Associated?

A. Kaposi Sarcoma B. Multicentric Castleman Disease C. Primary Effusion Lymphoma D. Burkitt Lymphoma

Answer: D. Burkitt Lymphoma = EBV (HHV-4) Associated, Not HHV-8.

  1. Kaposi Sarcoma in HIV+ Patient — 1st Line Management?

A. Liposomal Doxorubicin B. Radiation C. ART (Immune Reconstitution) D. Rituximab

Answer: C. ART = 1st Line for HIV-Associated KS (Immune Reconstitution Induces Regression).

  1. SARS-CoV-2 Sexual Transmission — Current Classification?

A. Confirmed STI B. Not Classified as STI (No Confirmed Sexual Transmission) C. Probable STI D. Theoretical Only

Answer: B. RNA in Semen ≠ Infectious Virus; No Confirmed Sexual Transmission Cases; Respiratory Dominant.

  1. Ebola Virus — Semen PCR Persistence & Survivor Guidance?

A. Weeks, No Restrictions B. Months-Years (Median 115d); Condoms/Abstinence Until 2 Neg PCR or 12 Months C. Days, 30-Days Abstinence D. Not Detected in Semen

Answer: B. Semen RNA Median 115 Days (Max >500d); Infectious Virus Cultured ~500d; WHO: 2 Neg Semen PCR or 12 Months.

  1. Congenital CMV — Most Common Sequela?

A. Microcephaly B. Sensorineural Hearing Loss C. Chorioretinitis D. Hydrocephalus

Answer: B. Sensorineural Hearing Loss = Most Common Sequela of Congenital CMV (Can Be Progressive/Late-Onset).

  1. Zika Prevention — Male Partner Exposed, Couple Planning Pregnancy?

A. Condoms 1 Month B. Condoms/Abstinence 3 Months (WHO) / 6 Months (CDC) C. No Restrictions D. Vaccination Available

Answer: B. WHO: 3 Months; CDC Conservative: 6 Months for Men with Zika Exposure.

  1. HEV Vaccine — Global Availability?

A. WHO Prequalified, Global B. Licensed China Only (Hecolin®/p239), Not Global C. Europe/US Approved D. In Development Only

Answer: B. Hecolin® (p239) Licensed in China Only (Efficacy >95% GT1/4); Not WHO Prequalified/Globally Available.

12. 📋 SBAs (5)

  1. 28-year-old pregnant woman (20 weeks), Partner Returned from Brazil (Zika Endemic) 2 Weeks Ago. Asymptomatic. Best Prevention Counselling?

A. No Risk, Reassure B. Condoms Throughout Pregnancy (Partner Asymptomatic Sexual Transmission Possible) C. Amniocentesis Now D. Termination Advised

Answer: B. Asymptomatic Sexual Transmission Documented; Condoms/Abstinence Throughout Pregnancy Recommended if Partner Exposed.

  1. 45-year-old HIV+ Man (CD4 350, on ART), Chronic HEV GT3 (RNA Positive 6 Months, Fibrosis F2). Management?

A. No Treatment, Monitor B. Reduce Immunosuppression if Possible + Ribavirin 600-1000mg/Day × 3 Months C. PegIFN-α D. Sofosbuvir/Velpatasvir

Answer: B. Chronic HEV GT3 in Immunocompromised → Reduce Immunosupp + Ribavirin 3 Months (Off-Label, Monitor Haemolysis).

13. 🔑 Answer Keys

MCQsSBAs
1-B, 2-B, 3-C, 4-D, 5-C, 6-B, 7-B, 8-B, 9-B, 10-B1-B, 2-B

Version: Full FCPS/MRCP Template Upgrade

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 15.1.16 Pelvic Inflammatory Disease Pid

Related: [[Urethritis Syndrome (Mucopurulent vs Non-Gonococcal)]], [[Chlamydia trachomatis (Genital, LGV, Trachoma)]], [[Neisseria gonorrhoeae Infections (Uncomplicated and Disseminated)]], [[Syphilis (Primary, Secondary, Latent, Tertiary)]], [[Genital Ulcer Disease Syndrome (GUD)]]

[!important]
PID = ascending infection from cervix/vagina → uterus, tubes, ovaries, peritoneum. Empirical treatment mandatory — delay causes infertility, ectopic pregnancy, chronic pain. Low threshold for treatment: "better to overtreat than undertreat."

1. Learning Objectives

  • Apply clinical diagnostic criteria (CDC minimal vs enhanced)
  • Initiate empirical treatment immediately upon suspicion
  • Differentiate PID from mimics (appendicitis, ectopic pregnancy, ovarian torsion)
  • Manage severe PID (hospitalisation, IV antibiotics, surgical complications)
  • Implement partner management and follow-up

2. Definition and Pathogenesis

  • PID = inflammation of upper female genital tract (endometrium, fallopian tubes, ovaries, pelvic peritoneum)
  • Ascending infection: Cervix → Endometrium → Fallopian tubes → Peritoneum
  • Aetiology: Polymicrobial — sexually transmitted (GC, CT, M. genitalium) + endogenous (anaerobes, G. vaginalis, Enterobacteriaceae, Strep)
  • Risk factors: Young age, multiple partners, new partner, IUD (first 3 weeks), prior PID, STI history, douching

3. Diagnostic Criteria (CDC / BASHH)

Minimal Criteria (Treat if ANY present + lower genital tract inflammation)

CriterionDescription
Cervical motion tenderness (chandelier sign)Pain on moving cervix
Uterine tendernessAdnexal/uterine tenderness on bimanual exam
Adnexal tendernessOvarian/tubal tenderness
PLUS: Evidence of lower genital tract inflammation (cervical mucopus, +ve NAAT GC/CT, raised WBC, ESR/CRP)

Enhanced Criteria (Increase Specificity)

CriterionThreshold
Fever>38.3°C
Abnormal cervical/vaginal dischargeMucopus, NAAT +ve
Raised inflammatory markersESR >30, CRP >20, WBC >10
ImagingTransvaginal US: thickened tubes >6mm, fluid in cul-de-sac, tubo-ovarian complex
LaparoscopyPurulent material, friable tubes, adhesions (gold standard)
Clinical judgment paramount — "Low threshold for treatment" as delay = infertility/ectopic

4. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionKey Differentiators
Acute appendicitisRLQ pain, migration from periumbilical, no cervical motion tenderness, no discharge
Ectopic pregnancy+ve pregnancy test, vaginal bleeding, adnexal mass, pain lateralised
Ovarian torsionSudden severe unilateral pain, nausea/vomiting, no fever/discharge
EndometriosisChronic cyclic pain, dysmenorrhoea, dyspareunia, no acute fever
Ovarian cyst ruptureSudden pain, often mid-cycle, no cervical tenderness
DiverticulitisLLQ pain, bowel symptoms, no cervical tenderness
IUD complicationActinomycosis, perforation; recent insertion?

5. Empirical Treatment (Immediate — Do Not Delay for Results)

Outpatient (Mild–Moderate PID)

RegimenDoseDuration
1st-line (BASHH/CDC)Ceftriaxone 500mg IM single dose + Doxycycline 100mg BD PO + Metronidazole 400mg BD PO14 days
Alternative (if cephalosporin allergy)Gentamicin 240mg IM single dose + Metronidazole 400mg BD PO + Doxycycline 100mg BD PO14 days
Alternative (if metronidazole intolerant)Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Doxycycline 100mg BD (no metronidazole if anaerobic cover not needed)14 days
Doxycycline 14d = critical for C. trachomatis + M. genitalium + anaerobes

Inpatient (Severe PID / Tubo-Ovarian Abscess / Pregnancy / Failed Outpatient)

RegimenDoseDuration
IV 1st-lineCeftriaxone 2g IV q12h + Doxycycline 100mg BD PO/IV + Metronidazole 500mg IV q8h24h after clinical improvement → switch to outpatient regimen to complete 14d
AlternativeClindamycin 900mg IV q8h + Gentamicin 2mg/kg IV q8h (or 5mg/kg q24h)24h after improvement → switch
Switch criteria: Afebrile 24h, improving pain, tolerating oral → complete 14d total

6. Specific Clinical Scenarios

Tubo-Ovarian Abscess (TOA)

  • Diagnosis: Ultrasound (complex adnexal mass, thick walls, septations) / CT / MRI
  • Management: IV antibiotics + drainage (image-guided percutaneous) if >5cm or failing antibiotics
  • Surgery: Laparoscopic salpingo-oophorectomy if ruptured / failing medical management
  • Duration: 14 days IV + possible extended course

Fitz-Hugh-Curtis Syndrome (Perihepatitis)

  • Features: RUQ pain, pleural rub, "violin string" adhesions on laparoscopy
  • Pathogens: GC, CT
  • Treatment: Standard PID regimen

PID in Pregnancy

  • Rare (cervical mucus plug protective)
  • High risk: Preterm labour, sepsis, fetal loss
  • Antibiotics: Ceftriaxone + Azithromycin (avoid doxycycline/metronidazole in 1st trimester)
  • Admit: IV therapy, obstetric review

IUD and PID

  • Risk: Highest in first 3 weeks post-insertion (Actinomyces, GC, CT)
  • Management: Do NOT routinely remove IUD if mild PID; treat antibiotics, remove if no improvement 48–72h
  • Actinomyces: Penicillin G IV × 2w → Amoxicillin PO × 6–12m

7. Partner Management

  • All partners within 6 months (GC/CT) should be notified, tested, treated empirically
  • Expedited Partner Therapy (EPT): Provide medication for partners without clinical assessment
  • Abstain until both partners completed treatment + 7 days
  • Test of cure: Not routine; if symptoms persist → repeat NAAT, consider reinfection/resistance

8. Complications and Sequelae

ComplicationFrequency (Untreated)
Infertility (tubal factor)10–15% after 1 episode; ↑ with recurrences
Ectopic pregnancy6–10x risk; tubal damage
Chronic pelvic pain18–30%
Tubo-ovarian abscess5–15% of hospitalised PID
Fitz-Hugh-Curtis~5–10%
Sepsis / peritonitisRare with prompt treatment

9. Follow-Up

TimepointAction
48–72hClinical review (outpatient); should be improving
2 weeksEnd of treatment review; symptom resolution
4–6 weeksTest of cure (NAAT GC/CT) if symptoms persist; reinfection screen
6 monthsReinfection screen (high recurrence risk)

10. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • PID = clinical diagnosis — low threshold for treatment
  • Minimal criteria: Cervical motion/uterine/adnexal tenderness + inflammation
  • Empirical Rx: Ceftriaxone IM + Doxycycline 14d + Metronidazole 14d
  • Inpatient: IV Ceftriaxone + Doxycycline + Metronidazole
  • Doxycycline 14d essential (CT, M. genitalium, anaerobes)
  • Don't remove IUD routinely — treat first, remove if no improvement 48–72h
  • TOA: IV antibiotics + percutaneous drainage if >5cm
  • Partner notification 6 months; EPT encouraged
  • Sequelae: Infertility, ectopic, chronic pain ↑ with each episode

11. Common Viva Questions

  1. What are the CDC minimal criteria for PID diagnosis?
  2. What is the empirical outpatient treatment for PID?
  3. When do you admit PID?
  4. How do you manage tubo-ovarian abscess?
  5. What are the long-term sequelae of PID?

12. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

  • Doxycycline 14 days — not 7d (misses CT, M. genitalium)
  • Metronidazole — covers anaerobes (often overlooked)
  • IUD removal — NOT routinely; only if no improvement 48–72h
  • Severe PID = IV therapy; TOA = drainage >5cm
  • Fitz-Hugh-Curtis = perihepatitis (RUQ pain) + PID
  • Pregnancy = Ceftriaxone + Azithromycin (avoid doxy/metro)
  • Test of cure not routine — reinfection common

13. Mnemonics

  • PID minimal criteria: Cervical motion + Uterine + Adnexal Tenderness = CUT
  • Outpatient PID Rx: Ceftriaxone + Doxycycline + Metronidazole = CDM
  • Inpatient PID Rx: IV Ceftriaxone + Doxycycline + Metronidazole
  • TOA: Tube-Ovary Abscess → IV + Drainage
  • Fitz-Hugh-Curtis: FHC = RUQ pain + PID

14. One-Page Revision Summary

PID = ascending infection → tubes/ovaries/peritoneum

  • Diagnose: CUT (Cervical motion + Uterine + Adnexal Tenderness) + inflammation
  • Outpatient: Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Doxycycline 100mg BD 14d + Metronidazole 400mg BD 14d
  • Inpatient: IV Ceftriaxone 2g q12h + Doxycycline 100mg BD + Metronidazole 500mg q8h
  • Doxycycline 14d essential (CT, M. genitalium, anaerobes)
  • TOA >5cm: IV + drainage
  • IUD: don't remove routinely; treat first
  • Sequelae: infertility, ectopic, chronic pain

15. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • Minimal diagnostic criteria (CUT + inflammation)
  • Empirical outpatient regimen (CDM: Ceftriaxone + Doxycycline + Metronidazole)
  • Inpatient regimen (IV + drainage for TOA)
  • Doxycycline 14 days (not 7)
  • Don't routinely remove IUD
  • Partner notification 6 months, EPT

Should Know

  • Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome (perihepatitis)
  • TOA management (IV + drainage >5cm)
  • PID in pregnancy (azithromycin instead of doxy/metro)
  • Differential: appendicitis, ectopic, torsion
  • Test of cure not routine

Nice to Know

  • Actinomyces with IUD (penicillin G long-course)
  • Long-term fertility outcomes
  • Cost-effectiveness of screening
  • M. genitalium role in PID
  • Probiotics for metronidazole side effects

16. MCQs (10)

  1. CDC minimal diagnostic criteria for PID require:

A. All three: cervical motion + uterine + adnexal tenderness

B. Any one of: cervical motion OR uterine OR adnexal tenderness + inflammation

C. Fever + abdominal pain + discharge

D. Ultrasound showing TOA

  1. First-line empirical outpatient treatment for PID:

A. Azithromycin 1g + Metronidazole

B. Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Doxycycline 100mg BD 14d + Metronidazole 400mg BD 14d

C. Doxycycline 100mg BD 7d

D. Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Azithromycin 1g

  1. Doxycycline duration in PID treatment:

A. 7 days

B. 14 days

C. 21 days

D. 28 days

  1. Inpatient PID regimen:

A. Oral Ceftriaxone + Doxycycline

B. IV Ceftriaxone 2g q12h + Doxycycline 100mg BD + Metronidazole 500mg q8h

C. Ceftriaxone IM + Metronidazole only

D. Clindamycin + Gentamicin only

  1. When to remove IUD in PID:

A. Always immediately

B. If no clinical improvement after 48–72h of antibiotics

C. Only if Actinomyces suspected

D. Never remove

  1. Tubo-ovarian abscess >5cm — management:

A. Antibiotics only

B. Image-guided percutaneous drainage + IV antibiotics

C. Immediate laparotomy

D. Medical management only if <10cm

  1. Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome is:

A. Tubo-ovarian abscess

B. Perihepatitis (RUQ pain) associated with PID

C. Ovarian cyst rupture

C. Ectopic pregnancy

  1. PID in pregnancy — antibiotic avoidance:

A. Ceftriaxone

B. Doxycycline (contraindicated) + Metronidazole (avoid 1st trimester)

C. Metronidazole only

D. Azithromycin only

  1. Partner notification period for PID (GC/CT):

A. 3 months

B. 6 months

C. 1 year

D. None

  1. Long-term sequelae of PID include all EXCEPT:

A. Tubal infertility

B. Ectopic pregnancy

C. Endometriosis

D. Chronic pelvic pain

  1. Test of cure after PID treatment:

A. Routine at 2 weeks

B. Not routine (only if symptoms persist)

C. Mandatory at 4 weeks

D. Mandatory at 6 months

17. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 24-year-old woman, lower abdominal pain 3 days, fever 38°C, vaginal discharge. Exam: cervical motion tenderness, adnexal tenderness, mucopus at cervix. NAAT pending. Best immediate management:

A. Await NAAT results

B. Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Doxycycline 100mg BD 14d + Metronidazole 400mg BD 14d

C. US pelvis first

D. Doxycycline 100mg BD 7d only

  1. Same patient returns at 72h, no improvement, temp 39°C, RUQ pain. US: complex adnexal mass 6cm with septations. Best next step:

A. Continue outpatient antibiotics

B. Admit for IV Ceftriaxone + Doxycycline + Metronidazole + percutaneous drainage (TOA >5cm)

C. Change to IV Clindamycin + Gentamicin

D. Laparoscopy immediately

  1. A 28-year-old with Cu-IUD inserted 2 weeks ago, presents with PID (minimal criteria met). Best management:

A. Remove IUD immediately, then antibiotics

B. Start antibiotics; remove IUD only if no improvement at 48–72h

C. Antibiotics only, never remove IUD

D. Remove IUD, no antibiotics needed

  1. A 20-year-old pregnant at 16 weeks presents with PID (adnexal tenderness, cervical motion tenderness, mucopus). Best antibiotics:

A. Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Doxycycline 100mg BD 14d + Metronidazole 400mg BD 14d

B. Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Azithromycin 1g PO (avoid doxycycline/metronidazole)

C. Doxycycline 100mg BD 14d only

C. Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Metronidazole 400mg BD 14d

  1. A 30-year-old post-PID treatment (completed 14d), asymptomatic. Test of cure:

A. Routine NAAT at 2 weeks

B. Not routine (only if symptoms persist/reinfection suspected)

C. NAAT at 4 weeks

D. Culture at 6 weeks

  1. A patient with TOA 4cm on US, stable, on IV antibiotics 48h, improving. Next step:

A. Continue IV antibiotics, repeat US in 1 week

B. Continue IV antibiotics, switch to oral at 24h afebrile, complete 14d total

C. Drainage now

D. Laparoscopy

  1. A 26-year-old with PID, partner treated 2 weeks ago for GC. Patient's NAAT: CT positive, GC negative. Patient completed doxycycline 14d. Partner management:

A. No further action

B. Partner needs CT treatment (doxycycline 14d) — expedited therapy

C. Partner needs GC retreatment

D. Partner needs both GC + CT retreatment

  1. A 22-year-old with Cu-IUD, PID. NAAT: GC negative, CT positive. Treated with antibiotics. At 72h, no improvement. Best action:

A. Change antibiotics

B. Remove IUD (no improvement at 48–72h)

C. Add metronidazole

D. Laparoscopy

  1. A patient with Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome. Typical presentation:

A. RUQ pain, fever, no pelvic signs

B. RUQ pain + PID signs (cervical motion tenderness) + pleural rub

C. Epigastric pain, vomiting

D. Jaundice, RUQ pain

  1. Risk of infertility after one episode of PID:

A. <1%

B. 10–15%

C. 50%

D. 100%

  1. A 25-year-old with recurrent PID (3rd episode). Best prevention counselling:

A. Remove IUD

B. Condoms + regular screening + prompt treatment of STIs + partner notification

C. Prophylactic antibiotics

D. Hysterectomy

18. Flashcards

  • Q: PID minimal criteria

A: Cervical motion + Uterine + Adnexal Tenderness (any 1) + inflammation = CUT

  • Q: Outpatient PID Rx

A: Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Doxycycline 100mg BD 14d + Metronidazole 400mg BD 14d (CDM)

  • Q: Inpatient PID Rx

A: IV Ceftriaxone 2g q12h + Doxycycline 100mg BD + Metronidazole 500mg q8h

  • Q: Doxycycline duration

A: 14 days (NOT 7 — covers CT, M. genitalium, anaerobes)

  • Q: TOA >5cm

A: IV antibiotics + percutaneous drainage

  • Q: IUD in PID

A: Don't remove routinely; only if no improvement 48–72h

  • Q: Fitz-Hugh-Curtis

A: Perihepatitis + PID (RUQ pain + pleural rub)

  • Q: Partner notification

A: 6 months; EPT encouraged

  • Q: Pregnancy + PID

A: Ceftriaxone + Azithromycin (avoid doxy/metronidazole)

  • Q: Sequelae

A: Infertility, ectopic, chronic pain ↑ with recurrences

19. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQ 1: B — Any ONE of CUT + inflammation.

MCQ 2: B — CDM = Ceftriaxone + Doxycycline 14d + Metronidazole 14d.

MCQ 3: B — 14 days (covers CT, M. genitalium, anaerobes).

MCQ 4: B — IV Ceftriaxone 2g q12h + Doxycycline + Metronidazole.

MCQ 5: B — Remove only if no improvement at 48–72h.

MCQ 6: B — TOA >5cm = percutaneous drainage + IV antibiotics.

MCQ 7: B — RUQ pain + PID = Fitz-Hugh-Curtis (perihepatitis).

MCQ 8: B — Pregnancy = Ceftriaxone + Azithromycin (avoid doxy/metro).

MCQ 9: B — 6 months for GC/CT partners.

MCQ 9: C — Endometriosis not PID sequela.

MCQ 10: B — Not routine; only if symptoms persist.

SBA 1: B — Minimal criteria met + signs = immediate empirical CDM.

SBA 2: B — TOA >5cm = IV antibiotics + drainage.

SBA 3: B — IUD removal only if no improvement 48–72h.

SBA 4: B — Pregnancy = avoid doxycycline/metronidazole → Azithromycin.

SBA 5: B — Not routine; only if symptoms persist.

SBA 6: B — Improving = continue oral to complete 14d total.

SBA 7: B — CT+ partner = expedited CT treatment (doxycycline 14d).

SBA 8: B — No improvement 72h despite correct antibiotics → remove IUD.

SBA 9: B — RUQ + pleural rub + PID signs = Fitz-Hugh-Curtis.

SBA 10: B — 10–15% infertility after 1 episode.

Status: Full FCPS/MRCP topic note completed — 2026-06-13

Topic Overview

1. Learning Objectives
2. Definition and Pathogenesis
3. Diagnostic Criteria (CDC / BASHH)
Minimal Criteria (Treat if ANY present + lower genital tract inflammation)
Enhanced Criteria (Increase Specificity)
4. Differential Diagnosis
5. Empirical Treatment (Immediate — Do Not Delay for Results)
Outpatient (Mild–Moderate PID)
Inpatient (Severe PID / Tubo-Ovarian Abscess / Pregnancy / Failed Outpatient)
6. Specific Clinical Scenarios
Tubo-Ovarian Abscess (TOA)
Fitz-Hugh-Curtis Syndrome (Perihepatitis)
PID in Pregnancy
IUD and PID
7. Partner Management
8. Complications and Sequelae
9. Follow-Up
10. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points
