▸ 15.1.17 Syphilis Primary Secondary Latent Tertiary

Related: [[Genital Ulcer Disease Syndrome (GUD)]], [[Syphilis Serology Interpretation (VDRL/RPR, TPHA/FTA-ABS)]], [[Congenital Syphilis]], [[Neurosyphilis]], [[HIV Infection and AIDS]]

[!important]
Treponema pallidum = spirochete, cannot be cultured. Stages: Primary (chancre) → Secondary (rash, systemic) → Latent (seropositive, asymptomatic) → Tertiary (gumma, cardiovascular, neuro). Penicillin = only reliable cure; no resistance documented.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Recognise clinical features of each syphilis stage
  • Interpret serology (non-treponemal + treponemal) correctly
  • Apply stage-appropriate penicillin regimens
  • Manage neurosyphilis, congenital syphilis, penicillin allergy
  • Understand Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction and partner notification

2. Aetiology and Transmission

  • T. pallidum subsp. pallidum = microaerophilic spirochete
  • Transmission: Sexual (genital/anal/oral), vertical (congenital), blood (rare)
  • Incubation: 10–90 days (mean 21 days)
  • Infectious stages: Primary, Secondary, Early Latent (<1 year)

3. Clinical Stages

Primary Syphilis (10–90 days post-exposure)

FeatureDescription
ChancreSingle, painless, clean-based, indurated ulcer at inoculation site
LocationGenital (penis, vulva, cervix), anal, oral, fingers
LymphadenopathyRegional, bilateral, firm, non-tender, rubbery
DurationHeals spontaneously in 3–6 weeks (untreated)
SerologyNon-treponemal (RPR/VDRL) may be negative early; treponemal (TPHA) becomes positive

Secondary Syphilis (4–10 weeks post-chancre)

FeatureDescription
Skin rashMaculopapular, truncal, palmar/plantar involvement, copper-coloured, non-pruritic
MucosalSnail-track ulcers (oral), condylomata lata (genital/anal, infectious)
SystemicFever, malaise, lymphadenopathy (generalised, non-tender), hepatitis, nephritis
NeurologicalAseptic meningitis, cranial nerve palsies, ophthalmic (uveitis, optic neuritis)
DurationWeeks to months; relapses possible in first 2 years

Latent Syphilis (Seropositive, Asymptomatic)

CategoryDefinitionInfectivity
Early Latent<1 year (primary/secondary within 12 months)Yes (relapse to secondary possible)
Late Latent≥1 year (or unknown duration)No (except vertical transmission)
DurationYears to decadesCSF examination needed if neurological symptoms

Tertiary Syphilis (Years–Decades Post-Infection, ~30% Untreated)

ManifestationFeatures
GummatousGummas = granulomatous lesions (skin, bone, liver, testis); destructive
CardiovascularAortitis → aortic regurgitation, aneurysm (ascending), coronary ostial stenosis
NeurosyphilisSee separate note
OtherBowel strictures, optic atrophy, Argyll Robertson pupils

Neurosyphilis (Can Occur at Any Stage)

FormTimingFeatures
AsymptomaticAnyCSF abnormalities only
MeningealEarly (months)Headache, meningism, cranial nerve palsies
MeningovascularMonths–yearsStroke (endarteritis), seizures
General ParesisLate (10–30y)Dementia, personality change, tremor, Argyll Robertson pupils
Tabes DorsalisLate (20–30y)Posterior column loss (ataxia, lightning pains, absent reflexes), Argyll Robertson pupils

Congenital Syphilis (Vertical Transmission)

  • Transmission: All stages (highest in primary/secondary); placenta after 14 weeks
  • Early (<2y): Rhinorrhoea ("snuffles"), rash, hepatosplenomegaly, bony changes, pseudoparalysis
  • Late (>2y): Hutchinson's triad (notched incisors, interstitial keratitis, 8th nerve deafness), saddle nose, saber shins, Clutton's joints
  • Diagnosis: Maternal serology + infant serology/CSF/long bones X-ray/PCR
  • Treatment: Penicillin G (IV/IM) — see separate note

4. Serological Diagnosis

Test TypeExamplesUse
Non-treponemal (NTT)RPR (Rapid Plasma Reagin), VDRL (Venereal Disease Research Lab)Screening, quantitative (titre = disease activity), monitor treatment
Treponemal (TT)TPHA (T. pallidum Haemagglutination), FTA-ABS, EIA/CIA, T. pallidum Particle Agglutination (TPPA)Confirmatory, qualitative (reactive/non-reactive), remains positive for life

Testing Algorithms

AlgorithmSequence
TraditionalNTT (RPR/VDRL) screen → if reactive → TT (TPHA/FTA-ABS) confirm
Reverse (Automated)TT (EIA/CIA) screen → if reactive → NTT (quantitative) for activity

Interpretation Framework

NTT (RPR/VDRL)TT (TPHA/FTA-ABS)Interpretation
NegativeNegativeNo syphilis
PositivePositiveActive/treated syphilis (quantify NTT for activity)
NegativePositiveTreated syphilis, late latent, or false negative NTT (prozone/early primary)
PositiveNegativeFalse positive NTT (autoimmune, pregnancy, infections), early primary (pre-seroconversion), or treated with NTT not yet negative
Prozone effect: Very high antibody titre → false negative NTT (dilute serum to resolve)

5. Treatment (Penicillin = Only Reliable Cure)

Stage-Based Regimens

StageRegimenAlternative (Penicillin Allergy)
Primary / Secondary / Early Latent (<1yr)Benzathine Penicillin G 2.4MU IM single doseDoxycycline 100mg BD × 14d (or Ceftriaxone 1g IM/IV daily × 10–14d)
Late Latent (≥1yr or unknown) / Tertiary (non-neuro)Benzathine Penicillin G 2.4MU IM weekly × 3 doses (total 7.2MU)Doxycycline 100mg BD × 28d
Neurosyphilis / Ocular / OtosyphilisPenicillin G 18–24MU IV daily (2–4MU q4h) × 14dCeftriaxone 2g IV daily × 14d +/− Probenecid (some guidelines)
Congenital SyphilisPenicillin G 50,000U/kg IV q12h (≤7d) / q8h (>7d) × 10dCeftriaxone (if no meningitis); desensitise if allergic
Note: No penicillin resistance documented; doxycycline/ceftriaxone alternatives less well studied for neurosyphilis

Jarisch-Herxheimer Reaction

  • Timing: 2–12 hours after first penicillin dose
  • Features: Fever, rigors, hypotension, tachycardia, worsening rash, headache
  • Mechanism: Cytokine release (TNF-α, IL-6) from spirochetal lysis
  • Management: Warn patient; supportive (fluids, antipyretics); do NOT stop penicillin
  • Risk higher: Secondary, early latent, high spirochetal load

6. Partner Notification and Contact Tracing

StageLook-back Period
Primary3 months + duration of symptoms
Secondary6 months + duration of symptoms
Early Latent (<1yr)1 year
Late Latent / TertiaryNot routinely (except pregnant partners, children)
CongenitalMother + siblings
Expedited Partner Therapy (EPT): Provide penicillin/alternative for partners where possible

7. Follow-Up and Test of Cure

StageClinical Follow-UpSerological Follow-Up (NTT Titre)
Primary / Secondary1, 3, 6, 12 months4-fold (2 dilution) drop at 6m = cure; non-reactive at 12m = adequate
Early Latent6, 12, 24 months4-fold drop at 12m
Late Latent / Tertiary12, 24 months4-fold drop at 24m
Neurosyphilis6, 12, 24 monthsCSF NTT + cell count/protein at 6, 12, 24m
Treatment failure: NTT titre fails to drop 4-fold by 12m (early) / 24m (late) → re-treat + CSF if neuro possible

8. Syphilis in HIV

  • Co-infection common: HIV ↑ syphilis transmission/acquisition
  • Atypical presentations: Multiple chancres, severe secondary, rapid progression to neuro
  • Serology: NTT titres may be lower; false negatives more common (prozone); TPHA may be delayed
  • Treatment: Same penicillin regimens; CSF examination recommended for ALL HIV+ syphilis (higher neuro risk)
  • Follow-up: More frequent (3, 6, 9, 12, 24 months); lower threshold for re-treatment

9. Syphilis in Pregnancy

  • Screening: All pregnant women at booking + 28w + delivery (high prevalence areas)
  • Vertical transmission: All stages; highest in primary/secondary; after 14 weeks gestation
  • Treatment: Benzathine penicillin (stage-appropriate) — only therapy proven to prevent congenital syphilis
  • Penicillin allergy: Desensitisation mandatory (doxycecline contraindicated in pregnancy)
  • Infant evaluation: If mother treated <4w before delivery / inadequate / no treatment → infant workup + treatment

10. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • Chancre = painless, clean, indurated + non-tender regional nodes
  • Secondary = truncal + palmar/plantar rash + condylomata lata + systemic
  • Latent = seropositive + asymptomatic; early <1yr (infectious), late ≥1yr
  • Tertiary = gumma (skin/bone/liver), aortic regurgitation/aneurysm, neurosyphilis
  • Neurosyphilis = IV Penicillin G 18–24MU × 14d (not benzathine)
  • Serology: NTT (RPR/VDRL) = quantitative, activity; TT (TPHA/FTA) = confirmatory, qualitative
  • Penicillin only cure; no resistance; benzathine for non-neuro; IV for neuro
  • Jarisch-Herxheimer: warn patient; supportive; don't stop penicillin
  • Partner notification: 3m (primary), 6m (secondary), 1y (early latent)
  • HIV co-infection: CSF exam all cases; lower threshold for neuro

11. Common Viva Questions

  1. Describe the chancre of primary syphilis.
  2. What is the treatment for neurosyphilis?
  3. How do you interpret RPR negative, TPHA positive?
  4. What is the Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction?
  5. How do you manage syphilis in pregnancy with penicillin allergy?

12. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

  • Benzathine penicillin does NOT cross BBB — cannot treat neurosyphilis
  • Doxycycline = alternative for non-neuro; contraindicated in pregnancy
  • Late latent = ≥1 year or unknown duration → 3 weekly doses benzathine
  • Prozone effect = false negative RPR in high titre (dilute serum)
  • RPR negative, TPHA positive = treated syphilis or early primary or prozone
  • Condylomata lata = secondary syphilis (infectious); not HPV warts
  • Argyll Robertson pupil = accommodates but does not react to light (neurosyphilis)

13. Mnemonics

  • Stages: Primary → Secondary → Latent → Tertiary (PSLT)
  • Chancre: Clean, Hard, Asingle, Non-tender, Cternal, Rubbery nodes
  • Secondary: Palms/Soles + Copper rash + Condylomata Lata
  • Tertiary: Gumma, Aortitis, Neurosyphilis (GAN)
  • Jarisch-Herxheimer: Just Happens after Rx; Fever, Hypotension
  • Penicillin: Benzathine for non-neuro; IV for neuro

14. One-Page Revision Summary

Syphilis = T. pallidum

  • Primary: painless chancre + non-tender nodes
  • Secondary: truncal + palmar/plantar rash + condylomata lata
  • Latent: seropositive, asymptomatic (early <1y, late ≥1y)
  • Tertiary: gumma, aortitis, neuro
  • Neuro: IV Penicillin G 18–24MU × 14d
  • Serology: NTT (RPR) quantitative + TT (TPHA) confirmatory
  • Rx: Benzathine 2.4MU IM (1°/2°/early latent); 3× weekly (late latent/tertiary)
  • JH reaction: warn, supportive, don't stop penicillin
  • HIV: CSF exam all cases

15. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • Chancre features + differential
  • Secondary rash (palms/soles + condylomata lata)
  • Latent definitions (early <1yr, late ≥1y)
  • Neurosyphilis = IV Penicillin G (NOT benzathine)
  • Serology interpretation (NTT + TT)
  • Benzathine penicillin regimens by stage
  • Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction
  • Partner notification periods

Should Know

  • Congenital syphilis features + workup
  • Syphilis in HIV (CSF all cases, atypical presentations)
  • Pregnancy management (desensitisation if allergic)
  • Treatment failure criteria (4-fold titre drop)
  • Prozone effect

Nice to Know

  • Cardiovascular syphilis (aortitis, aneurysm)
  • General paresis vs tabes dorsalis distinction
  • Otosyphilis (hearing loss, vertigo)
  • Syphilis vaccine development

16. MCQs (10)

  1. Primary syphilitic chancre is classically:

A. Multiple, painful, shallow

B. Single, painless, clean-based, indurated

C. Multiple, purulent, ragged

D. Beefy-red, bleeding, granulomatous

  1. Secondary syphilis rash characteristically involves:

A. Face only

B. Trunk + palms + soles

C. Genitals only

D. Scalp only

  1. Condylomata lata are seen in:

A. Primary syphilis

B. Secondary syphilis

C. Tertiary syphilis

D. Congenital syphilis

  1. Late latent syphilis definition:

A. <6 months

B. <1 year

C. ≥1 year (or unknown duration)

D. <2 years

  1. Neurosyphilis treatment:

A. Benzathine Penicillin G 2.4MU IM weekly × 3

B. Penicillin G 18–24MU IV daily × 14d

C. Doxycycline 100mg BD × 28d

C. Ceftriaxone 1g IM single dose

  1. Benzathine Penicillin G for late latent syphilis:

A. Single dose

B. 2.4MU IM weekly × 3 doses

C. 2.4MU IM weekly × 5 doses

D. Daily × 14d

  1. Serology interpretation: RPR negative, TPHA positive:

A. No syphilis

B. Treated syphilis / late latent / prozone / early primary

C. Active syphilis

D. False positive TPHA

  1. Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction — timing:

A. 24–48 hours

B. 2–12 hours

C. 1–2 weeks

D. Immediate

  1. Partner notification period for secondary syphilis:

A. 3 months

B. 6 months

C. 1 year

D. 2 years

  1. Syphilis in pregnancy — penicillin allergy management:

A. Doxycycline 100mg BD × 14d

B. Azithromycin 2g PO

C. Desensitisation to penicillin (mandatory)

D. Ceftriaxone 1g IM

17. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 28-year-old man presents with a single, painless, clean-based, indurated ulcer on the penile shaft, bilateral non-tender inguinal nodes. RPR 1:32, TPHA positive. Best treatment:

A. Doxycycline 100mg BD × 14d

B. Benzathine Penicillin G 2.4MU IM single dose

C. Ceftriaxone 1g IM daily × 10d

D. Benzathine Penicillin G weekly × 3 doses

  1. A 32-year-old woman, 16 weeks pregnant, RPR 1:64, TPHA positive. No prior treatment. Penicillin allergy (anaphylaxis). Best management:

A. Doxycycline 100mg BD × 14d

B. Azithromycin 2g PO

C. Penicillin desensitisation, then Benzathine Penicillin G stage-appropriate

D. Ceftriaxone 1g IM daily × 10d

  1. A 45-year-old man with aortic regurgitation, diastolic murmur, widened pulse pressure. RPR 1:16, TPHA positive. No neurological symptoms. Likely tertiary manifestation:

A. Gumma

B. Cardiovascular (aortitis → aortic regurgitation)

C. Neurosyphilis

D. General paresis

  1. A 30-year-old HIV+ man, CD4 350, RPR 1:8, TPHA positive, no prior treatment. Asymptomatic. CSF: 5 WBC, protein 55. Best management:

A. Benzathine Penicillin G 2.4MU IM single dose

B. Penicillin G 18–24MU IV daily × 14d (neurosyphilis in HIV = treat as neuro)

C. Doxycycline 100mg BD × 28d

D. Ceftriaxone 2g IV daily × 14d

  1. A 25-year-old woman treated for primary syphilis (Benzathine Penicillin 2.4MU IM). Day 2: fever 39°C, rigors, hypotension, tachycardia. Diagnosis:

A. Sepsis

B. Anaphylaxis

C. Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction

D. Serum sickness

  1. Same patient — management of JH reaction:

A. Stop penicillin

B. Supportive (fluids, antipyretics); continue penicillin; warn patient

C. Adrenaline

D. Steroids

  1. A 35-year-old man, RPR negative, TPHA positive, no prior treatment, no symptoms. Most likely:

A. No syphilis

B. Prozone effect (dilute serum for RPR) OR early primary OR treated syphilis

C. False positive TPHA

D. Late latent (RPR can revert negative)

  1. A neonate born to mother with untreated secondary syphilis (RPR 1:128). Infant workup:

A. No workup needed

B. RPR (quantitative) + CSF (VDRL, cell count, protein) + long bones X-ray + CBC + LFT

C. TPHA only

D. Physical exam only

  1. Argyll Robertson pupil — characteristic:

A. Dilated, fixed

B. Accommodates but does not react to light

C. Constricted, reacts to light

D. Unequal, reacts to light

  1. A 40-year-old man with personality change, memory loss, tremor, hyperreflexia, Argyll Robertson pupils. CSF: lymphocytic pleocytosis, high protein, positive VDRL. Diagnosis:

A. Meningovascular syphilis

B. General paresis (dementia paralytica)

C. Tabes dorsalis

D. Asymptomatic neurosyphilis

18. Flashcards

  • Q: Primary chancre

A: Single, painless, clean, indurated, non-tender nodes

  • Q: Secondary rash

A: Truncal + palms/soles + condylomata lata

  • Q: Early latent

A: <1yr, infectious

  • Q: Late latent

A: ≥1yr, not infectious

  • Q: Neurosyphilis Rx

A: Penicillin G 18–24MU IV × 14d (NOT benzathine)

  • Q: Benzathine for late latent

A: 2.4MU IM weekly × 3 doses

  • Q: JH reaction

A: 2–12h post-penicillin; fever, rigors, hypotension; warn, support, don't stop

  • Q: RPR neg, TPHA pos

A: Treated / late latent / prozone / early primary

  • Q: Partner notification

A: 1°=3m, 2°=6m, early latent=1y

  • Q: Syphilis + HIV

A: CSF exam ALL; lower neuro threshold

19. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQ 1: B — Chancre = single, painless, clean-based, indurated.

MCQ 2: B — Truncal + palmar/plantar = secondary.

MCQ 3: B — Condylomata lata = secondary syphilis.

MCQ 4: C — Late latent = ≥1 year or unknown duration.

MCQ 5: B — Neurosyphilis = IV Penicillin G 18–24MU × 14d (benzathine doesn't cross BBB).

MCQ 6: B — Late latent = 2.4MU IM weekly × 3 doses.

MCQ 7: B — RPR neg, TPHA pos = treated, late latent, prozone, or early primary.

MCQ 8: B — JH reaction 2–12h post-penicillin.

MCQ 9: B — 2° = 6 months look-back.

MCQ 10: C — Pregnancy + penicillin allergy = desensitisation mandatory (doxy contraindicated).

SBA 1: B — Primary syphilis = single dose benzathine penicillin.

SBA 2: C — Pregnancy + penicillin allergy = desensitisation (doxy/azithro contraindicated/insufficient).

SBA 3: B — Aortic regurgitation = cardiovascular syphilis (aortitis).

SBA 4: B — HIV + syphilis + CSF abnormality = neurosyphilis → IV Penicillin G.

SBA 5: C — JH reaction = supportive, continue penicillin, warn.

SBA 6: B — Supportive, don't stop penicillin.

SBA 7: B — RPR neg + TPHA pos + no treatment = prozone / early primary / treated.

SBA 8: B — Congenital workup = infant RPR + CSF + long bones + CBC/LFT.

SBA 9: B — Argyll Robertson = accommodates, no light reflex.

SBA 10: B — Dementia + Argyll Robertson pupils = general paresis.

Status: Full FCPS/MRCP topic note completed — 2026-06-13

Secondary Syphilis (4–10 weeks post-chancre)

Stage-Based Regimens
Jarisch-Herxheimer Reaction

7. Follow-Up and Test of Cure

Must Know

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 15.1.18 Urethritis Syndrome Mucopurulent Vs Non Gonococcal

Related: [[Neisseria gonorrhoeae Infections (Uncomplicated and Disseminated)]], [[Chlamydia trachomatis (Genital, LGV, Trachoma)]], [[Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (PID)]], [[Epididymo-Orchitis]]

[!important]
Syndromic management is the cornerstone of STI control — treat empirically based on syndrome while awaiting lab confirmation. Urethritis = discharge + dysuria ± itch. Distinguish gonococcal (GC) vs non-gonococcal (NGU).

1. Learning Objectives

  • Apply syndromic approach to urethral discharge
  • Differentiate gonococcal vs non-gonococcal urethritis clinically and microscopically
  • Know empirical treatment regimens (dual therapy for GC)
  • Understand partner notification and test-of-cure principles

2. Definition

  • Urethritis: Urethral inflammation → discharge ± dysuria
  • Gonococcal urethritis (GC): Caused by Neisseria gonorrhoeae
  • Non-gonococcal urethritis (NGU): Chlamydia trachomatis (50%), M. genitalium, Ureaplasma, TV, HSV
  • Mucopurulent cervicitis (MPC): Female equivalent of urethritis

3. Aetiology

SyndromeCommon PathogensProportion
GonococcalNeisseria gonorrhoeae20–30% (varies by setting)
Non-gonococcal (NGU)Chlamydia trachomatis~50% of NGU
Mycoplasma genitalium15–25%
Ureaplasma urealyticum10–20%
Trichomonas vaginalis<5%
HSV, adenovirus<5%
No pathogen identified20–30%

4. Clinical Features

FeatureGonococcalNon-Gonococcal (Chlamydia)
Incubation2–7 days7–21 days
DischargeCopious, purulent, yellow-greenScant, mucoid, clear-white
DysuriaModerate-severeMild-moderate
Meatal erythemaProminentMild
ComplicationsEpididymitis, DGI, strictureEpididymitis, reactive arthritis, PID (♀)

5. Microscopy (Gram Stain Urethral Smear)

FindingInterpretation
≥5 PMNL/hpf + Intracellular Gram-negative diplococciGonococcal (presumptive)
≥5 PMNL/hpf + No organismsNon-gonococcal (presumptive)
<5 PMNL/hpfNormal / non-specific
Note: Female endocervical smear less reliable for GC; culture/NAAT preferred.

6. Diagnostic Testing (NAAT = Gold Standard)

TestSpecimenSensitivityNotes
NAAT (PCR/TMA)Urine (first-catch), urethral swab, vaginal swab>95%Preferred for GC & CT
Culture (GC)Urethral/endocervical/rectal/pharyngeal90–95%Needed for AST (resistance)
MicroscopyUrethral smear (male)90% (GC)Rapid, but operator-dependent
POC NAATUrine/swab90–95%Emerging (e.g., GeneXpert)

7. Empirical Treatment (Syndromic — Treat Before Results)

Male Urethritis (Empirical Dual Therapy)

Treat for BOTH GC + CT simultaneously (high co-infection rate, rising GC resistance)
RegimenDoseNotes
Ceftriaxone 500mg IM (1g if >150kg) + Doxycycline 100mg BD × 7dWHO/BASHH 1st-lineCovers GC (incl. resistant) + CT + M. genitalium (partial)
Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Azithromycin 1g PO**AlternativeIf doxycycline contraindicated (pregnancy)
Gentamicin 240mg IM + Azithromycin 2g PO**Cephalosporin allergySC/IM

Mucopurulent Cervicitis (Female) — Empirical

RegimenDose
Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Doxycycline 100mg BD × 7d1st-line
Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Azithromycin 1g POAlternative

NGU Confirmed (CT Negative) — M. genitalium Suspected

  • Moxifloxacin 400mg OD × 7–10d (if macrolide-resistant)
  • Doxycycline 100mg BD × 7d → then Azithromycin 500mg day 1, 250mg days 2–5 (UK guideline)

8. Partner Management

  • All partners within 2 weeks (GC) / 4 weeks (CT) / 3 months (syphilis) / 6 months (HIV) should be notified, tested, and treated empirically
  • Expedited Partner Therapy (EPT): Give patient medication/prescription for partners without clinical assessment
  • Abstain 7 days after treatment completion (both partners)

9. Complications

SyndromeComplications
GCEpididymo-orchitis, DGI (arthritis-dermatitis), stricture, perihepatitis (Fitz-Hugh-Curtis)
CTEpididymitis, reactive arthritis (SARA), PID, infertility, ectopic pregnancy, neonatal pneumonia/conjunctivitis
M. genitaliumPersistent NGU, cervicitis, PID, possible infertility

10. Test of Cure (TOC)

PathogenTOC TimingMethod
GC72 hours (if pelvic/pharyngeal) / not routine if uncomplicated genitalNAAT
CTNot routine (if compliant); if pregnant or symptoms persist → 4–6 weeksNAAT
M. genitalium4–6 weeks (high resistance risk)NAAT + resistance testing

11. Antimicrobial Resistance — Gonorrhoea

  • Ceftriaxone resistance emerging — dual therapy essential
  • Ciprofloxacin resistance >90% — do not use
  • Azithromycin resistance rising — prefer doxycycline
  • Surveillance: GASP (Gonorrhoea Antimicrobial Surveillance Programme)

12. Special Situations

SituationAdjustment
PregnancyAvoid doxycycline → Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Azithromycin 1g PO
Penicillin/cephalosporin allergyGentamicin 240mg IM + Azithromycin 2g PO
Pharyngeal GCHarder to eradicate → Ceftriaxone 1g IM + Azithromycin 2g PO
DGI (Disseminated Gonococcal Infection)Ceftriaxone 1g IV q24h → step down to oral after 24–48h improvement
Neonatal conjunctivitisCeftriaxone 50mg/kg IM/IV single dose (max 125mg)

13. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • Urethritis = dual therapy (GC + CT) — ceftriaxone + doxycycline
  • Gram stain: ≥5 PMNL + intracellular GNDC = GC; ≥5 PMNL + no organisms = NGU
  • TOC for GC at 72h if pharyngeal/pelvic; not routine for uncomplicated genital
  • CT TOC not routine unless pregnant or symptoms persist
  • M. genitalium → suspect if NGU persists after doxycycline; moxifloxacin
  • Pharyngeal GC harder to clear → higher dose ceftriaxone + azithromycin

14. Common Viva Questions

  1. What is the empirical treatment for male urethritis?
  2. How do you differentiate gonococcal from non-gonococcal urethritis on microscopy?
  3. What is the test of cure for gonorrhoea?
  4. How do you treat M. genitalium urethritis?
  5. What is the management of pharyngeal gonorrhoea?

15. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

  • Azithromycin 1g single dose for CT is NO LONGER 1st-line (resistance) → doxycycline 7d
  • TOC for uncomplicated genital GC NOT routinely required — only if pharyngeal/pelvic or symptoms persist
  • Treating NGU with azithromycin alone misses M. genitalium — doxycycline first
  • Cephalosporin allergy → gentamicin + azithromycin (not just azithromycin)
  • Female GC often asymptomatic — screen partners of male GC cases

16. Mnemonics

  • Urethritis dual therapy: Ceftriaxone (GC) + Doxycycline (CT)
  • Microscopy: PMNL + diplococci = GC; PMNL only = NGU
  • TOC: GC pharyngeal/pelvic at 72h; CT not routine
  • M. genitalium: Doxycycline first, then moxifloxacin

17. One-Page Revision Summary

Urethritis = Dual Therapy

  • GC: purulent, 2–7d incubation, Gram− diplococci in PMNL
  • NGU: mucoid, 7–21d, no organisms on Gram stain
  • Treat: Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Doxycycline 100mg BD × 7d
  • TOC: GC pharyngeal/72h; CT not routine
  • Partner notification: 2w (GC), 4w (CT)

18. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • Dual therapy for urethritis (ceftriaxone + doxycycline)
  • GC vs NGU microscopy differentiation
  • Partner notification timeframes
  • TOC indications (GC pharyngeal/pelvic)
  • M. genitalium as cause of persistent NGU

Should Know

  • Pharyngeal GC → higher dose ceftriaxone + azithromycin
  • DGI presentation and management
  • Cephalosporin allergy alternative (gentamicin + azithromycin)
  • Neonatal GC prevention (eye prophylaxis)

Nice to Know

  • M. genitalium resistance testing (macrolide vs fluoroquinolone)
  • GASP surveillance data
  • Expedited partner therapy evidence base

19. MCQs (10)

  1. First-line empirical treatment for male urethritis (BASHH/WHO):

A. Azithromycin 1g single dose

B. Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Doxycycline 100mg BD × 7d

C. Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Azithromycin 1g

D. Ciprofloxacin 500mg BD × 3d

  1. Gram stain urethral smear showing ≥5 PMNL/hpf with intracellular Gram-negative diplococci:

A. Non-gonococcal urethritis

B. Gonococcal urethritis (presumptive)

C. Normal

D. M. genitalium

  1. Most common cause of non-gonococcal urethritis:

A. Mycoplasma genitalium

B. Ureaplasma urealyticum

C. Chlamydia trachomatis

D. Trichomonas vaginalis

  1. Test of cure for uncomplicated genital gonorrhoea:

A. Not routinely required

B. NAAT at 72 hours

C. Culture at 1 week

D. NAAT at 4 weeks

  1. Ceftriaxone dose for pharyngeal gonorrhoea:

A. 250mg IM

B. 1g IM

C. 500mg IM

D. 2g IV

  1. First-line treatment for M. genitalium urethritis (UK):

A. Azithromycin 1g

B. Doxycycline 100mg BD × 7d

C. Doxycycline 100mg BD × 7d → Azithromycin 500mg then 250mg × 4d (or moxifloxacin)

D. Moxifloxacin 400mg × 7d

  1. Partner notification period for gonorrhoea:

A. 4 weeks

B. 2 weeks

C. 3 months

D. 6 months

  1. Disseminated gonococcal infection (DGI) — classic triad:

A. Arthritis, dermatitis, tenosynovitis

B. Meningitis, arthritis, dermatitis

C. Arthritis-dermatitis syndrome + tenosynovitis + fever

D. Endocarditis, arthritis, rash

  1. Neonatal ophthalmia neonatorum prophylaxis:

A. Erythromycin ointment

B. Ceftriaxone 50mg/kg IM/IV single dose (or topical povidone-iodine)

C. Azithromycin oral

D. No prophylaxis needed

  1. Alternative if cephalosporin allergy for GC:

A. Azithromycin 2g

B. Gentamicin 240mg IM + Azithromycin 2g PO

C. Doxycycline 100mg BD × 7d

D. Ciprofloxacin 500mg BD

20. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 24-year-old man presents with purulent urethral discharge for 3 days. Gram stain: ≥5 PMNL, intracellular GNDC. Best empirical treatment:

A. Azithromycin 1g

B. Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Doxycycline 100mg BD × 7d

C. Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Azithromycin 1g

D. Ciprofloxacin 500mg BD

  1. Same patient, also has pharyngeal gonorrhoea (oral sex). Adjusted treatment:

A. Same as genital

B. Ceftriaxone 1g IM + Azithromycin 2g PO

C. Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Doxycycline

D. Gentamicin + Azithromycin

  1. A 30-year-old woman, partner treated for GC. She is asymptomatic. Testing:

A. No testing needed

B. NAAT (urine/endocervical) for GC + CT + empirical treatment

C. Culture only

D. Wait for symptoms

  1. A 28-year-old man treated for NGU (doxycycline 7d). Returns 3 weeks later with persistent mucoid discharge. NAAT: CT negative, GC negative. Next step:

A. Repeat doxycycline

B. Suspect M. genitalium → moxifloxacin 400mg OD × 7–10d (or extended azithromycin)

C. Azithromycin 1g

D. No further treatment

  1. A pregnant woman at 28 weeks diagnosed with gonorrhoea. Treatment:

A. Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Doxycycline 100mg BD × 7d

B. Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Azithromycin 1g PO

C. Ceftriaxone 250mg IM only

D. Ciprofloxacin 500mg BD

  1. A man with urethritis, NAAT positive for CT only. He was treated with azithromycin 1g (old guideline). Returns with persistent symptoms. Likely reason:

A. Reinfection

B. Azithromycin resistance in CT / M. genitalium co-infection (doxycycline now 1st-line)

C. Non-compliance

D. Wrong diagnosis

  1. M. genitalium resistance testing — what is tested?

A. Beta-lactamase

B. Macrolide (23S rRNA) and fluoroquinolone (parC/gyrA) mutations

C. Doxycycline MIC

D. Azithromycin MIC only

  1. A 22-year-old man with asymptomatic GC on screening. TOC required?

A. Yes, NAAT at 72h

B. No, uncomplicated genital GC — no routine TOC

C. Yes, culture at 1 week

D. Yes, NAAT at 4 weeks

  1. Expedited partner therapy (EPT) for CT — what is given?

A. Ceftriaxone + doxycycline

B. Doxycycline 100mg BD × 7d (or azithromycin 1g if pregnant)

C. Ceftriaxone only

C. Azithromycin 1g only

  1. A man with severe penicillin/cephalosporin allergy and gonorrhoea. Best alternative:

A. Azithromycin 2g

B. Gentamicin 240mg IM + Azithromycin 2g PO

C. Doxycycline 100mg BD × 7d

D. Ciprofloxacin 500mg BD × 3d

21. Flashcards

  • Q: Urethritis empirical Rx

A: Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Doxycycline 100mg BD × 7d

  • Q: GC microscopy

A: ≥5 PMNL + intracellular GNDC

  • Q: NGU microscopy

A: ≥5 PMNL + no organisms

  • Q: Most common NGU cause

A: Chlamydia trachomatis (~50%)

  • Q: TOC uncomplicated GC

A: Not routinely required

  • Q: TOC CT

A: Not routine (unless pregnant/symptoms persist)

  • Q: Pharyngeal GC

A: Ceftriaxone 1g IM + Azithromycin 2g

  • Q: M. genitalium 1st-line

A: Doxycycline 7d → extended azithromycin OR moxifloxacin

  • Q: Partner notification

A: GC 2w, CT 4w, Syphilis 3m

  • Q: Cephalosporin allergy GC

A: Gentamicin 240mg IM + Azithromycin 2g PO

22. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQ 1: B — Dual therapy covers GC + CT (doxycycline now preferred over azithromycin for CT).

MCQ 2: B — Intracellular Gram-negative diplococci in PMNL = GC.

MCQ 3: C — CT ~50% of NGU.

MCQ 4: A — Uncomplicated genital GC: no routine TOC.

MCQ 5: B — Pharyngeal GC harder to eradicate → 1g ceftriaxone.

MCQ 6: C — UK: doxycycline 7d → azithromycin 500mg/250mg×4d; or moxifloxacin.

MCQ 7: B — GC partner notification 2 weeks.

MCQ 8: C — DGI = arthritis-dermatitis + tenosynovitis + fever.

MCQ 9: B — Ceftriaxone 50mg/kg IM/IV single dose (or povidone-iodine).

MCQ 10: B — Gentamicin + azithromycin for cephalosporin allergy.

SBA 1: B — Dual therapy is 1st-line for all male urethritis.

SBA 2: B — Pharyngeal GC → 1g ceftriaxone + 2g azithromycin.

SBA 3: B — Asymptomatic partner of GC = test + empirical dual therapy.

SBA 4: B — Persistent NGU post-doxycycline = M. genitalium → moxifloxacin.

SBA 5: B — Pregnancy: avoid doxycycline → ceftriaxone + azithromycin.

SBA 6: B — Azithromycin resistance rising; M. genitalium missed.

SBA 7: B — 23S rRNA (macrolide) + parC/gyrA (fluoroquinolone) mutations.

SBA 8: B — Uncomplicated genital GC = no routine TOC.

SBA 9: B — EPT for CT = doxycycline 7d (or azithromycin if pregnant).

SBA 10: B — Gentamicin + azithromycin for cephalosporin allergy.

Mucopurulent Cervicitis (Female) — Empirical
NGU Confirmed (CT Negative) — M. genitalium Suspected

▸ 15.2 15.2 3
▸ 15.2.01 2 1 Chlamydia

Parent Topic: STI MOC → STI Hierarchy

Status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

Priority: ⭐⭐⭐ HIGHEST (FCPS/MRCP — Most Common Bacterial STI, LGV, Neonatal, Screening)

Source: Davidson 24th Ed Ch 13; WHO/BASHH/CDC Guidelines; FCPS/MRCP Syllabus; NICE CG110

🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Recognise Chlamydia trachomatis clinical manifestations (Genital, LGV, Extra-genital, Neonatal)
  • [ ] Apply diagnostic algorithms (NAAT, Culture, POC) and interpret results
  • [ ] Apply evidence-based treatment (Doxycycline, Azithromycin, Amoxicillin in Pregnancy)
  • [ ] Manage LGV (L1/L2/L3 Serovars) and Extra-genital infections (Pharyngeal, Rectal, Conjunctival)
  • [ ] Manage Neonatal Chlamydia (Ophthalmia Neonatorum, Pneumonia)
  • [ ] Implement Screening, Partner Notification, Test of Cure strategies
  • [ ] Answer viva: "Chlamydia screening in pregnancy" and "LGV vs Classic Chlamydia" and "Test of Cure timing"

🧠 Core Concept: Chlamydia trachomatis Biology

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Chlamydia trachomatis
↓
Obligate Intracellular Bacterium
↓
Developmental Cycle
Serovars
Pathogenesis
↓
Replication → EB Release → Cell Lysis
Entry → Endosome → RB
A-K] → Genital Infections (D-K
L1, L2, L3] → **LGV** (Invasive
A-C] → **Trachoma** (Ocular
Invasion → Epithelial Cells → Inflammation
Immune Evasion → Persistence, Reinfection
Scarring → Fibrosis → Sequelae (PID, Ectopic, Infertility)

1️⃣ Chlamydia trachomatis — Genital Infection

Epidemiology

ParameterDetail
Global Incidence~129 Million New Cases/Year (WHO 2023)
Prevalence3.8% (Women 15-49), 2.7% (Men 15-49)
Age Peak15-24 Years (Highest Incidence)
Asymptomatic Rate70-80% Women, 50% Men — Silent Epidemic
TransmissionSexual (Vaginal, Anal, Oral), Vertical (Mother→Child)
Co-infection10-40% with Gonorrhoea; HIV Co-infection ↑ Risk

Clinical Presentation

SexSymptomaticAsymptomaticKey Features
MaleUrethritis (50%)50%Dysuria, Urethral Discharge (Mucopurulent), Meatal Erythema, Epididymo-orchitis (Complication)
FemaleCervicitis (30%)70-80%Cervicitis (Mucopurulent Discharge, Ectopy, Friability), PID (↑ Risk), Dyspareunia, Intermenstrual Bleeding
RectalProctitis (MSM)Often AsymptomaticMucus Discharge, Tenesmus, Rectal Pain
PharyngealRarely SymptomaticMost AsymptomaticUsually Asymptomatic Carriage
Key: 70-80% Women Asymptomatic → Screening Essential (Opportunistic & Targeted)

2️⃣ Lymphogranuloma Venereum (LGV)

FeatureDetail
SerovarsL1, L2, L3 (Invasive, Invade Lymphatics)
EpidemiologyMSM Outbreaks (Europe, NA), Endemic in Africa, SE Asia, S. America
TransmissionSexual (Anal > Vaginal), MSM Outbreaks (Proctitis)

Clinical Stages

StageFeaturesDuration
PrimaryPainless Papule/Ulcer (Genital/Rectal), Transient, Often MissedDays-Weeks
SecondarySuppurative Inguinal/Femoral Buboes (Matted, Fluctuant), Proctocolitis (Rectal Pain, Tenesmus, Bloody Mucus, Constipation), Fever, ConstitutionalWeeks-Months
TertiaryChronic — Strictures, Fistulas, Rectal Strictures, Elephantiasis (Genital), Frozen Pelvis (Frozen Pelvis Syndrome)Years

Diagnosis

TestUtility
NAAT (CT + LGV Specific)1st Line — Differentiates LGV (L1/L2/L3) from Non-LGV CT
SerologyComplement Fixation (CF) — Historical, Low Sensitivity
CultureRarely Available, Specialised Labs

Treatment (LGV)

RegimenDose & Duration
Doxycycline100mg BD × 21 Days (Longer than Genital CT)
AlternativeErythromycin 500mg QID × 21d (Pregnancy)
Partner ManagementAll Partners Last 60 Days — Screen, Treat, Counselling

3️⃣ Extra-Genital Infections

Pharyngeal Chlamydia

FeatureDetail
PrevalenceHigh in MSM (Pharyngeal CT ~3-5%), Low in Heterosexual
SymptomsUsually Asymptomatic, Sore Throat (Rare)
TestingNAAT (Pharyngeal Swab) — Not Routine Unless Risk
TreatmentSame as Genital (Doxycycline/Azithromycin)

Rectal Chlamydia (Including LGV)

FeatureDetail
Risk GroupsMSM (Receptive Anal), Women (Anal Sex)
SymptomsProctitis (Pain, Mucus, Blood, Tenesmus), LGV: Proctocolitis, Buboes
TestingNAAT (Rectal Swab) — LGV-Specific PCR if Proctitis
TreatmentLGV: Doxy 100mg BD × 21d; Non-LGV: Standard CT Regimen

Conjunctival Chlamydia

FeatureDetail
TransmissionAutoinoculation (Genital→Eye), Direct Contact
ClinicalFollicular Conjunctivitis, Mucopurulent Discharge, Preauricular Lymphadenopathy
DiagnosisNAAT (Conjunctival Swab), Giemsa (Inclusion Bodies)
TreatmentSystemic (Doxy/Azithro) + Topical (Tetracycline/Erythromycin Ointment)

4️⃣ Neonatal Chlamydia

Ophthalmia Neonatorum

FeatureDetail
OnsetDay 5-14 Post-Partum (Incubation 5-14 Days)
ClinicalMucopurulent Conjunctivitis, Eyelid Oedema, Conjunctival Injection
ComplicationsCorneal Scarring, Blindness (If Untreated)
DiagnosisNAAT (Conjunctival Swab), Giemsa (Inclusion Bodies)

| Treatment | Systemic: Azithromycin 20mg/kg/dose OD × 3d (Oral) OR Erythromycin 50mg/kg/d ÷4 × 14d

Topical: Erythromycin/Tetracycline Ointment QID × 14d |

Neonatal Pneumonia

FeatureDetail
Onset3-16 Weeks Post-Partum
ClinicalStaccato Cough, Tachypnoea, Wheeze, Hypoxaemia, No Fever, CXR: Bilateral Diffuse Infiltrates, Hyperinflation
DiagnosisNAAT (Nasopharyngeal Swab), CXR (Bilateral Diffuse Infiltrates, Hyperinflation)
TreatmentAzithromycin 20mg/kg/dose OD × 10-14 Days (Preferred), Erythromycin Alternative

4️⃣ Diagnosis — Algorithms & Interpretation

Diagnostic Algorithm

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Suspected Chlamydia
↓
Symptomatic?
↓
NAAT (Urine/Swab) 1st Line
↓
NAAT Result
↓
Treat + Partner Notify Test of Cure (GC: 2w, CT: 4w)
Consider Other Causes MG, TV, HSV, Urethral Syndrome
↓
Test of Cure (CT: 4 Weeks) If Pregnant: Repeat at 36w

Diagnostic Test Comparison

TestSensitivitySpecificityTATSampleBest For
NAAT (PCR/TMA/SDA)>95%>98%1-24hUrine, Swab (Endocervical, Vaginal, Urethral, Rectal, Pharyngeal)1st Line (All Sites)
Culture70-80%100%48-72hSwabAST, Legal, Treatment Failure
POC NAAT90-95%97-99%20-90minSwab/UrineRapid/Outreach
Rapid Antigen70-80%95-98%10-30minSwabNot Recommended (Low Sensitivity)
SerologyLowLowDaysSerumNot for Acute Diagnosis (Past Infection)

Specimen Collection

SiteSpecimenTransport
Male UrethralFirst-Catch Urine (20-30mL, Hold ≥1h) OR Urethral SwabNAAT Transport Medium
Female EndocervicalEndocervical Swab (Remove Mucus First)NAAT Transport Medium
Female VaginalSelf-Collected Vaginal Swab (Validated)NAAT Transport Medium
RectalRectal Swab (Insert 2-3cm)NAAT Transport Medium
PharyngealPharyngeal SwabNAAT Transport Medium
ConjunctivalConjunctival SwabNAAT Transport Medium
Key: Urine = Non-Invasive, Good for Screening; Swab = Better for Symptomatic, Extra-Genital Sites

4️⃣ Treatment — Evidence-Based Regimens

Uncomplicated Genital Chlamydia

Population1st LineAlternativeDuration
Adults/Adolescents (≥12y)Doxycycline 100mg BD × 7 DaysAzithromycin 1g Stat (Single Dose)7 Days / Single Dose
PregnancyAzithromycin 1g Stat (Safe)Amoxicillin 500mg TDS × 7dSingle Dose / 7 Days
BreastfeedingAzithromycin 1g Stat (Compatible)Erythromycin 500mg QID × 7-14dSingle Dose
Allergy (Tetracycline/Macrolide)Levofloxacin 500mg OD × 7dMoxifloxacin 400mg OD × 7d7 Days
Key: Doxycycline 1st Line (Higher Cure Rate, Rectal Efficacy); Azithro 1g Stat = Convenience, Pregnancy Safe

Rectal Chlamydia (Non-LGV)

PopulationRegimen
AllDoxycycline 100mg BD × 7 Days (Preferred — Better Rectal Cure)

Lymphogranuloma Venereum (LGV)

RegimenDuration
Doxycycline 100mg BD × 21 Days21 Days (Longer than Genital CT)
AlternativeErythromycin 500mg QID × 21d (Pregnancy)

Neonatal Chlamydia

ConditionRegimen
Ophthalmia NeonatorumAzithromycin 20mg/kg/dose OD × 3 Days (Oral) + Topical Erythromycin/Tetracycline Ointment QID × 14d
PneumoniaAzithromycin 20mg/kg/dose OD × 10-14 Days (Preferred)

5️⃣ Partner Notification & Test of Cure

Partner Notification

TemplateAction
Index PatientNotify All Partners Last 60 Days (or Last Partner if >60 Days)
Methods1. Patient Referral (Preferred) 2. Provider Referral 3. Contract Referral (Time-Limited)
EPT (Expedited Partner Therapy)Legal Where Permitted: Provide Medication to Index Patient for Partner(s) — CT: Doxy 100mg BD × 7d; GC: Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Azithro 1g

Test of Cure (TOC)

InfectionTimingTest
Gonorrhoea2 Weeks Post-TreatmentNAAT (or Culture + AST)
Chlamydia4 Weeks Post-TreatmentNAAT (Avoid <3w — False +)
LGV4-6 WeeksNAAT
M. genitalium4 WeeksNAAT (Resistance Testing)
Pregnancy3rd Trimester (36w)Repeat NAAT (Ensure Cure Pre-Delivery)
Key: No TOC <3 Weeks (Residual DNA → False Positive NAAT)

5️⃣ Screening & Public Health

Screening Recommendations (UK/NICE/CDC)

PopulationRecommendation
Sexually Active Women <25yAnnual CT/GC Screen (Opportunistic, Self-Sample)
Pregnant WomenBooking: CT/GC (If <25y or Risk Factors)
MSM3-Site Testing (Urethral, Pharyngeal, Rectal) Annual (3-6m if High Risk)
Pregnant Women <25yRoutine CT/GC Screen at Booking
Termination of PregnancyRoutine CT/GC Screen
HIV PositiveAnnual CT/GC/Syphilis (3-Site if MSM)

Partner Notification & EPT

StrategyDescription
Patient ReferralIndex Patient Informs Partners (Preferred — Empowers Patient)
Provider ReferralHealth Staff Notifies Partners (Confidential)
EPT (Expedited Partner Therapy)Legal Where Permitted: Index Patient Delivers Medication to Partner(s) — CT: Doxy 100mg BD × 7d; GC: Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Azithro 1g; TV: Metronidazole 2g Stat

6️⃣ Complications & Sequelae

ComplicationPathophysiologyLong-Term Impact
Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (PID)Ascending Infection → Endometritis, Salpingitis, PeritonitisInfertility (Tubal Factor), Ectopic Pregnancy (↑ 6-10x), Chronic Pelvic Pain
Epididymo-orchitisAscending Urethral InfectionInfertility (Obstructive), Chronic Scrotal Pain
Perihepatitis (Fitz-Hugh-Curtis)Peri-hepatic InflammationRUQ Pain, Violin String Adhesions
Reactive Arthritis (ReA)Post-Chlamydial (HLA-B27 Association)Asymmetric Oligoarthritis, Conjunctivitis, Urethritis (Reiter's Triad)
InfertilityTubal Damage (PID), Epididymal ObstructionPrimary/Secondary Infertility
Ectopic PregnancyTubal Damage (PID)↑ 6-10x Risk
PerinatalOphthalmia Neonatorum, PneumoniaBlindness, Chronic Lung Disease

7️⃣ Lymphogranuloma Venereum (LGV) — Detailed

AspectDetail
Causative AgentC. trachomatis Serovars L1, L2, L3 (Invasive)
EpidemiologyMSM Outbreaks (Europe, NA), Endemic (Africa, SE Asia, Caribbean)
TransmissionAnal Sex (MSM), Vaginal Sex (Endemic Areas)
Clinical Stages1°: Painless Ulcer → 2°: Suppurative Buboes, Proctocolitis → 3°: Strictures, Fistulas, Elephantiasis
DiagnosisNAAT (LGV-Specific PCR) — Differentiates L1/L2/L3 from Genital CT
TreatmentDoxycycline 100mg BD × 21 Days (Longer than Genital CT)
Partner ManagementAll Partners Last 60 Days — Screen, Treat, Counselling

⚡ FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Epidemiology129M New Cases/Year (Most Common Bacterial STI), 70-80% Women Asymptomatic, Peak 15-24y
ClinicalUrethritis (M), Cervicitis/PID (F), Rectal/Pharyngeal (MSM), LGV (MSM, Buboes, Proctitis)
DiagnosisNAAT (PCR/TMA/SDA) = 1st Line (Urine/Swab), >95% Sens/Spec; Culture for AST
TreatmentDoxycycline 100mg BD × 7d (1st Line); Azithromycin 1g Stat (Alt, Pregnancy); LGV: Doxy 21d
PregnancyAzithromycin 1g Stat (Safe); Avoid Doxycycline (Teratogenic)
LGVSerovars L1-3, Doxy 100mg BD × 21d, MSM Outbreaks, Proctitis/Buboes
NeonatalConjunctivitis (Day 5-14) → Azithro 20mg/kg OD × 3d + Topical; Pneumonia (3-16w, Staccato Cough) → Azithro 20mg/kg × 10-14d
Test of CureCT: 4 Weeks (NAAT); Pregnancy: Repeat at 36w; LGV: 4-6 Weeks
ScreeningWomen <25y Annual, MSM 3-Site Annual, Pregnancy <25y, HIV+ Annual
LGVSerovars L1/L2/L3, Doxy 21d, MSM Proctitis/Buboes
ComplicationsPID → Infertility, Ectopic, Chronic Pain; Reactive Arthritis (HLA-B27)
ScreeningWomen <25y Annual, MSM 3-Site Annual, Pregnancy <25y

🎤 Viva Questions (Expected Answers)

#QuestionExpected Answer
1Chlamydia — most common clinical presentation in women?Asymptomatic (70-80%); Symptomatic: Cervicitis (Mucopurulent Discharge, Ectopy, Friability), PID
2Chlamydia treatment in pregnancy?Azithromycin 1g Stat (Single Dose) — Safe, Effective; Doxycycline Contraindicated (Teratogenic)
3LGV vs non-LGV Chlamydia — key differences?LGV (Serovars L1-L3): Invasive, Lymphatic Spread, Buboes, Proctocolitis, Strictures; Treatment: Doxy 21d vs 7d for Non-LGV
4Test of Cure for Chlamydia — when & how?4 Weeks Post-Treatment (NAAT); Pregnancy: Repeat at 36w; Avoid <3w (False +ve Residual DNA)
5Neonatal chlamydial pneumonia — presentation & treatment?3-16 Weeks, Staccato Cough, Tachypnoea, CXR Bilateral Diffuse Infiltrates → Azithromycin 20mg/kg/d OD × 10-14d
6LGV vs non-LGV Chlamydia treatment difference?LGV: Doxy 100mg BD × 21 Days; Non-LGV Genital: Doxy 7d
7Chlamydia test of cure — timing & method?4 Weeks Post-Treatment (NAAT); Avoid <3w (False +ve); Pregnancy: Repeat at 36w
8Chlamydia in pregnancy — treatment?Azithromycin 1g Stat (Safe); Avoid Doxycycline (Teratogenic); Amoxicillin 500mg TDS × 7d (Alternative)
9LGV clinical presentation in MSM?Proctocolitis (Rectal Pain, Mucus/Blood, Tenesmus), Suppurative Inguinal Buboes, Strictures/Fistulas (Late)
10Chlamydia screening — who & when?Women <25y Annual, MSM 3-Site Annual (Urethral/Pharyngeal/Rectal), Pregnancy <25y, MSM High Risk 3-6 Monthly

🧩 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
"Chlamydia is rare"NO. Most Common Bacterial STI (129M New Cases/Year Globally)
"Chlamydia always symptomatic"NO. 70-80% Women Asymptomatic, 50% Men Asymptomatic — Screening Essential
"Doxycycline safe in pregnancy"NO. Contraindicated (Teratogenic — Teeth/Bone); Azithromycin 1g Stat is Safe
"AZM 1g = Doxy 7d in Efficacy"NO. Doxycycline Superior (Higher Cure Rate, Better Rectal Efficacy); AZM = Convenience/Pregnancy
"LGV = Just Another Chlamydia"NO. Different Serovars (L1-L3), Invasive, Lymphatic Spread, Doxy 21d vs 7d, Proctitis/Buboes/Strictures
"Test of Cure = Immediately After Treatment"NO. Wait 4 Weeks (Residual DNA → False Positive); Pregnancy: Repeat at 36 Weeks
"LGV = Only in Tropics"NO. MSM Outbreaks in Europe/N. America (L2b Variant), Endemic in Africa/SE Asia
"Rectal Chlamydia = Always LGV"NO. Most Rectal CT = Non-LGV (Genital Serovars D-K); LGV = Specific Proctocolitis/Buboes
"Azithromycin 1g = Inferior to Doxy"For Genital CT: Non-Inferior (Uncomplicated); For Rectal: Doxy Superior; Pregnancy: Azithro Only Option
"Test of Cure at 2 Weeks"NO. 4 Weeks Minimum (Residual DNA → False Positive); Pregnancy = 36 Weeks
Mnemonic: CHLAMYDIA MASTER
Chlamydia: Most Common Bacterial STI (129M/Year Global)
High Prevalence: 15-24y Peak, 70-80% Women Asymptomatic
LGV: L1/L2/L3 Serovars, Doxy 21d, MSM Proctitis/Buboes
Asymptomatic: 70-80% Women, 50% Men — Screening Essential
MAT: Pregnancy → Azithromycin 1g Stat (Safe); Doxy Contraindicated
Y (Why Screen?): Asymptomatic → PID → Infertility/Ectopic/Infertility
Dx: NAAT 1st Line (Urine/Swab), >95% Sens/Spec; Culture for AST
Immunology: Obligate Intracellular, EB→RB Cycle, Immune Evasion
Antibiotic: Doxy 100mg BD × 7d (1st Line), Azi 1g Stat (Pregnancy)
Treatment LGV: Doxy 100mg BD × 21d (Longer)
Neonatal: Ophthalmia (Day 5-14) → Azi 20mg/kg × 3d; Pneumonia (3-16wk) → Azi 20mg/kg × 10-14d
Test of Cure: 4 Weeks (NAAT), Pregnancy 36w, LGV 4-6w
Rectal CT: Doxy 7d (Better Rectal Cure); LGV Rectal = 21d Doxy
Reproductive Sequelae: PID → Infertility, Ectopic, Chronic Pain
AMR: Rare, But LGV Emerging (Monitor)
Screening: Women <25y Annual; MSM 3-Site Annual; Pregnancy <25y; HIV+ Annual
Test of Cure: 4 Weeks (NAAT), Pregnancy 36w, LGV 4-6w
Risk Factors: <25y, New Partner, Multiple Partners, Inconsistent Condom
Antibiotic: Doxy 100mg BD × 7d (1st Line), Azi 1g Stat (Pregnancy)

💬 Exam Answer Modes

FormatPromptKey Points
Long Essay"Describe the clinical manifestations, diagnosis, and management of genital Chlamydia trachomatis infection."Epidemiology, Clinical (M/F/Rectal/Pharyngeal/LGV/Neonatal), Diagnosis (NAAT 1st Line), Treatment (Doxy 1st Line, Azi in Pregnancy, LGV 21d), Complications (PID, Infertility, Ectopic, Reactive Arthritis), Neonatal, Screening, Partner Notification, Test of Cure
Short Note"Chlamydia in pregnancy — diagnosis and management."NAAT 1st Line, Azithromycin 1g Stat (Safe), Avoid Doxycycline, Test of Cure at 36w, Partner Notification, Neonatal Prophylaxis if Untreated
Viva"25-year-old pregnant woman at 12 weeks tests positive for Chlamydia. Management?"Azithromycin 1g Stat (Safe in Pregnancy), Test of Cure at 36 Weeks, Partner Notification/Treatment, Counselling, Retest at 36w
Ward Round"MSM with rectal pain, mucus discharge. NAAT positive for Chlamydia. LGV PCR negative. Management?"Doxycycline 100mg BD × 7 Days (Non-LGV Rectal CT), Test of Cure at 4 Weeks, Partner Notification, HIV/Syphilis Screen, Condom Counselling
Last-Night"CT: 129M/yr, 70-80% F Asympt. NAAT 1st Line. Tx: Doxy 7d (1st), Azi 1g (Preg). LGV: L1-L3, Doxy 21d. Neonate: Ophthalmia (Azi 20mg/kg×3d), Pneumonia (Azi 20mg/kg×10-14d). TOC: 4w (NAAT), Preg=36w. LGV: Doxy 21d. Screen: <25y F Annual, MSM 3-Site Annual. Complications: PID, Infertility, Ectopic, Reactive Arthritis."Compressed.

📌 Summary

  • Chlamydia trachomatis: Most Common Bacterial STI (129M/Year), 70-80% Women Asymptomatic, Peak 15-24y
  • Clinical: Urethritis/Cervicitis/PID, Rectal/Pharyngeal (MSM), LGV (L1-L3) — Invasive, Buboes, Proctitis
  • Diagnosis: NAAT 1st Line (Urine/Swab, >95% Sens/Spec), Culture for AST
  • Treatment: Doxycycline 100mg BD × 7d (1st Line); Azithromycin 1g Stat (Pregnancy); LGV: Doxy 100mg BD × 21d
  • Pregnancy: Azithromycin 1g Stat (Safe); Avoid Doxycycline (Teratogenic)
  • Neonatal: Ophthalmia (Day 5-14) → Azithro 20mg/kg OD × 3d + Topical; Pneumonia (3-16w) → Azithro 20mg/kg × 10-14d
  • LGV: Serovars L1, L2, L3; Doxy 100mg BD × 21d; MSM Proctitis, Buboes, Strictures
  • Test of Cure: 4 Weeks (NAAT); Pregnancy → 36 Weeks; LGV 4-6 Weeks
  • Screening: Women <25y Annual, MSM 3-Site Annual, Pregnancy <25y, HIV+ Annual
  • Complications: PID → Infertility, Ectopic, Chronic Pain; Neonatal Ophthalmia/Pneumonia; Reactive Arthritis

❓ MCQs (10)

  1. Most common clinical presentation of Chlamydia in women?

A. Pelvic Inflammatory Disease B. Asymptomatic (70-80%) C. Cervicitis D. Urethritis

Answer: B. 70-80% of women with Chlamydia are asymptomatic.

  1. Chlamydia treatment in pregnancy — recommended regimen?

A. Doxycycline 100mg BD × 7 Days B. Azithromycin 1g Single Dose C. Erythromycin 500mg QID × 14 Days D. Ofloxacin 400mg BD × 7 Days

Answer: B. Azithromycin 1g Stat — Safe in Pregnancy; Doxycycline Contraindicated.

  1. Lymphogranuloma Venereum (LGV) — causative serovars?

A. A-K B. L1, L2, L3 C. A, B, Ba D. D-K

Answer: B. LGV caused by Serovars L1, L2, L3 (Invasive, Lymphotropic).

  1. LGV treatment duration vs. Non-LGV Chlamydia?

A. Same (7 Days) B. 21 Days (vs 7 Days for Non-LGV) C. 14 Days D. Single Dose

Answer: B. LGV requires Doxycycline 100mg BD × 21 Days (vs 7 Days for Non-LGV).

  1. Neonatal chlamydial conjunctivitis — presentation & treatment?

A. Day 1-2, Topical Only B. Day 5-14, Systemic Azithromycin 20mg/kg OD × 3 Days + Topical C. Day 1-3, IV Ceftriaxone D. Day 3-5, Oral Erythromycin

Answer: B. Onset Day 5-14, Systemic Azithromycin 20mg/kg OD × 3 Days + Topical Erythromycin/Tetracycline Ointment.

  1. Chlamydia test of cure — recommended timing?

A. 1 Week B. 2 Weeks C. 4 Weeks D. 8 Weeks

Answer: C. 4 Weeks Post-Treatment (NAAT); Avoid <3 Weeks (False Positive Residual DNA).

  1. LGV treatment duration vs Non-LGV Chlamydia?

A. 7 Days B. 10 Days C. 21 Days D. Single Dose

Answer: C. LGV requires Doxycycline 100mg BD × 21 Days (vs 7 Days for Non-LGV).

  1. Chlamydia screening — UK recommendation for women?

A. Annual for All Women B. Annual for Women <25 Years C. Every 5 Years D. Only If Symptomatic

Answer: B. Opportunistic Annual Screening for Sexually Active Women <25 Years (NICE).

  1. Chlamydia in pregnancy — first-line treatment?

A. Doxycycline 100mg BD × 7 Days B. Azithromycin 1g Single Dose C. Erythromycin 500mg QID × 14 Days D. Amoxicillin 500mg TDS × 7 Days

Answer: B. Azithromycin 1g Stat — Safe in Pregnancy; Doxycycline Contraindicated.

  1. LGV serovars?

A. A-K B. L1, L2, L3 C. D-K D. A, B, Ba

Answer: B. LGV Caused by Serovars L1, L2, L3 (Invasive, Lymphotropic).

📋 SBAs (10)

  1. 25-year-old pregnant woman at 12 weeks tests positive for Chlamydia on NAAT. Best treatment?

A. Doxycycline 100mg BD × 7 Days B. Azithromycin 1g Stat C. Erythromycin 500mg QID × 14 Days D. Ceftriaxone 500mg IM

Answer: B. Azithromycin 1g Stat — Safe in Pregnancy; Doxycycline Contraindicated.

  1. MSM with rectal pain, mucus discharge. NAAT positive for Chlamydia, LGV PCR negative. Management?

A. Doxycycline 100mg BD × 21 Days B. Doxycycline 100mg BD × 7 Days C. Ceftriaxone 500mg IM D. Azithromycin 1g

Answer: B. Non-LGV Rectal Chlamydia → Doxycycline 100mg BD × 7 Days.

  1. Neonate with mucopurulent conjunctivitis at Day 10 of life. Mother had untreated Chlamydia. Treatment?

A. Topical Erythromycin Only B. Systemic Azithromycin 20mg/kg/dose OD × 3 Days + Topical Erythromycin Ointment C. IV Ceftriaxone D. Oral Doxycycline

Answer: B. Systemic Azithromycin 20mg/kg/dose OD × 3 Days + Topical Erythromycin Ointment QID × 14 Days.

  1. MSM with painful proctitis, bloody mucus, inguinal buboes. NAAT positive for CT, LGV PCR positive. Treatment?

A. Doxycycline 100mg BD × 7 Days B. Doxycycline 100mg BD × 21 Days C. Azithromycin 1g Stat D. Ceftriaxone IM

Answer: B. LGV Confirmed → Doxycycline 100mg BD × 21 Days.

  1. Chlamydia test of cure — when to repeat NAAT?

A. 1 Week B. 2 Weeks C. 4 Weeks D. 8 Weeks

Answer: C. 4 Weeks Post-Treatment (NAAT); Avoid <3 Weeks (Residual DNA False Positive).

MCQsSBAs
1-B, 2-B, 3-B, 4-B, 5-B, 6-B, 7-B, 8-B, 9-B, 10-B1-B, 2-B, 3-B, 4-B, 5-B

▸ 15.2.02 2 2 Gonorrhoea

Parent Topic: STI MOC → STI Hierarchy

Status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

Priority: ⭐⭐⭐ HIGHEST (FCPS/MRCP — AMR Crisis, Dual Therapy, Pharyngeal/Rectal, DGI, Neonatal)

Source: Davidson 24th Ed Ch 13; WHO/BASHH/CDC Guidelines; FCPS/MRCP Syllabus; NICE NG162; WHO GASP

🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Recognise N. gonorrhoeae clinical manifestations (Urethritis, Cervicitis, PID, DGI, Pharyngeal, Rectal, Conjunctival)
  • [ ] Apply antimicrobial resistance (AMR) knowledge — Ceftriaxone resistance, dual therapy, alternative regimens
  • [ ] Apply evidence-based treatment algorithms for all sites (Genital, Pharyngeal, Rectal, DGI, Neonatal)
  • [ ] Implement diagnostic algorithms (NAAT, Culture + AST, Microscopy) and interpret results
  • [ ] Manage Disseminated Gonococcal Infection (DGI) — Arthritis-Dermatitis Syndrome, Septic Arthritis
  • [ ] Manage Neonatal Gonorrhoea (Ophthalmia Neonatorum, Scalp Abscess, DGI)
  • [ ] Implement Test of Cure, Partner Notification, AMR Surveillance strategies
  • [ ] Answer viva: "Gonorrhoea AMR" and "DGI presentation" and "Pharyngeal gonorrhoea treatment" and "Neonatal ophthalmia"

🧠 Core Concept: Neisseria gonorrhoeae Biology & AMR

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Neisseria gonorrhoeae
↓
Gram-Negative Diplococcus
↓
Obligate Human Pathogen
Virulence Factors
Antimicrobial Resistance Mechanisms
Clinical Syndromes
↓
Pili (Adherence, Antigenic Variation)
Opa Proteins (Invasion, Immune Evasion)
LPS (LOS, Endotoxic)
IgA1 Protease (Mucosal Immunity Evasion)
Chromosomal Mutations penA (Ceftriaxone), gyrA/parC (Ciprofloxacin), mtrR (Azithromycin), penB (Penicillin)
Plasmid-Mediated TetM (Tetracycline), TEM-1 (Penicillinase)
Efflux Pumps (MtrCDE)
Genital (Urethritis, Cervicitis, PID)
Extra-Genital (Pharyngeal, Rectal, Conjunctival)
DGI (Arthritis-Dermatitis, Septic Arthritis)
Neonatal (Ophthalmia, Scalp Abscess, DGI)

1️⃣ Gonorrhoea — Clinical Manifestations

Epidemiology

ParameterDetail
Global Incidence~82 Million New Cases/Year (WHO 2023)
Prevalence0.9% (Women 15-49), 0.7% (Men 15-49)
Age Peak15-24 Years (Highest Incidence)
Asymptomatic Rate10-20% Men, 50-80% Women, >90% Pharyngeal/Rectal
TransmissionSexual (Vaginal, Anal, Oral), Vertical (Mother→Child)
Co-infection10-40% with Chlamydia; HIV Co-infection ↑ Risk
AMR ThreatWHO Priority 2 (High) — Ceftriaxone Resistance Emerging Globally

Clinical Presentation by Site

SiteSymptomaticKey FeaturesComplications
Male Urethra80-90%Urethritis: Dysuria, Purulent Urethral Discharge, Meatal Erythema/EdemaEpididymo-orchitis, Stricture, DGI
Female Cervix30-50%Cervicitis: Mucopurulent Discharge, Ectopy, Friability, ICB/IM BleedingPID, Infertility, Ectopic, Chronic Pelvic Pain
RectalOften Asymptomatic (>90%)Proctitis: Mucus, Blood, Tenesmus, Pain (If Symptomatic)Proctitis, Strictures, Anal Fissures
PharyngealRarely Symptomatic (>90% Asymptomatic)Sore Throat, Exudate (Rare)Reservoir for AMR, Transmission
ConjunctivalAcuteHyperacute Conjunctivitis: Copious Purulent Discharge, Eyelid Edema, ChemosisCorneal Ulceration, Perforation, Blindness
DGI0.5-3%Arthritis-Dermatitis Syndrome (Migratory Polyarthralgia, Tenosynovitis, Pustular Rash) → Septic Arthritis (Monoarticular)Septic Arthritis, Endocarditis, Meningitis

1️⃣ Disseminated Gonococcal Infection (DGI)

Pathophysiology

MechanismDetail
BacteremiaGonococci Enter Bloodstream → Haematogenous Spread
Immune ComplexesIgG-Gonococcal Antigen Complexes → Vasculitis, Dermatitis
Direct InvasionJoints, Skin, Tendons, Pericardium, Meninges

Clinical Presentation

SyndromeFeaturesPrevalence
Arthritis-Dermatitis SyndromeFever, Migratory Polyarthralgia, Tenosynovitis (Wrist/Ankle), Pustular Rash (Vesiculopustular, Acral/Distal Extremities), Palpable Purpura~60%
Septic ArthritisMonoarticular (Knee > Wrist > Ankle), Severe Pain, Effusion, Fever, No Rash~40%
OtherEndocarditis, Meningitis, Hepatitis, MyocarditisRare
Key: DGI = Bacteremia + Arthritis/Dermatitis; Blood Culture Often Negative; Joint Fluid Culture + NAAT for Diagnosis

Diagnosis

TestUtility
Joint Fluid (NAAT + Culture + AST)Gold Standard (Crucial for AST)
Blood CultureOften Negative (<30% Positive)
Mucosal Sites (NAAT + Culture)Urethral, Cervical, Pharyngeal, Rectal — Identify Source
Skin Lesion Biopsy/NAATVesiculopustular Lesions

Treatment (DGI)

RegimenDose
Ceftriaxone1g IV/IM q24h (or q12h if Severe) × 7 Days (Switch to Oral When Improving)
Alternative (If Ceftriaxone Allergy)Gentamicin 240mg IM Stat + Azithromycin 2g PO Stat (Or Azithro 1g Weekly × 2)
PlusDoxycycline 100mg BD × 7d (Co-infection CT Cover)
Joint ManagementAspiration (Repeated if Needed), Immobilisation, Physiotherapy

2️⃣ Antimicrobial Resistance (AMR) — The Global Crisis

Resistance Mechanisms & Surveillance

AntimicrobialResistance MechanismGlobal Status (WHO GASP 2023)
PenicillinpenA (PBP2 Mutation), TEM-1 Plasmid (Penicillinase)High Resistance Global (Historically 1st Drug)
TetracyclineTetM Plasmid (Ribosomal Protection), MutationsHigh Resistance
Ciprofloxacin (Fluoroquinolones)gyrA/parC Mutations (DNA Gyrase/Topoisomerase IV)High Resistance >50% Global; Do Not Use Empirically
Azithromycin (Macrolides)23S rRNA Mutations, Efflux Pumps (MtrCDE), ermRising Resistance (MIC Creep); Not Monotherapy
Cefixime (Oral Cephalosporins)penA Mutations, Mosaic penADeclining Susceptibility; Not Preferred for Pharyngeal
Ceftriaxone (Injectable Cephalosporins)penA Mutations (Mosaic penA XXXIV), porB, mtrREmerging Resistance (MIC Creep, Ceftriaxone-R Strains Reported)

WHO GASP (Gonococcal Antimicrobial Surveillance Programme)

ComponentAction
Sentinel SurveillanceSentinel Sites, Representative Sampling (Geographic, Population)
AMR TestingCulture + AST (Ceftriaxone, Ciprofloxacin, Azithromycin, Cefixime, Spectinomycin)
Molecular SurveillancepenA (Ceftriaxone), gyrA/parC (Ciprofloxacin), 23S rRNA (Azithromycin), mtrR (Efflux)
Data SharingWHO GASP Database, National AMR Surveillance, AMR Action Plans

Ceftriaxone Resistance — Critical Threat

FeatureDetail
MechanismpenA Mosaic Mutations (XXXIV, XIX, etc.), porB, mtrR Efflux, pilQ
Resistance LevelMIC >0.125 mg/L (EUCAST/CLSI Breakpoints)
Geographic SpreadJapan, China, Europe, Australia, USA — Global Spread
ResponseDual Therapy, Higher Dose Ceftriaxone, Alternative Regimens, Spectinomycin (If Available)

2️⃣ Treatment — Evidence-Based Regimens

Uncomplicated Genital Gonorrhoea

Population1st Line (Dual Therapy)Alternative (If Ceftriaxone Allergy/Unavailable)
Adults/Adolescents (>45kg)Ceftriaxone 500mg IM Stat + Azithromycin 1g PO StatGentamicin 240mg IM Stat + Azithromycin 2g PO Stat
Weight <45kgCeftriaxone 250mg IM Stat + Azithromycin 1g PO StatGentamicin 120mg IM Stat + Azithromycin 2g PO Stat
PregnancyCeftriaxone 500mg IM Stat + Azithromycin 1g PO Stat (Safe)Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Azithro 1g (Only Option)
Ceftriaxone AllergyGentamicin 240mg IM Stat + Azithromycin 2g PO StatCefixime 400mg PO + Azithro 1g (Less Effective Pharyngeal)
Key: Dual Therapy Mandatory (Ceftriaxone + Azithromycin) — Suppresses Resistance, Treats Co-Infection (CT)

Pharyngeal Gonorrhoea

Population1st LineNotes
AllCeftriaxone 1g IM Stat + Azithromycin 1g PO StatHigher Dose Ceftriaxone (1g); Test of Cure Mandatory at 2 Weeks
Ceftriaxone AllergyGentamicin 240mg IM Stat + Azithromycin 2g PO StatPreferred Alternative
Key: Pharyngeal GC Harder to Treat — Higher Ceftriaxone Dose (1g), Test of Cure Mandatory at 2 Weeks

Rectal Gonorrhoea

Population1st LineNotes
AllCeftriaxone 500mg IM Stat + Azithromycin 1g PO StatStandard Dose; Co-infection Cover (CT) with Azithro/Doxy

Gonorrhoea in Pregnancy

RegimenSafety
Ceftriaxone 500mg IM Stat + Azithromycin 1g PO StatSafe in All Trimesters (Ceftriaxone B, Azithro B)
AlternativeCeftriaxone 500mg IM + Azithro 1g (Only Evidence-Based Option)
Contraindicated in Pregnancy: Doxycycline, Ciprofloxacin, Ofloxacin, Gentamicin (Ototoxicity/Nephrotoxicity Risk)

3️⃣ Disseminated Gonococcal Infection (DGI) — Treatment

PhaseRegimen
Initial (Parenteral)Ceftriaxone 1g IV/IM q24h (or q12h if Severe) × 7 Days
Step-Down (Oral)Cefixime 400mg BD PO (When Clinical Improvement, Total 7 Days)
Ceftriaxone AllergyGentamicin 240mg IM Stat + Azithromycin 2g PO Stat (Then Azithro 1g Weekly × 2)
Co-Infection CoverDoxycycline 100mg BD × 7d (Added for CT, MG)
Joint ManagementRepeated Aspiration, Immobilisation, Physiotherapy

3️⃣ Neonatal Gonorrhoea

Ophthalmia Neonatorum

FeatureDetail
OnsetDay 2-5 Post-Partum (Incubation 2-5 Days) — Earlier than Chlamydia
ClinicalHyperacute Conjunctivitis: Copious Purulent Discharge, Marked Eyelid Edema, Chemosis, Periorbital Cellulitis
ComplicationsCorneal Ulceration, Perforation, Blindness (Rapid Onset, Hours)
DiagnosisGram Stain (Gram-Negative Intracellular Diplococci — Presumptive), NAAT, Culture + AST
TreatmentCeftriaxone 50mg/kg (Max 125mg) IM/IV Single Dose + Saline Irrigation
ProphylaxisErythromycin/Tetracycline Ointment at Birth (Prevents GC/CT)

Neonatal Scalp Abscess

FeatureDetail
CauseFetal Scalp Electrode or Forceps/Vacuum Trauma → Gonococcal Inoculation
ClinicalAbscess (Fluctuant, Purulent), Cellulitis
TreatmentIncision & Drainage + Ceftriaxone 50mg/kg IM/IV × 7 Days

Neonatal DGI

FeatureDetail
OnsetDay 7-14 Post-Partum
ClinicalSepsis, Arthritis (Septic), Meningitis, Dermatitis
TreatmentCeftriaxone 50mg/kg IV/IM q24h × 7-10 Days + Supportive

4️⃣ Diagnosis — Algorithms & Interpretation

Diagnostic Algorithm

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Suspected Gonorrhoea
↓
Symptomatic?
↓
Microscopy (Gram Stain) Rapid, Bedside
& F & G
↓
Gram Stain: Intracellular Gram-Neg Diplococci?
Treat + Partner Notify Test of Cure (GC: 2 Weeks)
↓
Presumptive GC → Treat Empirically
NAAT + Culture + AST Send Both
Culture + AST Results Guide Resistance Management

Diagnostic Test Comparison

TestSensitivitySpecificityTATSampleBest Use
Gram Stain (Urethral M)95% (Symptomatic Male)>99%MinutesUrethral SmearPresumptive Dx (Male Urethral)
Gram Stain (Female/Extra)Poor (<50%)HighMinutesSwabNot Reliable (Female Cervical, Pharyngeal, Rectal)
NAAT (PCR/TMA/SDA)>95%>98%1-24hUrine, Swab1st Line (Screening, All Sites)
Culture + AST90-95%100%48-72hSwabGold Standard (Viability, AST Mandatory)
POC NAAT90-95%97-99%20-90minSwab/UrineRapid/Outreach
Microscopy (Joint Fluid)VariableHighMinutesJoint AspirateDGI Presumptive

Specimen Collection

SiteSpecimenTransport
Male UrethralFirst-Catch Urine (Hold ≥1h) OR Urethral SwabNAAT Medium / Amies/Modified Thayer-Martin (Culture)
Female CervicalEndocervical Swab (Remove Mucus)NAAT Medium / Culture Medium
RectalRectal Swab (2-3cm)NAAT Medium / Culture Medium
PharyngealPharyngeal Swab (Posterior Oropharynx)NAAT Medium / Culture Medium
ConjunctivalConjunctival SwabNAAT Medium / Culture Medium
DGI (Joint)Joint Fluid Aspirate (Sterile)NAAT Medium / Culture Medium (Blood Culture Bottles)
DGI (Blood)Blood Culture Bottles (Aerobic/Anaerobic)Blood Culture System
Key: NAAT = 1st Line for Screening/ALL Sites; Culture + AST = Mandatory for Resistance Surveillance, Treatment Failure, DGI

4️⃣ Test of Cure (TOC) & Partner Notification

Test of Cure

InfectionTimingTestNotes
Genital GC2 Weeks Post-TreatmentNAAT (or Culture)Mandatory for Pharyngeal GC
Pharyngeal GC2 Weeks Post-TreatmentNAAT (or Culture + AST)Mandatory (Higher Failure Rate)
Rectal GC2 Weeks Post-TreatmentNAATIf High Risk / Treatment Failure
DGI2-4 Weeks Post-TreatmentNAAT + Culture (Joint/Blood)Clinical + Microbiological Cure
NeonatalEnd of TreatmentNAAT/CultureOphthalmia (Day 1), DGI (End of Rx)
Key: GC TOC at 2 Weeks (vs CT 4 Weeks); Pharyngeal TOC Mandatory; Avoid <1 Week (False +ve)

Partner Notification & EPT (Expedited Partner Therapy)

StrategyDescription
Patient ReferralIndex Patient Informs Partners (Preferred — Empowers Patient)
Provider ReferralHealth Staff Notifies Partners (Confidential)
Contract ReferralTime-Limited Agreement (Patient Notifies or Provider Does)
EPT (Expedited Partner Therapy)Legal Where Permitted: Index Patient Delivers Medication — GC: Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Azithro 1g (Requires Injection)
GC EPT Complexity: Ceftriaxone IM Requires Injection — Provider Referral Preferred for GC; CT/TV EPT Easier (Oral).

5️⃣ Screening & Public Health

Screening Recommendations (UK/NICE/CDC/WHO)

PopulationRecommendation
Sexually Active Women <25yAnnual GC/CT Screen (Opportunistic, Urine/Swab)
Pregnant WomenBooking: GC/CT (If <25y or Risk Factors)
MSM3-Site Testing (Urethral, Pharyngeal, Rectal) Annual (3-6m if High Risk)
Pregnant Women <25yRoutine GC/CT Screen at Booking
MSM3-Site NAAT (Urethral, Pharyngeal, Rectal) Annual (3-6m if High Risk)
HIV PositiveAnnual CT/GC/Syphilis (3-Site if MSM)
Sex WorkersQuarterly HIV, Syphilis, GC/CT; Bi-annual HBV/HCV
Termination of PregnancyRoutine GC/CT Screen

5️⃣ AMR Management — Clinical Decision Pathway

⬇ Clinical Pathway
GC Diagnosed
↓
Treat: Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Azithro 1g PO
↓
Send Culture + AST Mandatory for Resistance Surveillance
↓
AST Results
↓
Continue Standard Therapy TOC at 2w
Escalate/Alternative Expert Advice Required
Ceftriaxone 1g IM (Higher Dose) Drop Azithro if Resistant
Consider Ciprofloxacin If Ceftriaxone Allergy/Unavailable
↓
Expert Consultation Spectinomycin (If Available) Gentamicin + Azithro Higher Dose Ceftriaxone 1g IM

⚡ FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Epidemiology82M New Cases/Year, Peak 15-24y, Asymptomatic 50-80% (F), 10-20% (M), >90% Pharyngeal/Rectal
ClinicalUrethritis (M: Purulent Discharge), Cervicitis/PID (F), Pharyngeal/Rectal (Asymptomatic), DGI (Arthritis-Dermatitis, Septic Arthritis), Neonatal (Ophthalmia Day 2-5)
DiagnosisMale Urethral: Gram Stain (Intracellular Gram-Neg Diplococci) = Presumptive; All Sites: NAAT 1st Line; Culture + AST Mandatory for Resistance Surveillance, Treatment Failure, DGI
TreatmentCeftriaxone 500mg IM + Azithromycin 1g PO (Dual Therapy); Pharyngeal: Ceftriaxone 1g IM; Pregnancy: Same Dual Therapy
AMRCiprofloxacin Resistance >50% Global — Do Not Use; Azithromycin MIC Creep; Ceftriaxone Resistance Emerging (MIC >0.125) — Dual Therapy, Higher Dose (1g IM)
DGIArthritis-Dermatitis Syndrome (Migratory Polyarthralgia, Tenosynovitis, Pustular Rash) + Septic Arthritis; Ceftriaxone 1g IV/IM Daily × 7d
NeonatalOphthalmia (Day 2-5, Hyperacute) → Ceftriaxone 50mg/kg IM/IV Single Dose; Scalp Abscess → I&D + Ceftriaxone
Test of Cure2 Weeks (NAAT) — Mandatory for Pharyngeal; DGI/Neonatal: End of Rx
Partner NotificationProvider Referral Preferred for GC (IM Injection); EPT Complex for GC
ScreeningMSM 3-Site Annual, Women <25y Annual, Pregnancy <25y, HIV+ Annual
AMRCiprofloxacin R >50% (Do Not Use); Ceftriaxone Resistance Emerging — Dual Therapy, Higher Dose, Alternatives (Gentamicin+Azithro)

🎤 Viva Questions (Expected Answers)

#QuestionExpected Answer
1Gonorrhoea — current first-line treatment?Ceftriaxone 500mg IM Stat + Azithromycin 1g PO Stat (Dual Therapy); Pharyngeal: Ceftriaxone 1g IM; Pregnancy: Same Dual Therapy (Safe)
2Why dual therapy for gonorrhoea?Suppresses Resistance (Ceftriaxone + Azithromycin Target Different Mechanisms), Treats Co-Infection (Chlamydia), Higher Cure Rate
3Gonorrhoea AMR — ciprofloxacin resistance?>50% Global Resistance — Do Not Use Empirically; Mechanisms: gyrA/parC Mutations
4Pharyngeal gonorrhoea — treatment & TOC?Ceftriaxone 1g IM + Azithromycin 1g PO (Higher Ceftriaxone Dose); Test of Cure Mandatory at 2 Weeks (NAAT)
5. Disseminated Gonococcal Infection (DGI) — presentation & management?Arthritis-Dermatitis Syndrome (Migratory Polyarthralgia, Tenosynovitis, Pustular Rash) or Septic Arthritis (Monoarticular); Ceftriaxone 1g IV/IM Daily × 7 Days; Joint Aspiration, Physiotherapy
6. Neonatal ophthalmia — gonorrhoea vs chlamydia onset?GC: Day 2-5 (Hyperacute, Copious Purulent); CT: Day 5-14 (Mucopurulent); GC Treatment: Ceftriaxone 50mg/kg IM/IV Single Dose
7. Gonorrhoea antimicrobial resistance — current threat?Ceftriaxone Resistance Emerging (MIC >0.125 mg/L); penA Mosaic Mutations; Dual Therapy, Higher Dose Ceftriaxone 1g IM, Alternatives (Gentamicin+Azithro)
8. Test of Cure for GC — timing & method?2 Weeks (NAAT) — Mandatory for Pharyngeal; Avoid <1 Week (False Positive); DGI/Neonatal: End of Treatment
9. Gonorrhoea in pregnancy — treatment?Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Azithromycin 1g PO (Safe in All Trimesters); Doxycycline/Ciprofloxacin/Gentamicin Contraindicated
10. Partner notification for gonorrhoea — EPT complexity?EPT Complex for GC (Ceftriaxone IM Requires Injection); Provider Referral Preferred; CT/TV EPT Easier (Oral)

🧩 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
"Gonorrhoea always symptomatic"NO. 50-80% Women Asymptomatic, >90% Pharyngeal/Rectal Asymptomatic — Screening Essential
"Ciprofloxacin still works for GC"NO. >50% Global Resistance — Do Not Use; Only If AST Confirms Susceptibility (Rare)
"Azithromycin 1g = Monotherapy for GC"NO. Dual Therapy Mandatory (Ceftriaxone + Azithromycin) — Resistance Suppression
"Pharyngeal GC = Same Treatment as Genital"NO. Higher Ceftriaxone Dose (1g IM), Test of Cure Mandatory at 2 Weeks
"GC TOC = 4 Weeks like CT"NO. GC TOC = 2 Weeks; CT TOC = 4 Weeks
"Gram Stain = Gold Standard for GC"NO. Culture + AST = Gold Standard (Viability, AST); Gram Stain = Presumptive (Male Urethral Only)
"GC EPT = Easy Like CT"NO. GC EPT Complex (Ceftriaxone IM Injection Required); Provider Referral Preferred
"Ceftriaxone Resistance = Not a Problem"NO. Emerging Global Threat; penA Mosaic Mutations, MIC Creep — Dual Therapy, Higher Dose, Alternatives
"DGI = Only Arthritis"NO. Arthritis-Dermatitis Syndrome (Migratory Polyarthralgia, Tenosynovitis, Pustular Rash) OR Septic Arthritis
"Neonatal GC Ophthalmia = Same Onset as CT"NO. GC: Day 2-5 (Hyperacute, Copious Purulent); CT: Day 5-14 (Mucopurulent, Milder)
Mnemonic: GONORRHOEA AMR MASTER
Gonorrhoea: 82M/Year, Peak 15-24y, Asymptomatic 50-80% F
Obligate Human: Gram-Negative Diplococcus, Intracellular, Pili, Opa, LOS
NAAT 1st Line: All Sites, Urine/Swab, >95% Sens/Spec
Ocular: Hyperacute Conjunctivitis (Day 2-5) — Ceftriaxone 50mg/kg IM
Resistance: Cipro >50% (No Use), Azi MIC Creep, Ceftriaxone Emerging (MIC >0.125)
Regimen: Dual Therapy — Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Azi 1g PO
Higher Dose: Pharyngeal = Ceftriaxone 1g IM (Not 500mg)
Ophthalmia Neonatorum: Day 2-5, Hyperacute — Ceftriaxone 50mg/kg IM
Epidemiology: 82M/Year, 50-80% F Asympt, >90% Pharyngeal/Rectal Asympt
Antimicrobial: Ceftriaxone Resistance Emerging (penA Mosaic), Dual Therapy, Higher Dose, Alternatives
Arthritis-Dermatitis: DGI (Migratory Polyarthralgia, Tenosynovitis, Pustular Rash)
Septic Arthritis: Monoarticular (Knee > Wrist > Ankle)
Epidemiology: 82M/Year, Peak 15-24y, Co-infection CT 10-40%
AMR Surveillance: WHO GASP (Culture+AST, penA/gyrA/23S/mtrR)
Screening: MSM 3-Site Annual, Women <25y Annual, Pregnancy <25y
Test of Cure: 2 Weeks (NAAT) — Mandatory Pharyngeal
Options (Resistance): Gentamicin 240mg IM + Azi 2g, Spectinomycin, Higher Ceftriaxone 1g
Treatment Dual: Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Azi 1g PO (Dual Mandatory)
In pregnancy: Same Dual Therapy (Safe), No Doxy/Cipro/Genta
Options Ceftriaxone Allergy: Gentamicin 240mg IM + Azi 2g PO
Neonatal: Ophthalmia (Day 2-5) → Ceftriaxone 50mg/kg IM; Scalp Abscess → I&D + Ceftriaxone
Resistance: Cipro No, Azi Creep, Ceftriaxone Emerging

💬 Exam Answer Modes

FormatPromptKey Points
Long Essay"Describe the current management of gonorrhoea including antimicrobial resistance."Dual Therapy (Ceftriaxone + Azithromycin), Pharyngeal (1g Ceftriaxone), Pregnancy (Safe Dual), AMR (Ciprofloxacin R, Ceftriaxone Emerging, penA, Alternatives: Gentamicin+Azithro, Spectinomycin), DGI (Ceftriaxone 1g IV), Neonatal (Ceftriaxone 50mg/kg), TOC (2w), Partner Notification (Provider Referral)
Short Note"Disseminated Gonococcal Infection — clinical features and treatment."Arthritis-Dermatitis Syndrome (Migratory Polyarthralgia, Tenosynovitis, Pustular Rash) + Septic Arthritis (Monoarticular); Ceftriaxone 1g IV/IM Daily × 7d; Joint Aspiration, Physiotherapy
Viva"25-year-old man with 3 days of dysuria and purulent urethral discharge. Gram stain shows intracellular Gram-negative diplococci. How will you treat?"Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Azithromycin 1g PO (Dual Therapy); NAAT + Culture/AST; Partner Notification (Provider Referral); TOC at 2 Weeks (NAAT); HIV/Syphilis Screen
Ward Round"MSM with pharyngeal gonorrhoea on NAAT. Treatment and follow-up?"Ceftriaxone 1g IM + Azithromycin 1g PO (Higher Ceftriaxone Dose); Test of Cure Mandatory at 2 Weeks (NAAT); 3-Site Testing (Urethral, Rectal); HIV/Syphilis Screen; Condom Counselling
Last-Night"GC: 82M/yr, Dual Therapy (Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Azi 1g PO), Pharyngeal 1g Ceftriaxone + TOC 2w mandatory. AMR: Cipro >50% No Use, Azi Creep, Ceftriaxone Emerging (penA). DGI: Arthritis-Dermatitis + Septic Arthritis, Ceftriaxone 1g IV x7d. Neonatal: Ophthalmia Day 2-5, Ceftriaxone 50mg/kg IM. TOC: 2w NAAT. Screen: MSM 3-Site Annual. TOC: GC 2w, CT 4w."Compressed.

📌 Summary

  • Epidemiology: 82M/Year, Peak 15-24y, Asymptomatic 50-80% (F), >90% Pharyngeal/Rectal
  • Clinical: Urethritis (M), Cervicitis/PID (F), Pharyngeal/Rectal (Asymptomatic), DGI (Arthritis-Dermatitis, Septic Arthritis), Neonatal Ophthalmia (Day 2-5)
  • Diagnosis: Male Urethral: Gram Stain (Intracellular Gram-Neg Diplococci) = Presumptive; All Sites: NAAT 1st Line; Culture+AST Mandatory for Resistance Surveillance
  • Treatment: Dual Therapy — Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Azithromycin 1g PO; Pharyngeal: Ceftriaxone 1g IM; Pregnancy: Same Dual Therapy
  • AMR: Ciprofloxacin Resistance >50% — Do Not Use; Ceftriaxone Resistance Emerging (MIC >0.125) — Dual Therapy, Higher Dose (1g IM), Alternatives (Gentamicin+Azithro, Spectinomycin)
  • DGI: Arthritis-Dermatitis Syndrome + Septic Arthritis — Ceftriaxone 1g IV/IM Daily × 7 Days
  • Neonatal: Ophthalmia (Day 2-5) — Ceftriaxone 50mg/kg IM/IV Single Dose; Scalp Abscess → I&D + Ceftriaxone
  • Test of Cure: 2 Weeks (NAAT) — Mandatory for Pharyngeal; DGI/Neonatal: End of Rx
  • Screening: MSM 3-Site Annual, Women <25y Annual, Pregnancy <25y, HIV+ Annual
  • Partner Notification: Provider Referral Preferred for GC (IM Injection); EPT Complex for GC

❓ MCQs (10)

  1. Current first-line treatment for uncomplicated genital gonorrhoea?

A. Ciprofloxacin 500mg BD × 7d B. Ceftriaxone 500mg IM Stat + Azithromycin 1g PO Stat C. Azithromycin 1g Stat D. Ceftriaxone 500mg IM Stat

Answer: B. Dual Therapy Mandatory (Ceftriaxone + Azithromycin).

  1. Pharyngeal gonorrhoea treatment differs from genital by:

A. Longer Duration B. Higher Ceftriaxone Dose (1g IM vs 500mg IM) C. Different Antibiotic D. Oral Ceftriaxone

Answer: B. Pharyngeal: Ceftriaxone 1g IM (Higher Dose) + Mandatory TOC at 2 Weeks.

  1. Ciprofloxacin for gonorrhoea — current recommendation?

A. 1st Line B. Do Not Use — >50% Global Resistance C. Alternative if Allergic D. Use with Azithromycin

Answer: B. >50% Global Resistance — Do Not Use Empirically; Only If AST Confirms Susceptibility.

  1. Disseminated Gonococcal Infection — classic clinical presentation?

A. Monoarticular Septic Arthritis Only B. Arthritis-Dermatitis Syndrome (Migratory Polyarthralgia, Tenosynovitis, Pustular Rash) + Septic Arthritis C. Only Pustular Rash D. Only Fever and Arthralgia

Answer: B. Arthritis-Dermatitis Syndrome + Septic Arthritis (Two Major Syndromes).

  1. Neonatal gonococcal ophthalmia — typical onset vs chlamydia?

A. Same Onset B. GC: Day 2-5 (Hyperacute); CT: Day 5-14 (Mucopurulent) C. GC Later D. CT Earlier

Answer: B. GC: Day 2-5 (Hyperacute, Copious Purulent); CT: Day 5-14 (Mucopurulent, Milder).

  1. Gonorrhoea test of cure — timing for pharyngeal infection?

A. 1 Week B. 2 Weeks (Mandatory) C. 4 Weeks D. 8 Weeks

Answer: B. 2 Weeks (Mandatory for Pharyngeal GC) — Higher Failure Rate.

  1. Gonorrhoea in pregnancy — safe treatment?

A. Ciprofloxacin 500mg BD B. Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Azithromycin 1g PO C. Doxycycline 100mg BD D. Gentamicin IM

Answer: B. Dual Therapy Safe in Pregnancy; Doxy/Cipro/Genta Contraindicated.

  1. Ceftriaxone resistance in gonorrhoea — current status?

A. Non-Existent B. Emerging (MIC >0.125 mg/L), penA Mosaic Mutations C. Widespread >50% D. Only in Japan

Answer: B. Emerging Globally (MIC >0.125 mg/L); penA Mosaic Mutations; Dual Therapy, Higher Dose, Alternatives.

  1. Gonorrhoea test of cure — minimum time after treatment?

A. 3 Days B. 2 Weeks C. 4 Weeks D. 8 Weeks

Answer: B. 2 Weeks (GC TOC); CT = 4 Weeks.

  1. Partner notification for gonorrhoea — EPT complexity?

A. Simple Oral Meds B. Complex — Ceftriaxone IM Injection Required C. Not Needed D. Provider Referral Illegal

Answer: B. GC EPT Complex (Ceftriaxone IM Injection); Provider Referral Preferred.

📋 SBAs (10)

  1. 25M, purulent urethral discharge, Gram stain: intracellular Gram-negative diplococci. Treatment?

A. Azithromycin 1g B. Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Azithromycin 1g PO C. Ciprofloxacin 500mg BD D. Ceftriaxone 500mg IM Alone

Answer: B. Dual Therapy (Ceftriaxone + Azithromycin).

  1. MSM with pharyngeal gonorrhoea on NAAT. Correct management?

A. Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + TOC 4w B. Ceftriaxone 1g IM + Azithro 1g + TOC 2w Mandatory C. Oral Cefixime 400mg D. Azithromycin 1g Alone

Answer: B. Pharyngeal: Higher Ceftriaxone Dose (1g IM) + Mandatory TOC at 2 Weeks.

  1. MSM with migratory polyarthralgia, tenosynovitis, pustular rash on hands/feet. Blood culture negative. Diagnosis?

A. Rheumatic Fever B. DGI (Arthritis-Dermatitis Syndrome) C. SLE D. Reactive Arthritis

Answer: B. DGI Arthritis-Dermatitis Syndrome (Migratory Polyarthralgia, Tenosynovitis, Pustular Rash).

  1. Neonate with hyperacute conjunctivitis at Day 3. Gram stain: Gram-neg diplococci. Treatment?

A. Topical Erythromycin Only B. Ceftriaxone 50mg/kg IM/IV Single Dose C. Oral Azithromycin D. IV Penicillin

Answer: B. Ceftriaxone 50mg/kg IM/IV Single Dose + Saline Irrigation.

  1. GC TOC — minimum interval after treatment completion?

A. 3 Days B. 2 Weeks C. 4 Weeks D. 6 Weeks

Answer: B. 2 Weeks (GC TOC); CT = 4 Weeks.

🔑 Answer Keys

MCQsSBAs
1-B, 2-B, 3-B, 4-B, 5-B, 6-B, 7-B, 8-B, 9-B, 10-B1-B, 2-B, 3-B, 4-B, 5-B
