▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 15.2.03 2 3 Syphilis

Parent Topic: STI MOC → STI Hierarchy

Status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

Priority: ⭐⭐⭐ HIGHEST (FCPS/MRCP — Complex Staging, Serology, Neurosyphilis, Congenital, Penicillin Allergy)

Source: Davidson 24th Ed Ch 13; WHO/BASHH/CDC Guidelines; FCPS/MRCP Syllabus; NICE NG177; WHO CST

🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Master syphilis staging (Primary, Secondary, Early/Late Latent, Tertiary, Neurosyphilis, Congenital)
  • [ ] Interpret syphilis serology (Non-Treponemal: RPR/VDRL; Treponemal: TPPA/FTA-ABS) and apply Reverse vs Traditional Algorithm
  • [ ] Apply stage-specific penicillin treatment (Dose, Route, Duration) and manage Penicillin Allergy (Desensitisation)
  • [ ] Diagnose and manage Neurosyphilis (CSF VDRL, CSF PCR, High-Dose Penicillin G IV)
  • [ ] Diagnose and manage Congenital Syphilis (Maternal Treatment, Neonatal Evaluation, Penicillin G IV)
  • [ ] Manage Jarisch-Herxheimer Reaction and Treatment Failure/Re-infection
  • [ ] Apply Syphilis in Pregnancy protocols (Screening, Treatment, Congenital Prevention)
  • [ ] Answer viva: "Syphilis staging & treatment" and "Serology interpretation" and "Neurosyphilis vs Meningovascular" and "Congenital syphilis prevention"

🧠 Core Concept: Syphilis Natural History & Staging

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Treponema pallidum Infection
↓
Primary Syphilis Chancre (Painless, Indurated, Clean Base) Regional Lymphadenopathy (Non-Tender) 3w-90d Post-Exposure
Congenital Syphilis Transplacental Transmission Early (<2y) / Late (>2y) Manifestations
↓
Secondary Syphilis Systemic Dissemination Rash (Palms/Soles, Maculopapular) Condylomata Lata, Mucous Patches Generalised Lymphadenopathy Constitutional Symptoms 4-10w Post-Chancre
↓
Latent Syphilis
↓
& F
Late Latent Low Infectivity Asymptomatic, Seropositive
↓
Tertiary Syphilis (Gummatous, Cardiovascular, Neurosyphilis)
↓
Gummatous: Granulomatous Lesions (Skin, Bone, Liver)
Cardiovascular: Aortitis, Aortic Regurgitation, Aneurysm
Neurosyphilis: Meningovascular, General Paresis, Tabes Dorsalis

1️⃣ Syphilis Staging — Clinical Features

Primary Syphilis

FeatureDetail
Incubation10-90 Days (Median 21 Days)
ChancrePainless, Indurated, Clean Base, Well-Defined Margins, Single (Multiple in HIV), Heals Spontaneously in 3-6 Weeks
LymphadenopathyRegional, Non-Tender, Rubbery, Non-Suppurative
SerologyRPR/VDRL Negative Early (Seroconversion 1-4 Weeks Post-Chancre); TPPA/FTA-ABS Positive Earlier
Duration3-6 Weeks Untreated → Heals Spontaneously

Secondary Syphilis

FeatureDetail
Timing4-10 Weeks Post-Chancre (Overlap Possible)
RashMaculopapular, Palms & Soles (Pathognomonic), Trunk, Extremities, Face, Non-Pruritic, Copper-Coloured
Mucous MembranesCondylomata Lata (Moist, Flat, Infectious Papules — Perineum/Anal), Mucous Patches (Oral/Genital), Snail-Track Ulcers
SystemicGeneralised Lymphadenopathy (Rubbery, Non-Tender), Fever, Malaise, Headache, Pharyngitis, Hepatosplenomegaly, Iritis, Nephritis
OtherAlopecia (Moth-Eaten), Periostitis, Glomerulonephritis
Duration2-6 Weeks (Waxing/Waning), Resolution → Latent

Latent Syphilis

StageDefinitionSerologyInfectivityDuration
Early Latent<1 Year Post-Infection (or <2 Years CDC)RPR/VDRL High Titre (≥1:16), TPPA+Infectious (Early Relapse to Secondary)Variable
Late Latent>1 Year Post-Infection (or >2 Years CDC)RPR/VDRL Low/Declining, TPPA+Non-Infectious (Sexual)Years-Decades
Key Distinction: Early Latent = <1 Year (or <2 Years CDC); Late Latent = >1 Year (Duration Unknown → Assume Late Latent)

Tertiary Syphilis (Late Manifestations)

TypeFeaturesTiming
GummatousGummas (Granulomatous, Necrotic Lesions) — Skin (Ulcers), Bone (Osteitis), Liver (Hepatitis), Testes, Brain, Heart; Slowly Progressive, Scarring3-30 Years
CardiovascularAortitis (Ascending Aorta), Aortic Regurgitation, Aortic Aneurysm (Saccular, Ascending), Coronary Ostial Stenosis → Angina/MI10-30 Years
NeurosyphilisSee BelowAny Time (Can Occur Early)

Neurosyphilis (Neurological Syphilis)

SyndromeFeaturesCSF Findings
AsymptomaticNormal Neurological ExamCSF Abnormalities (↑ WBC, ↑ Protein, +VDRL)
MeningealHeadache, Nuchal Rigidity, Cranial Nerve Palsies (III, VI, VII), Photophobia↑ Lymphocytes, ↑ Protein, CSF-VDRL +ve
MeningovascularStroke (Ischaemic) in Young, Cranial Nerve Palsies, Seizures, Hemiparesis, Aphasia↑ WBC, ↑ Protein, CSF-VDRL +ve
General Paresis (Dementia Paralytica)Personality Change, Cognitive Decline, Memory Loss, Delusions, Apathy, Argyll Robertson Pupils (Accommodate, Not React to Light)↑ WBC, ↑ Protein, CSF-VDRL +ve
Tabes DorsalisLightning Pains (Lancinating Leg Pains), Sensory Ataxia, Loss of Proprioception/Vibration, Argyll Robertson Pupils, Absent Reflexes, Charcot Joints↑ WBC, ↑ Protein, CSF-VDRL +ve
Ocular SyphilisUveitis, Optic Neuritis, Retinitis, Interstitial Keratitis↑ WBC, ↑ Protein, CSF-VDRL +ve
Otic SyphilisSensorineural Hearing Loss, Tinnitus, Vertigo↑ WBC, ↑ Protein, CSF-VDRL +ve
Key: Neurosyphilis Can Occur at ANY STAGE (Even Primary/Secondary); CSF Examination Mandatory if Neurological Signs, HIV+, Treatment Failure, Late Latent/Tertiary

1️⃣ Congenital Syphilis

Transmission & Risk

FactorRisk
TransplacentalAny Stage of Pregnancy (Highest 2nd/3rd Trimester)
Maternal StagePrimary/Secondary: 70-100%; Early Latent: 40%, Late Latent: 10%; Treated: <1-2% if Adequate Treatment ≥30 Days Before Delivery
TimingNo Barrier After 16-18 Weeks Gestation; Treponemes Cross Placenta Freely

Clinical Classification

CategoryFeatures
Early Congenital (<2 Years)Snuffles (Bloody, Mucopurulent Rhinitis), Rash (Maculopapular, Palms/Soles, Vesiculobullous), Hepatosplenomegaly, Jaundice, Pneumonitis, Osteochondritis (Symmetric Long Bone Lesions), Pseudoparalysis (Parrot's — Painful Limb Movement), Lymphadenopathy, Nephrotic Syndrome, Thrombocytopenia
Late Congenital (>2 Years)Hutchinson's Triad: Hutchinson's Teeth (Notched, Peg-Shaped Incisors), Interstitial Keratitis, 8th Nerve Deafness; Other: Saddle Nose, Saber Shins, Clutton's Joints (Knee Effusions), Mulberry Molars, Rhagades

Diagnosis — Neonatal Evaluation

InvestigationIndicationInterpretation
Maternal SerologyAll Pregnant WomenRPR/VDRL + TPPA (Stage Maternal Infection)
Neonatal RPR/VDRLCompared to MaternalNeonatal Titre ≥4x Maternal → Probable/Congenital; Same/Declining → Monitor
Treponemal Test (TPPA/FTA-ABS)ConfirmatoryPositive = Exposed (Maternal IgG Transfer); IgM FTA-ABS / PCR / Dark Field = Active Infection
CSF ExaminationAll Probable/Confirmed CasesVDRL, WBC, Protein — Neurosyphilis if Abnormal
Long Bone X-RaysProbable/ConfirmedOsteochondritis (Metaphyseal Lucencies, Periostitis)
CBC, LFT, CoagulationBaselineThrombocytopenia, Anaemia, Elevated LFTs
Dark Field / PCR (Lesion Fluid)If Lesions PresentDirect Detection of T. pallidum

2️⃣ Syphilis Serology — Interpretation Mastery

Test Types

CategoryTestsPrincipleCharacteristics
Non-Treponemal (NTT)RPR (Rapid Plasma Reagin), VDRL (Venereal Disease Research Lab)Detect Anti-Cardiolipin (Lipoidal) AntibodiesQuantitative (Titre), Sensitive for Active Disease, Correlates with Activity, Prozone Phenomenon (False -ve at High Titre), Biological False Positives (BFP) (Autoimmune, Viral, Pregnancy, Age)
Treponemal (TT)TPPA (Treponema pallidum Particle Agglutination), FTA-ABS (Fluorescent Treponemal Antibody Absorption), EIA/CIA (Enzyme/Chemiluminescence Immunoassay), Western BlotDetect Anti-T. pallidum Specific AntibodiesQualitative (Positive/Negative), Highly Specific, Lifelong Positivity (Even After Cure), Does Not Correlate with Activity

Testing Algorithms

AlgorithmSequenceAdvantage
TraditionalNTT (RPR/VDRL) → If Positive → TT (TPPA/FTA-ABS)Cheaper, Detects Active Disease First, Quantifies Activity
Reverse (CDC/WHO Recommended)TT (EIA/CIA/TPPA) → If Positive → NTT (RPR/VDRL) for TitresHigher Sensitivity (Detects Early Primary, Latent, Treated), Automated, High-Throughput

Serology Interpretation Matrix

Clinical StageNTT (RPR/VDRL)TT (TPPA/FTA-ABS)Notes
PrimaryNegative → Positive (Seroconversion 1-4w)Positive EarlyTT Positive Before NTT; Repeat NTT if Clinical Suspicion
SecondaryHigh Titre (1:64 to 1:1024)PositivePeak NTT Activity
Early LatentPositive (Moderate-High)PositiveHigh Relapse Risk
Late LatentLow/Declining Titre (<1:16) or NegativePositiveNTT May Be Negative
Tertiary (Gumma/CV)Often NegativePositiveNTT Insensitive in Late Disease
NeurosyphilisCSF-VDRL +ve (Specific)CSF TPPA/FTA-ABSCSF-VDRL = Diagnostic (High Specificity, Low Sensitivity)
CongenitalNeonatal Titre ≥4x MaternalNeonatal Positive (Maternal IgG Transfer = Positive)IgM FTA-ABS / PCR = Active Infection; Maternal IgG = Passive Transfer
Treated (Cured)Titre Decline ≥4-Fold (2 Dilutions)Remains Positive (Lifelong)Serofast (Persistent Low Titre) Common; NTT Negative = Cure
Re-infectionTitre Rise ≥4-Fold (2 Dilutions)Remains PositiveDistinguished from Serofast
Key Concepts:
- Seroconversion = NTT Negative → Positive
- Serofast = Persistent Low NTT Titre After Treatment (<1:4) — Not Treatment Failure
- Prozone Phenomenon = False Negative NTT at Very High Titre → Dilute Serum
- Biological False Positive (BFP) = Positive NTT, Negative TT — Autoimmune, Viral, Pregnancy, Age

Treatment Response & Follow-Up

ParameterExpected Response
NTT Titre (RPR/VDRL)≥4-Fold Decline (2 Dilutions) by 6 Months (1°/2°), 12-24 Months (Latent/Tertiary)
SerofastPersistent Low Titre (<1:4) After Adequate Treatment — Not Failure
Treatment FailureTitre Rise ≥4-Fold (Re-infection/Failure) or No Decline by 6-12 Months
Re-infectionTitre Rise ≥4-Fold (After Prior Decline) — Treat Again

CSF Analysis for Neurosyphilis

ParameterNormalNeurosyphilis
WBC (Lymphocytes)<5/μL>5-20/μL (Mild-Moderate)
Protein<45 mg/dL>45 mg/dL
CSF-VDRLNegativePositive (Specific, Low Sensitivity ~50-70%)
CSF FTA-ABS/TPPANegativePositive (High Sensitivity, Low Specificity)
GlucoseNormalNormal or Low
Diagnosis of Neurosyphilis: Reactive CSF-VDRL + Clinical/CSF Abnormalities (No Single Test Perfect)

2️⃣ Treatment — Stage-Specific Penicillin Regimens

Penicillin G — The Only Proven Curative Agent

StageRegimenDose & RouteDurationAlternative (Penicillin Allergy)
Primary / Secondary / Early LatentBenzathine Penicillin G2.4 Million Units IM Single DoseSingle DoseDesensitise → Penicillin (Preferred); Doxycycline 100mg BD × 14d (Non-Pregnant); Ceftriaxone 1g IM/IV Daily × 10-14d (Limited Data)
Late Latent / Tertiary (Gummatous, Cardiovascular)Benzathine Penicillin G2.4 Million Units IM Weekly × 3 Doses (Total 7.2 MU)3 Weekly DosesDesensitise → Penicillin; Doxycycline 100mg BD × 28d (Non-Pregnant); Ceftriaxone 1-2g IV Daily × 14d
Neurosyphilis (All Forms)Aqueous Penicillin G IV18-24 Million Units/Day (3-4 MU q4h) OR 18-24 MU Continuous Infusion10-14 DaysDesensitise → Penicillin G IV; Ceftriaxone 2g IV Daily × 14d (Alternative)
Congenital SyphilisAqueous Penicillin G IV50,000 Units/kg/dose q12h (First 7 Days) → q8h (Day 8-10)10 Days TotalDesensitise → Penicillin (Only Proven); Procaine Penicillin 50,000 U/kg IM Daily × 10d (If IV Access Difficult)
Critical: Penicillin G is the ONLY PROVEN Curative Agent for Syphilis in Pregnancy and Neurosyphilis; No Oral Alternatives Proven for Neurosyphilis/Congenital

Penicillin Allergy Management

ApproachDetail
DesensitisationPreferred for ALL Stages (Especially Pregnancy, Neurosyphilis, Congenital); Oral/IV Protocols (Gradual Dose Escalation Over Hours); Specialist Allergist/ID Supervision
Alternative (Non-Pregnant, Non-Neurosyphilis)Doxycycline 100mg BD — Primary/Secondary/Early Latent: 14 Days; Late Latent/Tertiary: 28 Days
Alternative (Pregnancy)NO PROVEN ORAL ALTERNATIVE — Desensitisation Mandatory
Ceftriaxone1-2g IV/IM Daily × 10-14 Days (Limited Data, Not 1st Line); Not for Neurosyphilis/Congenital as 1st Choice
AzithromycinNOT RECOMMENDED (Resistance Documented, Treatment Failures)

Jarisch-Herxheimer Reaction (JHR)

FeatureDetail
MechanismMassive Treponemal Lysis → Release of Lipoproteins → Cytokine Storm (TNF-α, IL-6, IL-8)
Incidence~50% Secondary, 10-25% Primary, <10% Latent; Higher in HIV+
Timing2-12 Hours Post-Treatment (Peak 4-6h), Resolves in 12-24h
SymptomsFever, Chills, Rigors, Headache, Myalgia, Worsening of Rash, Hypotension, Tachycardia
ManagementSupportive: Antipyretics (Paracetamol), Fluids, Do NOT Delay/Stop Penicillin; Prednisolone Prophylaxis (1mg/kg Pre-Treatment) Optional in Severe Cases
DifferentialAnaphylaxis (Urticaria, Angioedema, Wheeze, Stridor — Immediate <1h) — Treat as Anaphylaxis

2️⃣ Neurosyphilis — Detailed Management

Indications for CSF Examination (Lumbar Puncture)

IndicationMandatory / Recommended
Neurological SymptomsMandatory (Headache, Cranial Nerve Palsy, Sensory/Motor Deficits, Cognitive Changes)
HIV InfectionMandatory (All Stages — Higher Neurosyphilis Risk, Atypical Serology)
Treatment FailureMandatory (Titre Rise/No Decline)
Late Latent / Tertiary SyphilisRecommended (If Duration Unknown)
Congenital SyphilisMandatory (All Probable/Confirmed Cases)
Ocular/Otic SyphilisMandatory

CSF Diagnostic Criteria for Neurosyphilis

FindingSignificance
CSF-VDRL ReactiveHighly Specific (~99%), Low Sensitivity (~50-70%) — Diagnostic if Positive
CSF WBC >5/μL (Lymphocytes)Supportive (Mild Pleocytosis)
CSF Protein >45 mg/dLSupportive
CSF FTA-ABS/TPPA ReactiveHigh Sensitivity (~90%), Low Specificity — Negative Rules Out
CSF PCR (T. pallidum)Emerging, High Sensitivity/Specificity — Not Widely Available
Diagnosis: Reactive CSF-VDRL + Clinical/CSF Abnormalities = Definite Neurosyphilis; Clinical + CSF Abnormalities + Reactive Serology = Probable Neurosyphilis

Neurosyphilis Treatment Algorithm

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Neurosyphilis Diagnosed
↓
Penicillin Allergy?
↓
& E
Desensitisation Possible?
↓
Follow-Up: CSF at 6m If CSF Abnormal Persists → Re-treat
Ceftriaxone 2g IV Daily × 14d (Alternative — Limited Data)
↓
If HIV+: Monitor Closer CSF at 3m, 6m, 12m

2️⃣ Syphilis in Pregnancy & Congenital Prevention

Maternal Screening & Staging

TimingAction
Booking (First Trimester)Universal Screening: RPR/VDRL + TPPA (Reverse Algorithm Preferred)
High Risk / High Prevalence AreasRepeat at 28-32 Weeks & Delivery
Positive ScreenStage Maternal Infection (Clinical, Serology, CSF if Indicated)

Treatment in Pregnancy

Maternal StageRegimenComments
Primary / Secondary / Early LatentBenzathine Penicillin G 2.4MU IM Single DoseOnly Proven Treatment; Desensitise if Allergic; Treat ≥30 Days Before Delivery
Late Latent / Unknown DurationBenzathine Penicillin G 2.4MU IM Weekly × 3 DosesTreat ≥30 Days Before Delivery
NeurosyphilisPenicillin G IV 18-24 MU/Day × 10-14 DaysDesensitise if Allergic
Penicillin AllergyDesensitisation → Penicillin (Mandatory — No Proven Alternative)Doxycycline/Tetracycline Contraindicated (Teratogenic)
Critical: Adequate Maternal Treatment ≥30 Days Before Delivery = <1-2% Congenital Transmission; Untreated/Inadequate = 40-100% Transmission (Stage-Dependent)

Congenital Syphilis — Neonatal Evaluation & Management

Classification & Management Algorithm

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Maternal Syphilis Positive
↓
Infant Evaluation
↓
Neonatal RPR/VDRL Titre Compared to Maternal
↓
Neonatal Titre ≥4x Maternal?
↓
**Probable/Confirmed Congenital Syphilis** → Full Workup (CSF, X-Ray, CBC, LFT) → **Penicillin G IV 50,000 U/kg q12h × 7d → q8h × 3d (Total 10d)**
Abnormal Clinical/X-Ray/CSF?
↓
Maternal Treatment Adequate? (≥30d Before Delivery, 4-Fold Decline)
↓
**Unlikely Congenital** → Observe, Serial RPR (Monthly × 3m) → Ensure Titres Decline
**Consider Treatment** → Procaine Penicillin 50,000 U/kg IM Daily × 10d (If Follow-Up Uncertain)

Neonatal Treatment Regimens

ScenarioRegimen
Proven/Probable Congenital (Abnormal CSF/X-Ray/Clinical/Neonatal Titre ≥4x Maternal)Aqueous Penicillin G IV 50,000 Units/kg/dose q12h (Days 1-7) → q8h (Days 8-10) — Total 10 Days
Possible Congenital (Maternal Untreated/Inadequate, Normal Infant Workup)Procaine Penicillin G 50,000 Units/kg IM Daily × 10 Days (If IV Access Difficult / Follow-Up Uncertain)
Unlikely Congenital (Maternal Adequately Treated, Normal Infant)Observation Only — Serial RPR/VDRL Monthly × 3 Months (Ensure Decline)

4️⃣ Special Situations & Complications

Syphilis in HIV Infection

AspectImpact
SerologyAtypical: Lower NTT Titres, Delayed Seroconversion, False-Negative NTT (Prozone), BFP More Common
ClinicalMore Severe: Multiple Chancres, Rapid Progression, Neurosyphilis More Common/At Earlier Stage
CSF ExaminationMandatory at All Stages (Higher Neurosyphilis Risk)
TreatmentSame Penicillin Regimens; Close Follow-Up (NTT Monthly × 3m, Then 3-Monthly × 2y); CSF at 6m if Neurosyphilis
JHRMore Frequent/Severe (Cytokine Dysregulation)

Treatment Failure & Re-infection

ScenarioDefinitionManagement
Treatment FailureNTT Titre No 4-Fold Decline by 6-12 Months (or Rise)CSF Examination Mandatory; Re-treat As Per Stage (Usually Late Latent Regimen: BPC 2.4MU Weekly × 3)
Re-infectionNTT Titre Rise ≥4-Fold After Previous DeclineTreat as New Infection (Stage-Appropriate Regimen); Partner Notification
SerofastPersistent Low NTT Titre (<1:4) After Adequate TreatmentNot Failure — Monitor, No Re-Treatment Needed

3️⃣ Syphilis — Public Health & Surveillance

Notifiable Disease Reporting

DiseaseReporting Timeframe
Syphilis (Infectious: Primary, Secondary, Early Latent)Within 24-72 Hours (Notifiable)
Late Latent / Tertiary / CongenitalWithin 1 Week (Notifiable)
Syphilis in PregnancyImmediate (Maternal Safeguarding Team Referral)

Partner Notification

StrategyDescription
Patient ReferralIndex Patient Informs Partners (Preferred)
Provider ReferralHealth Staff Notifies (Confidential)
Contract ReferralTime-Limited Agreement
Lookback PeriodPrimary: 3 Months + Duration of Symptoms; Secondary: 6 Months + Duration; Early Latent: 1 Year; Late Latent: Long-Term Partners

Surveillance Indicators (WHO)

IndicatorTarget
Congenital Syphilis Rate<50 Cases per 100,000 Live Births (Elimination Target)
Maternal Syphilis Screening Coverage>95% at First Antenatal Visit
Maternal Treatment Adequacy>95% Treated ≥30 Days Before Delivery
Partner Notification Coverage>80% of Partners Treated

⚡ FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Staging1°: Chancre (Painless, Indurated), 2°: Rash (Palms/Soles), Condylomata Lata, Mucous Patches, Early Latent (<1y), Late Latent (>1y), Tertiary: Gumma, CV (Aortitis), Neurosyphilis
SerologyNTT (RPR/VDRL): Quantitative, Activity; TT (TPPA/FTA-ABS): Qualitative, Lifelong — Reverse Algorithm (TT→NTT) Preferred
Serology InterpretationPrimary: NTT -ve → +ve (Seroconversion); 2°: High NTT (+ve TT); Latent: Low/Declining NTT (+ve TT); Tertiary: NTT Often -ve (+ve TT)
TreatmentPenicillin G ONLY Proven Cure; 1°/2°/Early Latent: BPC 2.4MU IM Single; Late Latent/Tertiary: BPC 2.4MU IM Weekly × 3; Neurosyphilis: Penicillin G IV 18-24MU/d × 10-14d; Congenital: Pen G IV 50,000U/kg q12h × 7d → q8h × 3d
Penicillin AllergyDesensitisation → Penicillin (Preferred, Mandatory in Pregnancy/Neurosyphilis/Congenital); Doxycycline 100mg BD × 14-28d (Non-Pregnant, Non-Neurosyphilis)
NeurosyphilisCSF VDRL +ve (Specific); Penicillin G IV 18-24MU/d × 10-14d; CSF F/U at 6m
CongenitalMaternal Treatment ≥30d Before Delivery = Prevention; Neonatal Pen G IV 50,000U/kg q12h × 7d → q8h × 3d
JHR2-12h Post-Tx: Fever, Chills, Worsening Rash — Supportive, Do NOT Stop Penicillin
Syphilis in HIVAtypical Serology, More Severe, Neurosyphilis Common — CSF Mandatory All Stages
ScreeningPregnancy: Booking (All); High Risk: Repeat 28-32w, Delivery; MSM: Annual

🎤 Viva Questions (Expected Answers)

#QuestionExpected Answer
1Syphilis staging — clinical features of each stage?1°: Painless Indurated Chancre + Non-Tender Lymphadenopathy; 2°: Maculopapular Rash (Palms/Soles), Condylomata Lata, Mucous Patches, Generalised Lymphadenopathy; Early Latent (<1y): Asymptomatic, Seropositive; Late Latent (>1y): Asymptomatic, Low/Declining RPR; Tertiary: Gumma, Aortitis, Neurosyphilis
2Syphilis serology — NTT vs TT, interpretation?NTT (RPR/VDRL): Quantitative, Correlates with Activity, Can Be False +ve/Prozone; TT (TPPA/FTA-ABS): Qualitative, Specific, Lifelong Positive; Reverse Algorithm: TT First → If Positive → NTT for Titres
3Penicillin treatment by stage — regimens?1°/2°/Early Latent: BPC 2.4MU IM Single; Late Latent/Tertiary: BPC 2.4MU IM Weekly × 3; Neurosyphilis: Pen G IV 18-24MU/d × 10-14d; Congenital: Pen G IV 50,000U/kg q12h × 7d → q8h × 3d
4Neurosyphilis — diagnosis & management?CSF VDRL +ve (Specific, Low Sens); CSF Pleocytosis, High Protein; Pen G IV 18-24MU/d × 10-14d; Desensitise if Allergic; CSF F/U at 6m
5. Congenital syphilis — prevention & neonatal management?Maternal BPC ≥30d Before Delivery = Prevention; Neonatal: Pen G IV 50,000U/kg q12h × 7d → q8h × 3d (10d Total); Maternal Adequate Treatment + Declining Titres = Observe Neonate
6. Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction — features & management?2-12h Post-Tx: Fever, Chills, Myalgia, Worsening Rash, Hypotension; Supportive (Antipyretics, Fluids), Do NOT Stop Penicillin; Prednisolone Prophylaxis Optional
7. Syphilis in pregnancy — management algorithm?Screen at Booking (All); Positive: Stage → BPC Regimen (Stage-Based); Treat ≥30d Before Delivery; Allergy → Desensitise → Penicillin; Doxycycline Contraindicated
8. NTT vs TT — which is quantitative, which is qualitative?NTT (RPR/VDRL) = Quantitative (Titre); TT (TPPA/FTA-ABS) = Qualitative (Positive/Negative)
9. Late latent syphilis — definition & treatment?>1 Year (or Unknown Duration); BPC 2.4MU IM Weekly × 3 Doses
10. Biologic false positive (BFP) RPR — causes?Autoimmune (SLE, APS), Viral Infections (EBV, CMV, Hepatitis), Pregnancy, Age, TB, Leprosy, Drugs (Procainamide)

🧩 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
"Syphilis = Only Genital Disease"NO. Systemic: Skin, Mucous Membranes, Bone, Liver, CV, CNS, Eyes, Ears, Placenta
"RPR Positive = Active Syphilis"NO. RPR = Quantitative Activity Marker; Can Be BFP, Prozone, Serofast, Persist After Cure; TT (TPPA) = Lifelong Positive
"Late Latent = Not Infectious"YES (Sexually); But Can Transmit Vertically (Pregnancy); Treat as Infectious for Partner Notification
"Neurosyphilis = Only Late Stage"NO. Can Occur At ANY Stage (Even Primary/Secondary); HIV Increases Early Risk
"Penicillin Allergy = Use Doxycycline"NO. Pregnancy/Neurosyphilis/Congenital: NO PROVEN ALTERNATIVE — Desensitise → Penicillin Mandatory
"Ceftriaxone = Alternative to Penicillin for Neurosyphilis"NO. Limited Data, Not 1st Line; Penicillin G IV Only Proven; Ceftriaxole = Alternative if Desensitisation Absolutely Impossible
"RPR Negative = No Syphilis"NO. Primary: RPR Negative Early (Seroconversion 1-4w); Late Latent/Tertiary: RPR Can Be Negative; Prozone = False Negative at High Titre
"All Syphilis Stages Same Penicillin Dose"NO. Early Stages = Single Dose BPC; Late Stages = 3 Weekly Doses BPC; Neurosyphilis/Congenital = High-Dose IV Pen G
"Serofast = Treatment Failure"NO. Persistent Low Titre (<1:4) After Adequate Treatment — Not Failure, No Re-Treatment
"RPR Titre 4-Fold Decline = Cure at 3 Months"NO. 1°/2°: 6 Months; Latent/Tertiary: 12-24 Months; If No Decline by Expected Time → CSF/LP
Mnemonic: SYPHILIS MASTER SEROLOGY
Syphilis Stages: 1° (Chancre) → 2° (Rash/Palms-Soles/Condylomata Lata) → Early Latent (<1y) → Late Latent (>1y) → Tertiary (Gumma/Aortitis/Neurosyphilis)
Y (Why Penicillin?): Only Proven Cure — 1°/2°/Early Latent: BPC 2.4MU IM Single; Late Latent/Tertiary: BPC 2.4MU IM × 3 Weekly; Neurosyphilis: Pen G IV 18-24MU/d × 10-14d; Congenital: Pen G IV 50k/kg q12h × 7d → q8h × 3d
Penicillin Allergy: Desensitise → Penicillin (Preferred, Mandatory Pregnancy/Neuro/Congenital); Doxy 14-28d (Non-Pregnant/Non-Neurosyphilis)
HIV Co-infection: Atypical Serology, More Severe, Neurosyphilis Common → LP Mandatory All Stages
In Pregnancy: Screen All (Booking); Positive → BPC Stage-Based; Treat ≥30d Before Delivery; Allergy → Desensitise
Latent Syphilis: Early (<1y) = BPC Single; Late (>1y/Unknown) = BPC × 3 Weekly
Interpretation Serology: NTT (RPR/VDRL) = Quantitative, Activity; TT (TPPA/FTA-ABS) = Qualitative, Lifelong
Serology Algorithms: Traditional (NTT→TT) vs Reverse (TT→NTT) — Reverse Preferred (WHO/CDC)
Congenital: Maternal BPC ≥30d Before Delivery = Prevention; Neonatal Pen G IV 50k/kg q12h × 7d → q8h × 3d; Maternal Adequate + Declining = Observe
Jarisch-Herxheimer: 2-12h Post-Tx (Fever, Chills, Worsening Rash) — Supportive, Do NOT Stop Penicillin
Antimicrobial: Penicillin G ONLY Proven; No Oral Alternatives for Neuro/Congenital/Pregnancy
Lumbar Puncture: Indicated: Neuro Symptoms, HIV All Stages, Treatment Failure, Late Latent/Tertiary, Congenital
Treatment Failure: No 4-Fold NTT Decline by 6-12m → LP Mandatory; Re-treat as Late Latent (BPC × 3)
Re-infection: NTT Rise ≥4-Fold After Prior Decline → Treat as New Infection
Serofast: Persistent Low NTT (<1:4) After Adequate Tx — Not Failure, Monitor
Syphilis Congenital: Maternal Treatment ≥30d Before Delivery = Prevention

🗺️ Mind Map

Syphilis
Staging
Primary: Chancre (Painless, Indurated)
Secondary: Rash (Palms/Soles), Condylomata Lata, Mucous Patches
Early Latent: <1y, Seropositive
Late Latent: >1y, Low/Declining RPR
Tertiary: Gumma, Aortitis, Neurosyphilis
Serology
NTT (RPR/VDRL): Quantitative, Activity, Prozone, BFP
TT (TPPA/FTA-ABS): Qualitative, Lifelong
Algorithms: Traditional (NTT→TT) vs Reverse (TT→NTT) [Preferred]
Interpretation: Stage-Based Patterns
Treatment
Penicillin G ONLY Proven Cure
1°/2°/Early Latent: BPC 2.4MU IM Single
Late Latent/Tertiary: BPC 2.4MU IM × 3 Weekly
Neurosyphilis: Pen G IV 18-24MU/d × 10-14d
Congenital: Pen G IV 50k/kg q12h × 7d → q8h × 3d
Allergy: Desensitise → Penicillin (Mandatory Pregnancy/Neuro/Congenital)
Neurosyphilis
CSF VDRL +ve (Specific)
Pen G IV 18-24MU/d × 10-14d
LP Indications: Neuro Sx, HIV, Tx Failure, Late Latent, Congenital
CSF F/U at 6m
Congenital
Maternal BPC ≥30d Before Delivery = Prevention
Neonatal Pen G IV 50k/kg q12h × 7d → q8h × 3d
Maternal Adequate + Declining → Observe
Special
HIV: Atypical Serology, Neuro Common, LP Mandatory
JHR: 2-12h Post-Tx (Fever, Chills, Worsening Rash) → Supportive, Don't Stop Pen
Screening: Pregnancy (Booking), MSM (Annual), High Risk (Repeat)

💬 Exam Answer Modes

FormatPromptKey Points
Long Essay"Describe the clinical features, diagnosis, and management of syphilis including staging, serology, and treatment."Staging (1° Chancre, 2° Rash/Palms-Soles/Condylomata Lata, Latent, Tertiary), Serology (NTT vs TT, Reverse Algorithm), Treatment (Penicillin G Stage-Specific), Neurosyphilis (CSF VDRL, Pen G IV), Congenital (Prevention, Neonatal Pen G), JHR, Penicillin Allergy (Desensitisation), HIV Co-infection
Short Note"Syphilis serology — NTT vs TT, interpretation, and algorithms."NTT (RPR/VDRL): Quantitative, Activity, Prozone, BFP; TT (TPPA/FTA-ABS): Qualitative, Lifelong; Reverse Algorithm (TT→NTT) Preferred; Stage-Based Interpretation (1°: NTT -ve→+ve; 2°: High NTT; Latent: Low NTT; Tertiary: NTT -ve)
Viva"25-year-old man with painless genital ulcer, RPR 1:128, TPPA positive. Stage and treatment?"Primary Syphilis (Chancre, High RPR, Positive TPPA) → Benzathine Penicillin G 2.4MU IM Single Dose; Partner Notification, HIV/Syphilis Screen, Follow-Up RPR at 3, 6, 12 Months
Ward Round"Pregnant woman at 28 weeks with early latent syphilis (RPR 1:32, TPPA+). Management?"Benzathine Penicillin G 2.4MU IM Single Dose (Early Latent); Treat ≥30 Days Before Delivery; Monitor RPR at Delivery, Neonatal Follow-Up; Allergy → Desensitise → Penicillin; Doxycycline Contraindicated
Last-Night"Stages: 1° Chancre, 2° Rash/Condylomata/Mucous Patches, Latent Early/Late, Tertiary Gumma/Aortitis/Neuro. Serology: NTT=Quantitative(RPR/VDRL), TT=Qualitative(TPPA/FTA-ABS), Reverse Algorithm. Tx: BPC 2.4MU IM Single (1°/2°/Early Latent), ×3 Weekly (Late Latent/Tertiary), Pen G IV 18-24MU/d (Neuro), Pen G IV 50k/kg (Congenital). Allergy: Desensitise→Pen. JHR: 2-12h Fever/Chills/Rash—Supportive. Congenital: Maternal BPC ≥30d=Prevention, Neonatal Pen G IV 10d. HIV: LP Mandatory. Screen: Pregnancy Booking."Compressed.

📌 Summary

  • Staging: 1° Chancre (Painless, Indurated), 2° Rash (Palms/Soles), Condylomata Lata, Mucous Patches, Early Latent (<1y), Late Latent (>1y), Tertiary (Gumma, Aortitis, Neurosyphilis)
  • Serology: NTT (RPR/VDRL) = Quantitative, Activity; TT (TPPA/FTA-ABS) = Qualitative, Lifelong — Reverse Algorithm (TT→NTT) Preferred
  • Interpretation: 1°: NTT Negative→Positive; 2°: High Titre NTT; Latent: Low/Declining NTT; Tertiary: NTT Often Negative
  • Treatment: Penicillin G ONLY Proven Cure — 1°/2°/Early Latent: BPC 2.4MU IM Single; Late Latent/Tertiary: BPC 2.4MU IM Weekly × 3; Neurosyphilis: Pen G IV 18-24MU/d × 10-14d; Congenital: Pen G IV 50,000U/kg q12h × 7d → q8h × 3d (Total 10d)
  • Penicillin Allergy: Desensitise → Penicillin (Mandatory in Pregnancy, Neurosyphilis, Congenital); Doxycycline 100mg BD × 14-28d (Non-Pregnant, Non-Neurosyphilis Only)
  • Neurosyphilis: CSF VDRL Positive (Specific, Low Sensitivity); Pen G IV 18-24MU/d × 10-14d; CSF Follow-Up at 6 Months
  • Congenital Prevention: Maternal BPC ≥30 Days Before Delivery; Neonatal Pen G IV 10 Days if Proven/Probable
  • JHR: 2-12 Hours Post-Treatment — Fever, Chills, Worsening Rash — Supportive, Do NOT Stop Penicillin
  • Syphilis in HIV: Atypical Serology, More Severe, Neurosyphilis Common — LP Mandatory at All Stages
  • Screening: Pregnancy (Booking, Universal); MSM (Annual); High Risk (Repeat 28-32w, Delivery)

❓ MCQs (10)

  1. Primary syphilis — characteristic chancre features?

A. Painful, Purulent, Multiple B. Painless, Indurated, Clean Base, Non-Tender Lymphadenopathy C. Painful, Soft, Undermined Edges D. Multiple, Tender, Suppurative

Answer: B. Painless, Indurated, Clean Base, Non-Tender Regional Lymphadenopathy.

  1. Syphilis serology — which test is quantitative and correlates with disease activity?

A. TPPA B. FTA-ABS C. RPR / VDRL (Non-Treponemal) D. Western Blot

Answer: C. NTT (RPR/VDRL) = Quantitative, Activity Marker; TT = Qualitative, Lifelong.

  1. Late latent syphilis definition & treatment?

A. <1 Year, BPC Single Dose B. >1 Year (or Unknown), BPC 2.4MU IM Weekly × 3 Doses C. >2 Years, No Treatment D. <1 Year, BPC × 3 Weekly

Answer: B. Late Latent = >1 Year (or Unknown Duration) → Benzathine Penicillin G 2.4MU IM Weekly × 3 Doses.

  1. Neurosyphilis — most specific CSF test?

A. CSF FTA-ABS B. CSF Protein C. CSF VDRL D. CSF WBC Count

Answer: C. CSF VDRL = Highly Specific (~99%), Low Sensitivity; Diagnostic if Positive.

  1. Syphilis in pregnancy — prevention of congenital syphilis?

A. Treat at Delivery B. Treat ≥30 Days Before Delivery (Stage-Appropriate Penicillin) C. Treat at 36 Weeks Only D. No Treatment Needed if RPR Low

Answer: B. Maternal Penicillin Treatment ≥30 Days Before Delivery = Prevention (<1-2% Transmission).

  1. Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction — timing and management?

A. Immediate (<1h), Stop Penicillin B. 2-12 Hours Post-Treatment, Supportive (Do NOT Stop Penicillin) C. 24-48 Hours, Steroids D. 1 Week, Antihistamines

Answer: B. 2-12 Hours Post-Treatment (Fever, Chills, Worsening Rash) → Supportive, Do NOT Stop Penicillin.

  1. Penicillin allergy in neurosyphilis — correct management?

A. Ceftriaxone 2g IV Daily B. Desensitisation → Penicillin G IV (Only Proven Cure) C. Doxycycline 100mg BD D. Azithromycin

Answer: B. Desensitisation → Penicillin G IV Mandatory (Only Proven Cure for Neurosyphilis/Congenital/Pregnancy).

  1. Neonatal congenital syphilis — proven/probable case treatment?

A. Procaine Penicillin IM 10d B. Aqueous Penicillin G IV 50,000 U/kg q12h × 7d → q8h × 3d (Total 10d) C. Ceftriaxone IV D. Azithromycin

Answer: B. Aqueous Penicillin G IV 50,000 U/kg/dose q12h (Days 1-7) → q8h (Days 8-10) = Total 10 Days.

  1. Syphilis screening in pregnancy — when?

A. 36 Weeks Only B. Booking (First Trimester) — Universal; Repeat 28-32w & Delivery if High Risk C. Only If Symptoms D. 20 Weeks Only

Answer: B. Universal at Booking; Repeat at 28-32 Weeks & Delivery if High Risk/High Prevalence.

  1. Biologic false positive (BFP) RPR — common causes?

A. HIV, Syphilis B. Autoimmune (SLE, APS), Viral (EBV, CMV), Pregnancy, Age, TB, Leprosy, Drugs C. Recent Vaccination D. Hepatitis Only

Answer: B. Autoimmune (SLE, APS), Viral Infections (EBV, CMV, Hepatitis), Pregnancy, Age, TB, Drugs.

  1. Syphilis treatment failure — definition?

A. RPR Negative at 6 Months B. No 4-Fold Decline in RPR by 6-12 Months (or Rise) C. Persistent Low RPR D. TT Becomes Negative

Answer: B. No 4-Fold Decline in RPR by 6-12 Months (or Titre Rise) = Treatment Failure; CSF Mandatory.

📋 SBAs (10)

  1. 25M with painless genital ulcer, RPR 1:128, TPPA+. Stage & treatment?

A. Secondary, Ceftriaxone B. Primary, Benzathine Penicillin G 2.4MU IM Single Dose C. Early Latent, Doxycycline D. Secondary, BPC × 3 Weekly

Answer: B. Primary Syphilis (Chancre, High RPR, TPPA+) → BPC 2.4MU IM Single Dose.

  1. Late latent syphilis (RPR 1:4, TPPA+, acquired 5 years ago). Treatment?

A. BPC 2.4MU IM Single Dose B. BPC 2.4MU IM Weekly × 3 Doses C. Doxycycline 100mg BD × 28d D. No Treatment Needed

Answer: B. Late Latent (>1y) → BPC 2.4MU IM Weekly × 3 Doses.

  1. Pregnant woman at 28 weeks with secondary syphilis (RPR 1:256). Management?

A. Ceftriaxone B. Benzathine Penicillin G 2.4MU IM Single Dose C. Doxycycline D. Azithromycin

Answer: B. BPC 2.4MU IM Single Dose (Stage-Appropriate); Treat ≥30d Before Delivery; Allergy → Desensitise.

  1. Neurosyphilis — CSF finding most specific for diagnosis?

A. CSF FTA-ABS Positive B. CSF VDRL Positive C. CSF Protein >45 mg/dL D. CSF WBC 10/μL

Answer: B. CSF VDRL Positive = Highly Specific (~99%), Diagnostic if Positive.

  1. Congenital syphilis prevention — maternal treatment timing?

A. At Delivery B. ≥30 Days Before Delivery C. 36 Weeks Gestation D. 20 Weeks Gestation

Answer: B. Maternal Adequate Penicillin Treatment ≥30 Days Before Delivery = Prevention (<1-2% Transmission).

🔑 Answer Keys

MCQsSBAs
1-B, 2-C, 3-B, 4-C, 5-B, 6-B, 7-B, 8-B, 9-B, 10-B1-B, 2-B, 3-B, 4-B, 5-B

▸ 15.3 15.3 6
▸ 15.3.01 3 2 Hsv

Parent Topic: STI MOC → STI Hierarchy

Status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

Priority: ⭐⭐⭐ HIGHEST (FCPS/MRCP — Common, Recurrent, Neonatal, Serology, Suppressive Therapy)

Source: Davidson 24th Ed Ch 13; WHO/BASHH/CDC Guidelines; FCPS/MRCP Syllabus; NICE NG196

1. 🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Differentiate HSV-1 vs HSV-2 epidemiology, clinical features, recurrence patterns
  • [ ] Diagnose primary vs recurrent genital herpes and extra-genital manifestations (Pharyngeal, Rectal, Sacral, Neonatal)
  • [ ] Apply diagnostic algorithms (NAAT, PCR, Culture, Serology, Tzanck) and interpret results
  • [ ] Apply episodic vs suppressive antiviral therapy (Acyclovir, Valacyclovir, Famciclovir)
  • [ ] Manage HSV in Pregnancy (Neonatal Prevention, Cesarean Indication, Acyclovir Suppression)
  • [ ] Manage Neonatal HSV (Disseminated, CNS, SEM — Classification & Treatment)
  • [ ] Manage HSV in Immunocompromised (Severe Mucocutaneous, Resistance)
  • [ ] Answer viva: "Primary vs Recurrent HSV" and "Neonatal HSV Classification" and "Cesarean Indication" and "Suppressive Therapy Indications"

2. 🧠 Core Concept: HSV Biology & Pathogenesis

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Herpes Simplex Virus
↓
& C
HSV-2 (Genital)
↓
Alpha-Herpesvirus
Transmission
↓
Enveloped, dsDNA
Neurotropic
Latency in Sensory Ganglia
Direct Contact (Viral Shedding)
Asymptomatic Shedding (Major Driver)
Vertical (Neonatal)
↓
Reactivation Triggers
Clinical Manifestations
↓
Stress, UV, Fever, Trauma, Immunosuppression, Hormonal
Primary Infection
Recurrent Episodes
Asymptomatic Shedding
Neonatal HSV
Extra-Genital (Whitelow, Keratitis, Encephalitis)

1️⃣ HSV-1 vs HSV-2 — Epidemiology & Clinical Differences

FeatureHSV-1HSV-2
Traditional SiteOrolabial (Cold Sores)Genital
Current TrendIncreasing Genital HSV-1 (Oral Sex)Still Predominantly Genital
Global Seroprevalence67% (<50y) — 3.7 Billion13% (15-49y) — 491 Million
Genital InfectionRising (30-50% New Genital Herpes = HSV-1)Declining Proportion
Recurrence Rate (Genital)Low (~1/Year After 1st Year)High (~4-5/Year)
Asymptomatic SheddingLess Frequent (Genital)More Frequent (Genital)
Neonatal TransmissionHigher Risk (Primary Infection in Pregnancy)Lower Risk (But More Common Source)
Primary Episode SeverityOften MildOften Severe
Key: HSV-1 = Increasing Cause of Genital Herpes; HSV-2 = More Frequent Recurrences, More Asymptomatic Shedding

2️⃣ Clinical Manifestations

Primary Genital Herpes (First Episode)

FeatureDetail
Incubation2-12 Days (Median 4 Days)
Systemic SymptomsFever, Malaise, Myalgia, Headache, Inguinal Lymphadenopathy (Bilateral, Tender)
Local SymptomsPain, Burning, Pruritus → Multiple Vesicles → Painful Ulcers (Coalesce), Mucopurulent Discharge
Lesion DistributionVulva, Vagina, Cervix, Penis, Perianal, Thighs, Buttocks
Duration (Untreated)2-4 Weeks (Viral Shedding ~12 Days)
ComplicationsAseptic Meningitis (10-15%, HSV-2), Urinary Retention (Sacral Radiculopathy), Proctitis (MSM), Pharyngitis (Oral Sex)

Recurrent Genital Herpes

FeatureDetail
ProdromeBurning, Tingling, Pruritus at Recurrence Site (Hours-Days Before Lesions)
LesionsFewer Vesicles/Ulcers, Smaller, Less Painful, Shorter Duration
Systemic SymptomsAbsent or Mild (No Fever, Minimal Lymphadenopathy)
Duration (Untreated)5-10 Days (Viral Shedding ~5 Days)
FrequencyHSV-2: 4-5/Year (Range 0-12+); HSV-1: ~1/Year
TriggersStress, UV, Fever, Trauma, Immunosuppression, Menstruation, Friction

Extra-Genital Manifestations

ManifestationFeatures
Herpetic WhitlowVesicular/Pustular Lesions on Fingers (Healthcare Workers, Autoinoculation), Painful, Tender
Herpes KeratitisDendritic Ulcer (Cornea), Pain, Photophobia, Blurred Vision — Ophthalmic Emergency
Herpes EncephalitisHSV-1 (Temporal Lobe), Fever, Confusion, Seizures, Focal Deficits — Acute Emergency
Eczema HerpeticumDisseminated Vesicles on Eczematous Skin, Fever, Malaise — Dermatological Emergency
Sacral/RadiculomyelitisUrinary Retention, Saddle Anaesthesia, Leg Weakness (S2-S4 Roots)
Herpes GladiatorumWrestlers/Contact Sports — Face, Neck, Arms

3️⃣ Diagnosis — Algorithms & Interpretation

Diagnostic Algorithm

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Suspected Genital Herpes
↓
Lesions Present?
↓
NAAT (PCR) — Preferred (Vesicle Fluid, Ulcer Base Swab)
Type-Specific Serology (IgG) HSV-1 vs HSV-2 gG-Based
↓
NAAT Result
Serology Result
↓
Type Subtyping (HSV-1 vs HSV-2) → Counselling, Prognosis, Tx
Consider Alternative (Syphilis, Chancroid, LGV, Aphthosis)
Genital Herpes Likely (HSV-2 = Genital)
Genital HSV-1 Possible (Oral→Genital) or Past Oral
Dual Infection or Cross-Reactivity

Diagnostic Test Comparison

TestSensitivitySpecificityTATBest Use
NAAT (PCR)>95%>98%1-24h1st Line (Lesions) — Vesicle Fluid/Ulcer Swab
Viral Culture70-80%100%24-48hViability, Typing, Resistance (If NAAT Unavailable)
Type-Specific Serology (IgG)90-95%95-98%Hours-DaysAsymptomatic Screening, Partner Testing, Pregnancy — gG-Based (HSV-1 gG1, HSV-2 gG2)
Tzanck Smear60-70%80-90%MinutesBedside (Multinucleated Giant Cells) — Not Type-Specific
DFA (Direct Fluorescent Antibody)80-90%95%HoursRapid (Lesion Smear) — Type-Specific
Non-Type-Specific SerologyPoorPoor—NOT RECOMMENDED (Cross-Reactivity, Confusing)
Key: NAAT = 1st Line for Lesions; Type-Specific IgG (gG-Based) = 1st Line for Asymptomatic/Pregnancy/Partner Testing

Type-Specific Serology Interpretation

ResultInterpretation
HSV-2 IgG +veGenital Herpes Likely (HSV-2 = Genital) — Counsel on Transmission, Recurrence, Suppressive Therapy
HSV-1 IgG +ve, HSV-2 IgG -vePast Oral HSV-1 OR Genital HSV-1 (If Genital Symptoms) — Increasing Genital HSV-1
Both +veDual Infection OR Cross-Reactivity (Older Assays) — gG-Based = High Specificity
Both -veSusceptible — Primary Infection Risk (Neonatal Risk if Partner +ve)
Important: IgM Not Recommended (Poor Specificity, Persistent After Primary); IgG gG-Based = Standard

4️⃣ Treatment — Antiviral Therapy

Antiviral Agents

DrugBioavailabilityHalf-LifeDosing FrequencyKey Advantage
Acyclovir15-30% (Oral)2.5-3.3h5x Daily (Standard)Cheapest, IV Formulation
Valacyclovir54% (Prodrug → Acyclovir)2.5-3.3h2-3x DailyBetter Bioavailability, Less Frequent Dosing
Famciclovir77% (Prodrug → Penciclovir)2.5-3.3h2-3x DailyLonger Intracellular Half-Life, Good Compliance
Mechanism: Viral Thymidine Kinase → Phosphorylation → DNA Polymerase Inhibition → Chain Termination

Episodic Treatment (Primary & Recurrent)

EpisodeRegimen (Choose One)
PrimaryAcyclovir 400mg 5x Daily × 7-10 Days OR Valacyclovir 1g BD × 7-10 Days OR Famciclovir 250mg TDS × 7-10 Days
RecurrentAcyclovir 400mg 5x Daily × 5 Days OR Valacyclovir 500mg BD × 3-5 Days OR Famciclovir 125mg BD × 5 Days (Or Famciclovir 1g Stat × 2 Doses 12h Apart)
InitiationWithin 24h of Prodrome/Lesion Onset — Earlier = Better
Key: Start Within 24h of Prodrome/Onset; Valacyclovir/Famciclovir Preferred (Better Compliance, Less Frequent Dosing)

Suppressive Therapy (Chronic Suppression)

IndicationCriteria
Frequent Recurrences≥6 Episodes/Year (Or ≥4 if Severe/Distressing)
Psychosocial ImpactSignificant Anxiety, Relationship Issues, Sexual Dysfunction
Discordant CoupleHSV-2 +ve Partner → Reduce Transmission to Susceptible Partner
Pregnancy (3rd Trimester)HSV-2 +ve or Primary HSV in Pregnancy → 36 Weeks to Delivery
ImmunocompromisedHIV, Transplant, Immunosuppression — Reduce Severity/Shedding

Suppressive Regimens

DrugRegimenDurationMonitoring
Acyclovir400mg BD (Or 200mg QID)6-12 Months (Reassess)Renal Function (If Renal Impairment: Dose Adjust)
Valacyclovir500mg OD (500mg BD if HIV+)6-12 MonthsRenal Function
Famciclovir250mg BD6-12 MonthsHepatic Function
Reassessment: After 6-12 Months — Attempt Drug Holiday (Stop 3-6 Months, Reassess Recurrence Frequency)

Treatment Efficacy (Suppressive Therapy)

OutcomeReduction
Recurrence Frequency70-80%
Asymptomatic Shedding80-90%
Transmission to Partner~50% (Valacyclovir 500mg OD)

5️⃣ HSV in Pregnancy & Neonatal Prevention

Maternal HSV Infection Risk to Neonate

Maternal ScenarioNeonatal RiskManagement
Primary HSV (1st/2nd Trimester)Low (<1%) (Maternal Antibodies Transfer)Acyclovir 400mg TDS 36w→Delivery (Consider)
Primary HSV (3rd Trimester/Peripartum)HIGH (30-50%) (No Maternal Antibodies, High Viral Load)Acyclovir 400mg TDS 36w→Delivery; Cesarean if Active Lesions/Prodrome at Labour
Recurrent HSV (Any Trimester)Low (1-3%) (Maternal Antibodies Protective)Acyclovir 400mg TDS 36w→Delivery (Standard); Cesarean ONLY if Active Lesions/Prodrome at Labour
Primary HSV at LabourVERY HIGH (50%+)Urgent Cesarean (Within 4-6h of Membrane Rupture) + Neonatal Acyclovir IV
Unknown Status (Lesions at Labour)Assume PrimaryCesarean (If Within 4-6h of Rupture) + Maternal/Neonatal Acyclovir

Suppressive Therapy in Pregnancy

ScenarioRegimen
HSV-2 +ve (Recurrent)Acyclovir 400mg TDS from 36 Weeks → Delivery (Standard)
Primary HSV in 3rd TrimesterAcyclovir 400mg TDS from Diagnosis → Delivery + Cesarean if Active Lesions at Labour
HIV Co-infectionValacyclovir 500mg BD (Preferred — Better Bioavailability)

Cesarean Section Indications

ScenarioRecommendation
Active Genital Lesions at Onset of LabourElective Cesarean (Before Membrane Rupture)
Prodromal Symptoms (Pain, Tingling) at LabourCesarean (Viral Shedding Likely)
Primary HSV in 3rd Trimester/PeripartumCesarean (Ideally Before Membrane Rupture, ≤4-6h After Rupture)
Recurrent HSV (No Lesions/Prodrome)Vaginal Delivery OK (Suppressive Therapy from 36w)
Membrane Rupture >4-6h with Active LesionsCesarean Benefit Diminished — Vaginal Delivery + Neonatal Prophylaxis

6️⃣ Neonatal HSV — Classification & Management

Classification (Kimberlin)

TypeFeaturesMortality (Untreated)Sequelae
SEM (Skin, Eye, Mouth)Vesicles on Skin, Conjunctivitis, Oral Mucosa4%Minimal if Treated Early
CNS (Central Nervous System)Encephalitis (Seizures, Lethargy, Irritability, Bulging Fontanelle), CSF Pleocytosis15%Significant Neurodevelopmental Delay (50%+)
DIS (Disseminated)Multi-Organ (Hepatitis, Pneumonitis, Coagulopathy, Thrombocytopenia, Adrenal Necrosis, Skin Lesions ± CNS)57%High Neurodevelopmental Disability (Survivors)
Note: ~45% SEM, 30% CNS, 25% DIS; Overlap Common (SEM + CNS = CNS Disease)

Neonatal HSV — Diagnosis

TestSampleTiming
HSV PCR (NAAT)CSF (Mandatory), Blood, Surface Swabs (Conjunctiva, Oral, Rectal, Skin Lesions)At Suspicion / 24-48h Post-Birth (If High Risk)
Viral CultureCSF, Blood, Surface SwabsIf PCR Unavailable
CSF AnalysisWBC, Protein, Glucose, HSV PCRMandatory (CNS/DIS Classification)
Liver FunctionALT, AST, CoagulationDIS Monitoring
Critical: HSV PCR on CSF = Gold Standard for CNS/DIS; Surface Swabs (Conjunctiva, Oral, Rectal) at 24h for SEM

Neonatal HSV — Treatment (IV Acyclovir)

ClassificationRegimenDuration
SEMAcyclovir 20mg/kg IV q8h14 Days
CNSAcyclovir 20mg/kg IV q8h21 Days
DISAcyclovir 20mg/kg IV q8h21 Days (Longer if Persistent Viremia)
Key: Acyclovir IV 20mg/kg q8h; Adjust for Renal Function; CSF PCR at End of Therapy (CNS/DIS) — If +ve → Extend

Neonatal HSV — Post-Treatment Suppression

RegimenDuration
Oral Acyclovir 300mg/m²/dose TDS6 Months (Post-IV) — Improves Neurodevelopmental Outcomes (CNS/DIS)

6️⃣ HSV in Immunocompromised & Resistance

Immunocompromised (HIV, Transplant, Chemotherapy)

FeatureDetail
ClinicalSevere, Extensive, Chronic, Non-Healing Ulcers, Atypical Presentations, Higher Dissemination Risk
DiagnosisNAAT + Culture (Resistance Testing)
TreatmentHigher Doses/Longer Durations — Acyclovir 400mg 5x Daily (Episodic), Suppressive: Valacyclovir 500mg BD (HIV)
ResistanceThymidine Kinase (TK) Mutations → Acyclovir/Valacyclovir/Famciclovir Cross-Resistance
Resistant HSV TreatmentFoscarnet 40mg/kg IV q8h (TK Mutants); Cidofovir 5mg/kg IV Weekly (Alternative); Topical Cidofovir/Imuquimod (Mucocutaneous)

HSV Resistance

MutationMechanismCross-Resistance
TK (Thymidine Kinase)Defective PhosphorylationAcyclovir/Valacyclovir/Famciclovir (All)
DNA PolymeraseAltered Drug BindingFoscarnet/Cidofovir (Usually Sensitive)

6️⃣ Counselling & Partner Management

Key Counselling Points

TopicKey Messages
TransmissionAsymptomatic Shedding = Major Driver; HSV-2 > HSV-1; Condoms Reduce Risk ~30-50%
RecurrenceProdrome = Infectious; Suppressive Therapy Reduces Frequency/Shedding/Transmission
PregnancyPrimary = High Risk; Recurrent = Low Risk; Cesarean if Active Lesions at Labour
NeonatalRare but Serious; Suppression 36w→Delivery Reduces Risk
PsychosocialStigma, Anxiety, Relationship Impact — Counselling, Support Groups, Accurate Information
Partner TestingType-Specific Serology — If Partner -ve → Discordant Couple → Suppressive Therapy + Condoms
DisclosureEthical/Legal Duty (Varies by Jurisdiction) — Before Sexual Contact

3. ⚡ FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
HSV-1 vs HSV-2HSV-1: Orolabial → Increasing Genital (Oral Sex); HSV-2: Predominantly Genital; HSV-2 > Recurrences, Asymptomatic Shedding
Primary vs RecurrentPrimary: Systemic (Fever, Lymphadenopathy), Severe, 2-4 Weeks; Recurrent: Prodrome, Fewer Lesions, Shorter (5-10 Days), No Systemic
DiagnosisNAAT (PCR) = 1st Line (Lesions); Type-Specific IgG (gG-Based) = Asymptomatic/Pregnancy/Partner; Tzanck = Bedside (Multinucleated Giant Cells)
Episodic TreatmentAcyclovir 400mg 5x/d × 7-10d (Primary), ×5d (Recurrent); Valacyclovir 1g BD × 7-10d (Primary), 500mg BD × 3-5d (Recurrent)
Suppressive Therapy≥6 Recurrences/Year, Discordant Couple, Pregnancy (36w→Delivery), Psychosocial; Valacyclovir 500mg OD / Acyclovir 400mg BD
PregnancyPrimary 3rd Trimester = HIGH Risk (Cesarean if Active Lesions at Labour); Recurrent = Low Risk (Vaginal OK if No Lesions); Suppression 36w→Delivery
Neonatal HSVSEM (Skin/Eye/Mouth) = 14d IV Acyclovir; CNS = 21d IV Acyclovir + 6mo Oral Suppression; DIS = 21d IV Acyclovir
Neonatal PreventionPrimary at Labour = Cesarean + Neonatal IV Acyclovir; Recurrent = Suppression 36w→Delivery, Cesarean if Active Lesions
ResistanceTK Mutations → Acyclovir/Valacyclovir/Famciclovir Cross-R → Foscarnet IV
Suppressive Therapy ReducesRecurrences 70-80%, Shedding 80-90%, Transmission ~50%

4. 🎤 Viva Questions (Expected Answers)

#QuestionExpected Answer
1Primary vs Recurrent genital herpes — clinical differences?Primary: Fever, Bilateral Tender Lymphadenopathy, Multiple Vesicles/Ulcers, 2-4 Weeks; Recurrent: Prodrome (Tingling/Burning), Fewer/Smaller Lesions, No Systemic Symptoms, 5-10 Days
2HSV-1 vs HSV-2 genital infection — key differences?HSV-1: Increasing Genital (Oral Sex), Lower Recurrence Rate (~1/Year), Less Shedding; HSV-2: Predominantly Genital, High Recurrence (~4-5/Year), More Asymptomatic Shedding
3Neonatal HSV classification & treatment durations?SEM (Skin/Eye/Mouth): IV Acyclovir 20mg/kg q8h × 14 Days; CNS: IV Acyclovir 20mg/kg q8h × 21 Days + 6mo Oral Suppression; DIS: IV Acyclovir 20mg/kg q8h × 21 Days
4HSV in pregnancy — management algorithm?Primary 3rd Trimester/Peripartum = HIGH Risk → Cesarean if Active Lesions at Labour + Neonatal IV Acyclovir; Recurrent = Low Risk → Vaginal OK if No Lesions; Suppression 36w→Delivery; Primary at Labour = Cesarean Within 4-6h of Membrane Rupture
5. Neonatal HSV — when is Cesarean indicated?Active Genital Lesions at Onset of Labour; Prodromal Symptoms at Labour; Primary HSV in 3rd Trimester/Peripartum; Membrane Rupture <4-6h with Active Lesions
6. HSV suppressive therapy — indications & regimen?≥6 Recurrences/Year, Discordant Couple, Pregnancy (36w→Delivery), Psychosocial Distress; Valacyclovir 500mg OD or Acyclovir 400mg BD; Reassess at 6-12 Months
7. HSV resistance — mechanism & treatment?TK (Thymidine Kinase) Mutations → Acyclovir/Valacyclovir/Famciclovir Cross-Resistance → Foscarnet 40mg/kg IV q8h (IV Only); Cidofovir Alternative
8. HSV diagnosis — when to use NAAT vs Serology?NAAT (PCR) = Lesions Present (1st Line, Type-Specific); Type-Specific IgG (gG-Based) = Asymptomatic, Pregnancy, Partner Testing, No Lesions
9. HSV in HIV — differences in management?Higher Doses/Longer Duration; Episodic: Acyclovir 400mg 5x/d (Higher); Suppressive: Valacyclovir 500mg BD; Resistance More Common (TK Mutations)
10. Cesarean for HSV — timing relative to membrane rupture?Before Membrane Rupture (Ideally); Within 4-6 Hours of Rupture if Already Ruptured; Benefit Diminished After 6h

5. 🧩 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
"HSV-1 = Only Oral, HSV-2 = Only Genital"NO. HSV-1 Increasingly Causes Genital Herpes (Oral Sex); HSV-2 Can Cause Oral (Oral Sex)
"Recurrent HSV = Always Mild"NO. Can Be Severe (Immunocompromised, Extensive, Prolonged); Sacral Radiculopathy (Urinary Retention) Possible
"Asymptomatic = Not Infectious"NO. Asymptomatic Shedding = Major Driver of Transmission (HSV-2 > HSV-1)
"Suppressive Therapy = Cure"NO. Reduces Frequency/Shedding/Transmission — Not Eradication; Latency Persists
"Cesarean = 100% Prevents Neonatal HSV"NO. Risk Reduced ~85%; Transascending Infection Possible (Membrane Rupture to Delivery); Also Intrauterine Infection Rare
"Acyclovir Resistance = Just Use Valacyclovir"NO. TK Mutation = Cross-Resistance to Acyclovir/Valacyclovir/Famciclovir → Foscarnet
"HSV IgM = Acute Infection"NO. IgM Poor Specificity, Persists, Not Recommended; Type-Specific IgG (gG-Based) = Standard
"All Genital Ulcers = HSV"NO. DDx: Syphilis (Painless Indurated), Chancroid (Painful+Suppurative Buboes), LGV (Painless Primary→Buboes), Behçet, Aphthous, Drug Eruption
"Suppressive Therapy = Lifelong Mandatory"NO. Reassess at 6-12 Months; Drug Holiday (Stop 3-6 Months), Reassess Recurrence
"HSV-1 Genital = Less Severe Than HSV-2"Primary HSV-1 Can Be Severe; But Recurrences Much Less Frequent (~1/Year vs 4-5/Year)
Mnemonic: HSV MASTER MANAGEMENT
HSV-1 vs HSV-2: HSV-1 Oral→Genital (Oral Sex), Low Recurrence; HSV-2 Genital, High Recurrence/High Shedding
Serology: Type-Specific IgG (gG-Based) = Standard; IgM NOT Recommended
Viral NAAT: Lesions → PCR (1st Line, Type-Specific); No Lesions → Type-Specific IgG
Primary vs Recurrent: Primary = Systemic (Fever, LAD), Severe, 2-4w; Recurrent = Prodrome, Fewer Lesions, 5-10d
Episodic Tx: Acyclovir 400mg 5x/d ×7-10d (Primary), ×5d (Recurrent); Valacyclovir 1g BD (Primary), 500mg BD (Recurrent)
Suppressive: ≥6/Year, Discordant Couple, Pregnancy (36w→Delivery), Psychosocial; Valacyclovir 500mg OD / Aci 400mg BD
Resistance: TK Mutations → ACV/VCV/FCV Cross-R → Foscarnet IV
Pregnancy: Primary 3rd Tri = HIGH Risk (Cesarean if Active); Recurrent = Low Risk; Suppression 36w→Delivery
Neonatal: SEM (14d), CNS (21d + 6mo Oral Suppression), DIS (21d) — IV Acyclovir 20mg/kg q8h
Cesarean Indication: Active Lesions/Prodrome at Labour; Primary 3rd Tri/Peripartum; Vaginal OK if Recurrent + No Lesions
Treatment Adjustment: HIV: Higher Doses/Longer (Aci 400mg 5x/d, Val 500mg BD); Renal: Dose Adjust
HSV Encephalitis: HSV-1, Temporal Lobe, Fever/Confusion/Seizures — IV Acyclovir 10mg/kg q8h × 14-21d (Emergency)
Extra-Genital: Whitlow (Fingers), Keratitis (Dendritic Ulcer - Emergency), Eczema Herpeticum
Triggers: Stress, UV, Fever, Trauma, Menstruation, Immunosuppression
Key Counselling: Asymptomatic Shedding = Transmission; Suppressive Reduces 50%; Discordant Couple = Suppression + Condoms
In Pregnancy: Primary = High Risk; Recurrent = Low Risk; Suppression 36w→Delivery
Neonatal: SEM 14d, CNS 21d+6mo Oral, DIS 21d — IV Aci 20mg/kg q8h
Monitoring: Suppressive: Reassess 6-12mo (Drug Holiday); Neonatal: Neurodevelopmental Follow-Up

6. 🗺️ Mind Map

HSV
Types
HSV-1: Oral→Genital, Low Recurrence
HSV-2: Genital, High Recurrence, High Shedding
Clinical
Primary: Systemic, Severe, 2-4w
Recurrent: Prodrome, Fewer Lesions, 5-10d
Extra-Genital: Whitlow, Keratitis, Encephalitis, Eczema Herpeticum
Diagnosis
Lesions: NAAT (PCR) 1st Line, Type-Specific
Asymptomatic: Type-Specific IgG (gG-Based)
Bedside: Tzanck (Multinucleated Giant Cells)
Treatment
Episodic: Aci 400mg 5x/d / Val 1g BD
Suppressive: Val 500mg OD / Aci 400mg BD
Indications: ≥6/yr, Discordant, Pregnancy, Psychosocial
Resistance: TK Mutations → Foscarnet IV
Pregnancy
Primary 3rd Tri: HIGH Risk, Cesarean
Recurrent: Low Risk, Vaginal OK
Suppression 36w→Delivery
Neonatal
SEM: 14d IV Aci
CNS: 21d IV Aci + 6mo Oral
DIS: 21d IV Aci
Prevention: Suppression 36w, Cesarean if Active Lesions
Resistance
TK Mutations → Foscarnet
HIV: Higher Doses, Resistance More Common

7. 📅 Spaced Repetition Tracker

ReviewDateScore (0–5)Notes
Day 1
Day 3
Day 7
Day 14
Day 30
Day 90

SectionMaxScore%
HSV-1 vs HSV-2 / Primary vs Recurrent3
Diagnosis (NAAT, Serology, Tzanck)3
Episodic & Suppressive Therapy3
Pregnancy & Neonatal HSV4

Total20

9. 💬 Exam Answer Modes

FormatPromptKey Points
Long Essay"Describe the clinical features, diagnosis, and management of genital herpes including special situations in pregnancy and neonate."HSV-1 vs HSV-2, Primary vs Recurrent, NAAT/Serology, Episodic/Suppressive Tx, Pregnancy Algorithm (Primary=High Risk/Cesarean, Recurrent=Low Risk/Suppression), Neonatal Classification (SEM/CNS/DIS) & Treatment, Resistance (TK Mutations→Foscarnet), Counselling
Short Note"Neonatal HSV — classification, diagnosis, and treatment."SEM (Skin/Eye/Mouth): IV Acyclovir 20mg/kg q8h × 14d; CNS (Encephalitis): IV Acyclovir 20mg/kg q8h × 21d + 6mo Oral Suppression; DIS (Disseminated): IV Acyclovir 20mg/kg q8h × 21d; PCR CSF = Gold Standard; Prevention: Suppression 36w→Delivery, Cesarean if Active Lesions
Viva"25-year-old pregnant woman at 34 weeks with first episode genital herpes. Management?"Primary Genital HSV in 3rd Trimester = HIGH Risk; Acyclovir 400mg TDS from Diagnosis → Delivery; MRI/US for Fetal Assessment; Cesarean if Active Lesions/Prodrome at Onset of Labour (Ideally Before Membrane Rupture, ≤4-6h After Rupture); Neonatal IV Acyclovir 20mg/kg q8h × 14-21d Based on Classification; Maternal/Neonatal HSV PCR at Birth
Ward Round"3-day-old neonate with vesicular rash, lethargy, seizures. CSF: Lymphocytic pleocytosis, HSV PCR positive. Diagnosis and management?"Neonatal HSV — CNS Disease (Encephalitis); IV Acyclovir 20mg/kg IV q8h × 21 Days; Supportive Care (Anticonvulsants, Fluids, Respiratory Support); Oral Acyclovir Suppression 300mg/m² TDS × 6 Months Post-IV; Neurodevelopmental Follow-Up; Maternal HSV Status + Serology
Last-Night"HSV-1/2: Oral/Genital, Recurrence/Shedding. Primary vs Recurrent: Systemic/Severe/2-4w vs Prodrome/Mild/5-10d. NAAT (Lesions), IgG (Asymptomatic). Episodic: Aci 400mg 5x/d (Prim 7-10d, Rec 5d). Suppressive: ≥6/yr, Discordant, Preg 36w→Del, Val 500mg OD. Preg: Primary 3rd Tri=High/Cesarean, Rec=Low/Vaginal OK. Neonatal: SEM 14d, CNS 21d+6mo Oral, DIS 21d. Resist: TK Mut→Foscarnet. Cesarean: Active Lesions/Prodrome at Labour."Compressed.

10. 📌 Summary

  • HSV-1 vs HSV-2: HSV-1 (Oral→Increasing Genital, Lower Recurrence); HSV-2 (Predominantly Genital, Higher Recurrence ~4-5/Year, More Asymptomatic Shedding)
  • Primary Genital Herpes: Systemic (Fever, Bilateral Tender Lymphadenopathy), Multiple Vesicles/Ulcers, 2-4 Weeks
  • Recurrent Genital Herpes: Prodrome (Tingling/Burning), Fewer/Smaller Lesions, No Systemic Symptoms, 5-10 Days
  • Diagnosis: NAAT (PCR) = 1st Line (Lesions, Type-Specific); Type-Specific IgG (gG-Based) = Asymptomatic/Pregnancy/Partner Screening
  • Episodic Treatment: Acyclovir 400mg 5x Daily × 7-10 Days (Primary), ×5 Days (Recurrent); Valacyclovir 1g BD (Primary), 500mg BD (Recurrent)
  • Suppressive Therapy: Indications: ≥6 Recurrences/Year, Discordant Couple, Pregnancy (36w→Delivery), Psychosocial; Valacyclovir 500mg OD / Acyclovir 400mg BD
  • Pregnancy: Primary 3rd Trimester = HIGH Neonatal Risk → Cesarean if Active Lesions at Labour; Recurrent = Low Risk → Vaginal Delivery OK if No Lesions; Suppression 36w→Delivery Standard
  • Neonatal HSV: SEM (Skin/Eye/Mouth) = 14d IV Acyclovir; CNS (Encephalitis) = 21d IV Acyclovir + 6mo Oral Suppression; DIS (Disseminated) = 21d IV Acyclovir
  • Resistance: TK Mutations → Acyclovir/Valacyclovir/Famciclovir Cross-Resistance → Foscarnet IV
  • Cesarean Indication: Active Genital Lesions at Labour, Prodrome at Labour, Primary HSV 3rd Trimester/Peripartum
  • HSV in HIV: Higher Doses, Longer Duration, Resistance More Common — Valacyclovir 500mg BD for Suppression

11. ❓ MCQs (10)

  1. Primary genital herpes — characteristic systemic features?

A. Asymptomatic B. Fever, Malaise, Bilateral Tender Inguinal Lymphadenopathy C. Only Local Lesions D. Pruritus Only

Answer: B. Fever, Malaise, Bilateral Tender Inguinal Lymphadenopathy.

  1. HSV-1 vs HSV-2 genital infection — which has higher recurrence rate?

A. HSV-1 B. HSV-2 C. Equal D. Neither Recur

Answer: B. HSV-2: ~4-5 Recurrences/Year; HSV-1: ~1/Year.

  1. Neonatal HSV — CNS disease treatment duration?

A. 14 Days B. 21 Days IV Acyclovir + 6 Months Oral Suppression C. 10 Days D. 7 Days

Answer: B. CNS: IV Acyclovir 20mg/kg q8h × 21 Days + Oral Acyclovir Suppression × 6 Months.

  1. HSV suppressive therapy — indication?

A. 1 Recurrence/Year B. ≥6 Recurrences/Year C. No Recurrences D. Only in Pregnancy

Answer: B. ≥6 Episodes/Year (or ≥4 if Severe/Distressing), Discordant Couple, Pregnancy (36w→Delivery), Psychosocial.

  1. Cesarean section for HSV — when indicated?

A. All HSV+ Women B. Active Genital Lesions or Prodrome at Onset of Labour C. Recurrent HSV Only D. Never Indicated

Answer: B. Active Lesions/Prodrome at Labour; Primary HSV 3rd Trimester/Peripartum.

  1. HSV resistance — mechanism and treatment?

A. DNA Pol Mutation → Foscarnet B. TK (Thymidine Kinase) Mutation → Acyclovir/Valacyclovir/Famciclovir Cross-Resistance → Foscarnet IV C. Efflux Pump → Cidofovir D. Beta-Lactamase → Acyclovir

Answer: B. TK Mutation → Cross-Resistance to Acyclovir/Valacyclovir/Famciclovir → Foscarnet IV.

  1. HSV in pregnancy — primary infection at 36 weeks. Management?

A. Vaginal Delivery B. Cesarean (Ideally Before Membrane Rupture) + Maternal/Neonatal IV Acyclovir C. Expectant Management D. Induction of Labor

Answer: B. Primary HSV in 3rd Trimester/Peripartum → Cesarean (Before Rupture/Within 4-6h) + Maternal/Neonatal IV Acyclovir.

  1. HSV diagnosis — test of choice for active lesions?

A. Type-Specific IgG B. NAAT (PCR) — Type-Specific C. Tzanck Smear D. Viral Culture

Answer: B. NAAT (PCR) = 1st Line for Active Lesions (High Sensitivity/Specificity, Type-Specific).

  1. Neonatal HSV — SEM (Skin, Eye, Mouth) treatment duration?

A. 7 Days B. 14 Days C. 21 Days D. 28 Days

Answer: B. SEM: IV Acyclovir 20mg/kg q8h × 14 Days.

  1. HSV resistance — TK mutation cross-resistance pattern?

A. Acyclovir Only B. Acyclovir, Valacyclovir, Famciclovir (All) C. Foscarnet Only D. Cidofovir Only

Answer: B. TK Mutation → Cross-Resistance to Acyclovir, Valacyclovir, Famciclovir → Foscarnet IV.

12. 📋 SBAs (10)

  1. Pregnant woman at 34 weeks with first-episode genital herpes. Management?

A. Expectant Vaginal Delivery B. Acyclovir 400mg TDS → Delivery + Cesarean if Active Lesions at Labour C. Cesarean at 36 Weeks D. No Treatment Needed

Answer: B. Primary HSV 3rd Trimester → Acyclovir 400mg TDS → Delivery + Cesarean if Active Lesions/Prodrome at Labour.

  1. Neonate with vesicular rash, conjunctivitis, oral ulcers. CSF normal. Classification & treatment?

A. CNS, 21d IV Aci + 6mo Oral B. SEM, 14d IV Aci C. DIS, 21d IV Aci D. CNS, 14d IV Aci

Answer: B. SEM (Skin/Eye/Mouth) → IV Acyclovir 20mg/kg q8h × 14 Days.

  1. Woman with 8 recurrences/year genital HSV-2, distressed. Best management?

A. Episodic Acyclovir Each Episode B. Suppressive Valacyclovir 500mg OD C. No Treatment D. Cesarean Section

Answer: B. Suppressive Therapy Indicated (≥6/Year): Valacyclovir 500mg OD or Acyclovir 400mg BD.

  1. HSV-2 positive woman, 3rd trimester, no lesions. Delivery plan?

A. Elective Cesarean B. Vaginal Delivery (Suppressive Acyclovir 400mg TDS from 36w) C. Induction at 37 Weeks D. Cesarean at 38 Weeks

Answer: B. Recurrent HSV, No Lesions → Vaginal Delivery OK + Suppressive Therapy from 36 Weeks.

  1. Immunocompromised patient with acyclovir-resistant HSV. Treatment?

A. Increase Acyclovir Dose B. Foscarnet 40mg/kg IV q8h C. Valacyclovir C. Famciclovir

Answer: B. TK Mutation → Cross-Resistance → Foscarnet 40mg/kg IV q8h (IV Only).

13. 🔑 Answer Keys

MCQsSBAs
1-B, 2-B, 3-B, 4-B, 5-B, 6-B, 7-B, 8-B, 9-B, 10-B1-B, 2-B, 3-B, 4-B, 5-B

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 15.3.02 3 3 Hpv

Parent Topic: STI MOC → STI Hierarchy

Status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

Priority: ⭐⭐⭐ HIGHEST (FCPS/MRCP — Cancer Prevention, Vaccination, Screening, Cancer Links)

Source: Davidson 24th Ed Ch 13; WHO/IARC; CDC/BASHH; FCPS/MRCP Syllabus; NICE NG188

1. 🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Classify HPV types (High-Risk vs Low-Risk) and their disease associations
  • [ ] Understand HPV natural history (Transient vs Persistent Infection → CIN → Cancer)
  • [ ] Apply HPV vaccination schedules (9-valent, 2-dose <15y, 3-dose ≥15y, Catch-up, Males)
  • [ ] Apply cervical screening algorithms (HPV Primary Screening, Cytology Triage, Colposcopy Referral)
  • [ ] Manage anogenital warts (Patient-Applied, Provider-Administered, Surgical)
  • [ ] Manage HPV-related cancers (Cervical, Anal, Oropharyngeal, Penile, Vulvar, Vaginal)
  • [ ] Apply HPV in special populations (HIV, Immunocompromised, Pregnancy, MSM)
  • [ ] Answer viva: "HPV types & cancer risk" and "Vaccination schedule" and "Screening algorithm" and "Wart treatment"

2. 🧠 Core Concept: HPV Biology & Natural History

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Human Papillomavirus
↓
Non-Enveloped, dsDNA Virus
↓
>200 Types Identified
Natural History
↓
High-Risk (Oncogenic)
Low-Risk (Non-Oncogenic)
Transient Infection (90% Clear in 1-2 Years)
Persistent Infection (High-Risk) → CIN → Cancer
Immune Evasion (E6/E7 Oncoproteins)
↓
HPV 16, 18 (70% Cervical Cancer)
HPV 31, 33, 45, 52, 58 (Additional 20%)
HPV 35, 39, 51, 56, 59, 66, 68 (Probable/High-Risk)
HPV 6, 11 (90% Genital Warts)
HPV 40, 42, 43, 44, 54, 61, 70, 72, 81
Carcinogenesis Pathway
↓
E6 → p53 Degradation (Apoptosis Block)
E7 → pRb Degradation (Cell Cycle Dysregulation)
Genomic Instability → Integration → Cancer

1️⃣ HPV Classification & Disease Associations

High-Risk (Oncogenic) HPV Types

GroupTypesCancer ContributionKey Associations
Highest Risk16, 18~70% Cervical CancerCervical, Anal, Oropharyngeal, Penile, Vulvar, Vaginal
High Risk31, 33, 45, 52, 58~20% Cervical CancerSame Cancers
Probable High Risk26, 53, 66, 67, 68, 70, 73, 82Minor ContributionCervical, Anal
Possible High Risk30, 34, 35, 39, 40, 42, 43, 44, 45, 51, 52, 54, 55, 56, 57, 59, 61, 62, 64, 67, 69, 70, 71, 72, 73, 81, 82, 83, 84, 85, 86, 87, 89, 90, 91Minimal—

Low-Risk (Non-Oncogenic) HPV Types

TypesDisease Association
6, 11~90% Genital Warts (Condyloma Acuminata), Recurrent Respiratory Papillomatosis (RRP), Low-Grade Cervical Lesions (LSIL)
40, 42, 43, 44, 54, 61, 70, 72, 81, 89Minor (Rare Warts, LSIL)

IARC Classification (2012/2020)

GroupTypesDefinition
Group 1 (Carcinogenic)16, 18, 31, 33, 35, 39, 45, 51, 52, 56, 58, 59Sufficient Evidence
Group 2A (Probably Carcinogenic)68Limited Evidence Human, Sufficient Animal
Group 2B (Possibly Carcinogenic)26, 53, 66, 67, 70, 73, 82Limited Evidence
Group 3 (Not Classifiable)Low-Risk Types (6, 11, etc.)Inadequate Evidence

1️⃣ HPV Natural History & Carcinogenesis

Infection Outcomes

OutcomeProbabilityTimeline
Transient Clearance~90%1-2 Years (Median 8-12 Months)
Persistence~10%>2 Years → Risk of CIN2+
Progression to CIN2/3~5-10% of Persistent3-10 Years
Progression to Invasive Cancer<1% of CIN310-20+ Years

Carcinogenesis Pathway (High-Risk HPV)

StepMolecular Mechanism
1. Viral EntryHPV Infects Basal Keratinocytes (Microtrauma)
2. Viral ReplicationEpisomal (Productive) in Differentiating Epithelium
3. Immune EvasionE6/E7 Oncoproteins Downregulate HLA, Interferon Response
4. Cell Cycle DysregulationE7 → pRb Degradation → E2F Release → Uncontrolled S-Phase Entry
5. Apoptosis BlockE6 → p53 Degradation → Failed DNA Repair/Apoptosis
6. Genomic InstabilityChromosomal Aberrations, Integration into Host Genome
7. Clonal ExpansionIntegrated HPV (E6/E7 Overexpression) → CIN → Cancer
Key: E6 → p53 Degradation (Apoptosis Block); E7 → pRb Degradation (Cell Cycle Dysregulation)

2️⃣ Clinical Manifestations

Anogenital Warts (Condyloma Acuminata)

FeatureDetail
Causative TypesHPV 6, 11 (90% of Warts)
AppearanceFleshy, Cauliflower-like, Pedunculated or Sessile, Skin-Coloured/Whitish
DistributionPerianal, Perineal, Vulvar, Vaginal, Cervical, Penile, Scrotal, Urethral, Anal
SymptomsOften Asymptomatic; Pruritus, Bleeding, Dyspareunia, Discharge
DifferentialCondyloma Lata (Syphilis), Molluscum, Seborrheic Keratosis, Skin Tags, SCC

Cervical Intraepithelial Neoplasia (CIN)

GradeHistologyHPV AssociationManagement
CIN1 (LSIL)Low-Grade (Basal 1/3)High/Low-RiskObservation (90% Regress), HPV Test at 12 Months
CIN2 (HSIL)High-Grade (Basal 2/3)High-RiskTreatment (Excision/Ablation) OR Observation (Selected)
CIN3 (HSIL / CIS)High-Grade (Full Thickness)High-RiskTreatment (Excision Preferred)

HPV-Related Cancers

Cancer SiteHPV AttributionHigh-Risk TypesScreening
Cervical~100%16, 18 (70%), 31, 33, 45, 52, 58HPV Primary Screening (Cytology Triage)
Anal~90%16, 18MSM/HIV: Annual Cytology/HPV
Oropharyngeal~70% (HPV+)16 (Dominant)No Organised Screening
Vulvar~40-70%16, 18Visual Inspection, Biopsy
Vaginal~70%16, 18Cytology/HPV (Rare)
Penile~50%16, 18Visual Inspection, Biopsy

2️⃣ HPV Vaccination

Available Vaccines

VaccineTypes CoveredDosing Schedule
9-Valent (Gardasil 9)6, 11, 16, 18, 31, 33, 45, 52, 58 (90% Cervical Cancer, 90% Genital Warts)2-Dose (<15y: 0, 6-12m); 3-Dose (≥15y: 0, 2, 6m)
Quadrivalent (Gardasil)6, 11, 16, 18 (70% Cervical Cancer, 90% Warts)Same as 9vHPV
Bivalent (Cervarix)16, 18 (70% Cervical Cancer)Same as 9vHPV
9-Valent = Preferred (Broadest Protection); Quadrivalent/Bivalent = Acceptable if 9v Unavailable

Vaccination Schedules

Age Group9vHPV ScheduleNotes
<15 Years2 Doses: 0, 6-12 Months (Minimum Interval 5 Months)Preferred (Robust Immune Response)
≥15 Years3 Doses: 0, 2, 6 Months (Month 0, 1-2, 6)Immunocompromised: 3 Doses Regardless of Age
Catch-UpFemales ≤26 Years, Males ≤21 Years (Up to 26 for MSM/Immunocompromised)Shared Clinical Decision 27-45 Years
HIV/Immunocompromised3 Doses (0, 2, 6 Months) Regardless of AgeMSM ≤26 Years (Up to 26)
Key: 2-Dose <15y, 3-Dose ≥15y; Catch-Up to 26y (F), 21y (M) / 26y (MSM/Immunocomp); Shared Decision 27-45y

Vaccine Efficacy & Impact

OutcomeEfficacy (9vHPV)
HPV 6/11/16/18/31/33/45/52/58 Infection>90% (Type-Specific)
CIN2+ (HPV 16/18/31/33/45/52/58)>95%
Genital Warts (6/11)>90%
Vulvar/Vaginal/Anal Precancer>90%
Population Impact (Countries with High Coverage)>80% Reduction in Vaccine-Type HPV, >50% Reduction in CIN2+

Contraindications & Precautions

ContraindicationDetail
Anaphylaxis to Previous Dose/ComponentAbsolute Contraindication
PregnancyDefer Until Postpartum (Safety Data Reassuring, But Not Licensed in Pregnancy)
Moderate/Severe Acute IllnessDefer
Immunocompromised3 Doses Regardless of Age (Safety: Inactivated, Non-Live)

3️⃣ Cervical Screening — HPV Primary Screening Algorithm

WHO/UK/IARC Recommended Algorithm (HPV Primary Screening)

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Cervical Sample (LBC/HPV Test)
↓
HPV Test Result
↓
Return to Routine Recall (3-5 Years Age-Dependent)
Partial Genotyping (HPV 16/18 vs Other HR)
↓
Immediate Colposcopy Referral
Reflex Cytology
↓
Colposcopy Referral
Repeat HPV Test in 12 Months
↓
Colposcopy Referral

Key Screening Parameters

ParameterRecommendation
Start Age25 Years (UK/WHO) / 21-25 Years (Country-Specific)
Interval3 Years (25-49), 5 Years (50-64) / 5 Years (HPV Primary)
Stop Age64-65 Years (If Adequate Negative History)
TestHPV Primary (PCR/TMA) + Reflex Cytology (Non-16/18 HR HPV)
Self-SamplingValidated (Improves Coverage)

Colposcopy & Treatment Guidelines

FindingManagement
CIN1Observation (12-Month HPV Test) — 90% Regress
CIN2Excision (LLETZ/LEEP) Preferred OR Ablation (If Small/Peripheral)
CIN3 / CISExcision (LLETZ/LEEP) Mandatory — Diagnostic & Therapeutic
CGIN (Adenocarcinoma In Situ)Excision (Cone Biopsy) — Hysterectomy if Completed Family

Post-Treatment Follow-Up (Test of Cure)

TimelineTestAction
6 Months Post-TreatmentHPV Test (+ Cytology)HPV Negative → Routine Recall; HPV Positive → Colposcopy
18 MonthsHPV TestIf Negative → Routine Recall

3️⃣ Anogenital Warts — Management

Treatment Options

CategoryAgent/MethodRegimenClearance RateRecurrence
Patient-Applied (Topical)Podophyllotoxin 0.15% Cream/GelBD × 3 Days On, 4 Days Off (Cycles ≤4)70-90%20-30%
Imiquimod 5% CreamHS 3x/Week (Alternate Nights) × 8-16 Weeks50-80%10-20%
Sinecatechins 15% OintmentTDS × 16 Weeks50-60%Low
Provider-Administered (Office)Cryotherapy (Liquid Nitrogen)Freeze-Thaw-Freeze Cycles70-90%20-30%
Trichloroacetic Acid (TCA) 80-90%Weekly × 4-6 Weeks70-85%20-30%
Podophyllin Resin 10-25%Weekly (Less Used, Toxicity)30-70%High
SurgicalExcision, Electrocautery, Laser (CO2), Loop ExcisionSingle Session90-100%10-20%

Treatment Algorithm (Warts)

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Anogenital Warts
↓
Patient Preference / Anatomy
↓
Podophyllotoxin or Imiquimod
Cryotherapy / TCA / Surgical
Cryotherapy / TCA / Surgical (Avoid Podophyllotoxin/Imiquimod)
Surgical / Cryotherapy / TCA (Higher Recurrence, Consider HPV Vaccine)

Wart Treatment in Special Populations

PopulationPreferredAvoid
PregnancyCryotherapy, TCA, SurgicalPodophyllotoxin (Teratogenic), Imiquimod (Limited Data)
Immunocompromised (HIV, Transplant)Surgical, CryotherapyTopical (Lower Efficacy, Higher Recurrence)
Children (Non-Sexual Transmission Possible)Cryotherapy, TCAPodophyllotoxin, Imiquimod
Urethral/Intravaginal/Intra-anal WartsSpecialist Referral (Cryotherapy, Laser, Surgical)Topical (Ineffective)

4️⃣ HPV in Special Populations

HIV / Immunocompromised

AspectImpact
HPV PrevalenceHigher (Higher Acquisition, Lower Clearance)
HPV TypesMultiple Concurrent Infections (High-Risk + Low-Risk)
Disease ProgressionFaster (CIN → Cancer), Higher Grade Lesions
WartsMore Extensive, Refractory, Higher Recurrence
ScreeningAnnual Cervical Screening (Cytology + HPV); Anal Cytology/HPV Annual (MSM/HIV)
Vaccination3 Doses (0, 2, 6m) Regardless of Age; Catch-Up to 26y (MSM to 26y)
TreatmentHigher Recurrence; Surgical Preferred for Warts; Excision for CIN

Men Who Have Sex with Men (MSM)

AspectRecommendation
Anal Cancer Risk~20-40x Higher (vs General Population); HIV+ = 80-100x
ScreeningAnnual Anal Cytology + HPV Testing (If HIV+); Consider Age 35+
Vaccination9vHPV Up to Age 26 (Catch-Up); MSM ≤26y Priority
WartsPerianal/Intra-anal Common; Surgical/Laser Preferred

Pregnancy

AspectManagement
WartsCryotherapy/TCA/Surgical (Avoid Podophyllotoxin/Imiquimod); Cesarean NOT Indicated for Warts
Cervical LesionsColposcopy Safe; Treatment Deferred Postpartum (Unless Invasive Cancer Suspected)
VaccinationDefer Until Postpartum (Not Licensed in Pregnancy)
CesareanNOT Indicated for Genital Warts (Rare Laryngeal Papillomatosis)

4️⃣ Non-Cervical HPV-Related Cancers

CancerHPV AttributionScreeningHigh-Risk Types
Anal~90%Annual Anal Cytology/HPV (MSM/HIV)16, 18
Oropharyngeal~70% (HPV+)No Organised Screening16 (Dominant)
Vulvar~40-70%Visual Inspection + Biopsy16, 18
Vaginal~70%Cytology/HPV (Rare)16, 18
Penile~50%Visual Inspection + Biopsy16, 18

4️⃣ Recurrent Respiratory Papillomatosis (RRP)

FeatureDetail
CausativeHPV 6, 11
TransmissionVertical (Mother→Infant at Delivery), Rare Horizontal
PresentationHoarseness, Stridor, Airway Obstruction (Laryngeal Papillomas)
FormsJuvenile-Onset (JORRP) (<12y, More Aggressive); Adult-Onset (AORRP)
TreatmentSurgical Debridement (Microdebrider/CO2 Laser); Adjuvant: Cidofovir (Intralesional), Bevacizumab (Intralesional), HPV Vaccine (Adjunct)
HPV VaccineAdjunct Therapy (Reduces Recurrence, Not Licensed as Treatment)

3. ⚡ FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
HPV TypesHigh-Risk: 16, 18 (70% Cervical Ca), 31, 33, 45, 52, 58; Low-Risk: 6, 11 (90% Warts)
Natural History90% Clear in 1-2 Years; Persistence → CIN2/3 → Cancer (10-20+ Years); E6→p53, E7→pRb
Vaccine (9vHPV)Types: 6,11,16,18,31,33,45,52,58; 2-Dose <15y (0,6-12m); 3-Dose ≥15y (0,2,6m); Catch-Up F≤26y, M≤21y (MSM/Immunocomp ≤26y)
ScreeningHPV Primary + Reflex Cytology; HPV -ve → Routine Recall; HPV 16/18 +ve → Colposcopy; Other HR HPV + Cytology → Colposcopy if Abnormal, Repeat 12m if Normal
CIN ManagementCIN1: Observe (12m HPV); CIN2: Excision Preferred; CIN3: Excision Mandatory; Test of Cure 6m (HPV Test)
Anogenital WartsHPV 6, 11; Topical (Podophyllotoxin, Imiquimod); Office (Cryo, TCA); Surgical (Laser/Excision); Pregnancy: Cryo/TCA/Surgical Only
Screening AlgorithmHPV Primary → If 16/18+ → Colposcopy; Other HR HPV → Cytology → Abnormal=Colpo, Normal=Repeat 12m
Non-Cervical CancersAnal (90% HPV, Screen MSM/HIV), Oropharyngeal (70% HPV+, 16 Dominant), Vulvar/Vaginal/Penile (40-70%)
Vaccination9vHPV: 2-Dose <15y (0,6-12m), 3-Dose ≥15y (0,2,6m); Catch-Up F≤26y, M≤21y (MSM/Immunocomp ≤26y)
HIV/Immunocompromised3 Doses (Any Age); Annual Cervical/Anal Screening; More Extensive Disease, Higher Recurrence

4. 🎤 Viva Questions (Expected Answers)

#QuestionExpected Answer
1HPV types — high-risk vs low-risk and associated diseases?High-Risk: 16, 18 (70% Cervical Ca), 31, 33, 45, 52, 58 → Cervical, Anal, Oropharyngeal, Penile, Vulvar, Vaginal Cancer; Low-Risk: 6, 11 → 90% Genital Warts, RRP
2HPV natural history and carcinogenesis?90% Clear in 1-2y; Persistent HR HPV → CIN → Cancer (10-20y); E6→p53 Degradation (Apoptosis Block), E7→pRb Degradation (Cell Cycle Dysregulation)
3HPV vaccination — 9vHPV schedule and catch-up?<15y: 2 Doses (0, 6-12m); ≥15y: 3 Doses (0, 2, 6m); Catch-Up: F≤26y, M≤21y (MSM/Immunocomp ≤26y); Shared Decision 27-45y
4Cervical screening algorithm — HPV primary screening?HPV Primary → -ve → Routine Recall; 16/18 +ve → Colposcopy; Other HR HPV +ve → Reflex Cytology → Abnormal → Colposcopy, Normal → Repeat 12m
5. CIN management by grade?CIN1: Observe (12m HPV Test); CIN2: Excision (LLETZ) Preferred; CIN3: Excision Mandatory (LLETZ/Cone); Test of Cure 6m (HPV Test)
6. Anogenital warts — treatment options?Patient-Applied: Podophyllotoxin 0.15% (BD ×3d On/4d Off), Imiquimod 5% (3x/Week); Provider: Cryotherapy, TCA 80-90%, Surgical (Laser/Excision); Pregnancy: Cryo/TCA/Surgical Only
7. HPV screening algorithm — HPV primary with reflex cytology?HPV -ve → Routine Recall (3-5y); HPV 16/18 +ve → Immediate Colposcopy; Other HR HPV +ve → Reflex Cytology → Abnormal → Colposcopy, Normal → Repeat HPV in 12m → If +ve → Colposcopy
8. HPV vaccination — schedule for 14-year-old vs 16-year-old?14y: 2 Doses (0, 6-12m); 16y: 3 Doses (0, 2, 6m); Catch-Up: F≤26y, M≤21y (MSM/Immunocomp ≤26y)
9. Non-cervical HPV cancers — screening recommendations?Anal: Annual Cytology/HPV (MSM/HIV); Oropharyngeal: No Organised Screening (HPV 16 Dominant); Vulvar/Vaginal/Penile: Visual Inspection + Biopsy
10. HPV vaccine in pregnancy and immunocompromised?Pregnancy: Defer Until Postpartum (Not Licensed); Immunocompromised/HIV: 3 Doses (0, 2, 6m) Regardless of Age

5. 🧩 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
"HPV = Only Cervical Cancer"NO. Anal (90%), Oropharyngeal (70%), Vulvar/Vaginal (40-70%), Penile (50%) — All HPV-Related
"HPV Vaccine = Only for Girls"NO. Both Genders; Prevents Anal, Oropharyngeal, Penile Cancers + Warts in Males
"HPV Vaccine = Only for Virgins"NO. Benefit Even If Sexually Active (Protects Against Types Not Yet Acquired)
"All HPV = Cancer"NO. Low-Risk (6, 11) = Warts, RRP; High-Risk = Cancer; 90% Clear Spontaneously
"HPV Vaccine = Only for <15y"NO. Catch-Up to 26y (F), 21y (M) / 26y (MSM/Immunocomp); Shared Decision 27-45y
"CIN1 = Treat Immediately"NO. CIN1 = Observe (90% Regress); 12-Month HPV Test, Treat if Persistent/Progresses
"HPV Test Positive = Cancer"NO. HPV +ve = Infection; Reflex Cytology/Colposcopy Needed for Risk Stratification
"Cesarean Prevents HPV Transmission to Infant"NO. RRP (HPV 6/11) Rare; Cesarean NOT Indicated for Warts; Vaccination = Prevention
"HPV Vaccine = Only for <15y"NO. Catch-Up to 26y (F), 21y (M) / 26y (MSM/Immunocomp); Shared Decision 27-45y
"All HPV Types = Same Cancer Risk"NO. 16, 18 = 70% Cervical Cancer; Other HR = Additional 20%; Low-Risk = Warts Only
Mnemonic: HPV MASTER CANCER PREVENTION
HPV Types: High-Risk (16,18,31,33,45,52,58) = Cancer; Low-Risk (6,11) = Warts
Pathogenesis: E6→p53 (Apoptosis Block), E7→pRb (Cell Cycle Dysregulation) → Genomic Instability → Cancer
Vaccine: 9vHPV (6,11,16,18,31,33,45,52,58); 2-Dose (<15y), 3-Dose (≥15y); Catch-Up F≤26, M≤21 (MSM≤26)
Screening: HPV Primary → -ve = Routine; 16/18 +ve = Colpo; Other HR → Cyto → Abnormal = Colpo
CIN Management: CIN1 Observe (12m HPV); CIN2 Excise; CIN3 Excise Mandatory; TOC 6m HPV Test
Warts: HPV 6/11; Topical (Podophyllotoxin, Imiquimod), Office (Cryo, TCA), Surgical
Warts Pregnancy: Cryo/TCA/Surgical ONLY (No Podophyllotoxin/Imiquimod)
Anal Cancer: MSM/HIV → Annual Anal Cyto/HPV
Oropharyngeal: HPV 16 Dominant, No Organised Screening
Vaccine Schedule: <15y = 2 Doses (0, 6-12m); ≥15y = 3 Doses (0, 2, 6m)
Catch-Up: F ≤26, M ≤21 (MSM/Immunocomp ≤26); Shared Decision 27-45
Cervical Screen: HPV Primary → -ve = Recall; 16/18 = Colpo; Other HR → Cyto
CIN: CIN1 Observe; CIN2/3 Excise; TOC 6m HPV
HIV/Immunocompromised: 3 Doses Vaccine; Annual Cyto+HPV; Anal Cyto Annual
RRP: HPV 6/11, Juvenile/Adult Onset, Surgical Debridement, Vaccine Adjunct
Prevention: Vaccine = Primary Prevention; Screening = Secondary Prevention
