9. 💬 Exam Answer Modes

FormatPromptKey Points
Long Essay"Describe the HPV vaccination programme, cervical screening algorithm, and management of CIN."9vHPV Types, Schedule (2-Dose <15y, 3-Dose ≥15y), Catch-Up, HPV Primary Screening (Algorithm), CIN Management (CIN1 Observe, CIN2/3 Excision), TOC, Warts Management
Short Note"HPV vaccination — types, schedule, and catch-up recommendations."9vHPV: 6,11,16,18,31,33,45,52,58; <15y: 2 Doses (0,6-12m); ≥15y: 3 Doses (0,2,6m); Catch-Up F≤26, M≤21 (MSM≤26); HIV/Immunocomp: 3 Doses Any Age
Viva"25-year-old woman with HPV 16 positive, cytology normal. Next step?"HPV 16 Positive → Immediate Colposcopy Referral (Per HPV Primary Screening Algorithm: HPV 16/18 Positive → Colposcopy Regardless of Cytology)
Ward Round"25-year-old woman, CIN2 on biopsy. Management?"Excision (LLETZ/LEEP) Preferred (Diagnostic & Therapeutic); Test of Cure at 6 Months (HPV Test); Contraception Until Healed; Follow-Up: HPV Test at 6 Months, then Routine Recall if Negative
Last-Night"HPV: HR 16,18,31,33,45,52,58 (Cancer); LR 6,11 (Warts). E6→p53, E7→pRb. Vaccine 9vHPV: 2-dose<15y, 3-dose≥15y, Catch-Up F≤26/M≤21(MSM≤26). Screen: HPV Primary→-ve=Recall; 16/18+=Colpo; Other HR→Cyto→Abnormal=Colpo, -ve=Repeat 12m. CIN1=Observe; CIN2/3=Excise (LLETZ). TOC 6m HPV. Warts: Podophyllotoxin, Imiquimod, Cryo, TCA, Surgical. Preg: Cryo/TCA/Surg. Anal: Annual MSM/HIV. Oropharyngeal: 16 Dominant, No Screen."Compressed.

10. 📌 Summary

  • HPV Types: High-Risk (16, 18, 31, 33, 45, 52, 58) = Cancer; Low-Risk (6, 11) = Warts, RRP
  • Natural History: 90% Clear in 1-2 Years; Persistence → CIN2/3 → Cancer (10-20+ Years); E6→p53 Degradation, E7→pRb Degradation
  • Vaccination: 9vHPV (6,11,16,18,31,33,45,52,58); <15y: 2 Doses (0, 6-12m); ≥15y: 3 Doses (0, 2, 6m); Catch-Up: F≤26, M≤21 (MSM/Immunocomp ≤26); HIV: 3 Doses Any Age
  • Screening: HPV Primary → Negative = Routine Recall; HPV 16/18 Positive = Colposcopy; Other HR HPV + Cytology → Abnormal = Colposcopy, Normal = Repeat 12 Months
  • CIN Management: CIN1 = Observe (12-Month HPV Test); CIN2 = Excision (LLETZ) Preferred; CIN3 = Excision Mandatory; Test of Cure at 6 Months (HPV Test)
  • Anogenital Warts: HPV 6, 11; Patient-Applied (Podophyllotoxin, Imiquimod); Provider (Cryotherapy, TCA, Surgical); Pregnancy: Cryo/TCA/Surgical Only
  • Non-Cervical Cancers: Anal (90% HPV, Annual Screen MSM/HIV), Oropharyngeal (70% HPV+, 16 Dominant, No Screen), Vulvar/Vaginal/Penile (40-70%)
  • Special Populations: HIV/Immunocompromised = 3-Dose Vaccine, Annual Screen, More Extensive Disease; Pregnancy = Defer Vaccine, Cryo/TCA/Surgical for Warts Only

11. ❓ MCQs (10)

  1. HPV types most commonly associated with cervical cancer?

A. 6, 11 B. 16, 18 C. 31, 33 D. 45, 52

Answer: B. HPV 16, 18 = ~70% of Cervical Cancer.

  1. 9-valent HPV vaccine — which additional types beyond quadrivalent?

A. 6, 11, 16, 18 B. 31, 33, 45, 52, 58 C. 26, 53, 66 D. 40, 42, 43

Answer: B. 9vHPV Adds 31, 33, 45, 52, 58 to 6, 11, 16, 18.

  1. HPV vaccination schedule for a 14-year-old girl?

A. 3 Doses (0, 1, 6 Months) B. 2 Doses (0, 6-12 Months) C. Single Dose D. 3 Doses (0, 2, 6 Months)

Answer: B. <15 Years = 2 Doses (0, 6-12 Months).

  1. Cervical screening — HPV primary algorithm: HPV 16 positive, cytology normal. Next step?

A. Repeat in 12 Months B. Immediate Colposcopy Referral C. Reflex Cytology C. Routine Recall

Answer: B. HPV 16/18 Positive → Immediate Colposcopy Referral (Regardless of Cytology).

  1. CIN1 management?

A. Immediate Excision B. Observation with 12-Month HPV Test C. Ablation D. Cone Biopsy

Answer: B. CIN1 = Observe (90% Regress); 12-Month HPV Test.

  1. CIN3 management?

A. Observation B. Excision Mandatory (LLETZ/Cone Biopsy) C. Ablation Only D. Hysterectomy

Answer: B. CIN3 = High-Grade, Excision Mandatory (LLETZ or Cone Biopsy).

  1. Anogenital warts — causative HPV types?

A. 16, 18 B. 6, 11 C. 31, 33 D. 45, 52

Answer: B. HPV 6, 11 = ~90% of Genital Warts.

  1. Anogenital warts in pregnancy — preferred treatment?

A. Podophyllotoxin B. Cryotherapy / TCA / Surgical C. Imiquimod D. Podophyllin Resin

Answer: B. Cryotherapy, TCA, Surgical Only (Avoid Podophyllotoxin/Imiquimod in Pregnancy).

  1. Anal cancer screening — recommended for?

A. All Adults B. MSM and HIV-Positive Individuals (Annual Cytology/HPV) C. Women Only D. Not Recommended

Answer: B. MSM and HIV-Positive → Annual Anal Cytology/HPV Testing.

  1. Oropharyngeal cancer — dominant HPV type?

A. HPV 18 B. HPV 16 C. HPV 6 D. HPV 11

Answer: B. HPV 16 = Dominant in HPV+ Oropharyngeal Cancer (~90% of HPV+).

  1. HPV vaccine in pregnancy?

A. Safe, Recommended B. Defer Until Postpartum (Not Licensed in Pregnancy) C. Only 2nd Trimester D. Only 3rd Trimester

Answer: B. Defer Until Postpartum (Not Licensed in Pregnancy; Safety Data Reassuring But Not Licensed).

12. 📋 SBAs (10)

  1. 25-year-old woman, HPV 16 positive, cytology normal. Next step?

A. Repeat in 12 Months B. Immediate Colposcopy Referral C. Reflex Cytology D. Routine Recall

Answer: B. HPV 16/18 Positive → Immediate Colposcopy (Algorithm).

  1. 20-year-old woman, CIN2 on biopsy. Management?

A. Observation B. Excision (LLETZ/LEEP) Preferred C. Ablation D. Hysterectomy

Answer: B. CIN2 = Excision (LLETZ) Preferred.

  1. 14-year-old girl, HPV vaccination schedule?

A. 3 Doses (0, 1, 6 Months) B. 2 Doses (0, 6-12 Months) C. Single Dose D. 3 Doses (0, 2, 6 Months)

Answer: B. <15 Years = 2 Doses (0, 6-12 Months).

  1. Pregnant woman with extensive vulval warts. Treatment?

A. Podophyllotoxin B. Cryotherapy / TCA / Surgical C. Imiquimod D. Podophyllin Resin

Answer: B. Cryotherapy / TCA / Surgical Only (Avoid Podophyllotoxin/Imiquimod).

  1. MSM, HIV-positive, age 30. Anal screening recommendation?

A. Not Recommended B. Annual Anal Cytology + HPV Testing C. Every 3 Years D. Every 5 Years

Answer: B. Annual Anal Cytology + HPV Testing (MSM/HIV).

13. 🔑 Answer Keys

MCQsSBAs
1-B, 2-B, 3-C, 4-B, 5-B, 6-B, 7-B, 8-B, 9-B, 10-B1-B, 2-B, 3-B, 4-B, 5-B

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 15.3.03 3 4 Hepatitis B C

Parent Topic: STI MOC → STI Hierarchy

Status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

Priority: ⭐⭐ HIGH (FCPS/MRCP — Sexual Transmission, Chronic Disease, Vaccination, DAA Therapy, Co-infection with HIV)

Source: Davidson 24th Ed Ch 13; WHO/EASL/AASLD; FCPS/MRCP Syllabus; NICE NG224

1. 🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Differentiate Hepatitis B vs C sexual transmission, natural history, and management
  • [ ] Apply Hepatitis B vaccination strategies (Universal, Risk-Based, Post-Exposure)
  • [ ] Interpret Hepatitis B serology (HBsAg, Anti-HBs, Anti-HBc IgM/IgG, HBeAg, Anti-HBe, HBV DNA)
  • [ ] Stage Hepatitis B (Acute, Chronic Inactive, Immune Active, HBeAg-Negative Chronic Hepatitis)
  • [ ] Interpret Hepatitis C serology (Anti-HCV, HCV RNA, Genotype)
  • [ ] Apply HCV Direct-Acting Antiviral (DAA) therapy (Pan-Genotypic, Duration, SVR)
  • [ ] Manage HBV/HCV in HIV Co-infection and Pregnancy
  • [ ] Apply Post-Exposure Prophylaxis (PEP) for HBV/HCV
  • [ ] Answer viva: "HBV Serology Interpretation" and "HCV DAA Regimens" and "HBV in Pregnancy" and "PEP for Needlestick"

2. 🧠 Core Concept: HBV vs HCV — Sexual Transmission Context

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Viral Hepatitis — Sexual Transmission
↓
& C
Hepatitis C (HCV)
↓
DNA Virus, Partially dsDNA
Highly Infectious Sexually
Vaccine Preventable
Chronic Carriage → Cirrhosis/HCC
Effective Suppression (NUCs)
Shared Risks
RNA Virus, Flavivirus
Less Infectious Sexually
No Vaccine
Curable with DAAs (SVR >95%)
No Chronic Carriage if Cured
↓
Multiple Partners, MSM, Sex Workers, HIV Co-infection
Needle Sharing, Perinatal, Occupational

1️⃣ Hepatitis B (HBV)

Epidemiology & Sexual Transmission

ParameterDetail
Global Prevalence296 Million Chronic (2019); 1.5 Million New Infections/Year
Sexual Transmission EfficiencyHigh (100x HIV, 10x HCV); MSM, Multiple Partners, Sex Workers = High Risk
Other RoutesPerinatal (Vertical), Percutaneous (Needles, Tattoo, medical), Horizontal (Household)
Incubation60-150 Days (Average 90 Days)

HBV Serology — Interpretation Masterclass

MarkerSignificanceInterpretation
HBsAgSurface AntigenCurrent Infection (Acute or Chronic); Positive >6 Months = Chronic
Anti-HBsSurface AntibodyImmunity (Vaccination or Resolved Infection); >10 mIU/mL = Protected
Anti-HBc TotalCore Antibody (IgG+IgM)Past or Current Infection (Never Positive from Vaccine Alone)
Anti-HBc IgMCore IgM AntibodyAcute/Recent Infection (<6 Months); Window Period Marker
HBeAge AntigenHigh Replicative State, High Infectivity
Anti-HBee AntibodyLower Replication, Seroconversion (Usually Good Prognosis)
HBV DNAViral LoadQuantitative Replication; Guides Treatment, Monitors Response

HBV Serology Interpretation Matrix

Clinical ScenarioHBsAgAnti-HBsAnti-HBc TotalAnti-HBc IgMHBeAgAnti-HBeHBV DNA
Susceptible–––––––
Immune (Vaccinated)–+–––––
Resolved Infection–++––+–
Acute HBV+–+++–High
Chronic Inactive Carrier+–+––+Low/Undetectable
Chronic Active (HBeAg +ve)+–+–+–High
HBeAg-Negative Chronic Hepatitis+–+––+Int/High
Window Period––++––Detectable
Isolated Anti-HBc––+–––Usually –
Key: Anti-HBc = Past/Current Infection (Never from Vaccine); Anti-HBs = Immunity (Vaccine or Resolved); HBsAg >6 Months = Chronic

2️⃣ Hepatitis B — Staging & Treatment Indications

Chronic HBV Staging (EASL/AASLD)

PhaseHBsAgHBeAgHBV DNAALTHistologyTreatment Indication
Immune Tolerant++Very High (>10⁷ IU/mL)NormalMinimalNo (Observe)
Immune Active (HBeAg +ve)++High (>10⁴ IU/mL)ElevatedInflammation/FibrosisYes
Inactive Carrier+–Low (<2,000 IU/mL)NormalMinimalNo (Monitor)
HBeAg-Negative Chronic Hepatitis+–>2,000 IU/mLElevatedInflammation/FibrosisYes
Reactivated+VariableRisingRisingWorseningYes (Urgent)
Treatment Goal: HBsAg Loss (Functional Cure); HBeAg Seroconversion; HBV DNA Suppression (<20 IU/mL); ALT Normalisation

3️⃣ Hepatitis B — Treatment (Nucleos(t)ide Analogues)

First-Line NUCs (Potent, High Barrier to Resistance)

DrugDose (Adult)Renal AdjustmentResistance BarrierKey Features
Tenofovir Alafenamide (TAF)25mg ODeGFR ≥15HighestPreferred (Bone/Renal Safety), Pregnancy Safe
Tenofovir Disoproxil Fumarate (TDF)245mg (300mg) ODeGFR ≥30HighestAlternative (Renal/Bone Toxicity)
Entecavir (ETV)0.5mg OD (1mg if NAIVE)eGFR <50: Dose AdjustHighAlternative (Resistance in NAIVE Low)
Preferred: TAF > TDF > ETV; Avoid Lamivudine/Adefovir/Telbivudine (Low Barrier, High Resistance)

Treatment Duration & Stopping Rules

ScenarioDurationStopping Criteria
HBeAg +ve Chronic HepatitisUntil HBeAg Seroconversion + 12 Months ConsolidationHBeAg Seroconversion + Anti-HBe + HBV DNA Undetectable × 12 Months
HBeAg -ve Chronic HepatitisLong-Term (Indefinite)HBsAg Loss (Rare); Consider Stop if HBsAg Loss + 12 Months
CirrhosisLifelongDo Not Stop (Risk of Decompensation)
Pregnancy (TDF/TAF/ETV)Throughout Pregnancy + 3-12 Months PostpartumPrevent Vertical Transmission

3️⃣ Hepatitis B — Special Situations

HBV in Pregnancy

AspectManagement
ScreeningUniversal HBsAg at Booking (All Pregnant Women)
High Viral Load (>200,000 IU/mL)TAF/TDF/ETV from 28-32 Weeks → 3-12 Months Postpartum (Prevent Vertical Transmission)
HBsAg +ve MotherInfant: HBIG (100 IU IM) + HBV Vaccine (0, 1, 6 Months) Within 24 Hours of Birth
BreastfeedingSafe (If Infant Received HBIG + Vaccine)
Antivirals in PregnancyTAF/TDF/ETV Safe (Preferred: TAF/TDF); Start 28-32 Weeks if HBV DNA >200,000 IU/mL

HBV/HCV Co-infection with HIV

AspectManagement
ART RegimenTDF/TAF + 3TC/FTC (Backbone Covers HBV)
HBV Mono-infected on NUCsContinue NUCs (Do Not Stop — Risk of Flare)
MonitoringHBV DNA, ALT q3-6 Months; HBsAg q6-12 Months
Hepatitis FlareART Initiation (IRIS) or NUC Stoppage — Manage Expectantly/Restart NUC

3️⃣ Hepatitis B — Vaccination & PEP

Hepatitis B Vaccination

PopulationScheduleIndication
Universal (Infants)Birth, 1, 6 Months (or 6, 10, 14 Weeks EPI)Universal
Adults Risk-Based0, 1, 6 Months (or Accelerated 0, 1, 2, 12 Months)MSM, Sex Workers, PWID, HIV, CKD, Prisoners, Healthcare Workers, Partners of HBsAg+
Non-Responders (Anti-HBs <10 mIU/mL)Repeat Series (Double Dose/Accelerated) → Check Anti-HBs 1-2m PostConsider Twinrix (A+B) if Also Hep A Indicated

Hepatitis B Post-Exposure Prophylaxis (PEP)

Exposure TypeSource HBsAgExposed StatusPEP Regimen
Percutaneous/Mucosal/Sexual+veUnvaccinated / Non-ResponderHBIG (0.06 mL/kg IM) + HBV Vaccine (0, 1, 6 Months) — Within 24h (Ideally), Up to 7 Days
+veVaccinated, Known Responder (Anti-HBs >10)No PEP Needed
+veVaccinated, Unknown ResponseTest Anti-HBs: If >10 → No PEP; If <10 → HBIG + Booster
Unknown/-veUnvaccinatedHBV Vaccine Series (0, 1, 6 Months)
Sexual ExposureHBsAg +ve PartnerSusceptible PartnerHBIG + Vaccine (Within 14 Days, Ideally <24h)

4️⃣ Hepatitis C (HCV)

Epidemiology & Sexual Transmission

ParameterDetail
Global Prevalence58 Million Chronic (2019); 1.5 Million New Infections/Year
Sexual Transmission EfficiencyLow (Overall); Higher in MSM (HIV+, Chemsex, Rough Sex, Mucosal Trauma)
Primary RoutePercutaneous (PWID, Unsafe Medical, Transfusion Pre-1992)
Incubation2 Weeks – 6 Months (Average 6-9 Weeks)

Natural History

StageProbabilityTimeline
Acute HCV15-25% Symptomatic2-26 Weeks
Spontaneous Clearance15-45% (Higher in Women, Young, IL28B CC)6 Months
Chronic HCV55-85%>6 Months
Cirrhosis15-30% over 20-30 YearsAccelerated by Alcohol, HIV, Age, Male, Obesity
HCC1-4%/Year (Cirrhosis)Surveillance Required

4️⃣ Hepatitis C — Diagnosis & Staging

Diagnostic Algorithm

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Suspected HCV
↓
Anti-HCV (EIA/CIA)
↓
No HCV Infection (If Recent Exposure → HCV RNA)
HCV RNA (Quantitative PCR)
↓
Resolved Infection (Past Exposure, Spontaneous Clearance)
Chronic HCV Infection
↓
Genotype/Subtype (1-6)
HCV RNA Quantification (Baseline)
Fibrosis Assessment (FibroScan, APRI, FIB-4, Biopsy)

HCV Serology & RNA Interpretation

ScenarioAnti-HCVHCV RNAInterpretation
Never Exposed––Susceptible
Acute HCV+ (Seroconversion)+Acute Infection
Chronic HCV++Chronic Infection
Resolved (Spontaneous/Treated)+–Past Infection, Cured
False Positive Anti-HCV+–Low Prevalence, Confirm RNA

HCV Genotypes & Clinical Relevance

GenotypeGlobal DistributionHistorical DAA RelevanceCurrent Pan-Genotypic DAAs
146% (USA/Europe)Subtype 1a/1b MattersPan-Genotypic (All)
220%Pan-Genotypic (All)
330% (Asia, PWID)Difficult (SVR Lower)Pan-Genotypic (All)
4Egypt, Middle East, AfricaPan-Genotypic (All)
5, 6RarePan-Genotypic (All)
Current Standard: Pan-Genotypic DAAs — Genotype Testing Optional (Still Useful for Epidemiology, Resistance, Regimen Selection in Some Guidelines)

4️⃣ Hepatitis C — Direct-Acting Antiviral (DAA) Therapy

Pan-Genotypic DAA Regimens (Current Standard)

RegimenCompositionDurationGenotypesKey Notes
Sofosbuvir/Velpatasvir (Epclusa)SOF 400mg + VEL 100mg OD12 Weeks1-6 (Pan-Genotypic)First-Line (All); Cirrhosis: Add Ribavirin if Decompensated
Glecaprevir/Pibrentasvir (Maviret)GLE 300mg + PIB 120mg OD (3 Tablets)8 Weeks (Non-Cirrhotic); 12 Weeks (Cirrhotic)1-6 (Pan-Genotypic)Shortest (8w Non-Cirrhotics); Renal Safe (eGFR <30); NOT in Decompensated Cirrhosis
Sofosbuvir/Velpatasvir/Voxilaprevir (Vosevi)SOF 400mg + VEL 100mg + VOX 100mg OD12 Weeks1-6 (Pan-Genotypic)Retreatment (Daa-Experienced)
Preferred First-Line: Sofosbuvir/Velpatasvir 12 Weeks (All Genotypes); Glecaprevir/Pibrentasvir 8 Weeks (Non-Cirrhotic) — Choice by Renal, Cirrhosis, Drug Interactions

DAA Regimen Selection Guide

Patient ProfilePreferred RegimenDuration
Treatment-Naive, Non-CirrhoticGlecaprevir/Pibrentasvir 8 Weeks OR Sofosbuvir/Velpatasvir 12 Weeks8 / 12 Weeks
Treatment-Naive, Compensated CirrhosisSofosbuvir/Velpatasvir 12 Weeks OR Glecaprevir/Pibrentasvir 12 Weeks12 Weeks
Decompensated Cirrhosis (Child-Pugh B/C)Sofosbuvir/Velpatasvir + Ribavirin 12 Weeks12 Weeks
DAA-Experienced (Treatment Failure)Sofosbuvir/Velpatasvir/Voxilaprevir 12 Weeks12 Weeks
Renal Impairment (eGFR <30 / Dialysis)Glecaprevir/Pibrentasvir (Renal Safe)8/12 Weeks
HIV Co-infectionSame as Mono-Infected (Check Drug Interactions with ART)Same Duration
PregnancyDefer DAA Until Postpartum—
Key: Glecaprevir/Pibrentasvir = Renal Safe, Shortest (8w); Sofosbuvir/Velpatasvir = Decompensated Safe (w/ RBV); Vosevi = Retreatment

Treatment Monitoring & SVR

TimepointTestTarget
BaselineHCV RNA, Genotype (Optional), Fibrosis, Renal/Hepatic FunctionBaseline
Week 4 (Optional)HCV RNARapid Virological Response (RVR) = Predictor of SVR
End of Treatment (EOT)HCV RNAUndetectable = EOT Response
12 Weeks Post-Treatment (SVR12)HCV RNASVR12 = Undetectable = CURE (>95%)
24 Weeks Post-Treatment (SVR24)HCV RNASVR24 = Confirmation (Optional)
SVR12 = Cure; Reinfection Possible (Risk Reduction Counselling); HCC Surveillance Continues if Cirrhosis

5️⃣ HCV — Special Situations

HCV in Pregnancy

AspectManagement
ScreeningUniversal Anti-HCV at Booking (Recommended by WHO/CDC)
DAA TherapyContraindicated in Pregnancy (No Safety Data); Defer Until Postpartum
Vertical Transmission~5-6% (Higher if HIV Co-infection, High Viral Load); No Proven Intervention
BreastfeedingSafe (If Nipples Intact); Avoid if Cracked/Bleeding Nipples
Infant TestingHCV RNA at 2-6 Months (Anti-HCV Passive Maternal Transfer); Confirm Chronic at 18 Months

HCV/HIV Co-infection

AspectManagement
DAA TherapySame Regimens/Durations as Mono-Infected (Check Drug Interactions with ART)
Key InteractionsGlecaprevir/Pibrentasvir ↔ Rifampicin (Contraindicated); Sofosbuvir/Velpatasvir ↔ Certain ART (Check Liverpool Database)
ART RegimenIntegrase Inhibitor Based Preferred (DTG, BIC, RAL) — Fewest DAA Interactions
MonitoringSVR12 = Cure; HCV Reinfection Risk Higher (Continue Risk Reduction)

HCV in Renal Impairment

eGFRPreferred Regimen
≥30Any Pan-Genotypic
<30 / DialysisGlecaprevir/Pibrentasvir (Renal Safe — No Dose Adjustment)

5️⃣ HCV Sexual Transmission & Prevention

AspectDetail
Sexual TransmissionLow Overall; Higher: MSM (HIV+, Chemsex, Group Sex, Mucosal Trauma), Multiple Partners, Co-existing STIs
PreventionCondoms (Reduce Risk), Treat HCV (SVR = No Transmission), Harm Reduction (PWID)
PEP for HCVNo Standard PEP (No Proven Post-Exposure Antiviral); Early Detection → Early Treatment
ReinfectionPossible After SVR (Continued Risk Behaviours → Reinfection Rate 1-5%/Year)

3. ⚡ FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
HBV SerologyHBsAg = Current Infection; Anti-HBs = Immunity; Anti-HBc = Past/Current; Anti-HBc IgM = Acute; HBeAg = High Replication; HBV DNA = Quantification
HBV PhasesImmune Tolerant (HBeAg+, High DNA, Normal ALT) → Immune Active (HBeAg+, High DNA, ↑ALT) → Inactive Carrier (HBeAg-, Low DNA, Normal ALT) → HBeAg-Negative CHB (HBeAg-, ↑DNA, ↑ALT)
HBV TreatmentTAF > TDF > ETV (High Barrier); HBeAg+ → Seroconversion + 12m Consolidation; HBeAg- → Long-Term/Indefinite; Cirrhosis = Lifelong
HBV VaccinationBirth, 1, 6m (Infants); Risk Adults: 0,1,6m; PEP: HBIG + Vaccine (≤24h/7d)
HCV Natural History15-45% Spontaneous Clearance; 55-85% Chronic → Cirrhosis (15-30% in 20-30y) → HCC
HCV DiagnosisAnti-HCV + HCV RNA (Quantitative PCR) = Chronic; Genotype Optional (Pan-Genotypic DAAs)
HCV DAA TherapyPan-Genotypic: SOF/VEL 12w, GLE/PIB 8w (Non-Cirrhotics), SOF/VEL/VOX 12w (Retreatment); SVR12 = Cure (>95%)
HCV in PregnancyScreen at Booking; DAA Contraindicated; Defer to Postpartum; Vertical Transmission ~5-6%
HBV/HCV in HIVTDF/TAF+3TC/FTC Covers HBV; HCV DAA Same Regimens (Check Interactions)
PEPHBV: HBIG + Vaccine (≤24h); HCV: No PEP (Early Detection/Treatment)
VaccinationHBV Universal (Birth, 1, 6m); Risk Adults 0,1,6m; No HCV Vaccine

4. 🎤 Viva Questions (Expected Answers)

#QuestionExpected Answer
1HBV serology interpretation — acute vs chronic vs immune?Acute: HBsAg+, Anti-HBc IgM+, HBeAg+; Chronic: HBsAg>6m, Anti-HBc IgG+, HBeAg Variable; Immune (Vaccine): Anti-HBs>10, Anti-HBc-; Resolved: Anti-HBs+, Anti-HBc+
2HBV treatment indications by phase?Immune Active (HBeAg+, ↑ALT, High DNA) → Treat; HBeAg-Negative CHB (HBeAg-, ↑ALT, DNA>2000) → Treat; Immune Tolerant/Inactive Carrier → Monitor
3First-line HBV antivirals and why?TAF > TDF > ETV (High Barrier, Potent); TAF Preferred (Renal/Bone Safety); Avoid Lamivudine/Adefovir/Telbivudine (Low Barrier)
4HBV in pregnancy — vertical transmission prevention?Infant: HBIG + HBV Vaccine (Within 24h); Mother: TAF/TDF/ETV from 28-32w if HBV DNA >200,000 IU/mL
5. HCV diagnosis algorithm?Anti-HCV + → HCV RNA (Quantitative); RNA + = Chronic; RNA – = Resolved; Genotype Optional (Pan-Genotypic DAAs)
6. Pan-genotypic DAA regimens — first-line?SOF/VEL 12w (All); GLE/PIB 8w (Non-Cirrhotic) / 12w (Cirrhotic) — Renal Safe; SOF/VEL/VOX 12w (Retreatment)
7. HCV in pregnancy — management?Screen at Booking; DAA Contraindicated → Defer Postpartum; Vertical Transmission ~5-6%; Breastfeeding Safe (If Nipples Intact); Infant HCV RNA at 2-6m
8. HBV/HCV PEP for needlestick injury?HBV: HBIG + Vaccine (Within 24h/7d) if Source +ve & Exposed Non-Responder; HCV: No PEP (Early RNA Testing, Treat if Positive)
9. HCV DAA treatment — SVR definition?SVR12 = HCV RNA Undetectable at 12 Weeks Post-Treatment = CURE (>95%)
10. HBV/HCV in HIV co-infection — key points?HBV: TDF/TAF+3TC/FTC in ART (Covers HBV); Continue NUCs if Already On; HCV: Same DAA Regimens (Check Interactions); SVR= Cure; Reinfection Risk Higher

5. 🧩 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
"HBV and HCV both have vaccines"NO. HBV = Vaccine Available; HCV = NO Vaccine
"HBeAg +ve = Always Treat"NO. Immune Tolerant (HBeAg+, High DNA, Normal ALT) = Monitor, NOT Treat
"HCV Genotype Matters for Current DAAs"NO. Pan-Genotypic DAAs (GLE/PIB, SOF/VEL) Cover All Genotypes 1-6; Genotyping Optional
"HCV Vaccine Exists"NO. NO HCV Vaccine (High Genetic Diversity); Only HBV Vaccine
"HCV Spontaneous Clearance = Immunity"NO. Reinfection Possible (No Protective Immunity); Risk Reduction Still Needed After SVR
"HBV Vaccine = Lifelong Immunity"Mostly Yes; Booster Not Routine; Anti-HBs >10 = Protected; Non-Responders: Repeat Series
"HCV PEP = Give DAAs"NO. No HCV PEP; Early Detection (RNA at 4-12 Weeks) → Early Treatment
"All Chronic HBV Need Treatment"NO. Immune Tolerant & Inactive Carrier = Monitor Only
"HCV Genotype 3 = Hardest to Treat"Historical; Current Pan-Genotypic DAAs (GLE/PIB, SOF/VEL) Achieve >95% SVR in GT3
"HBV = Sexual, HCV = Blood Only"NO. HBV = Highly Sexual (100x HIV); HCV = Low Sexual Overall, BUT Higher in MSM (HIV+, Chemsex, Trauma)
Mnemonic: HEPATITIS B vs C MASTER
HBV Serology: HBsAg (Current), Anti-HBs (Immune), Anti-HBc (Past/Current), Anti-HBc IgM (Acute), HBeAg (Replication), DNA (Quant)
Epidemiology: HBV 296M, HCV 58M Chronic
Phases HBV: Immune Tolerant (No Tx) → Immune Active (Tx) → Inactive (Monitor) → HBeAg-Neg CHB (Tx)
Antivirals HBV: TAF > TDF > ETV (High Barrier); Avoid LAM/ADV/TBV (Low Barrier)
Treatment Indication: Immune Active (HBeAg+, ↑ALT) + HBeAg-Neg CHB (↑ALT, DNA>2000) = Tx
In Pregnancy: HBV → HBIG + Vaccine (Infant) + TAF/TDF/ETV (Mother if DNA>200k); HCV → Screen, Defer DAA
Treatment HBV: HBeAg+ → Seroconversion +12m; HBeAg- → Long-Term/Indefinite; Cirrhosis = Lifelong
Immunity: Anti-HBs >10 = Protected; Non-Responder → Repeat Series; PEP = HBIG + Vaccine (24h/7d)
Sexual Transmission: HBV = High (100x HIV); HCV = Low (Except MSM HIV+/Chemsex)
CHC: Anti-HCV + RNA = Chronic; Genotype Optional (Pan-Genotypic); SVR12 = Cure
DAAs: SOF/VEL 12w (All); GLE/PIB 8w (Non-Cirrhotics)/12w (Cirrhotics); SOF/VEL/VOX 12w (Retreatment)
Renal: GLE/PIB Renal Safe (eGFR<30); SOF/VEL Dose Adjust
HCV in Pregnancy: Screen Booking; DAA Contraindicated → Defer; Vertical Trans ~5-6%; Breastfeeding Safe
In HIV: HBV → TDF/TAF+3TC/FTC in ART; HCV → Same DAA Regimens (Check Interactions)
SVR: SVR12 = Undetectable RNA 12w Post-Tx = CURE (>95%)
PEP: HBV: HBIG + Vaccine (24h/7d); HCV: No PEP (Early RNA → Treat)
Vaccines: HBV = Yes (Universal Birth, Risk Adults); HCV = NO Vaccine
Interaction Check: GLE/PIB ↔ Rifampicin (Contra); SOF/VEL ↔ ART (Check Liverpool DB)
Reinfection: HCV Reinfection Possible After SVR (Risk Reduction Essential)

6. 🗺️ Mind Map

Hepatitis B & C
HBV
Serology: HBsAg, Anti-HBs, Anti-HBc, HBeAg, DNA
Phases: Immune Tolerant → Immune Active → Inactive → HBeAg-Neg CHB
Tx: TAF > TDF > ETV
Indications: Immune Active, HBeAg-Neg CHB
Pregnancy: Infant HBIG+Vaccine; Mom TAF/TDF/ETV if DNA>200k
PEP: HBIG + Vaccine (24h/7d)
Vaccine: Universal Birth, Risk Adults
HCV
Natural History: 15-45% Clearance, 55-85% Chronic
Diagnosis: Anti-HCV + RNA = Chronic
Pan-Genotypic DAAs:
– SOF/VEL 12w (All)
– GLE/PIB 8w (Non-Cirrhotic) / 12w (Cirrhotic)
– SOF/VEL/VOX 12w (Retreatment)
SVR12 = Cure (>95%)
Pregnancy: Screen, Defer DAA, Vertical ~5%
Sexual: Low Overall, Higher MSM HIV+
HIV Co-infection: Same DAAs, Check Interactions
Renal: GLE/PIB Safe (eGFR<30)
Sexual Transmission
HBV: High (100x HIV)
HCV: Low Overall, ↑ MSM HIV+/Chemsex
PEP
HBV: HBIG + Vaccine (24h/7d)
HCV: No PEP (Early RNA → Treat)
HIV Co-infection
HBV: TDF/TAF+3TC/FTC in ART
HCV: Same DAAs, Check Interactions

9. 💬 Exam Answer Modes

FormatPromptKey Points
Long Essay"Compare and contrast Hepatitis B and C in terms of sexual transmission, diagnosis, treatment, and prevention."HBV: Highly Sexual, DNA Virus, Vaccine, NUCs (TAF>TDF>ETV), Phases, Chronicity; HCV: Low Sexual (↑ MSM), RNA Virus, No Vaccine, Curable (DAAs >95% SVR), Pan-Genotypic, PEP Differences
Short Note"HBV Serology Interpretation in a Patient with Acute Hepatitis."HBsAg+, Anti-HBc IgM+, HBeAg+, High HBV DNA, IgM Anti-HBc = Acute; Anti-HBs -, Anti-HBe -; HBV DNA High
Viva"Needlestick injury from HBsAg+ source. Exposed HCW unvaccinated. PEP?"HBIG 0.06 mL/kg IM + HBV Vaccine (0, 1, 6 Months) Within 24h (Ideally), Up to 7 Days; Test Anti-HBs at 1-2 Months Post-Series
Ward Round"30M, MSM, HIV+, HCV RNA positive, Genotype 1a, non-cirrhotic. DAA regimen?"Glecaprevir/Pibrentasvir 8 Weeks (Preferred — Renal Safe, Shortest) OR Sofosbuvir/Velpatasvir 12 Weeks; Check Drug Interactions with ART (DTG/BIC Preferred); SVR12 = Cure
Last-Night"HBV Serology: HBsAg=Current, Anti-HBs=Immune, Anti-HBc=Past/Current, IgM=Acute, HBeAg=Replication. Phases: Immune Tol (No Tx), Immune Active (Tx), Inactive (Monitor), HBeAg-Neg CHB (Tx). Tx: TAF>TDF>ETV. HCV: Anti-HCV+RNA=Chronic. Pan-Genotypic DAAs: SOF/VEL 12w, GLE/PIB 8w (Non-Cirr), SOF/VEL/VOX 12w (Retreat). SVR12=Cure. HBV PEP: HBIG+Vacc(24h). HCV: No PEP. Preg: HBV Infant HBIG+Vacc+Mom TAF if DNA>200k; HCV Screen, Defer DAA."Compressed.

10. 📌 Summary

  • HBV Serology: HBsAg = Current Infection; Anti-HBs = Immunity; Anti-HBc = Past/Current; Anti-HBc IgM = Acute; HBeAg = High Replication; HBV DNA = Quantification
  • HBV Phases: Immune Tolerant (HBeAg+, High DNA, Normal ALT) → NO TREATMENT; Immune Active (HBeAg+, ↑ALT, High DNA) → TREAT; Inactive Carrier (HBeAg-, Low DNA, Normal ALT) → MONITOR; HBeAg-Negative CHB (HBeAg-, ↑DNA >2000, ↑ALT) → TREAT
  • HBV Treatment: TAF > TDF > ETV (High Barrier); HBeAg+ → Seroconversion + 12m Consolidation; HBeAg- → Long-Term/Indefinite; Cirrhosis = LIFELONG
  • HBV Vaccination: Universal (Birth, 1, 6m); Risk Adults (0,1,6m); PEP: HBIG + Vaccine (≤24h/7d)
  • HCV Natural History: 15-45% Spontaneous Clearance; 55-85% Chronic → Cirrhosis (15-30% in 20-30y) → HCC
  • HCV Diagnosis: Anti-HCV + HCV RNA (Positive) = Chronic Infection; Genotype Optional (Pan-Genotypic DAAs)
  • HCV DAA Therapy: Pan-Genotypic: SOF/VEL 12w (All); GLE/PIB 8w (Non-Cirrhotic) / 12w (Cirrhotic); SOF/VEL/VOX 12w (Retreatment); SVR12 = CURE (>95%)
  • HCV in Pregnancy: Screen at Booking; DAA Contraindicated → Defer Postpartum; Vertical Transmission ~5-6%; Breastfeeding Safe (If Nipples Intact)
  • HBV/HCV in HIV: HBV: TDF/TAF+3TC/FTC in ART (Covers HBV); Continue NUCs; HCV: Same DAA Regimens (Check Drug Interactions)
  • PEP: HBV: HBIG + Vaccine (Within 24h, Up to 7 Days); HCV: No PEP (Early RNA Testing → Early Treatment)
  • Sexual Transmission: HBV = Highly Efficient (100x HIV); HCV = Low Overall, Higher in MSM (HIV+, Chemsex, Trauma)

11. ❓ MCQs (10)

  1. HBV serology — marker of acute infection?

A. HBsAg B. Anti-HBc IgM C. Anti-HBs D. HBeAg

Answer: B. Anti-HBc IgM = Acute/Recent Infection.

  1. HBV phase — immune tolerant phase characteristics?

A. HBeAg-, Low DNA, Normal ALT B. HBeAg+, High DNA, Normal ALT C. HBeAg-, High DNA, Elevated ALT D. HBsAg-, Anti-HBs+

Answer: B. Immune Tolerant = HBeAg+, High DNA (>10⁷), Normal ALT, No Treatment.

  1. First-line HBV antiviral — preferred agent?

A. Lamivudine B. Tenofovir Alafenamide (TAF) C. Adefovir D. Telbivudine

Answer: B. TAF > TDF > ETV (Highest Barrier, Renal/Bone Safety).

  1. HCV diagnosis — confirmatory test for chronic infection?

A. Anti-HCV B. HCV RNA (Quantitative PCR) C. Anti-HCV IgM D. Liver Biopsy

Answer: B. HCV RNA (Quantitative PCR) Positive = Chronic Infection.

  1. Pan-genotypic DAA regimen for treatment-naive non-cirrhotic?

A. SOF/VEL 8 Weeks B. GLE/PIB 8 Weeks (Preferred) C. SOF/VEL/VOX 12 Weeks D. SOF/VEL 24 Weeks

Answer: B. GLE/PIB 8 Weeks (Shortest, Renal Safe, Pan-Genotypic).

  1. HCV SVR definition?

A. RNA Negative at End of Treatment B. RNA Negative at 12 Weeks Post-Treatment (SVR12) C. RNA Negative at 24 Weeks D. Antibody Negative

Answer: B. SVR12 = HCV RNA Undetectable 12 Weeks Post-Treatment = Cure (>95%).

  1. HCV in pregnancy — DAA therapy?

A. Safe, Recommended B. Contraindicated → Defer Until Postpartum C. Only 2nd Trimester D. Only Genotype 1

Answer: B. DAA Contraindicated in Pregnancy → Defer Until Postpartum.

  1. HBV in pregnancy — infant prophylaxis?

A. HBV Vaccine Only B. HBIG (100 IU) + HBV Vaccine Within 24 Hours of Birth C. HBIG Only D. No Prophylaxis Needed

Answer: B. HBIG + HBV Vaccine Within 24 Hours of Birth (Standard of Care).

  1. HBV/HCV PEP for needlestick from HBsAg+ source — unvaccinated HCW?

A. HBV Vaccine Only B. HBIG (0.06 mL/kg IM) + HBV Vaccine (0, 1, 6 Months) Within 24h C. HBIG Only D. No PEP Needed

Answer: B. HBIG + HBV Vaccine Series (Within 24h Ideally, Up to 7 Days).

  1. Pan-genotypic DAA for treatment-experienced (retreatment)?

A. SOF/VEL 12 Weeks B. GLE/PIB 8 Weeks C. SOF/VEL/VOX 12 Weeks D. SOF/VEL 24 Weeks

Answer: C. SOF/VEL/VOX (Vosevi) 12 Weeks = Retreatment Regimen.

12. 📋 SBAs (10)

  1. 30M, HBsAg+, HBeAg+, HBV DNA 10⁸ IU/mL, ALT normal. Phase & management?

A. Immune Active, Treat B. Immune Tolerant, Monitor (No Treatment) C. Inactive Carrier, Monitor D. HBeAg-Neg CHB, Treat

Answer: B. Immune Tolerant (HBeAg+, High DNA, Normal ALT) → No Treatment.

  1. HCV treatment-naive, non-cirrhotic, any genotype. Preferred regimen?

A. SOF/VEL 12 Weeks B. GLE/PIB 8 Weeks C. SOF/VEL/VOX 12 Weeks D. SOF/VEL 24 Weeks

Answer: B. GLE/PIB 8 Weeks (Shortest, Renal Safe, Pan-Genotypic).

  1. Pregnant woman at 28 weeks, HBsAg+, HBV DNA 500,000 IU/mL. Management?

A. No Antiviral B. TAF/TDF/ETV from 28-32 Weeks → 3-12 Months Postpartum C. Cesarean Section D. HBIG Only

Answer: B. TAF/TDF/ETV from 28-32 Weeks (If DNA >200,000) → 3-12 Months Postpartum.

  1. HCV treatment-experienced (failed SOF/VEL), compensated cirrhosis. Retreatment?

A. SOF/VEL 24 Weeks B. SOF/VEL/VOX 12 Weeks (Vosevi) C. GLE/PIB 12 Weeks D. Interferon + Ribavirin

Answer: B. SOF/VEL/VOX (Vosevi) 12 Weeks = Standard Retreatment Regimen.

  1. Needlestick injury from HBsAg+ source. HCW unvaccinated. PEP?

A. HBV Vaccine Only B. HBIG + HBV Vaccine (0, 1, 6 Months) Within 24h C. HBIG Only D. No PEP

Answer: B. HBIG + HBV Vaccine Series (Within 24h Ideally, Up to 7 Days).

13. 🔑 Answer Keys

MCQsSBAs
1-B, 2-B, 3-B, 4-B, 5-B, 6-B, 7-B, 8-B, 9-B, 10-B1-B, 2-B, 3-B, 4-B, 5-B

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 15.3.04 3 5 Mpox

Parent Topic: STI MOC → STI Hierarchy

Status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

Priority: ⭐⭐⭐ HIGHEST (FCPS/MRCP — 2022 Global Outbreak, Sexual Transmission, Emerging STI, Vaccination)

Source: Davidson 24th Ed Ch 13; WHO/CDC/UKHSA; FCPS/MRCP Syllabus; NEJM/Lancet 2022-2024

1. 🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Understand Mpox virology (Orthopoxvirus, Clades Ia, Ib, II) and transmission dynamics
  • [ ] Recognise clinical presentations (Classic vs 2022 Outbreak: Genital/Perianal Predominant, Mucosal Lesions)
  • [ ] Apply diagnostic algorithms (PCR, Lesion Swab, Serology) and interpret results
  • [ ] Apply management strategies (Supportive, Tecovirimat, Vaccination, Infection Control)
  • [ ] Apply vaccination strategies (Pre-Exposure, Post-Exposure, Priority Groups)
  • [ ] Manage Mpox in special populations (HIV, Immunocompromised, Pregnancy, Children)
  • [ ] Apply infection prevention & control (Isolation, Contact Tracing, PPE)
  • [ ] Answer viva: "Mpox Clades" and "2022 Outbreak Clinical Features" and "Tecovirimat Indications" and "Vaccination Strategy"

2. 🧠 Core Concept: Mpox Virology & Transmission

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Mpox Virus (MPXV)
↓
Orthopoxvirus, dsDNA
↓
Clades
Transmission
Incubation: 5-21 Days (Median 6-13 Days)
↓
**Clade I (Congo Basin)** — Higher Severity, CFR ~10%
**Clade II (West African)** — Lower Severity, CFR ~1-3%
Close Contact (Skin-to-Skin, Lesion Fluid)
Respiratory Droplets (Prolonged Face-to-Face)
Fomites (Bedding, Towels, Sex Toys)
**Sexual Contact** (Genital/Perianal Mucosa — **2022 Outbreak Driver**)
Vertical (Mother→Fetus), Animal→Human (Zoonotic)
↓
**Clade IIa** — Endemic West Africa
**Clade IIb** — **2022 Global Outbreak** (B.1 Lineage)

1️⃣ Mpox Clades — Epidemiology & Severity

CladeGeographic OriginCase Fatality Rate (CFR)Clinical Severity2022 Outbreak
Clade I (Congo Basin)Central Africa (DRC)~10% (Up to 15% in Children)Severe (More Complications, Sepsis, Encephalitis)Not in 2022 Global Outbreak
Clade IIa (West African Endemic)West Africa~1-3%ModerateEndemic, Not 2022 Outbreak
Clade IIb (B.1 Lineage)Global (2022 Outbreak)<1% (0.1-0.3% in Non-Endemic)Milder (Less Systemic, Genital/Perianal Predominant)2022-2023 Global Outbreak (MSM Networks)
Key: 2022 Outbreak = Clade IIb (B.1 Lineage); >95% Cases in MSM; Genital/Perianal Lesions Predominant; Lower CFR

2️⃣ Clinical Presentation — 2022 Outbreak vs Classic

Classic Mpox (Clade I/IIa Endemic)

FeatureDetail
Prodrome (1-5 Days)Fever, Headache, Myalgia, Backache, Marked Lymphadenopathy (Generalised)
RashCentrifugal (Face → Extremities → Palms/Soles), Synchronous (Same Stage)
Lesion EvolutionMacule → Papule → Vesicle → Pustule → Crust → Scab (2-4 Weeks)
DistributionFace (95%), Palms/Soles (75%), Oral Mucosa (70%), Genital (30%)
ComplicationsSecondary Infection, Sepsis, Encephalitis, Corneal Scarring, Pneumonia

2022 Outbreak (Clade IIb) — Atypical Presentation

FeatureDetail
ProdromeOften Absent or Mild (Fever in ~50%, Lymphadenopathy Localised)
RashAnogenital Predominant (Genital, Perianal, Perioral), Often Single/Scattered Lesions
Lesion EvolutionAsynchronous (Different Stages Simultaneously), Pseudopustules (Umbilicated)
DistributionGenital (60-70%), Perianal (40-50%), Perioral/Oral (20-30%), Face/Extremities (<20%)
Mucosal LesionsProctitis (Rectal Pain, Bleeding, Tenesmus), Pharyngitis, Urethritis, Conjunctivitis
Systemic SymptomsMilder (Fever <50%, Lymphadenopathy Localised to Draining Nodes)
Atypical PresentationsSingle Genital Lesion (Mimics HSV/Syphilis), Tonsillitis, Proctitis Only, Penile Swelling
Key: 2022 Outbreak = Genital/Perianal Predominant, Often Mild Prodrome, Asynchronous Lesions, MSM Networks

3️⃣ Diagnosis — Algorithms & Interpretation

Case Definition (WHO/ICMR)

CategoryCriteria
SuspectedAcute Rash (Acute Onset) + Epidemiological Link (Contact/Travel) + No Other Explanation
ProbableSuspected + Orthopoxvirus Positive (PCR Non-Variola) OR Epidemiological Link + Compatible Rash
ConfirmedMPXV DNA Positive by PCR (Lesion Swab, Crust, Blood)

Diagnostic Algorithm

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Suspected Mpox
↓
Lesions Present?
↓
Lesion Swab (Vesicle/Pustule/Crust) → MPXV PCR (Orthopoxvirus/MPXV Specific)
Serology (IgM/IgG) Not for Acute Diagnosis
↓
PCR Result
↓
Confirmed Mpox → Type Clade (IIb vs I) if Available
Consider Alternative (HSV, Syphilis, VZV, Molluscum, Chancroid)
↓
Isolate, Contact Trace, Notify

Diagnostic Tests

TestSampleTimingUtility
MPXV PCR (Real-Time)Lesion Swab (Vesicle/Pustule Fluid, Crust, Scab)Acute (Day 1-21)Gold Standard (Type-Specific, Clade Differentiation)
Orthopoxvirus PCRLesion SwabAcuteScreening (If MPXV-Specific Unavailable)
Viral CultureLesion FluidAcuteReference Lab Only (BSL-3)
Serology (IgM/IgG)BloodConvalescent (Day 7-14+)Retrospective, Surveillance; Cross-Reactive with Vaccinia/Smallpox Vaccine
Electron MicroscopyLesion FluidAcuteResearch/Reference (Brick-Shaped Virions)
Key: Lesion Swab PCR = Gold Standard; Swab Base of Lesion (Unroof Vesicle/Pustule); Multiple Sites if Atypical

4️⃣ Management — Supportive & Antiviral

General Principles

PrincipleDetail
IsolationUntil All Lesions Crusted, Scabs Fallen Off, Fresh Skin Formed (~2-4 Weeks)
Supportive CareAnalgesia (Topical Lidocaine, Oral NSAIDs/Opioids), Hydration, Wound Care (Antiseptic, Prevent Secondary Infection), Antipyretics
Psychosocial SupportStigma Reduction, Mental Health, Sexual Health Counselling

Antiviral Therapy — Tecovirimat (First-Line)

IndicationDetail
Severe DiseaseExtensive Lesions (>100), Sepsis, Encephalitis, Ocular Involvement, Immunocompromised
High-Risk GroupsHIV (CD4<200), Uncontrolled HIV, Immunosuppression, Pregnancy, Children <8y, Atopic Dermatitis/Eczema, Comorbidities
ComplicationsProctitis (Severe Pain/Rectal Bleeding), Pharyngitis (Dysphagia), Urethritis (Retention), Secondary Bacterial Infection
DrugDose (Adult)DurationRouteKey Notes
Tecovirimat (TPOXX)600mg (3×200mg) BD14 DaysOral (Preferred)With High-Fat Meal (↑ Absorption); IV Formulation Available
Tecovirimat IV200mg q12h14 DaysIVIf Oral Not Tolerated/Malabsorption
Cidofovir5mg/kg IV Weekly2-3 DosesIVNephrotoxic (Probenecid + Hydration); Second-Line
Brincidofovir200mg Weekly × 3OralOralGI Toxicity; Second-Line
Tecovirimat Mechanism: VP37 Envelope Protein Inhibitor → Blocks Viral Egress → Orthopoxvirus Specific

Tecovirimat — Monitoring & Resistance

MonitoringFrequency
Liver Function (ALT/AST)Baseline, Weekly
Renal FunctionBaseline, Weekly (If IV)
Clinical ResponseDaily (Lesion Progression, Pain, Systemic Signs)
ResistanceVP37 Mutations (Emerging) — Monitor if Clinical Failure

5️⃣ Vaccination — Pre-Exposure (PrEP) & Post-Exposure (PEP)

Vaccines Available

VaccineTypeDosesKey Features
MVA-BN (JYNNEOS / IMVANEX / IMVAMUNE)Modified Vaccinia Ankara (Non-Replicating)2 Doses (28 Days Apart)Preferred (Safe in Immunocompromised, HIV, Pregnancy); No Visible "Take"
ACAM2000Live Replicating Vaccinia1 Dose (Percutaneous)Contraindicated in Immunocompromised, HIV, Pregnancy, Eczema; "Take" (Pustule) Forms
LC16m8Attenuated Vaccinia (LC16)1 DoseJapan, Limited Global Use
MVA-BN = Preferred for All Groups (Including Immunocompromised, HIV, Pregnancy)

Pre-Exposure Prophylaxis (PrEP) — Priority Groups

PriorityGroups
HighestMSM (High Risk: Multiple Partners, PrEP Users, Recent STIs), Sex Workers, Lab Workers (Orthopoxvirus), Healthcare Workers (High-Risk Exposure)
HighImmunocompromised (HIV CD4<200), Close Contacts of Cases
ConsiderHealthcare Workers (ID/EM/ Sexual Health), Travelers to Endemic Areas

Post-Exposure Prophylaxis (PEP)

TimingAction
≤4 Days Post-ExposureVaccine (MVA-BN) — Ideally Within 4 Days, Up to 14 Days (May Modify Disease)
>4 to ≤14 DaysVaccine (May Reduce Severity)
Co-AdministrationVaccine + Tecovirimat (If High-Risk Exposure + High-Risk Individual)
Exposure RiskPEP Recommendation
High (Unprotected Sex with Case, Household Contact, Needlestick)MVA-BN 2 Doses (Day 0, Day 28) + Tecovirimat (If High-Risk Individual)
Intermediate (Protected Sex with Condom Break, Brief Contact)MVA-BN 2 Doses (Day 0, Day 28)
Low (Brief Casual Contact, PPE Used)Monitor (No PEP)
PEP Window: Ideally ≤4 Days (Up to 14 Days for Vaccine); Tecovirimat Added for High-Risk Individuals (HIV CD4<200, Immunocompromised, Pregnancy, Severe Disease Risk)

6️⃣ Mpox in Special Populations

HIV Co-infection

AspectManagement
SeverityCD4<200: Severe, Prolonged, Disseminated, Higher Mortality
Antiretroviral TherapyContinue/Initiate ART (Immune Reconstitution Critical)
AntiviralTecovirimat Strongly Indicated (CD4<200); Drug Interactions (Tecovirimat ↔ ART — Check Liverpool Database)
ART & TecovirimatTecovirimat ↔ CYP3A4 Inducers/Inhibitors (Rifampicin, Efavirenz, Boosted PIs) — Dose Adjust/Monitor
VaccinationMVA-BN Safe (2 Doses) — Prioritise

Pregnancy & Breastfeeding

AspectManagement
SeverityPotentially Severe (Vertical Transmission Risk, Fetal Loss)
Vertical TransmissionPossible (Placental, Perinatal, Postnatal) — Fetal Loss, Preterm, Neonatal Mpox
AntiviralTecovirimat Safe (Animal Data) — Indicated if Severe/High-Risk
VaccinationMVA-BN Safe (Non-Replicating) — Can Be Given in Pregnancy
DeliveryCesarean if Active Genital Lesions (Reduce Neonatal Exposure); Vaginal if No Lesions
BreastfeedingAvoid if Active Lesions on Breast; Express/Discard Milk if Lesions Elsewhere

Children

AspectManagement
SeverityHigher CFR (Clade I), Complications (Encephalitis, Sepsis)
AntiviralTecovirimat Weight-Based Dosing (Oral Suspension Available)
VaccinationMVA-BN (Off-Label <18y, Use with Caution); ACAM2000 Contraindicated

6️⃣ Infection Prevention & Control (IPC)

Isolation Precautions

SettingPrecautions
HospitalContact + Droplet Precautions (Private Room, Negative Pressure if Aerosol-Generating Procedures)
CommunitySelf-Isolation at Home (Separate Room, Own Bathroom, No Visitors, Separate Utensils/Bedding)
DurationUntil All Lesions Crusted, Scabs Fallen Off, Fresh Skin Formed (~2-4 Weeks)

Contact Tracing & Monitoring

Contact CategoryMonitoringPEP
High-Risk (Sexual, Household, Unprotected Contact)21-Day Monitoring (Daily Symptom Check)MVA-BN PEP (≤4 Days Ideal, Up to 14 Days)
Intermediate (Healthcare with PPE Breach, Brief Contact)21-Day Self-MonitoringVaccine if High-Risk Individual
Low (Casual, PPE Intact)Self-Monitoring 21 DaysNone

Environmental Decontamination

SurfaceAgent
Non-Porous0.1% Sodium Hypochlorite (Bleach), 70% Ethanol, EPA-Registered Virucides
Porous (Bedding, Clothing)Hot Wash (≥60°C) + Detergent, Steam Cleaning
WasteCategory B Infectious Waste (Double Bag, Incinerate/Autoclave)

6️⃣ Complications & Sequelae

ComplicationFrequencyManagement
Secondary Bacterial InfectionCommonTopical/Systemic Antibiotics (Based on Culture)
Proctitis20-30% (MSM)Tecovirimat, Analgesia, Stool Softeners, Topical Steroids (If Inflammatory)
Pharyngitis/Esophagitis10-20%Analgesia, Hydration, Tecovirimat if Severe
Urethritis5-10%Analgesia, Catheterisation if Retention
Ocular MpoxRareOphthalmology Referral, Tecovirimat, Topical Antivirals (Trifluridine)
EncephalitisRare (<1%)ICU, Tecovirimat IV, Supportive
SepsisRareICU, Broad-Spectrum Antibiotics, Tecovirimat
Scarring/PigmentationCommonReassurance, Sunscreen, Dermatology Referral

3. ⚡ FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
CladesClade I (Congo) = Severe, CFR ~10%; Clade IIb (2022 Outbreak) = Milder, CFR <1%
TransmissionClose Contact (Skin-to-Skin, Sexual), Respiratory, Fomites; 2022 Outbreak = MSM Networks, Sexual Transmission Predominant
Clinical (2022 Outbreak)Genital/Perianal Predominant, Often Mild/No Prodrome, Asynchronous Lesions, Proctitis/Pharyngitis/Urethritis Common
DiagnosisLesion Swab PCR (Gold Standard); Swab Base of Lesion (Unroof Vesicle/Pustule)
IsolationUntil All Crusted, Scabs Fallen, Fresh Skin (2-4 Weeks)
AntiviralTecovirimat 600mg BD × 14d (Oral) — Severe Disease, High-Risk (HIV CD4<200, Immunocompromised, Pregnancy, Children)
VaccineMVA-BN (JYNNEOS) 2 Doses (28d Apart) — Safe in Immunocompromised, HIV, Pregnancy
PrEPMSM (High Risk), Sex Workers, Lab/HCW
PEPMVA-BN ≤4 Days (Up to 14 Days) + Tecovirimat if High-Risk
HIV Co-infectionCD4<200 = Severe; Tecovirimat Indicated; ART Continue; Drug Interactions
PregnancyMVA-BN Safe; Tecovirimat if Severe; Cesarean if Genital Lesions
IPCContact + Droplet; Isolate Until All Crusted/Scabs Fallen (2-4w); Contact Trace 21d

4. 🎤 Viva Questions (Expected Answers)

#QuestionExpected Answer
1Mpox clades — which caused 2022 global outbreak?Clade IIb (B.1 Lineage) — West African Clade, Milder, CFR <1%, MSM Sexual Networks
22022 outbreak clinical features vs classic mpox?Genital/Perianal Predominant, Mild/No Prodrome, Asynchronous Lesions, Proctitis/Pharyngitis/Urethritis Common, Often Single/Scattered Lesions
3Mpox diagnosis — gold standard test?Lesion Swab PCR (MPXV Specific) — Swab Base of Vesicle/Pustule/Crust
4Tecovirimat — indications, dose, duration?Severe Disease, High-Risk (HIV CD4<200, Immunocompromised, Pregnancy, Children, Complications); 600mg BD Oral × 14 Days (With Fatty Meal)
5. Mpox vaccination — which vaccine, schedule, priority groups?MVA-BN (JYNNEOS) 2 Doses (28 Days Apart); Priority: MSM (High Risk), Sex Workers, Lab/HCW, Immunocompromised (HIV CD4<200)
6. Mpox PEP — timing and regimen?MVA-BN Ideally Within 4 Days (Up to 14 Days) 2 Doses (Day 0, 28); Add Tecovirimat if High-Risk Individual (HIV CD4<200, Immunocompromised, Pregnancy)
7. Mpox in HIV — severity and management?CD4<200 = Severe, Prolonged, Disseminated; Tecovirimat Indicated; Continue ART; Drug Interactions (Tecovirimat ↔ CYP3A4); MVA-BN Safe (2 Doses)
8. Mpox in pregnancy — management?MVA-BN Safe (Non-Replicating); Tecovirimat if Severe/High-Risk; Cesarean if Active Genital Lesions; Breastfeeding Avoid if Breast Lesions
9. Mpox isolation duration?Until All Lesions Crusted, Scabs Fallen Off, Fresh Skin Formed (2-4 Weeks)
10. Mpox contact tracing — high-risk contacts PEP?MVA-BN 2 Doses (Day 0, 28) Ideally Within 4 Days (Up to 14 Days); Add Tecovirimat if High-Risk Individual

5. 🧩 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
"Mpox = Smallpox"NO. Mpox = Orthopoxvirus (Related to Smallpox/Variola), Lower CFR, Less Transmissible, Different Clades
"Mpox = Only MSM Disease"NO. MSM = 2022 Outbreak Driver (Sexual Networks); Can Spread to Anyone via Close Contact (Household, Healthcare, Community)
"Mpox = Only Genital Lesions"NO. Classic = Face/Extremities/Palms/Soles; 2022 Outbreak = Genital/Perianal Predominant (But Can Be Anywhere)
"Tecovirimat = For All Mpox Cases"NO. Indicated for Severe/High-Risk Only; Mild Cases = Supportive Care Only
"Mpox Vaccine = ACAM2000 (Smallpox Vaccine)"NO. MVA-BN (JYNNEOS) = Preferred (Non-Replicating, Safe in Immunocompromised/Pregnancy); ACAM2000 = Live, Contraindicated in Immunocompromised
"Mpox = Only Spread by Sex"NO. Close Contact (Skin-to-Skin, Household, Fomites, Respiratory Droplets); Sexual = Major Driver in 2022 Outbreak
"Mpox = Only in Africa"NO. 2022 Outbreak = Global (110+ Countries); Endemic in Central/West Africa (Clade I/IIa)
"Mpox = No Vaccine"NO. MVA-BN (JYNNEOS/IMVANEX) = Licensed, 2 Doses (28d Apart), Safe in Immunocompromised/Pregnancy
"Mpox = Always Severe"NO. 2022 Outbreak (Clade IIb) = Milder, CFR <1%; Clade I (Congo) = Severe, CFR ~10%
"Mpox = No Antiviral"NO. Tecovirimat (TPOXX) = Licensed, Oral/IV, 14 Days, VP37 Inhibitor
Mnemonic: MPOX OUTBREAK MASTER
Mpox Clades: I (Congo, Severe, CFR 10%); IIa (West Africa Endemic); IIb (2022 Global, Mild, CFR <1%)
Presentation 2022: Genital/Perianal Predominant, Mild/No Prodrome, Asynchronous Lesions, Proctitis/Pharyngitis/Urethritis
Orthopoxvirus: dsDNA, Brick-Shaped, Related to Smallpox/Vaccinia
X Transmission: Close Contact (Skin, Sexual, Respiratory, Fomites), 2022 = MSM Sexual Networks
Outbreak 2022: Clade IIb (B.1), MSM Networks, >95% MSM, Genital/Perianal Predominant
Biopsy/Diagnosis: Lesion Swab PCR (Gold Standard), Swab Base of Lesion
Reservoir: Rodents (Suspected), Primates (Spillover)
Epidemiology: 2022-23 Global: 87k+ Cases, 110+ Countries, >95% MSM, CFR <1%
Antiviral: Tecovirimat 600mg BD × 14d (Oral, Fatty Meal) — VP37 Inhibitor
Indications Tecovirimat: Severe, High-Risk (HIV CD4<200, Immunocomp, Pregnancy, Children, Complications)
Vaccine: MVA-BN (JYNNEOS) 2 Doses (Day 0, 28), Non-Replicating, Safe in HIV/Pregnancy
ACM2000: Live Replicating, Contraindicated in Immunocompromised/Pregnancy
L PR/PEP: PrEP (MSM High Risk, Lab/HCW); PEP (MVA-BN ≤4d, Up to 14d + Tecovirimat if High-Risk)
Isolation: Contact + Droplet, Until All Crusted/Scabs Fallen/Fresh Skin (2-4w)
Complications: Proctitis, Pharyngitis, Urethritis, Ocular, Encephalitis, Sepsis, Scarring
HIV Co-infection: CD4<200 = Severe; Tecovirimat Indicated; ART Continue; MVA-BN Safe 2 Doses
Pregnancy: MVA-BN Safe; Tecovirimat if Severe; Cesarean if Genital Lesions
Ocular: Ophthalmology Referral, Tecovirimat, Topical Trifluridine
Urethritis/Proctitis: Common in 2022 Outbreak, Tecovirimat + Symptomatic
Tecovirimat Resistance: VP37 Mutations (Emerging), Monitor Clinical Failure
Bleach/Decon: 0.1% Hypochlorite, 70% Ethanol, Hot Wash (≥60°C)

6. 🗺️ Mind Map

Mpox
Clades
I: Congo, Severe, CFR 10%
IIa: West Africa Endemic
IIb: 2022 Global, Mild, CFR <1%
Transmission
Close Contact (Skin, Sexual, Respiratory, Fomites)
2022: MSM Sexual Networks
Clinical
Classic: Centrifugal, Sync, Prodrome
2022 Outbreak: Genital/Perianal Predominant, Mild/No Prodrome, Async, Proctitis/Pharyngitis
Diagnosis
Lesion Swab PCR (Gold Standard)
Swab Base of Lesion
Treatment
Supportive Analgesia, Hydration, Wound Care
Tecovirimat 600mg BD x14d (Severe/High-Risk)
Cidofovir/Brincidofovir (2nd Line)
Vaccine
MVA-BN (JYNNEOS) 2 Doses (28d)
Safe in HIV, Pregnancy, Immunocompromised
ACAM2000: Live, Contraindicated Immunocomp
PrEP/PEP
PrEP: MSM High Risk, Sex Workers, Lab/HCW
PEP: MVA-BN ≤4d (Up to 14d) + Tecovirimat if High-Risk
Special Pops
HIV: CD4<200 Severe, Tecovirimat, ART Continue, MVA-BN Safe
Pregnancy: MVA-BN Safe, Tecovirimat if Severe, Cesarean if Lesions
Children: Higher Severity, Tecovirimat Weight-Based

7. 📅 Spaced Repetition Tracker

ReviewDateScore (0–5)Notes
Day 1
Day 3
Day 7
Day 14
Day 30
Day 90

8. 📝 Self-Test Scorecard

SectionMaxScore%
Virology, Clades, Epidemiology3
Clinical Presentation (Classic vs 2022)3
Diagnosis & Testing2

9. 💬 Exam Answer Modes

FormatPromptKey Points
Long Essay"Describe the clinical features, diagnosis, and management of Mpox with reference to the 2022 global outbreak."Clades (I/IIa/IIb), 2022 Outbreak (Clade IIb, MSM, Genital/Perianal, Mild Prodrome, Async Lesions), Diagnosis (Lesion PCR), Management (Supportive, Tecovirimat for Severe/High-Risk), Vaccination (MVA-BN 2 Doses, PrEP/PEP), IPC (Isolation, Contact Tracing), Special Pops (HIV, Pregnancy)
Short Note"Tecovirimat — indications, dosage, and mechanism of action."VP37 Envelope Protein Inhibitor → Blocks Viral Egress; Indications: Severe Disease, High-Risk (HIV CD4<200, Immunocompromised, Pregnancy, Children, Complications); 600mg BD Oral × 14 Days (With Fatty Meal); IV Available; Resistance: VP37 Mutations
Viva"30-year-old MSM with 5-day history of painful perianal lesions, no fever. PCR positive for Mpox. Management?"Mild Mpox (Clade IIb Typical) → Supportive Care (Analgesia, Hydration, Wound Care, Isolation Until Crusted); Monitor for Complications (Proctitis, Secondary Infection); Contact Tracing (PEP for High-Risk Contacts: MVA-BN ≤4d); Tecovirimat NOT Indicated (Not Severe/High-Risk); HIV Test, STI Screen
Ward Round"HIV+ patient (CD4 150) with Mpox, extensive lesions, rectal pain, bleeding. Management?"Severe Mpox in HIV (CD4<200) = High-Risk → Tecovirimat 600mg BD Oral × 14 Days (With Fatty Meal) + Supportive Care; Continue ART (Check Tecovirimat ↔ ART Interactions — CYP3A4); Monitor Liver/Renal Function; Isolation Until All Crusted; Contact Tracing + PEP for High-Risk Contacts; MVA-BN Vaccination (2 Doses) if Not Vaccinated
Last-Night"Clades: I (Congo, Severe), IIa (Endemic), IIb (2022 Global, Mild). 2022: Genital/Perianal, Mild/No Prodrome, Async, Proctitis. Dx: Lesion PCR. Mgmt: Supportive, Tecovirimat 600mg BD x14d (Severe/High-Risk: HIV CD4<200, Preg, Kids, Comp). Vaccine: MVA-BN 2 doses (28d). PrEP: MSM High Risk. PEP: MVA-BN ≤4d + Tecovirimat if High-Risk. HIV CD4<200: Severe, Tecovirimat. Preg: MVA-BN Safe, Tecovirimat if Severe, Cesarean if Lesions. Isolation: All Crusted/Scabs Fallen (2-4w). IPC: Contact+Droplet, Contact Trace 21d."Compressed.

10. 📌 Summary

  • Clades: I (Congo, Severe, CFR ~10%), IIa (West Africa Endemic), IIb (2022 Global Outbreak, Milder, CFR <1%)
  • 2022 Outbreak Clinical: Genital/Perianal Predominant, Mild/No Prodrome, Asynchronous Lesions, Proctitis/Pharyngitis/Urethritis Common
  • Diagnosis: Lesion Swab PCR (Gold Standard) — Swab Base of Vesicle/Pustule/Crust
  • Management: Supportive (Analgesia, Hydration, Wound Care); Tecovirimat 600mg BD × 14 Days for Severe/High-Risk (HIV CD4<200, Immunocompromised, Pregnancy, Children, Complications)
  • Vaccine: MVA-BN (JYNNEOS) 2 Doses (28 Days Apart) — Safe in Immunocompromised, HIV, Pregnancy
  • PrEP: MSM (High Risk), Sex Workers, Lab/HCW
  • PEP: MVA-BN ≤4 Days (Up to 14 Days) + Tecovirimat if High-Risk Individual
  • HIV Co-infection: CD4<200 = Severe; Tecovirimat Indicated; Continue ART; MVA-BN Safe (2 Doses)
  • Pregnancy: MVA-BN Safe; Tecovirimat if Severe; Cesarean if Active Genital Lesions
  • Isolation: Contact + Droplet Precautions Until All Lesions Crusted, Scabs Fallen Off, Fresh Skin Formed (2-4 Weeks)
  • Contact Tracing: 21-Day Monitoring; High-Risk Contacts → MVA-BN PEP (≤4 Days Ideal, Up to 14 Days) + Tecovirimat if High-Risk
  • Tecovirimat Resistance: VP37 Mutations (Emerging) — Monitor Clinical Failure

11. ❓ MCQs (10)

  1. Mpox clade responsible for 2022 global outbreak?

A. Clade I B. Clade IIa C. Clade IIb (B.1 Lineage) D. Clade III

Answer: C. Clade IIb (B.1 Lineage) — 2022 Global Outbreak.

  1. Classic Mpox (Clade I) clinical presentation?

A. Genital/perianal predominant, mild prodrome B. Centrifugal rash, synchronous lesions, marked lymphadenopathy, systemic prodrome C. Asynchronous lesions, no systemic symptoms D. Single genital lesion only

Answer: B. Centrifugal (Face→Extremities), Synchronous Lesions, Marked Lymphadenopathy, Systemic Prodrome (Fever, Myalgia).

  1. Gold standard diagnostic test for Mpox?

A. Serology (IgM/IgG) B. Lesion Swab PCR (MPXV Specific) C. Viral Culture D. Electron Microscopy

Answer: B. Lesion Swab PCR (MPXV Specific) — Gold Standard.

  1. Tecovirimat — mechanism of action?

A. DNA Polymerase Inhibitor B. VP37 Envelope Protein Inhibitor (Blocks Viral Egress) C. Thymidine Kinase Inhibitor D. Neuraminidase Inhibitor

Answer: B. VP37 Envelope Protein Inhibitor — Blocks Viral Egress (Orthopoxvirus Specific).

  1. Tecovirimat — indications for use?

A. All Mpox Cases B. Severe Disease, High-Risk (HIV CD4<200, Immunocompromised, Pregnancy, Children, Complications) C. Mild Cases Only D. Prophylaxis Only

Answer: B. Severe Disease, High-Risk (HIV CD4<200, Immunocompromised, Pregnancy, Children, Complications).

  1. Mpox vaccine — preferred for immunocompromised and pregnancy?

A. ACAM2000 B. MVA-BN (JYNNEOS/IMVANEX) — Non-Replicating C. LC16m8 D. Dryvax

Answer: B. MVA-BN (JYNNEOS/IMVANEX) — Non-Replicating, Safe in Immunocompromised, HIV, Pregnancy.

  1. Mpox PEP — optimal timing?

A. Within 24 Hours Only B. Ideally ≤4 Days Post-Exposure (Up to 14 Days for Vaccine) C. Within 72 Hours Only D. Up to 21 Days

Answer: B. Ideally ≤4 Days Post-Exposure (Up to 14 Days for Vaccine); Tecovirimat Added if High-Risk.

  1. Mpox isolation duration?

A. 7 Days from Onset B. Until All Lesions Crusted, Scabs Fallen Off, Fresh Skin Formed (2-4 Weeks) C. 14 Days from Onset D. 10 Days from Last Lesion

Answer: B. Until All Lesions Crusted, Scabs Fallen Off, Fresh Skin Formed (2-4 Weeks).

  1. Mpox in HIV — severity threshold for severe disease?

A. CD4 <350 B. CD4 <200 C. CD4 <500 D. Any HIV+

Answer: B. CD4 <200 = Severe, Prolonged, Disseminated, Higher Mortality — Tecovirimat Indicated.

  1. Mpox contact tracing — high-risk contacts PEP?

A. Monitor Only B. MVA-BN 2 Doses (Day 0, 28) Ideally ≤4 Days Post-Exposure + Tecovirimat if High-Risk Individual C. ACAM2000 D. Tecovirimat Only

Answer: B. MVA-BN 2 Doses (Day 0, 28) Ideally Within 4 Days (Up to 14 Days) + Tecovirimat if High-Risk Individual.

12. 📋 SBAs (10)

  1. 30M, MSM, painless perianal ulcer, no fever. Mpox PCR positive. Management?

A. Tecovirimat 600mg BD × 14d B. Supportive Care, Isolation, Contact Tracing (No Tecovirimat — Mild, Not High-Risk) C. Ceftriaxone D. Acyclovir

Answer: B. Mild Mpox (Clade IIb Typical) → Supportive Care Only; Tecovirimat for Severe/High-Risk Only.

  1. HIV+ patient (CD4 150) with Mpox, extensive lesions, rectal bleeding. Management?

A. Supportive Only B. Tecovirimat 600mg BD × 14d + Continue ART (Check Interactions) C. MVA-BN Vaccine Only D. Cidofovir

Answer: B. HIV CD4<200 = High-Risk → Tecovirimat 600mg BD × 14d + Continue ART (Check CYP3A4 Interactions).

  1. Pregnant woman at 28 weeks diagnosed with Mpox, mild disease. Vaccination?

A. ACAM2000 B. MVA-BN (Safe in Pregnancy, Non-Replicating) C. No Vaccine D. Defer Until Postpartum

Answer: B. MVA-BN Safe in Pregnancy (Non-Replicating); ACAM2000 Contraindicated.

  1. Mpox contact — household member, unprotected contact, immunocompetent. PEP?

A. No PEP Needed B. MVA-BN 2 Doses (Day 0, 28) Ideally Within 4 Days C. Tecovirimat Only D. ACAM2000

Answer: B. MVA-BN 2 Doses (Day 0, 28) Ideally Within 4 Days (Up to 14 Days).

  1. Mpox isolation — when can patient be released?

A. 7 Days After Onset B. All Lesions Crusted, Scabs Fallen Off, Fresh Skin Formed C. PCR Negative D. 14 Days After Onset

Answer: B. Until All Lesions Crusted, Scabs Fallen Off, Fresh Skin Formed (2-4 Weeks).

13. 🔑 Answer Keys

MCQsSBAs
1-C, 2-B, 3-B, 4-B, 5-B, 6-B, 7-B, 8-B, 9-B, 10-B1-B, 2-B, 3-B, 4-B, 5-B
