▸ 15.3.05 3 6 Other Viral Stis

Parent Topic: STI MOC → STI Hierarchy

Status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

Priority: ⭐⭐ HIGH (FCPS/MRCP — Emerging Viral STIs, Vertical Transmission, Co-infection)

Source: Davidson 24th Ed Ch 13; WHO/CDC; FCPS/MRCP Syllabus

1. 🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Recognise Cytomegalovirus (CMV) sexual transmission, congenital infection, and immunocompromised disease
  • [ ] Recognise Epstein-Barr Virus (EBV) sexual transmission, IM, and oncogenic potential
  • [ ] Recognise Zika Virus sexual transmission, congenital syndrome, and Guillain-Barré
  • [ ] Recognise Human Herpesvirus 6/7 (HHV-6/7) roseola and reactivation
  • [ ] Recognise Human T-lymphotropic Virus (HTLV-1/2) sexual transmission, ATL, HAM/TSP
  • [ ] Recognise Other emerging viral STIs (HEV, SARS-CoV-2, Monkeypox — see Mpox)
  • [ ] Apply diagnostic algorithms and management for each
  • [ ] Answer viva: "CMV in Pregnancy" and "Zika Congenital Syndrome" and "HTLV-1 ATL vs HAM/TSP"

2. 🧠 Core Concept: Other Viral STIs Spectrum

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Other Viral STIs
↓
Herpesviridae
Retroviridae
Flaviviridae
Hepeviridae
Coronaviridae
↓
CMV (HHV-5)
EBV (HHV-4)
HHV-6/HHV-7
HTLV-1/2
Zika Virus
Hepatitis C (See 3.4)
Dengue (Rare Sexual)
Hepatitis E (Rare Sexual)
SARS-CoV-2 (Rare Sexual)

1️⃣ Cytomegalovirus (CMV) — HHV-5

Epidemiology & Transmission

AspectDetail
Seroprevalence40-100% (Age/Geography Dependent)
TransmissionSexual (Semen, Cervical Secretions), Vertical (Mother→Fetus), Blood/Transplant, Breast Milk, Close Contact
Sexual TransmissionEfficient (Semen Shedding Common); MSM High Risk
ReactivationLatency in Myeloid Cells; Immunosuppression → Reactivation

Clinical Manifestations

PopulationSyndromeFeatures
ImmunocompetentMononucleosis-like (Fever, Pharyngitis, Lymphadenopathy, Hepatitis)Heterophile Negative (vs EBV), Self-Limited (2-6 Weeks)
Immunocompromised (HIV CD4<50, Transplant)CMV Disease (Retinitis, Colitis, Pneumonitis, Encephalitis, Polyradiculopathy)Sight/Life-Threatening; Ganciclovir/Valganciclovir/Foscarnet
Congenital CMV (Primary Maternal Infection)Symptomatic (10-15%): Petechiae, Hepatosplenomegaly, Jaundice, Sensorineural Hearing Loss, Microcephaly, Seizures, ChorioretinitisLeading Infectious Cause of Hearing Loss; Asymptomatic (85-90%) → Late-Onset Hearing Loss
Perinatal CMVUsually Asymptomatic; Premature/Immunocompromised → Pneumonitis, HepatitisBreast Milk Transmission (Freeze/HTST Pasteurisation Reduces Risk)

Diagnosis

TestSampleUtility
CMV PCR (Quantitative)Blood, Urine, CSF, Amniotic Fluid, TissueGold Standard (Viral Load, Diagnosis, Monitoring)
CMV IgM/IgGSerumIgM = Acute/Recent; IgG = Past (Low Specificity for Reactivation)
CMV Antigenemia (pp65)BloodRapid, Quantitative (Transplant Monitoring)
Viral CultureUrine, Saliva, BloodSlow (Weeks), Low Sensitivity
HistopathologyTissueOwl's Eye Inclusion Bodies (Pathognomonic)

Management

ScenarioTreatment
Immunocompetent MononucleosisSupportive (Antipyretics, Hydration, Avoid Contact Sports if Splenomegaly)
Immunocompromised (Retinitis, Colitis, Pneumonitis)IV Ganciclovir 5mg/kg q12h × 2-3 Weeks → Oral Valganciclovir 900mg BD × 8 Weeks (Maintenance)
Resistant CMVFoscarnet 60mg/kg IV q8h OR Cidofovir 5mg/kg IV Weekly
Congenital CMV (Symptomatic)Valganciclovir 16mg/kg BD × 6 Months (Improves Hearing/Developmental Outcomes)
Prevention (Transplant)Prophylaxis (Valganciclovir 900mg OD × 3-6 Months) OR Pre-emptive (PCR-Guided)

2️⃣ Epstein-Barr Virus (EBV) — HHV-4

Epidemiology & Transmission

AspectDetail
Seroprevalence>90% Adults
TransmissionSaliva (Kissing, "Kissing Disease"), Sexual (Semen, Cervical Secretions), Blood/Transplant
LatencyB-Cells (Latency Programs I-III); Reactivation in Immunosuppression

Clinical Manifestations

SyndromeFeatures
Infectious Mononucleosis (IM)Fever, Pharyngitis (Exudative), Lymphadenopathy (Posterior Cervical), Splenomegaly (50%), Hepatitis, Atypical Lymphocytosis (>10%)
Chronic Active EBV (CAEBV)Persistent IM >6 Months, High EBV DNA, Hepatosplenomegaly, Cytopenias, Organ Infiltration
EBV-Associated MalignanciesBurkitt Lymphoma (Endemic, t(8;14)), Nasopharyngeal Carcinoma (EBV+, EBV LMP1), Hodgkin Lymphoma (RS Cells EBV+), Post-Transplant Lymphoproliferative Disorder (PTLD), Gastric Carcinoma (10%)

Diagnosis

TestSampleUtility
Heterophile Antibody (Monospot)SerumRapid, IM Screening (Sensitivity 70-90%, Specificity 90%)
EBV-Specific SerologySerumVCA IgM (Acute), VCA IgG (Past), EBNA IgG (Past/Convalescent), EA-D IgG (Acute/Reactivation)
EBV PCRBlood, CSF, TissueQuantitative Viral Load (PTLD, CAEBV, Immunocompromised Monitoring)

Management

ScenarioTreatment
IMSupportive (Antipyretics, Hydration, Corticosteroids if Airway Obstruction/Severe Thrombocytopenia), Avoid Contact Sports 4-6 Weeks (Splenic Rupture Risk)
CAEBV / PTLDRituximab (Anti-CD20), Chemotherapy, Antivirals (Acyclovir/Valacyclovir/Ganciclovir — Limited Efficacy)
MalignanciesStandard Oncology Protocols (R-CHOP, Radiotherapy, etc.)

3️⃣ Zika Virus — Flaviviridae

Epidemiology & Transmission

AspectDetail
VectorAedes aegypti/albopictus (Day-Biting)
Sexual TransmissionConfirmed (Semen, Vaginal Secretions); Semen Shedding Up to 6 Months
Vertical TransmissionTransplacental — Congenital Zika Syndrome
OtherBlood Transfusion, Laboratory Exposure

Clinical Manifestations

SyndromeFeatures
Acute ZikaMild (Fever, Maculopapular Rash, Arthralgia, Conjunctivitis, Myalgia, Headache) — Self-Limited (2-7 Days)
Guillain-Barré Syndrome (GBS)Post-Infectious (1-3 Weeks), Acute Flaccid Paralysis, Areflexia, Albuminocytological Dissociation
Congenital Zika Syndrome (CZS)Microcephaly, Cerebral Calcifications, Ventriculomegaly, Arthrogryposis, Ocular Abnormalities, Hypertonia, Hearing Loss

Diagnosis

TestSampleTiming
Zika PCR (NAAT)Blood, Urine, Semen, CSF, Amniotic FluidAcute (Blood: Days 1-7, Urine: Days 1-14, Semen: Months)
Zika IgM/IgGSerumConvalescent (Week 2+); Cross-Reactivity with Dengue/Other Flaviviruses
PRNTSerumConfirmatory (Specific Neutralising Antibodies)

Management

ScenarioTreatment
Acute ZikaSupportive (Antipyretics, Hydration, Avoid NSAIDs if Dengue Possible)
GBSIVIG 2g/kg over 5 Days OR Plasma Exchange; ICU Support
Congenital Zika SyndromeMultidisciplinary (Neurology, Ophthalmology, Orthopaedics, Early Intervention); No Specific Antiviral
PregnancyAvoid Travel to Endemic Areas; Condoms/Abstinence if Partner Exposed; Serial Ultrasound (Microcephaly, Calcifications)

Zika Sexual Transmission Prevention

RecommendationDuration
Men (Symptomatic/Exposed)Condoms/Abstinence × 3 Months (Semen Shedding Up to 6 Months)
Women (Symptomatic/Exposed)Condoms/Abstinence × 2 Months
PreconceptionWait 3 Months (Men) / 2 Months (Women) After Exposure/Symptoms

4️⃣ Human T-lymphotropic Virus (HTLV-1/2) — Retroviridae

Epidemiology & Transmission

AspectHTLV-1HTLV-2
Endemic AreasJapan, Caribbean, South America, Africa, Middle East, MelanesiaAmericas (Native Americans, PWID)
TransmissionSexual, Vertical (Breast Milk), Blood/Transplant, Needle SharingSimilar
Seroprevalence10-20 MillionLower

Clinical Manifestations

SyndromeHTLV-1HTLV-2
Adult T-Cell Leukemia/Lymphoma (ATL)Aggressive T-Cell Malignancy (Leukemic, Lymphoma, Chronic, Smoldering)Rare/Not Associated
HTLV-1-Associated Myelopathy/Tropical Spastic Paraparesis (HAM/TSP)Chronic Progressive Spastic Paraparesis, Sensory Loss, Urinary Dysfunction, Erectile DysfunctionMilder/Similar (HAM/TSP-Like)
OtherUveitis, Infective Dermatitis, Bronchiectasis, Sjögren-LikeMild Neurological, Cutaneous

Diagnosis

TestUtility
HTLV-1/2 Antibody (EIA/ELISA)Screening
Western Blot / Line ImmunoassayConfirmatory (Type-Specific)
HTLV PCR (Proviral DNA)Quantitative Proviral Load (Risk Stratification for ATL/HAM)

Management

SyndromeTreatment
ATLLG15/SG/CHOP/EMC (Chemotherapy), Allogeneic SCT (Younger), Zidovudine + IFN-α (Induction)
HAM/TSPCorticosteroids (Pulse), Danazol, IFN-α, Mogamulizumab (Anti-CCR4) — Symptomatic (Baclofen, Gabapentin, Physiotherapy)
PreventionScreen Blood/Organ Donors, Avoid Breastfeeding (HTLV-1+ Mothers), Condoms, Safe Needles

5️⃣ Other Emerging Viral STIs

Hepatitis E (HEV) — Rare Sexual Transmission

AspectDetail
TransmissionFaecal-Oral (Water, Undercooked Meat), Zoonotic (Pigs), Blood, Vertical; Rare Sexual (Semen Detected)
ClinicalAcute Hepatitis (Self-Limited); Chronic in Immunocompromised (GT3/4)
PregnancyGT1: High Mortality (20-30%) in 3rd Trimester
TreatmentRibavirin (Chronic), Pegylated IFN-α; Supportive (Acute)

SARS-CoV-2 — Rare Sexual Transmission

AspectDetail
TransmissionRespiratory (Primary); Semen/Faeces RNA Detected — Sexual Transmission Theoretical/Rare
ClinicalCOVID-19 (Respiratory, Systemic, Long COVID)
Sexual PrecautionsCondoms/Masks if Partner Infected/Exposed

Human Herpesvirus 6/7 (HHV-6/7)

VirusSyndrome
HHV-6A/6BRoseola Infantum (Exanthem Subitum) — High Fever 3-5 Days → Defervescence → Maculopapular Rash; Reactivation: Encephalitis, Bone Marrow Suppression (Transplant)
HHV-7Roseola (Similar), Pityriasis Rosea (Associated), Reactivation in Immunocompromised

3. ⚡ FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

VirusKey Points
CMVSexual (Semen), Vertical, Transplant; Congenital = Leading Cause Hearing Loss; Immunocompromised = Retinitis/Colitis/Pneumonitis (Ganciclovir/Valganciclovir); Congenital Symptomatic = Valganciclovir × 6 Months
EBVSaliva (Kissing), Sexual; IM (Fever, Pharyngitis, Lymphadenopathy, Splenomegaly, Atypical Lymphs); Heterophile +ve, VCA IgM/IgG, EBNA; Malignancies: Burkitt, NPC, Hodgkin, PTLD
ZikaAedes Vector, Sexual (Semen 6 Months), Vertical → CZS (Microcephaly, Calcifications, Arthrogryposis, Ocular); GBS Post-Infectious; Pregnancy: Avoid Travel, Condoms 3m (M) / 2m (F)
HTLV-1Endemic Japan/Caribbean/SA/Africa; Sexual, Vertical (Breast Milk), Blood; ATL (Aggressive T-Cell Lymphoma), HAM/TSP (Spastic Paraparesis); Screen Blood/Donors, Avoid Breastfeeding
HTLV-2Americas, PWID; No ATL, Milder HAM/TSP
HEVFaecal-Oral, Zoonotic (Pigs), Rare Sexual; Chronic in Immunocomp (Ribavirin); Pregnancy GT1 = High Mortality
HHV-6/7HHV-6B = Roseola (High Fever → Rash); Reactivation Encephalitis (Transplant)

4. 🎤 Viva Questions (Expected Answers)

#QuestionExpected Answer
1CMV in pregnancy — congenital infection risk and management?Primary Infection = 30-40% Transmission; Symptomatic 10-15% (Hearing Loss, Microcephaly, Cholestasis, Thrombocytopenia); Symptomatic Neonate → Valganciclovir 16mg/kg BD × 6 Months (Improves Hearing/Development)
2EBV infectious mononucleosis — diagnosis and management?Clinical (Fever, Pharyngitis, Lymphadenopathy, Splenomegaly, Atypical Lymphs); Heterophile (Monospot) +ve; VCA IgM +ve, EBNA -ve = Acute; Supportive, Avoid Contact Sports 4-6w (Splenic Rupture)
3Zika virus — sexual transmission prevention?Men: Condoms/Abstinence × 3 Months (Semen Shedding Up to 6 Months); Women: × 2 Months; Preconception: Wait 3m (M) / 2m (F) After Exposure/Symptoms
4HTLV-1 — associated diseases and prevention?ATL (Aggressive T-Cell Leukemia), HAM/TSP (Spastic Paraparesis), Uveitis, Infective Dermatitis; Prevention: Screen Blood/Organ Donors, Avoid Breastfeeding (HTLV-1+ Mothers), Condoms, Safe Needles
5. CMV in immunocompromised — treatment?Ganciclovir 5mg/kg IV q12h → Valganciclovir 900mg BD (Maintenance); Resistant → Foscarnet/Cidofovir; Prophylaxis vs Pre-emptive (Transplant)
6. Zika congenital syndrome — features?Microcephaly, Cerebral Calcifications, Ventriculomegaly, Arthrogryposis, Ocular Abnormalities, Hypertonia, Hearing Loss
7. HTLV-1 ATL vs HAM/TSP — differentiate?ATL = Aggressive T-Cell Malignancy (Leukemic, Lymphoma); HAM/TSP = Chronic Progressive Spastic Paraparesis, Sensory Loss, Urinary Dysfunction
8. EBV malignancies — list?Burkitt Lymphoma (Endemic, t(8;14)), Nasopharyngeal Carcinoma, Hodgkin Lymphoma, PTLD, Gastric Carcinoma (10%)
9. HEV in pregnancy — risk?GT1: High Mortality (20-30%) in 3rd Trimester; Supportive, Ribavirin Contraindicated
10. HHV-6 — reactivation in transplant?Encephalitis, Bone Marrow Suppression, Graft Dysfunction — Ganciclovir/Foscarnet

5. 🧩 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
"CMV = Only Congenital"NO. Immunocompromised = Retinitis, Colitis, Pneumonitis, Encephalitis; Immunocompetent = Mononucleosis-like
"EBV = Only Mono"NO. Malignancies: Burkitt, NPC, Hodgkin, PTLD, Gastric; CAEBV, PTLD
"Zika = Only Vector-Borne"NO. Sexual Transmission Confirmed (Semen Up to 6 Months); Men Condoms 3m, Women 2m
"HTLV-1 = Only ATL"NO. HAM/TSP (Spastic Paraparesis) Also Major; Uveitis, Dermatitis, Bronchiectasis
"HTLV-1 = HTLV-2"NO. HTLV-1 = ATL + HAM/TSP; HTLV-2 = No ATL, Milder HAM/TSP
"HEV = Only Foodborne"NO. Rare Sexual (Semen); Chronic in Immunocomp (Ribavirin); Pregnancy GT1 = High Mortality
"SARS-CoV-2 = Sexual STI"NO. Respiratory Primary; Semen RNA Detected But Sexual Transmission Rare/Theoretical
"HHV-6 = Only Roseola"NO. Reactivation in Transplant = Encephalitis, Bone Marrow Suppression
Mnemonic: OTHER VIRAL STIs MASTER
Other Viral STIs: CMV, EBV, Zika, HTLV-1/2, HEV, HHV-6/7, SARS-CoV-2
Therapy CMV: Ganciclovir/Valganciclovir (1st Line), Foscarnet/Cidofovir (Resistant); Congenital → Valgan × 6m
HSV vs EBV: HSV = Painful Vesicles/Ulcers, Recurrent; EBV = Mono (Fever, Pharyngitis, Splenomegaly, Atypical Lymphs)
EBV Malignancies: Burkitt (t(8;14)), NPC, Hodgkin, PTLD, Gastric
Resistant CMV: Foscarnet, Cidofovir
EBV IM: Heterophile (Monospot), VCA IgM+, EBNA-
Retrovirus HTLV: HTLV-1 = ATL + HAM/TSP; HTLV-2 = No ATL, Milder HAM
Vertical CMV: Leading Cause Hearing Loss; Primary Infection = 30-40% Transmission
Adverse Pregnancy Zika: Microcephaly, Calcifications, Arthrogryposis, Ocular, Hypertonia
Longer Semen Zika: Men 3m Condoms/Abstinence; Women 2m; Preconception Wait 3m/2m
HEV Pregnancy: GT1 = High Mortality (20-30%) 3rd Tri; Ribavirin Contraindicated
Sexual Transmission: CMV (Semen), EBV (Saliva/Semen), Zika (Semen), HTLV (Sexual/Breast Milk/Blood), HEV (Rare Semen)
Transplant CMV: Prophylaxis (Valgan × 3-6m) vs Pre-emptive (PCR-Guided)
EBV Reactivation: PTLD, CAEBV, Immunosuppression
Roseola HHV-6: High Fever 3-5d → Defervescence → Maculopapular Rash
Zika Sexual: Men 3m, Women 2m Condoms/Abstinence; Preconception Wait 3m/2m

6. 🗺️ Mind Map

Other Viral STIs
CMV
Transmission: Sexual, Vertical, Transplant
Congenital: Hearing Loss, Microcephaly, Valganciclovir × 6m
Immunocomp: Retinitis, Colitis, Pneumonitis (Ganciclovir/Valganciclovir)
Mononucleosis-like: Immunocompetent
EBV
Mono: Fever, Pharyngitis, LAD, Splenomegaly, Atypical Lymphs
Heterophile+, VCA IgM+, EBNA-
Malignancies: Burkitt, NPC, Hodgkin, PTLD, Gastric
Zika
Vector: Aedes
Sexual: Semen (6m), Men 3m Condoms, Women 2m
CZS: Microcephaly, Calcifications, Arthrogryposis, Ocular, GBS
Pregnancy: Avoid Travel, Condoms 3m/2m
HTLV-1/2
HTLV-1: ATL, HAM/TSP, Endemic Japan/Caribbean/SA
HTLV-2: No ATL, Milder HAM, Americas/PWID
Prevention: Screen Blood, No Breastfeeding, Condoms
HEV
Faecal-Oral, Zoonotic (Pigs), Rare Sexual
GT1 Pregnancy: High Mortality
Chronic Immunocomp: Ribavirin
HHV-6/7
Roseola: Fever → Defervescence → Rash
Reactivation: Encephalitis (Transplant)
SARS-CoV-2
Respiratory Primary, Rare Sexual

7. 📅 Spaced Repetition Tracker

ReviewDateScore (0–5)Notes
Day 1
Day 3
Day 7
Day 14
Day 30
Day 90

8. 📝 Self-Test Scorecard

SectionMaxScore%
CMV (Congenital, Immunocompromised, Mononucleosis)4
EBV (IM, Malignancies, Diagnosis)3
Zika (CZS, Sexual Transmission, Pregnancy)4
HTLV-1/2 (ATL, HAM/TSP, Prevention)3
HEV, HHV-6/7, SARS-CoV-22
Cross-Cutting (Diagnosis, Management)2
Total20

9. 💬 Exam Answer Modes

FormatPromptKey Points
Long Essay"Describe the sexual transmission, congenital effects, and management of CMV, Zika, and HTLV-1."CMV: Sexual/Vertical/Transplant; Congenital (Hearing Loss, Microcephaly) → Valganciclovir × 6m; Immunocompromised (Ganciclovir/Valgan); Zika: Aedes, Sexual (Semen 6m), CZS (Microcephaly, Calcifications, Arthrogryposis, Ocular), GBS, Pregnancy Avoidance, Condoms 3m/2m; HTLV-1: ATL, HAM/TSP, Sexual/Vertical/Blood, Screen Blood/Donors, No Breastfeeding
Short Note"Zika virus congenital syndrome."Microcephaly, Cerebral Calcifications, Ventriculomegaly, Arthrogryposis, Ocular Abnormalities, Hypertonia, Hearing Loss; Caused by Vertical Transmission (1st/2nd Trimester Highest Risk); No Specific Antiviral; Multidisciplinary Support; Prevention: Avoid Travel, Condoms 3m (M)/2m (F)
Viva"Pregnant woman at 20 weeks returns from Zika-endemic area. Asymptomatic. Management?"Test (PCR Blood/Urine if <7 Days, IgM >7 Days); Serial Ultrasound (Microcephaly, Calcifications, Ventriculomegaly) Every 3-4 Weeks; Counselling on Sexual Transmission (Condoms 3m M / 2m F); If RT-PCR Positive → Serial Fetal Monitoring, Multidisciplinary Counselling
Ward Round"HIV+ patient (CD4 50) with blurred vision, floaters. Fundoscopy: Retinal hemorrhages, exudates. Diagnosis & management?"CMV Retinitis (HIV CD4<50) → IV Ganciclovir 5mg/kg q12h × 2-3 Weeks → Oral Valganciclovir 900mg BD Maintenance; Ophthalmology Referral (Intravitreal Ganciclovir/Foscarnet if Sight-Threatening); ART Optimisation (Immune Reconstitution)
Last-Night"CMV: Congenital (Hearing Loss, Valgan×6m), Immunocomp (Retinitis→Ganc/Valgan). EBV: Mono (Het+, VCA IgM+, EBNA-), Malignancies (Burkitt, NPC, Hodgkin, PTLD). Zika: CZS (Microcephaly, Calcifications, Arthrogryposis, Ocular), GBS, Sexual (Semen 6m, Men 3m/F 2m Condoms). HTLV-1: ATL (Aggressive T-Cell), HAM/TSP (Spastic Paraparesis), Screen Blood/Donors, No Breastfeeding. HEV: GT1 Pregnancy Mortality, Chronic Immunocomp (Ribavirin). HHV-6: Roseola, Reactivation Encephalitis."Compressed.

10. 📌 Summary

  • CMV: Sexual (Semen), Vertical, Transplant; Congenital = Leading Infectious Cause of Hearing Loss → Valganciclovir 16mg/kg BD × 6 Months; Immunocompromised (Retinitis/Colitis/Pneumonitis) → Ganciclovir/Valganciclovir
  • EBV: Saliva (Kissing), Sexual; Infectious Mononucleosis (Fever, Pharyngitis, Lymphadenopathy, Splenomegaly, Atypical Lymphocytes); Heterophile +ve, VCA IgM+; Malignancies: Burkitt, NPC, Hodgkin, PTLD, Gastric
  • Zika: Aedes Vector, Sexual (Semen Up to 6 Months) → Men: Condoms/Abstinence × 3 Months, Women: × 2 Months; Congenital Zika Syndrome (Microcephaly, Calcifications, Arthrogryposis, Ocular, Hypertonia, Hearing Loss); GBS Post-Infectious
  • HTLV-1: Endemic Japan/Caribbean/South America/Africa; Sexual, Vertical (Breast Milk), Blood; ATL (Aggressive T-Cell Lymphoma), HAM/TSP (Spastic Paraparesis); Prevention: Screen Blood/Donors, Avoid Breastfeeding, Condoms
  • HTLV-2: Americas, PWID; No ATL, Milder HAM/TSP
  • HEV: Faecal-Oral, Zoonotic (Pigs), Rare Sexual; GT1 Pregnancy = High Mortality (20-30%); Chronic in Immunocompromised → Ribavirin
  • HHV-6/7: Roseola Infantum (HHV-6B), Reactivation in Transplant → Encephalitis, Bone Marrow Suppression
  • SARS-CoV-2: Respiratory Primary, Rare Sexual Transmission (Semen RNA Detected)

11. ❓ MCQs (10)

  1. CMV congenital infection — leading long-term sequela?

A. Microcephaly B. Sensorineural Hearing Loss C. Cardiac Defects D. Renal Failure

Answer: B. Sensorineural Hearing Loss = Leading Long-Term Sequela of Congenital CMV.

  1. EBV infectious mononucleosis — characteristic serology?

A. Heterophile Negative, VCA IgG+ B. Heterophile Positive, VCA IgM+, EBNA- C. Heterophile Positive, VCA IgM-, EBNA+ D. All Negative

Answer: B. Heterophile Positive, VCA IgM+, EBNA- = Acute IM.

  1. Zika virus sexual transmission — duration of precautions for men?

A. 1 Month B. 3 Months C. 6 Months D. 12 Months

Answer: B. Men: Condoms/Abstinence × 3 Months (Semen Shedding Up to 6 Months).

  1. HTLV-1 — associated malignancy?

A. B-Cell Lymphoma B. Adult T-Cell Leukemia/Lymphoma (ATL) C. Hodgkin Lymphoma D. Burkitt Lymphoma

Answer: B. Adult T-Cell Leukemia/Lymphoma (ATL) — HTLV-1 Associated.

  1. Zika congenital syndrome — characteristic features?

A. Macrocephaly B. Microcephaly, Cerebral Calcifications, Arthrogryposis, Ocular Abnormalities C. Hydrocephalus Only D. Cardiac Defects

Answer: B. Microcephaly, Cerebral Calcifications, Ventriculomegaly, Arthrogryposis, Ocular Abnormalities, Hypertonia.

  1. CMV retinitis in HIV (CD4<50) — first-line treatment?

A. Acyclovir B. Ganciclovir 5mg/kg IV q12h → Valganciclovir 900mg BD C. Foscarnet D. Cidofovir

Answer: B. Ganciclovir IV → Valganciclovir Oral Maintenance (1st Line).

  1. HTLV-1 prevention — key measure for mothers?

A. Cesarean Section B. Avoid Breastfeeding C. Antivirals in Pregnancy D. Cesarean + Antivirals

Answer: B. Avoid Breastfeeding (Major Vertical Transmission Route via Breast Milk).

  1. Zika virus — semen shedding duration for sexual transmission precautions?

A. 1 Month B. Up to 6 Months (Men: 3 Months Condoms/Abstinence) C. 12 Months D. 3 Months

Answer: B. Semen Shedding Up to 6 Months → Men: Condoms/Abstinence × 3 Months.

  1. EBV-associated malignancy — endemic Burkitt lymphoma hallmark?

A. t(14;18) B. t(8;14) (MYC-IGH) C. t(9;22) D. t(11;14)

Answer: B. t(8;14) (MYC-IGH Translocation) = Hallmark of Endemic Burkitt Lymphoma.

  1. HEV in pregnancy — genotype with highest mortality?

A. GT3 B. GT1 C. GT4 D. GT2

Answer: B. HEV GT1 in Pregnancy (3rd Trimester) = High Mortality (20-30%).

12. 📋 SBAs (10)

  1. Newborn with petechiae, hepatosplenomegaly, hearing loss. Mother had flu-like illness at 20 weeks. Diagnosis?

A. Toxoplasmosis B. Congenital CMV C. Congenital Rubella D. Congenital Syphilis

Answer: B. Congenital CMV (Hearing Loss, Hepatosplenomegaly, Petechiae).

  1. Young adult with fever, pharyngitis, posterior cervical lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly. Monospot positive. Diagnosis?

A. Acute HIV B. EBV Infectious Mononucleosis C. CMV Mononucleosis D. Acute HCV

Answer: B. EBV IM (Classic Presentation + Heterophile Positive).

  1. Pregnant woman at 16 weeks returns from Zika-endemic area. Asymptomatic. Management?

A. No Testing B. PCR Blood/Urine if <7 Days, IgM if >7 Days; Serial Ultrasound (Microcephaly, Calcifications) C. Immediate Delivery D. Termination

Answer: B. Test (RT-PCR <7d, IgM >7d); Serial Ultrasound (Microcephaly, Calcifications); Counselling.

  1. HTLV-1 positive mother. Infant feeding recommendation?

A. Exclusive Breastfeeding B. Formula Feeding (Avoid Breastfeeding — Vertical Transmission) C. Mixed Feeding D. Pasteurised Breast Milk

Answer: B. Avoid Breastfeeding (Major Vertical Transmission Route via Breast Milk).

  1. HIV+ patient (CD4 30) with CMV retinitis. First-line treatment?

A. Acyclovir B. IV Ganciclovir 5mg/kg q12h → Oral Valganciclovir 900mg BD C. Foscarnet D. Cidofovir

Answer: B. IV Ganciclovir → Oral Valganciclovir Maintenance (Standard 1st Line).

13. 🔑 Answer Keys

MCQsSBAs
1-B, 2-B, 3-B, 4-C, 5-B, 6-B, 7-B, 8-B, 9-B, 10-B1-B, 2-B, 3-B, 4-B, 5-B

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 15: Sexually transmitted infections

status: full-fcps-mrcp-topic-note

Syphilis Serology Interpretation (VDRL/RPR, TPHA/FTA-ABS)

Related: [[Syphilis (Primary, Secondary, Latent, Tertiary)]], [[Genital Ulcer Disease Syndrome (GUD)]], [[Congenital Syphilis]], [[Neurosyphilis]], [[HIV Infection and AIDS]]

[!important]
Two-test algorithm: Non-treponemal (RPR/VDRL) = quantitative, screens + monitors activity. Treponemal (TPHA/FTA-ABS/EIA) = qualitative, confirms exposure. Traditional: RPR screen → TPHA confirm. Reverse: EIA/CIA screen → RPR quantify.

Learning Objectives

  • Differentiate non-treponemal vs treponemal tests
  • Interpret all serological patterns (active, treated, false positive, prozone)
  • Apply traditional vs reverse algorithms
  • Monitor treatment response (4-fold titre drop)
  • Interpret syphilis in pregnancy, HIV, and congenital settings

Test Classification

CategoryTestsWhat DetectsQuantitative?Use
Non-treponemal (NTT)RPR (Rapid Plasma Reagin), VDRL (Venereal Disease Research Lab)Anti-lipoidal antibodies (cardiolipin-cholesterol-lecithin)YES (titre = disease activity)Screening, monitoring, activity
Treponemal (TT)TPHA (T. pallidum Haemagglutination), FTA-ABS, EIA/CIA, TPPA, MHA-TPAnti-T. pallidum specific antibodiesNO (reactive/non-reactive)Confirmatory, lifelong positivity

Key Differences

FeatureNon-treponemal (RPR/VDRL)Treponemal (TPHA/FTA-ABS)
AntigenCardiolipin-cholesterol-lecithinT. pallidum specific proteins
QuantitativeYes (titre)No (reactive/non-reactive)
Sensitivity (early primary)70–80%85–95%
Sensitivity (late latency)May revert negativeRemains positive for life
SpecificityLower (false + in pregnancy, autoimmune, infections)High (>99%)
After treatmentTitre drops (4-fold = cure)Remains reactive (lifelong)
Cost/TimeCheap, rapidMore expensive, longer

Testing Algorithms

Traditional Algorithm (Low Prevalence / Resource-Limited)

Screen: RPR/VDRL
    │
    ├─ Non-reactive → No syphilis (unless early primary / prozone)
    └─ Reactive → Confirmatory TPHA/FTA-ABS
                │
                ├─ Positive → **Syphilis confirmed** (quantify RPR for activity)
                └─ Negative → False positive RPR (unless early primary / prozone)

Reverse Algorithm (Automated / High-Throughput / High Prevalence)

Screen: Treponemal EIA/CIA/CLIA (high sensitivity)
    │
    ├─ Non-reactive → No syphilis
    └─ Reactive → Reflex RPR/VDRL (quantitative)
                │
                ├─ Reactive RPR → **Active/treated syphilis** (RPR titre = activity)
                └─ Non-reactive RPR → **Treated / late latent / early primary / prozone**
                              │
                              ↓
                        Confirmatory TPHA/FTA-ABS (if not done)
                │
                ├─ Positive → Treated / late latent / prozone
                └─ Negative → False positive EIA (rare)

Interpretation Framework

RPR/VDRLTPHA/FTA-ABSInterpretation
NegativeNegativeNo syphilis (or very early primary <2w)
PositivePositiveActive/treated syphilis — quantify RPR for activity
NegativePositiveTreated / Late latent / Prozone / Early primary
PositiveNegativeFalse positive RPR (or early primary / prozone)

Prozone Effect (Hook Effect)

  • Mechanism: Excess antibody prevents lattice formation → false negative RPR/VDRL
  • Suspect: High clinical suspicion + negative RPR + positive TPHA + high clinical risk
  • Resolution: Dilute serum (1:10, 1:100, 1:1000) → retest RPR → titre appears
  • Common: Secondary syphilis, early latent, high-titre cases

Interpretation by Clinical Stage

StageRPR/VDRLTPHA/FTA-ABSNotes
Early PrimaryNegative/low (70–80% +)Positive (85–95%)RPR may be negative; repeat in 2w
SecondaryHigh titre (1:32–1:1024)PositivePeak reactivity
Early Latent (<1yr)Positive (moderate-high)PositiveInfectious
Late Latent (≥1yr)Low/negativePositiveNon-infectious (except vertical)
Tertiary (Gumma/Cardio/Neuro)Variable (often low/neg)PositiveRPR may be negative
Post-treatmentDeclining → non-reactive at 12–24mRemains positive (lifelong)4-fold drop at 6–12m = cure
Re-infectionRising (4-fold increase)Remains positiveCompare to baseline

Treatment Response Monitoring (RPR Titre)

Serological Cure Criteria

StageTimepoint for 4-fold (2 dilution) DropAdequate Response
Primary / Secondary / Early Latent6 months4-fold drop at 6m = cure
Late Latent / Tertiary (non-neuro)12 months4-fold drop at 12m
Neurosyphilis6, 12, 24 months (CSF VDRL + cell count/protein)CSF normalisation

Treatment Failure Criteria

ScenarioAction
No 4-fold drop by 6m (early) / 12m (late)Re-treat; CSF exam if neuro possible
4-fold rise (from nadir)Re-infection or treatment failure → re-treat
Persistent high titre >6m (early) / >12m (late)Evaluate for re-infection / treatment failure / CSF exam

Special Populations

Pregnancy

TestFrequencyAction
RPR + TPHABooking, 28w, delivery (high prevalence)Treat stage-appropriate
Penicillin allergyDesensitisation mandatoryDoxycycline contraindicated; azithromycin insufficient

HIV Co-infection

FeatureDifference
RPR titresMay be lower; false negatives more common
Prozone effectMore common
TPHAMay be delayed/weaker
Neurosyphilis riskHigher — CSF exam recommended for ALL
Treatment responseSlower serological decline; lower threshold for re-treatment

Congenital Syphilis (Infant)

TestInterpretation
Infant RPR (quantitative)≥4-fold > maternal = probable congenital
Infant TPHA/FTA-ABSPassive transfer of maternal IgG (positive until 15–18m)
Infant IgM (FTA-ABS 19S-IgM)Positive = congenital infection (doesn't cross placenta)
CSF VDRLReactive = neurosyphilis
Long bones X-rayMetaphyseal lucencies, periostitis
ManagementIf mother adequately treated >4w before delivery + infant RPR ≤ maternal → observation; else full workup + treatment

False Positive RPR (Biological False Positive - BFP)

CauseFrequency
PregnancyCommon
Autoimmune (SLE, APS)Common
Acute infections (viral, bacterial, TB, malaria)Common
VaccinationsTransient
MalignancyOccasional
Drugs (procainamide, hydralazine)Rare
Age >65Higher baseline
BFP: RPR positive, TPHA negative, usually low titre (<1:8), transient

Neurosyphilis CSF Interpretation

CSF FindingInterpretation
WBC >5/µL (lymphocytic)Abnormal
Protein >0.45 g/L (>45 mg/dL)Abnormal
CSF VDRL (reactive)Diagnostic (specific but insensitive)
CSF FTA-ABSSensitive but not specific (blood contamination)
CSF PCR (T. pallidum)High sensitivity; emerging
CSF VDRL reactive + clinical picture = neurosyphilis (VDRL specific but insensitive)
CSF VDRL non-reactive does NOT exclude neurosyphilis

Rapid Point-of-Care Tests

TestTargetUse Case
Dual RPR/TPHA cassetteCombinedField screening (antenatal, outreach)
Syphilis RST (Rapid Syphilis Test)TP onlyScreening (antenatal, key populations)
Syphilis + HIV comboTP + HIV AbIntegrated screening
POC = screening only — all reactive must be confirmed by lab-based quantitative RPR + TPHA

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • RPR/VDRL = quantitative, activity; TPHA/FTA-ABS = confirmatory, lifelong
  • Traditional: RPR screen → TPHA confirm; Reverse: EIA screen → RPR quantify
  • Prozone: High antibody → false negative RPR → dilute serum
  • Interpretation: RPR+ TPHA+ = active; RPR- TPHA+ = treated/late; RPR+ TPHA- = false positive
  • Treatment response: 4-fold RPR drop at 6m (early) / 12m (late) = cure
  • Re-infection: 4-fold rise from nadir
  • Neurosyphilis: CSF VDRL reactive = diagnostic; FTA-ABS sensitive not specific
  • Pregnancy: Screen booking/28w/delivery; penicillin only cure (desensitise if allergic)
  • HIV: Lower RPR titres; prozone more common; CSF exam ALL; slower decline

Common Viva Questions

  1. Differentiate RPR vs TPHA.
  2. What is the prozone effect and how to resolve?
  3. How do you interpret RPR positive, TPHA negative?
  4. What is the criteria for serological cure?
  5. How do you interpret syphilis serology in pregnancy?

Common Confusions / Exam Traps

  • RPR negative, TPHA positive = treated/late latent/prozone/early primary (NOT active disease)
  • RPR positive, TPHA negative = false positive (pregnancy, autoimmune, viral) OR early primary OR prozone
  • Prozone = false negative RPR in high titre → dilute serum
  • RPR titre drop = 4-fold = 2 dilutions (e.g., 1:64 → 1:16)
  • TPHA stays positive for life — does not monitor treatment
  • RPR monitors treatment — TPHA does not
  • Congenital syphilis = infant RPR >4x maternal + IgM positive
  • POC tests = screening only; ALL reactive need lab confirmation

Mnemonics

  • RPR vs TPHA: RPR = Quantitative (activity); TPHA = Qualitative (confirmation)
  • Algorithm: Traditional = RPR → TPHA; Reverse = EIA → RPR
  • Interpretation: R+ T+ = Active; R- T+ = Treated/Late; R+ T- = False +/Prozone/Early
  • Prozone: Dilute Serum → Re-test = DSR
  • Cure: 4-fold drop = 2 dilutions (e.g., 1:64 → 1:16)
  • Re-infection: 4-fold rise from nadir

One-Page Revision Summary

Syphilis Serology = NTT (RPR) + TT (TPHA)

  • RPR/VDRL: Quantitative, activity, drops with treatment
  • TPHA/FTA-ABS: Qualitative, lifelong, confirms exposure
  • Traditional: RPR screen → TPHA confirm
  • Reverse: EIA screen → RPR quantify
  • R+ T+ = Active; R- T+ = Treated/Late/Prozone; R+ T- = False +/Prozone
  • Prozone: High Ab → false - RPR → dilute serum
  • Cure: 4-fold RPR drop at 6m (early) / 12m (late)
  • Re-infection: 4-fold rise from nadir
  • Neurosyphilis: CSF VDRL = diagnostic; FTA-ABS = sensitive not specific
  • Pregnancy: Screen booking/28w/delivery; penicillin only; desensitise if allergic

Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • NTT (RPR) vs TT (TPHA) purpose and interpretation
  • Traditional vs reverse algorithm
  • Prozone effect and resolution
  • Serological patterns (R+/T+, R-/T+, R+/T-)
  • 4-fold titre drop = cure criteria
  • Re-infection = 4-fold rise
  • Neurosyphilis CSF interpretation
  • Pregnancy screening and management

Should Know

  • Congenital syphilis workup (infant RPR, IgM, CSF)
  • False positive RPR causes (BFP)
  • Reverse algorithm nuances
  • HIV co-infection serology differences
  • Rapid POC test limitations

Nice to Know

  • EIA/CIA automation
  • T. pallidum particle agglutination (TPPA) as TPHA alternative
  • IgM FTA-ABS for congenital
  • Cost-effectiveness of screening
  • Syphilis vaccine development

MCQs (10)

  1. RPR (non-treponemal) test measures:

A. Anti-T. pallidum antibodies

B. Anti-cardiolipin (lipoidal) antibodies

C. T. pallidum antigen

D. IgM only

  1. TPHA (treponemal) test:

A. Quantitative, monitors treatment

B. Qualitative, remains positive for life

C. Detects lipoidal antibodies

D. Best for treatment monitoring

  1. Traditional syphilis algorithm:

A. TPHA screen → RPR confirm

B. RPR screen → TPHA confirm

C. EIA screen → TPHA confirm

D. RPR screen → EIA confirm

  1. Prozone effect:

A. Low antibodies → false positive

B. High antibodies → false negative RPR

C. Low antibodies → false negative

D. High antibodies → false positive

  1. Prozone resolution:

A. Concentrate serum

B. Dilute serum (1:10, 1:100)

C. Use different test

C. Heat serum

  1. RPR negative, TPHA positive =

A. Active syphilis

B. Treated / late latent / prozone / early primary

C. False positive TPHA

D. No syphilis

  1. RPR reactive, TPHA non-reactive =

A. Active syphilis

B. Treated syphilis

C. False positive RPR / early primary / prozone

D. No syphilis

  1. Serological cure (4-fold drop) at:

A. 3 months

B. 6 months (early) / 12 months (late)

C. 12 months all

D. 24 months

  1. Re-infection =

A. 4-fold drop

B. 4-fold rise from nadir

C. Positive TPHA

D. Negative RPR

  1. Neurosyphilis CSF — diagnostic test:

A. CSF FTA-ABS

B. CSF VDRL (specific, insensitive)

C. CSF WBC

D. CSF Protein

  1. Pregnancy syphilis screening:

A. Once at booking

B. Booking, 28w, delivery (high prevalence)

C. Only if high risk

D. Only at delivery

SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 30-year-old man, RPR 1:64, TPHA positive. Treated with Benzathine penicillin. At 6 months, RPR 1:4. Interpretation:

A. Treatment failure

B. Cure (4-fold drop: 1:64 → 1:16 → 1:4)

C. Re-infection

D. Need CSF exam

  1. A 25-year-old woman, pregnant, RPR 1:8, TPHA negative. Repeat RPR with dilution: 1:64. TPHA positive. Explanation:

A. False positive RPR

B. Prozone effect (dilution reveals true titre)

C. Early primary syphilis

C. Re-infection

  1. A patient treated for secondary syphilis. At 12 months, RPR 1:2 (was 1:128). TPHA positive. Interpretation:

A. Treatment failure

B. Cure (1:128 → 1:64 → 1:32 → 1:16 → 1:8 → 1:4 → 1:2 = 6 dilutions = 64-fold drop)

C. Re-infection

D. Late latent relapse

  1. A patient with RPR positive, TPHA negative. No symptoms. Most likely:

A. Primary syphilis

B. False positive RPR (pregnancy, autoimmune, viral)

C. Late latent

D. Neurosyphilis

  1. A patient treated for late latent syphilis. At 6 months, RPR unchanged at 1:16. Next step:

A. Observe

B. Re-treat + CSF exam (no 4-fold drop by 12m = failure)

C. Increase penicillin dose

D. Switch to doxycycline

  1. A patient with syphilis, HIV+, CD4 200. CSF: WBC 10, protein 0.55, VDRL reactive. Diagnosis:

A. Asymptomatic neurosyphilis

B. Symptomatic neurosyphilis (CSF VDRL reactive = diagnostic)

C. CNS lymphoma

D. Aseptic meningitis

  1. A pregnant woman at 16 weeks, RPR 1:16, TPHA positive, no prior treatment. Penicillin allergy (anaphylaxis). Management:

A. Doxycycline 100mg BD × 14d

B. Azithromycin 2g PO

C. Penicillin desensitisation → Benzathine penicillin G stage-appropriate

D. Ceftriaxone 1g IM × 10d

  1. A 6-month-old infant, mother had untreated syphilis at delivery. Infant RPR 1:128 (maternal 1:64). Infant TPHA positive. Interpretation:

A. Passive antibodies only

B. Probable congenital syphilis (infant RPR >4-fold maternal)

C. False positive

D. Carrier

  1. A man treated for secondary syphilis 3 years ago. RPR now non-reactive, TPHA positive. Interpretation:

A. Relapse

C. Re-infection

B. Treated (RPR seroreverted; TPHA remains positive for life)

D. False positive TPHA

  1. A 40-year-old man, RPR positive, TPHA positive. Blood donation screening. Counselling:

A. No syphilis

B. Confirmed syphilis — needs staging, treatment, partner notification

C. False positive

D. Past treated infection (RPR should be negative)

Flashcards

  • Q: RPR vs TPHA

A: RPR = quantitative activity; TPHA = qualitative confirmation (lifelong)

  • Q: Traditional algorithm

A: RPR screen → TPHA confirm

  • Q: Reverse algorithm

A: EIA/CIA screen → RPR quantify

  • Q: Prozone

A: High Ab → false - RPR → dilute serum

  • Q: RPR neg, TPHA pos

A: Treated / late latent / prozone / early primary

  • Q: RPR pos, TPHA neg

A: False + / early primary / prozone

  • Q: 4-fold drop

A: 2 dilutions (e.g., 1:64 → 1:16)

  • Q: Cure

A: 4-fold drop at 6m (early) / 12m (late)

  • Q: Re-infection

A: 4-fold rise from nadir

  • Q: Neurosyphilis CSF

A: CSF VDRL = diagnostic (specific, insensitive)

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQ 1: B — Anti-cardiolipin/lipoidal antibodies.

MCQ 2: B — Qualitative, lifelong positive.

MCQ 3: B — Traditional = RPR screen → TPHA confirm.

MCQ 4: B — High antibody → false negative RPR.

MCQ 5: B — Dilute serum resolves prozone.

MCQ 6: B — Treated/late latent/prozone/early primary.

MCQ 7: C — False positive / early primary / prozone.

MCQ 8: B — 6m (early), 12m (late).

MCQ 9: B — 4-fold rise from nadir.

MCQ 10: B — CSF VDRL specific (insensitive) = diagnostic.

MCQ 11: B — Booking, 28w, delivery.

SBA 1: B — 1:64 → 1:4 = 4 dilutions = 16-fold drop (cure).

SBA 2: B — Prozone = high Ab → false - RPR; dilute reveals true titre.

SBA 3: B — 1:128 → 1:2 = 6 dilutions (64-fold) = cure.

SBA 4: B — RPR+ TPHA- = false positive (pregnancy, autoimmune, viral).

SBA 5: B — No 4-fold drop by 12m = failure → re-treat + CSF.

SBA 6: B — HIV + CSF VDRL reactive = neurosyphilis.

SBA 7: C — Pregnancy + penicillin allergy = desensitisation mandatory.

SBA 8: B — Infant RPR >4x maternal = probable congenital.

SBA 9: B — Treated = RPR seroreverts; TPHA lifelong positive.

SBA 10: B — RPR+ TPHA+ = confirmed syphilis → needs staging/treatment.
